ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like I’m a burden to my family, especially after losing my job last year. Growing up, I rarely received praise or encouragement, which made me feel invisible and unworthy. These feelings have become more intense during stressful times like financial struggles or conflicts with close friends.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t meet others’ expectations, I am a failure and will be rejected. People only value me when I’m useful to them. I must avoid causing trouble or inconvenience because I am inherently flawed and undeserving of kindness.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to isolate myself when I feel overwhelmed, hoping no one will notice my struggles. Sometimes I push people away before they can reject me. I also distract myself with TV or social media to avoid thinking about how bad I feel.
ccd.situation: I missed a deadline at work because I was overwhelmed, and now my manager is disappointed and distant. I keep replaying the moment I realized I messed up, feeling like I’ve ruined everything.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m useless and can’t do anything right.” “Everyone must think I’m a failure.” “I don’t deserve to be here or have a second chance.”
ccd.emotions: I feel deeply ashamed, hopeless, and sad. There’s also a heavy sense of anxiety and loneliness that makes it hard to breathe.
ccd.behaviors: I withdrew from my coworkers and stopped responding to messages. I avoided asking for help, convinced it wouldn’t make a difference. Later, I canceled plans with friends, feeling too defeated to face anyone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um... it’s been okay, I guess. I’ve mostly just kept to myself and tried to get through work. Actually, I lost track of time watching this documentary about deep-sea creatures—it’s wild how they survive in total darkness. Anyway, work’s just... yeah, fine, nothing special.
What thoughts or feelings came up for you while you were watching the documentary or during your workday?

During the documentary, I was actually kind of fascinated—like, those creatures just exist, no one really sees them, but they’re still there, you know? At work, though, it was different. I just kept worrying about messing up again, even if it was just small stuff. Oh, and I guess I felt sort of... disconnected, like I was underwater too or something.
When you notice those worries about messing up at work, what do you tell yourself in those moments?

Usually, I just think, “Don’t mess this up again,” or, “Everyone’s probably annoyed with you.” Sometimes I can’t stop replaying tiny mistakes in my head, even if no one else noticed. It’s like, I try to act normal, but part of me is just waiting for someone to say I shouldn’t be there. Oh—uh, I guess sometimes I even imagine what it would be like if I just… faded into the background completely.
How does imagining fading into the background affect the way you feel and behave afterward?

Hmm, well, I mostly just feel numb after that, like it doesn’t even matter what I do. I end up avoiding people—I ignore messages or pretend I’m busy. Then I usually end up scrolling on my phone for hours or watching random videos so I don’t have to think. Oh, that reminds me—my friend texted me about hanging out, but I left it on read because I just couldn’t deal with it.
What do you think might happen if you responded to your friend’s message, even just with a small reply?

I don’t know…I guess maybe it would be fine, but part of me worries I’d have to pretend everything’s okay, and that sounds exhausting. Or maybe I’d say the wrong thing and seem weird, and then they’d just stop reaching out. Actually, sometimes I feel like I’m just extra baggage in their life. Sorry, I’m kind of rambling—I just remembered this time in high school when I didn’t text someone back, and then we just stopped talking for good.
When you think back to that high school experience, what thoughts or feelings come up for you about yourself or your relationships?

Uh… I guess I mostly feel… embarrassed, and kind of like it was all my fault for not being a better friend. I worry people just move on easily if I’m not there, like I’m forgettable or not really needed. Sometimes I get this heavy, sinking feeling, like maybe I never really mattered that much. I don’t know—sometimes I just start thinking about all the times I’ve let people down, and it’s hard to stop.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like things happen to me without my control, especially in social settings where I end up feeling powerless or ignored. Growing up, I was frequently overlooked and told that I couldn’t change my circumstances, which made me believe I was stuck as a victim.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I speak up or try to change things, I’ll just be dismissed or hurt even more. People aren’t on my side, and it’s safer to keep my distance or avoid conflict because I can’t really influence what happens.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid social gatherings or stay on the sidelines during conversations to protect myself from rejection. Sometimes I withdraw emotionally or distract myself with unrelated topics when conversations get uncomfortable.
ccd.situation: At a party, I notice people laughing and talking but feel disconnected because no one seems to include me in their conversations, making me feel invisible and powerless.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “Why bother trying to join in? They don’t want me here. I’m just going to get ignored or talked over like always.”
ccd.emotions: I feel sad, lonely, and frustrated. There’s also a sense of helplessness and anxiety that I won’t be able to change anything.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet and avoid making eye contact, often drifting off into my own thoughts or changing the subject abruptly if someone tries to talk to me. Sometimes I leave early to avoid feeling worse.

messages.content: How has your week been for you so far?

Um, it’s been… okay, I guess. Work was kind of the same as usual, nothing exciting. Oh, but my neighbor’s cat got stuck in a tree again, which is the third time this month—funny how cats just keep doing that. Anyway, I didn’t really do much outside of that.
When you noticed the cat stuck in the tree, what were you thinking or feeling in that moment?

Oh, I mostly just watched from my window. I mean, I considered going out to help, but then there were already a bunch of people outside trying to get the cat down. It just made me feel a bit weird—like, I don’t know, out of place, like I wouldn’t really be needed even if I went over. Reminded me of school, actually, when everyone would rush to help the teacher and I’d just sort of hang back.
What do you think it meant about you back then when you felt out of place or like you wouldn’t be needed?

Hmm…I don’t know, I guess it just made me feel like I was kind of invisible. Like, if I wasn’t there, it wouldn’t really make any difference. Actually, I remember once during a group project in high school, no one even noticed when I didn’t show up for one of the meetings because they just went ahead without me. Sometimes I wonder if it’s just easier for people to forget I’m there.
What feelings come up for you now when you think about being invisible or forgotten like that?

Uh, I guess it makes me kind of sad, honestly. Sometimes there’s this sort of heavy feeling, like a lump in my chest or something. I try not to dwell on it too much, though—I usually just distract myself, maybe by watching TV or reading random articles online. The other day I ended up reading this really long piece about deep-sea creatures, which is wild, did you know some fish can literally glow in the dark?
When that sadness or lump in your chest comes up, what do you usually tell yourself about it or about yourself in those moments?

Um… I don’t know, I guess I just tell myself it’s not a big deal, or that I probably wouldn’t have made much difference anyway. Sometimes I just try to brush it off and think, “Well, that’s just how it is.” Other times I wonder if maybe it’s my own fault for not being more outgoing or interesting. But mostly I just end up zoning out or changing the subject in my head—like, suddenly I’ll remember I need to do laundry or I’ll start thinking about what to make for dinner.
When you tell yourself it’s not a big deal or that it’s your fault for not being more outgoing, how does that affect the way you feel or behave afterward?

Well, I guess it just makes me kind of…pull back even more. Like, I’ll avoid texting people or skip out on things where I might have to talk much. Sometimes I don’t even bother trying to start conversations, just in case it goes the way I expect. Oh, and then I end up spending way too long scrolling on my phone or watching videos about things I don’t even care about—yesterday I watched some guy build a table out of bottle caps for an hour.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt like my opinions didn’t matter at home, and no matter what I did, I couldn’t change how things were. Family conflicts usually ended with me feeling stuck and powerless to improve the situation.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to speak up, it won’t make any difference because I’m always going to be controlled or ignored. Asking for help or setting boundaries will just cause more trouble or rejection.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid family conversations or withdraw emotionally to keep the peace, even if it makes me feel lonely. Sometimes I distract myself with work or other activities to escape the stress at home.
ccd.situation: During a recent family gathering, when a disagreement started, I felt overwhelmed and like I couldn’t say what I really wanted without causing more arguments.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “There’s no way out of this. I’m stuck in this family dynamic and nobody is going to listen to me.” “If I try to change anything, it will only get worse.”
ccd.emotions: Frustrated, helpless, anxious, and cornered.
ccd.behaviors: I stayed quiet, avoided eye contact, and physically withdrew from the group, hoping to avoid further conflict. Later, I isolated myself in my room to calm down.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

It’s been... I don’t know, just kind of exhausting. Family stuff again, and I just don’t really want to talk about it. Honestly, I’m tired of feeling like nothing changes no matter what I do. I don’t even know why I bother coming here.
That sounds really tough—when you say nothing changes, what kinds of things have you noticed staying the same despite your efforts?

It’s always the same arguments at home. No one really listens, everyone just talks over each other. If I try to say something, it gets ignored or shot down, so what’s the point? I just end up feeling frustrated and honestly kind of angry.
When those arguments happen and you feel ignored or shot down, what thoughts usually go through your mind in those moments?

I usually just think, “Here we go again, nothing’s going to change.” Sometimes it feels like it doesn’t matter what I say, it just gets lost or dismissed. Honestly, a part of me just wants to walk away or disappear in those moments. It makes me wonder why I even bother trying to talk at all.
That feeling of wanting to walk away or disappear sounds really painful—what do you notice happens inside you emotionally or physically when you have those thoughts?

I get this tight feeling in my chest, and my stomach twists up. Mostly, I just feel really tense and on edge, like I want to escape. I get angry, but also kind of hopeless, like there’s no win here. Sometimes I just want to hide or shut everyone out completely.
It sounds like those moments leave you feeling overwhelmed and stuck—when you notice those physical sensations and feelings of hopelessness, what do you usually do next?

Honestly, I just shut down. I stop talking and try to make myself as invisible as possible. Sometimes I’ll go to my room and just distract myself on my phone or with work, anything to get away from all of it. I don’t see what else I can do—nothing seems to help.
When you take that time to distract yourself or withdraw, does it provide any relief or change how you feel afterward?

I guess it helps in the moment because at least I’m not dealing with them directly. But honestly, it doesn’t last. The whole thing just keeps replaying in my head, and I end up feeling alone and even more frustrated. It’s like I’m just avoiding the problem, but I don’t know what else to do.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced ongoing criticism from supervisors and peers at work, leading to feelings of inadequacy. Past experiences of being blamed for team failures have reinforced a negative view of themselves. They often recall being told they let others down, which has deepened their sense of worthlessness.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I don’t meet everyone’s expectations perfectly, I am a bad person." "Making mistakes means I am immoral and untrustworthy." "People will reject me if they see the real me." These thoughts create pressure to hide flaws and avoid vulnerability.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to overwork to avoid mistakes and gain approval, often staying late and skipping breaks. They avoid asking for help because they fear being seen as incompetent. Sometimes they withdraw socially at work to hide their perceived flaws.
ccd.situation: The patient is facing tight deadlines and critical feedback from their manager about a recent project. They feel intense pressure to perform flawlessly and fear being judged harshly.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I'm a failure and I’ll just mess everything up again." "If I don’t fix this perfectly, it means I’m a terrible person." "They’re going to think I’m dishonest or lazy."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, and a deep sense of sadness dominate their experience. They feel overwhelmed and hopeless about their ability to improve or meet expectations.
ccd.behaviors: They work excessively long hours, double-checking their work repeatedly. They avoid discussing their struggles with colleagues or supervisors. At the end of the day, they often isolate themselves and ruminate over perceived mistakes.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Uh... it's been okay, I guess. Work's been pretty busy—lots of stuff piling up and some feedback from my manager. I keep thinking about what I could’ve done better. Just feels like there’s a lot riding on getting things right.
When you think about what you could have done better, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind?

I keep replaying what happened and wonder if I’ve messed things up for everyone else. It’s like, if I make a mistake, I start thinking that maybe I’m not as good as people need me to be. Sometimes I worry people will think I’m not trustworthy or that I’m just not trying hard enough. I guess I just feel like I let people down.
That sounds really tough. When you notice those thoughts about letting people down or not being good enough, how do those thoughts affect how you feel or what you do afterward?

It just makes me feel really anxious—like there’s a knot in my stomach. I get kind of quiet at work and try to keep to myself, so no one notices if I’m struggling. I end up working late to make sure I don’t miss anything or mess up again. Even then, it's like I can't shake the feeling that it's not enough.
When you work late to avoid making mistakes, do you notice any changes in how effective or focused you feel compared to during regular work hours?

Honestly, I get pretty tired and start second-guessing myself more when I’m working late. I keep checking my work over and over, but it doesn’t really make me feel more confident about it. Sometimes I catch small errors, but mostly I just feel drained. It’s like no matter how much I do, it never feels quite right.
It sounds exhausting to keep double-checking without feeling confident. What do you usually tell yourself when you notice those doubts or second-guessing creeping in?

Usually, I just tell myself I have to keep going or people will think I’m not good enough. I think if I work harder, maybe I can make up for whatever I did wrong. Sometimes I tell myself I can’t afford to mess up again. I don’t really have much else I say to myself—it’s mostly just pushing myself to keep going.
When you push yourself to keep going like that, what impact does it have on your mood or energy by the end of the day?

By the end of the day, I’m completely wiped out. I usually just feel empty, like I don’t have anything left. Sometimes I feel really down, and I just want to be alone. It’s hard to shake off that heavy feeling, even after I leave work.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated feelings of rejection and neglect in past relationships, both personal and professional. They often felt overlooked and undervalued during childhood and adolescence, which has shaped their current self-view.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t meet everyone’s expectations, I will be rejected. People only like me if I’m perfect. I have to keep proving my worth to be accepted.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid speaking up at work to prevent conflict or criticism and overworks themselves to gain approval. They also withdraw socially when feeling overwhelmed by pressure.
ccd.situation: At work, the patient faces increasing demands and tight deadlines, feeling they must perform flawlessly to be accepted by colleagues and supervisors.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m not good enough to handle this.” “They probably think I’m useless.” “If I mess up, no one will want to work with me.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, fear of rejection, sadness, and a sense of loneliness.
ccd.behaviors: The patient works excessively long hours, avoids asking for help, and isolates themselves from coworkers during breaks to hide their perceived inadequacies.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been… alright, I guess. Work’s been really busy, so I’ve been a bit tired. Just trying to keep up with everything. Honestly, nothing too exciting.
When you say you’re trying to keep up with everything at work, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind during those busy moments?

Mostly I just worry that I’m going to mess something up. Sometimes it feels like everyone else has it together except me. I keep thinking that if I make a mistake, people will notice and think less of me. It gets kind of overwhelming.
That sounds stressful. When you notice those worries about making mistakes or what others might think, how do you usually respond or cope in the moment?

Usually, I just try to work harder and not let anyone see I’m struggling. I stay at my desk and don’t really talk to people during breaks. I don’t want to draw attention to myself or seem like I can’t handle things. So I just keep my head down and push through.
It sounds like you’re putting a lot of pressure on yourself to manage things quietly. How does pushing through without reaching out or taking breaks affect how you feel during and after work?

During work, I feel tense and anxious most of the time. Afterward, I’m just completely drained, like I have nothing left. Sometimes I feel pretty alone, like I’m just invisible to everyone. It’s hard to relax, even when I get home.
When you notice feeling tense, drained, or alone after work, what do you tell yourself about those feelings or about yourself in those moments?

Honestly, I just tell myself to suck it up and that I need to do better. I feel like maybe I’m just not strong enough, or I should be able to handle things like everyone else. Sometimes I wonder if people even notice I’m there at all. I mostly just keep it to myself and try not to think about it too much.
That sounds really tough to carry on your own. When you tell yourself you need to do better or question your strength, how does that impact your motivation or your mood throughout the day?

It definitely makes it harder to feel motivated. I just feel kind of stuck, like no matter how much I do, it’s never enough. My mood drops a lot, and I start to feel pretty hopeless. It’s like I’m just going through the motions.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored or powerless when family conflicts arose, and my opinions were rarely taken seriously. This has made me believe that I can’t influence what happens around me, especially in family matters.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to speak up, it won’t make any difference, and I’ll just get dismissed. People won’t listen to me because I’m not capable of changing things. It’s safer to stay quiet and avoid conflict.
ccd.coping_strategies: I keep my feelings to myself and avoid bringing up problems to prevent arguments. Sometimes I just go along with what others want, even if it bothers me, to keep the peace. I might also withdraw or avoid family gatherings to escape the stress.
ccd.situation: During a family discussion about plans or problems, I feel like my input is overlooked or ignored, and I can’t make my voice heard.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one cares what I think.” “I’m powerless to change anything.” “If I say something, it won’t matter.”
ccd.emotions: Frustration, sadness, and helplessness. Sometimes I also feel anxious or resigned.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet during family talks and avoid sharing my opinions. I might physically withdraw or emotionally shut down to protect myself from feeling ignored.

messages.content: How has your week been for you so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Nothing too exciting. There was a family dinner a couple of days ago, but I mostly just listened. I didn’t really say much.
When you noticed yourself mostly listening and not saying much at the dinner, what thoughts were going through your mind?
I just kept thinking that there wasn’t really a point in saying anything. Like, even if I tried, nobody would really take it seriously or listen. I guess I felt like it was easier to just keep quiet.
How did feeling like it was easier to keep quiet affect the way you felt during and after the dinner?

During the dinner, I felt kind of invisible, honestly. It was frustrating, but also familiar. Afterward, I just felt a bit sad—I guess kind of empty, too. Sometimes I wonder if anyone even notices I’m there.
When you reflect on those feelings of invisibility and sadness, what do you think those feelings are trying to tell you about what you need or want in those moments?

I think I just want to feel like I matter, you know? Like my thoughts or opinions actually count for something. It’d be nice to feel heard, instead of just fading into the background every time. I guess I wish I could speak up without worrying it won't change anything.
What do you think might happen if you tried speaking up more during those family dinners—what are you hoping for, and what are you worried about?

If I did try to speak up, I guess I’d hope for someone to actually listen or at least acknowledge what I’m saying. But honestly, I worry they’d just brush me off or ignore me, like before. I don’t really want to deal with that feeling again. It feels safer not to risk it.
When you imagine that you might be brushed off or ignored, what do you tell yourself about yourself or your place in the family?

I start thinking that maybe I just don’t matter as much as everyone else does. Like, my opinions just aren’t important. Sometimes it feels like I’m just there, but not really part of things. It makes me wonder if I’m ever really going to fit in or be valued.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like no matter what I do, people don’t really accept me. In past jobs, I’ve worried that my colleagues think I’m not good enough or that they’ll eventually push me out. This has made me keep to myself and avoid sharing too much at work.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I open up or show my true self, others will see my flaws and reject me. I must stay quiet and avoid drawing attention to avoid being judged or excluded. People’s approval is something I have to earn, but deep down, I don’t believe I ever will.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to stay very reserved and avoid speaking up in meetings. I often keep my opinions to myself and avoid socializing with coworkers outside of work. Sometimes I overwork to try to prove my worth silently, even though it leaves me exhausted.
ccd.situation: At work, I’m under pressure to contribute ideas during a team project, but I feel unsure about sharing because I fear others will reject or criticize me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I say something, they’ll think I’m stupid or annoying.” “They probably don’t want me here anyway.” “I’m going to mess this up and everyone will see I don’t belong.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and a deep sense of loneliness. I feel tense and afraid that I’m going to be rejected or excluded.
ccd.behaviors: I stay silent during meetings and avoid interacting with coworkers. I withdraw from group activities and focus on my tasks alone, trying to keep my head down.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too out of the ordinary. Work’s been a bit busy. I haven’t really done much else.
When you say work has been busy, what kind of thoughts or feelings come up for you during those busy moments?
I don’t know. I guess I just feel kind of tense when there’s a lot going on. Sometimes I worry I might mess something up. It’s just… stressful, I guess.
What do you notice happening in your body or mind when those worries about messing something up come up?
Um, I get kind of jittery, I think. My chest feels tight and it’s hard to focus. My mind just keeps replaying the same worries over and over. It’s… uncomfortable.
When your mind keeps replaying those worries, what kinds of thoughts tend to repeat themselves?

It’s usually stuff like, “What if I say or do the wrong thing?” Or, “People will think I don’t know what I’m doing.” Sometimes I just feel like I shouldn’t speak up at all. I don’t know, I guess I just think people notice when I mess up.
Have there been times when you spoke up or made a mistake at work, and things turned out better than you initially feared?
I mean… maybe a couple times. I don’t really remember much, though. Most of the time I just try not to put myself in that position. It just feels safer that way.
What do you think might happen if, instead of avoiding those situations, you tried speaking up and noticed what actually occurs afterward?
I don’t know. I guess part of me thinks I’d just end up embarrassing myself. Or people might look at me differently. It’s just hard to imagine it going well, honestly.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has struggled with managing finances for several years, often feeling overwhelmed by bills and budgeting. Past experiences include repeated job changes and financial setbacks, reinforcing a sense of inadequacy. Family messages growing up emphasized high achievement, making any financial difficulty feel like a personal failure.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t handle money perfectly, I’m going to mess everything up. People will see me as useless if I can’t control my finances. It’s safer to avoid dealing with money than risk proving I’m incompetent.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid looking at bills and financial statements, procrastinating on budgeting tasks. They may also withdraw socially when stressed about money to hide perceived failures. Occasionally, they seek reassurance from others but feel embarrassed doing so.
ccd.situation: Facing unexpected expenses and mounting bills, the patient feels unable to create a workable budget. They worry about not having enough money to cover essentials and fear disappointing family if they ask for help.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m completely useless with money." "I’ll never get this under control." "Everyone must think I’m a failure for not managing better."
ccd.emotions: Anxious, overwhelmed, ashamed, and defeated.
ccd.behaviors: Avoiding looking at bank statements or bills, procrastinating on financial tasks, withdrawing from conversations about money, and feeling unable to take proactive steps toward budgeting or seeking assistance.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it’s been okay, I guess. Just kind of stressful. Nothing really specific, just… normal stuff, I suppose.
When you say "stressful," what kinds of thoughts or worries tend to come up for you during those times?

I don’t know… I guess I just worry about stuff piling up. Sometimes I feel like I can’t keep up with everything, especially when things come up that I didn’t expect. It just makes me feel kind of anxious.
When you notice those feelings of anxiety about things piling up, what do you usually tell yourself in that moment?

Um… I mostly just think that I should be handling things better. Sometimes I tell myself I’m probably messing things up. I don’t really say much out loud, it’s just kind of this feeling in my head that I’m not doing enough.
That sounds really tough. When you have those thoughts about not doing enough or messing things up, how do those thoughts affect what you do next?

I guess I tend to just… avoid things. Like, I’ll put off dealing with stuff for a while. Usually I just distract myself or try not to think about it. I know that doesn’t help, but it’s hard to do anything else.
It makes sense that avoidance feels easier in the moment. Can you tell me about a recent time when you noticed yourself avoiding something you needed to do?

Um… yeah, I guess last week I got a bill in the mail, and I just left it on the table. I saw it sitting there, but I didn’t open it for a few days. I just didn’t want to look at it or think about it. I kept telling myself I’d get to it later.
When you thought about opening that bill, what feelings or images came up for you that made it hard to face it right away?

Honestly, I just felt really tense—like my chest tightened up. I pictured some huge number on the bill and me not being able to pay it. I worried that I’d already messed something up or missed something important. It just felt kind of hopeless, so I didn’t want to deal with it.

ccd.relevant_history: I often felt overlooked and criticized growing up, which made me believe I’m not good enough. Past relationships have reinforced my fear that I will let others down or be rejected.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t please others, they will be disappointed or leave me. I have to work hard to avoid being seen as a failure. Making mistakes means I’m worthless.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to agree with others even when I don’t want to, avoid conflict at all costs, and overextend myself to gain approval. Sometimes I withdraw when I feel like I’m failing.
ccd.situation: My partner didn’t respond to a message I sent and I started worrying that I did something wrong or that they’re upset with me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I must have messed up again.” “They probably think I’m annoying or useless.” “I’m such a failure; I can’t do anything right.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, shame, and a deep sense of helplessness.
ccd.behaviors: I send multiple follow-up messages to check in, try to act extra nice, and suppress my own needs to avoid conflict or rejection. Sometimes I isolate myself when I feel overwhelmed.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too out of the ordinary. I’ve just been trying to keep up with work and make sure everyone’s happy with me. Sorry, I probably don’t have much to complain about.
It sounds like you’ve been busy trying to meet others’ expectations—how do you feel when you’re focusing on making sure everyone’s happy with you?
I mean, I guess it feels normal for me. I just don’t want to let anyone down, you know? Sometimes it gets a bit stressful, but I can handle it. Honestly, it’s not a big deal.
When you notice that stress building up, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind about yourself or the situation?

Um… I guess I start wondering if I did something wrong, or if I’m bothering people. Like, I probably could’ve done things better. I just worry I’ll mess up and then people won’t want me around. Sorry, I know that probably sounds silly.
That worry sounds really tough to carry—what do you tell yourself when you start thinking you might have messed up or annoyed someone?

I usually just tell myself to try harder next time and not make a fuss about it. I try to make up for it by being extra helpful or nice, so maybe they’ll forget if I did something wrong. Sometimes I just try to ignore how I’m feeling, because I don’t want to seem needy or dramatic. I’m not sure if that’s the right way to handle it.
It sounds like you put a lot of pressure on yourself to fix things and keep your feelings hidden—how does that affect how you feel about yourself over time?

Yeah, I guess I do put pressure on myself, but I just don’t want to be a burden. Sometimes I feel pretty tired, or like I’m always worried about messing up. It can make me feel kind of… I don’t know, like I’m not good enough. But I try not to think about that too much.
When those feelings of not being good enough come up, what do you usually do or think to cope with them?

Usually I just try to distract myself—like, I’ll scroll on my phone or keep really busy. Sometimes I’ll reach out to people and try to be extra supportive, just to feel useful. Other times, I just shut down a bit and keep to myself. I don’t really talk about it with anyone.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has a long-standing history of feeling insecure about their appearance, often recalling childhood experiences where they felt rejected or ignored. Financial stress has recently intensified these feelings, making them believe they are less worthy or lovable.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I am not attractive, then people will not want to be close to me. I have to look a certain way to be accepted or valued. Money problems just prove I am failing and unattractive in every way.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Honestly? Not great. I can’t seem to get on top of anything lately, and everything just feels like too much. I don’t even know if talking helps. I just feel kind of stuck.
When you say you feel stuck, what kinds of thoughts are going through your mind in those moments?

I don’t know, it’s just… I keep thinking nothing’s going to get better, so what’s the point? I mess things up no matter what I do. I look in the mirror or at my bank account, and it just feels like proof I’m failing at life. Sorry, I know that sounds dramatic.

It doesn’t sound dramatic at all—those are really tough feelings. When you notice those thoughts about failing or things not getting better, how do you usually respond to yourself or what do you tend to do next?

Most of the time, I just…shut down. I’ll avoid people, or just scroll on my phone for hours. Sometimes I’ll cancel plans or just not answer texts because I don’t want anyone to see how much of a mess I am. I guess I just disappear a bit.
That sounds really isolating. When you avoid others or disconnect like that, what do you notice happens to your feelings or thoughts afterward?

Honestly, it just gets worse. I keep thinking about everything I’m messing up, and I can’t stop replaying it all in my head. It makes me feel even more alone, but I can’t bring myself to reach out. I just keep thinking people wouldn’t get it, or they’d just judge me.
It sounds like those thoughts about being judged or misunderstood are really strong—can you recall a recent time when you had one of those worries? What was going through your mind then?

Yeah, actually, the other day I bumped into an old friend at the store. I felt so embarrassed because I looked awful and was buying cheap stuff. The whole time, I was just thinking, “They probably think I’m pathetic.” I couldn’t wait to get out of there—I didn’t want them to see me like that.

That must have been really uncomfortable. When you noticed yourself thinking “They probably think I’m pathetic,” did you consider any other possible explanations for what your friend might have been thinking?
Not really, to be honest. In the moment it just felt like that had to be what they were thinking. I mean, why wouldn’t they? I looked terrible and I couldn’t even pretend things were okay.

I don’t know… I guess I just wonder if I’m doing enough. Sometimes I worry about making mistakes. It’s hard not to think about what could go wrong. I try not to dwell on it, though.

I usually just keep working and try to double-check everything. I don’t really ask anyone for help—feels easier to just handle it myself. Sometimes I’ll go over things a few extra times, just to be sure. Mostly I just try to stay out of people’s way.

I don’t know… I guess I just feel kind of worn out. Sometimes I wonder if I’m just making things harder for myself. But I can’t really shake the feeling that I need to be really careful. I don’t really feel any better about myself, if I’m honest.
What do you think would happen if you tried asking for help or sharing some of the workload with a colleague?
I’m not sure… I just worry they’d think I can’t handle things. Or maybe they’d be annoyed. I guess I’d rather not risk it. It just feels safer to keep quiet.
When those worries about others’ opinions come up, how do you usually respond to them?
I usually just keep it to myself. I try to push through and not let anyone see I’m struggling. I kind of put my head down and do the work. It’s just easier that way.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It's been okay, I guess. Just a lot on my mind with bills piling up. I probably could've handled things better. I just feel kind of tired.
When you say you could’ve handled things better, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind in those moments?
Mostly I just keep thinking I messed up. Like, if I’d been smarter about money, I wouldn’t be in this spot. It feels like I keep making the same mistakes. I don't know, it's frustrating.
What do you notice happening inside you emotionally and physically when you have these thoughts about messing up?
I feel kind of heavy in my chest, like a weight. My stomach knots up too. Emotionally, I just start feeling really down about myself. Sometimes I get anxious, like it’s hard to sit still.
When you notice those feelings of heaviness and anxiety, what do you usually do next? How do you respond or cope?

Honestly, I usually just keep it to myself. I’ll distract myself by working more or just scrolling on my phone. Sometimes I avoid looking at my bills altogether. I don’t really talk to anyone about it.
What do you think happens to your feelings when you choose to distract yourself or avoid the bills like that?

I guess they don’t really go away. It’s more like they just sit in the background, making me feel worse over time. Sometimes the anxiety comes back even stronger. It just feels easier to ignore it in the moment.
What might it be like to face those feelings directly, even for a short time, instead of pushing them away?

Honestly, it sounds kind of overwhelming. I worry it would just make me feel even worse, like I’d get stuck in those feelings. I’m not sure I’d know what to do with all of it. It just feels safer to avoid them.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

It’s been… okay, I guess. Work has been kind of tiring, and I went to this get-together with some people from the office. I just felt a bit out of place there. Nothing too dramatic, just the usual stuff, I suppose.
What was going through your mind when you felt out of place at the get-together?
Honestly, I just kept thinking that no one really wanted me there. I felt like if I tried to join in, I’d be interrupting or annoying them. It was like I didn’t fit in with the group at all.
When you noticed those thoughts, how did they affect what you did or how you felt in that moment?

I got really quiet and just sort of faded into the background. My chest felt tight, and I felt kind of embarrassed, like everyone could see I didn’t belong. After a while, I just wanted to leave, so I did. It was easier than trying to force myself to talk to anyone.
Looking back, can you think of any evidence that might challenge the idea that no one wanted you there?

I mean… I guess someone did say hi to me when I arrived. And a couple people smiled at me when they were talking. But I don’t know if that really means they wanted me there, or if they were just being polite. It’s hard for me to tell the difference.
What might it feel like to consider the possibility that those greetings and smiles were genuine signs that people were glad you were there?

It would feel… a bit strange, honestly. I’m not used to thinking that way. I guess it would make things feel less heavy, maybe a little less lonely. But it’s hard not to just doubt it or brush it off.
When those doubts come up, what do you usually say to yourself about your feelings or the situation?

Most of the time, I just tell myself that I’m probably imagining things, or that I’m being too sensitive. Sometimes I think, "You’re just not that interesting, so of course they wouldn’t want to talk to you." It’s like I just accept that I don’t really fit in. I try not to dwell on it, but it always kind of lingers in the back of my mind.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt powerless in my family, where decisions were made without my input and my feelings were dismissed. In past relationships, I’ve experienced situations where I was treated unfairly or ignored, reinforcing the sense that I have little control over how others treat me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I believe that no matter what I do, I won’t be able to change how others act toward me. I often think that standing up for myself will only make things worse or lead to rejection. Deep down, I feel like I am always at the mercy of other people’s choices and actions.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid confrontation and keep my feelings to myself because I fear it won’t help or might cause more pain. Sometimes, I withdraw emotionally or physically to protect myself from being hurt again. I also try to please others or go along with what they want to avoid conflict.

ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They don’t care what I think, just like everyone else.” “I’m always going to be ignored and walked over.” “There’s nothing I can do to change this; I’m stuck being the victim.”

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been… okay, I guess. Nothing too out of the ordinary happened, though I did have a few arguments with my partner about what we were going to do this weekend. I just kind of backed off and let them decide in the end, because I didn’t want to make a big deal out of it. Honestly, I feel a bit tired from stuff like that, but maybe that’s just normal?
When those arguments came up, what were some of the thoughts going through your mind that made you decide to back off?

Well, I think part of me just sort of assumed that they wouldn't really care what I wanted anyway, so there didn’t seem like much point in pushing it. I kept thinking, “If I try to argue, it’s just going to turn into a fight or make things awkward between us.” I guess I felt kind of anxious about making the situation worse. So, it just seemed easier to let it go, even though I wasn’t happy about it.

That sounds like a difficult spot to be in—feeling anxious about conflict but also unhappy about stepping back; when you notice those thoughts about things turning into a fight, what emotions come up for you?

Yeah, it’s definitely tough. When I start thinking about it possibly turning into a fight or things getting tense, I feel this heaviness, like a mix of dread and sadness. Sometimes I just feel really small and sort of invisible, like my feelings don’t really matter in the bigger picture. I also get frustrated with myself for not being able to just say what I want without worrying so much.
That heaviness and frustration sound really hard to carry—when you feel small or invisible in those moments, what do you usually do to cope or respond to those feelings?

Usually, I just go quiet and stop trying to explain my side. I might make an excuse to leave the room or distract myself with something else, like scrolling on my phone or doing chores. Sometimes I just agree with whatever’s being decided, even if I don’t like it, just to get it over with. Other times, I just keep everything bottled up and try to move past it, though it doesn’t really make the feeling go away.

It sounds like you have a few ways to manage those feelings, but they don’t always help you feel better inside; when you reflect on those moments afterward, what do you think about yourself or the situation?

Honestly, afterward I usually feel pretty disappointed in myself. I’ll replay the conversation in my head and wonder why I couldn’t just speak up or why I always let things go so easily. Sometimes I end up feeling like I’m just letting people walk all over me, and that makes me feel even worse. It’s like, no matter what I do, I still end up feeling powerless or like my opinions just don’t matter.

That sense of powerlessness sounds really painful—when you notice those thoughts about feeling like you’re letting people walk over you, how do you think they might be affecting the way you feel or act in those situations?

Yeah, it really gets to me. I think those thoughts make me even less likely to speak up next time, because I start believing it won’t make a difference anyway. It turns into this cycle where I just expect not to be heard, so I don’t bother trying, and then I feel worse about myself afterward. Honestly, it just makes me want to give up and keep my opinions to myself, even when something really matters to me.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been… okay, I guess. Nothing too out of the ordinary. I mostly kept busy with work. I didn’t really do much else.
When you say "nothing too out of the ordinary," what kinds of thoughts or feelings have come up for you during your work or free time?
I haven’t really thought about it much. I just try to get through the day, you know? Sometimes I feel a bit anxious, but I just try to ignore it. It’s easier not to focus on that stuff.
What kinds of situations or thoughts tend to bring up that anxiety for you?
Um… I guess when I’m around people, especially family. I get this feeling like I shouldn’t say much. Sometimes I worry that if I talk, I’ll say the wrong thing. So, I kind of keep to myself.
When you notice those worries about saying the wrong thing around family, what do you tell yourself in those moments?
I usually just tell myself to stay quiet. I think… if I don’t say anything, I can’t mess up. It feels safer that way. I don’t really trust that what I say would be okay.
How does staying quiet in those moments affect how you feel afterward and your connection with your family?
I don’t really feel great afterward. I just end up feeling kind of alone, like I’m not really part of things. But at least there’s no argument or anything. I guess I just sort of accept it.
What do you think might happen if you tried to share a small thought or feeling with your family, even if it felt a bit uncomfortable?
Honestly, I think it would just make things awkward. I worry they’d look at me weird or maybe get annoyed. I feel like I’d regret it after. It’s just easier not to try.

I believe that others will reject me once they see who I really am, so it’s safer to keep my distance or to not try at all. I feel like I have to be perfect to avoid being seen as evil or dangerous.

Sometimes I push people away before they can get close enough to see the “real me,” and other times I become overly self-critical or harsh with myself to try to control my negative feelings. At moments, I also withdraw completely, feeling like hiding is the only way to protect others from my “badness.”

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um… I guess it’s been okay, but honestly, it’s been a bit stressful. Work’s been pretty busy, and something happened that kind of threw me off. I’ve just been feeling a little on edge, I guess—it’s hard to explain. I’m not sure if it’s even worth bringing up, really.
It sounds like something at work has been weighing on you—what happened that threw you off?

Yeah, it’s… well, my manager asked if I wanted to take on this new responsibility. You’d think I’d be excited, but honestly, my first reaction was just this wave of anxiety. I kept thinking, “What if I can’t handle it?” and then I started worrying that I'd just mess everything up. I kind of froze and haven’t replied to the offer yet, and I’ve been avoiding talking to my manager because I don’t know what to say.
When you imagine taking on this new responsibility, what specific thoughts or images come to mind about what might happen?

I keep picturing myself making some big mistake in front of everyone, like forgetting something important or saying the wrong thing in a meeting. I imagine people looking disappointed or frustrated with me, maybe even talking about how I wasn’t up for it after all. Sometimes it’s almost like I can hear people thinking, “Why did we even trust them with this?” It just feels like all my flaws would be totally obvious if I tried to step up.
That sounds really tough—when you notice these thoughts about making mistakes and others’ reactions, how does that affect how you feel physically or emotionally in those moments?

Yeah, it’s actually kind of overwhelming, honestly. My chest gets tight, and I feel sort of shaky or restless, like I need to escape or hide. Emotionally, I just feel this sinking dread and embarrassment, like I’ve already messed up even if nothing’s happened yet. There’s this heavy sense of shame, and I get this urge to pull away from everyone or just disappear.
When you notice those feelings of dread and shame, what do you usually do next—how do you respond or cope in those moments?

Um… I guess I try to avoid whatever’s making me feel that way. So, like with the new responsibility at work, I just kind of put off replying and kept myself busy with other things so I wouldn’t have to think about it. I also stop talking as much to people around me, and I don’t share my ideas in meetings because I’m afraid I’ll say something stupid. Most of the time, I just find ways to keep my head down and not draw any attention to myself, even if it means missing out.
It sounds like avoiding the situation provides some relief but might also be limiting you—what do you think might happen if you did share your ideas or respond to your manager despite those fears?

Honestly, I worry that if I spoke up or accepted the responsibility, I’d just end up proving everyone right—that I’m not actually capable or trustworthy. Part of me imagines I’d say something wrong or make a mistake, and then people would see that I’m not who they thought I was. I’m scared they’d lose respect for me, or maybe even regret giving me a chance. I know it probably sounds dramatic, but that’s just where my mind goes.

ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid challenging tasks and withdraws from situations where they might be judged. They also engage in self-criticism and second-guess their decisions, which sometimes leads to procrastination.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been a little stressful. I’ve just been trying to keep up with things. Nothing major, really.
What kinds of thoughts go through your mind when you’re feeling stressed at work?

I usually just worry that I’m not doing things right. Sometimes I feel like I’m going to make a mistake, even with simple stuff. It’s like, I keep thinking I’m not measuring up, you know? And then I kind of freeze up.
When you notice yourself thinking you’re not measuring up or might make a mistake, what emotions do you experience in that moment?

I feel pretty anxious, mostly. Sometimes embarrassed, even if nobody’s said anything to me. There’s also this heavy feeling, like I’m letting people down. It just makes me want to hide or avoid things.
What do you usually do or how do you respond when you feel that anxious and want to hide or avoid things?

Honestly, I just try to keep my head down. I don’t really speak up in meetings or ask questions, even if I’m confused. Sometimes I’ll put off starting stuff, hoping it’ll get easier later. I just don’t want anyone to notice if I mess up.
Can you think of a specific recent situation at work where you felt this way and kept your head down or avoided speaking up? What happened right before and after?

Yeah, actually, last week my manager introduced a new project during a team meeting. As soon as she started explaining what we had to do, I felt nervous because I wasn’t sure I could handle it. I didn’t say anything even though I had questions, just nodded along. After the meeting, I just went back to my desk and tried to figure it out on my own, but I kept overthinking everything and barely got started.
When you thought about the new project and your ability to handle it, what specific thoughts went through your mind during and after the meeting?

During the meeting, I just kept thinking, “There’s no way I can do this,” or “I’m going to screw this up.” Afterward, it was more like, “Everyone else probably gets it but me,” and “If I ask for help, they’ll know I don’t belong here.” Those kinds of thoughts just kept looping in my head. It made it really hard to focus on actually starting the work.
