These experiences have reinforced a deep sense that no matter what I do, I won’t have the strength or resources to improve my situation. Over time, this has made me doubt my own abilities and feel increasingly isolated.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I believe that trying to take control or make changes is pointless because I don’t have the inner strength to make things better. I also worry that if I show any vulnerability, others will see me as weak and lose respect for me. These thoughts create a mindset where I expect failure and feel like giving up is the safest option.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid taking on new challenges or setting goals because I fear I will fail and prove myself powerless. Sometimes, I withdraw from social situations or don’t ask for help, convincing myself that no one can really support me anyway. When I do try, I often procrastinate or give up quickly to protect myself from feeling overwhelmed.
ccd.situation: I was recently offered a chance to lead a small project at work, which could help me grow professionally. However, I immediately felt overwhelmed and doubted my ability to handle the responsibility or meet expectations. The idea of stepping up felt daunting, and I started worrying about making mistakes or disappointing others.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to mess this up because I’m not strong enough to handle the pressure.” “If I ask for help, people will think I’m weak and unreliable.” “No matter what I do, I’ll just end up failing and proving I’m powerless.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious and scared, like a knot tightening in my chest. There’s also a deep sadness and frustration that I can’t seem to overcome these feelings or prove to myself that I’m capable. Sometimes, I feel hopeless and resigned, like nothing I do will change the outcome.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid volunteering for the project and try to delegate tasks or stay in the background as much as possible. I also procrastinate on any related work, distracting myself with less important activities. When forced to engage, I shut down emotionally and don’t communicate my needs or concerns, which makes the situation even harder to manage.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, I guess it’s been okay, but also kind of stressful. Work has been busy, and I’ve felt a bit on edge, honestly. There was this thing at work I was asked to do, and I just... I don’t know, I’ve been kind of caught up thinking about it all week. It’s hard to explain, but I just can’t shake this anxious feeling.
Can you tell me more about the specific thoughts that come up for you when you think about the task at work?

Yeah, um... when I think about it, I just keep thinking I’m probably not going to be able to do it right. Like, I imagine all these ways I could mess up, or just fall short of what people expect from me. I keep worrying that everyone will notice if I struggle or ask questions, and then they’ll think I’m not good enough. It just… puts a lot of pressure on me, and I end up doubting myself before I even start.
When those worries about not doing it right come up, how do you usually respond or cope with those feelings in the moment?

Honestly, I tend to just freeze up a bit or try to distract myself with other things that feel easier. Sometimes I’ll spend ages re-reading emails or overthinking the smallest details, hoping I won’t overlook anything important. Other times, I just avoid dealing with the task altogether—like, I’ll find something else to do or just put it off. It’s like, if I don’t start, then I can’t fail, you know?
That makes a lot of sense. When you notice yourself avoiding or getting stuck in those moments, what do you think that experience feels like in your body?

Um, it’s kind of hard to describe, but I usually feel this tightness in my chest, like it’s hard to breathe deeply. My shoulders get tense, and sometimes my stomach feels knotted or unsettled. There’s this heaviness, like I’m weighed down, and it just makes me want to curl up or disappear. It’s exhausting, honestly.
Thank you for sharing that. What do you notice tends to happen afterward—how do these physical sensations and feelings affect what you do next or how you think about yourself?

Afterward, I usually just feel pretty drained and frustrated with myself. I start thinking that I’m just not cut out for things like this, like maybe it’s just who I am. There’s this disappointment, and I end up beating myself up a bit for not handling things better. It kind of makes me want to avoid similar situations even more, because I don’t want to go through all of that again.

That sounds really tough. When you find yourself thinking that you’re “not cut out for things like this,” have you noticed any times when those thoughts might have been challenged or contradicted by something you did or experienced?

Um, I guess there have been a few times when things went better than I expected, but honestly, it’s hard for me to really believe it in the moment. Even if something works out, I usually end up thinking it was just luck, or maybe the task wasn’t actually that hard. It’s like I can’t really give myself credit, so I just go back to thinking I’m probably not good enough for bigger challenges. I don’t know why it’s so hard for me to see the positives.

ccd.relevant_history: I have struggled with feeling like I’m not good enough in relationships since childhood, and financial stress has always made me doubt my worth even more. Past experiences where I was criticized for money issues have reinforced these feelings.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m struggling financially, it means I’m a failure and people will reject me. I have to be perfect to be loved, but I never am. Others will see my money problems as proof I’m unlovable.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid talking about money or asking for help because I’m afraid of being judged. Sometimes I overwork myself to try to prove my value, but then I get exhausted and withdraw emotionally.
ccd.situation: I received a notice that I’m behind on some bills, and I’m worried about how my partner will react when they find out. I feel like this will confirm they don’t really care about me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They’re going to think I’m worthless and stop loving me.” “I can never get anything right, so why even try?” “If they knew how bad my finances are, they’d leave.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, ashamed, and deeply sad. There’s a heavy sense of loneliness and fear that I’m unlovable.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid talking to my partner about money and hide the bills. I isolate myself and sometimes snap at others out of frustration. I also distract myself with TV or social media to avoid facing these feelings.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been… I don’t know, not great. Stuff keeps piling up, honestly. I’m just tired of always feeling behind and messing things up. Can we just talk about something else?
It sounds like you’re feeling overwhelmed and frustrated—can you tell me more about what’s been piling up for you lately?

I mean, it’s just bills and things I probably should’ve handled already. Every time I think I’m caught up, something else comes in and knocks me down again. I honestly don’t even want to look at the mail anymore. What’s the point if it’s just more bad news?
When you think about opening the mail and seeing those bills, what kind of thoughts usually come up for you?

I don’t know, it’s just... embarrassing. I feel like I messed up again and it’s my fault things are this way. Mostly I just think, “Great, I screwed up again—what else is new?” It makes me not want to talk to anyone about it at all.
That sounds really tough, carrying that sense of blame and isolation. What do you notice happens to your mood or energy when you have those thoughts?

I just shut down, honestly. I get really tired and don’t want to do anything. Sometimes I end up just zoning out in front of the TV or scrolling on my phone for hours. It’s like, what’s the point in even trying when I just mess it up anyway?
When you find yourself zoning out or scrolling for hours, does that help you feel better in the moment, or does it leave you feeling the same or worse afterward?

It helps for a bit, I guess—it distracts me so I don’t have to think about everything. But after, I usually just feel even more guilty for wasting time. It’s like I let myself down twice—first with the bills, then by avoiding it. It just never feels like enough, no matter what I do.

It sounds like there’s a cycle of trying to cope and then feeling guilty afterward—what do you think might happen if you tried a different way of handling those moments when you feel like avoiding the bills?

I don’t know. I mean, maybe it’d be better, but it feels like if I actually face it, I’m just opening myself up to more disappointment. What if it’s even worse than I thought? Honestly, I’m not sure I could handle that right now.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like I can’t handle things on my own and that I always need support from others. Growing up, I was told I was too sensitive and that I should just try harder, which made me question my abilities and rely heavily on others for reassurance.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t please others, they won’t like me or will leave me alone. I have to put others’ needs before my own to be accepted and cared for. Being needy means I’m weak and unworthy of respect.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to avoid conflict and say yes to everything, even when I feel overwhelmed. I seek constant approval and reassurance from friends and family to feel secure. Sometimes I push my own needs aside to keep the peace.
ccd.situation: I have a lot of things to do today, but I’m worried that if I ask for help or say no to some requests, people will get upset with me or think I’m selfish.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I don’t help them, they’ll think I’m a bad person.” “I’m such a burden when I ask for help.” “I need to keep everyone happy or they’ll leave me.”
ccd.emotions: Anxious, guilty, overwhelmed, and lonely.
ccd.behaviors: I say yes to every request, even if it means sacrificing my own time and energy. I avoid expressing my true feelings or needs to prevent upsetting others. I overextend myself and then feel exhausted and resentful afterward.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been a bit busy, but I’m managing. I’ve just been trying to keep up with everything and make sure everyone’s happy with me. Nothing too out of the ordinary, really.
When you say you’re trying to make sure everyone’s happy with you, what thoughts go through your mind in those moments?

Oh, um… I just don’t want to let anyone down, you know? I keep thinking that if I don’t do what people ask, they’ll be disappointed or maybe even upset with me. I guess I worry a lot about people’s opinions. I just really want to be helpful and not cause any trouble.
That sounds like a lot of pressure to carry—when you notice those worries about disappointing others, how do they affect how you feel or act in your day-to-day life?

Yeah, I guess it does feel like a lot sometimes. When those worries come up, I usually just say yes to whatever people need, even if I’m already busy. I don’t really tell anyone if I’m feeling stressed—I just kind of keep it to myself and try to handle it. I worry that if I say no or seem overwhelmed, people might think badly of me.
When you say yes even if you’re already busy, what impact does that have on your stress levels or energy throughout the day?

Honestly, I do end up feeling really drained by the end of the day. Sometimes I’m so tired I can’t focus or enjoy anything for myself. I try not to show it, though—I don’t want anyone to think I can’t handle things. I just push through and hope it’ll be okay.
When you push through despite feeling drained, what messages are you telling yourself about your own needs or limits?

Um… I guess I tell myself that my needs aren’t as important as everyone else’s. I feel like if I need help or take a break, I’m being selfish or weak. It’s almost like I shouldn’t complain, because other people rely on me. I worry that if I start putting myself first, people might not want me around as much.
That sounds really tough—when you notice those thoughts about your needs being less important, have there been times when you challenged or questioned those beliefs?

Hmm… I don’t think I really have, to be honest. I usually just accept it and keep going. Sometimes I wonder if it’s okay to ask for help, but I end up feeling guilty or worried afterward. I guess I’m afraid of what might happen if I actually put my needs first.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated rejection and criticism in past romantic relationships, leading to a deep sense of not being valued or worthy of love. They also had limited emotional support during childhood, reinforcing feelings of being unlovable. These experiences have shaped their expectations and reactions in current relationships.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "I’m not good enough to be loved," "If I show my true self, others will leave me," and "I have to be perfect to keep people around." These beliefs create a constant fear of abandonment and a tendency to doubt others' affection.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient often withdraws emotionally to avoid potential rejection or criticism and tries to please others excessively to gain approval. They also distract themselves with work or hobbies to avoid confronting feelings of loneliness and inadequacy.
ccd.situation: The patient notices their partner didn’t respond to a text message right away, which triggers worries about being abandoned or unloved. They start ruminating about what they might have done wrong and fear that the partner is losing interest.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They must be upset with me,” “I’m such a burden, no one really wants to be around me,” and “Maybe they’re going to leave me because I’m not lovable.” These thoughts spiral quickly and feel very real to the patient.
ccd.emotions: The patient feels anxious, sad, and rejected, with a growing sense of hopelessness and loneliness. There is also underlying shame about their perceived flaws and unworthiness.
ccd.behaviors: In response, the patient either sends multiple messages seeking reassurance or withdraws and avoids contact to protect themselves from possible rejection. This push-pull pattern often creates tension and misunderstandings in the relationship.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um, it’s been… okay, I guess. Work was super busy, so that kept me distracted most of the time. I did have a weird moment with my partner though—he took a while to text me back and I started overthinking, but it was probably nothing. Actually, that reminds me, did you know my cat has started sleeping on my laptop? Totally ruined one of my reports the other day.
That sounds frustrating about your laptop and report—how did you feel when your cat disrupted your work like that?

Yeah, it was kind of annoying, but honestly, I think I was more upset with myself for not saving the file more often. I always feel like I’m supposed to be more on top of things, you know? The cat… well, at least she wanted to be close to me, which is kind of sweet. Anyway, it was just another thing piling on after a long day—oh, speaking of, did you see the weather this week? It’s been so gloomy.
It sounds like you have some high expectations for yourself—what thoughts tend to come up when you feel like you’re not “on top of things”?

Hmm… I guess I start thinking that I should’ve known better or done better. Like, if I’d just paid more attention, maybe I wouldn’t mess things up so much. It’s kind of a familiar feeling—like, I always seem to fall short, even with small things. Oh! And it always seems to happen when I’ve got a deadline or something important coming up—timing, right?
When you notice those thoughts about falling short or not doing enough, how do they affect your mood or what you do afterward?

Well, honestly, it makes me feel pretty lousy, like this heavy thing in my stomach. Sometimes I just want to drop everything and hide for a bit—like, I’ll go for a walk by myself or just scroll on my phone for hours. Or, I’ll suddenly get this urge to fix everything and try to prove I can handle it, even if I’m exhausted. Oh, that reminds me, I started reorganizing my kitchen at midnight the other day, which probably wasn’t the best idea since I spilled pasta everywhere.
It sounds like those feelings lead to some different reactions—both wanting to escape and wanting to fix things—how do you decide which way to respond when you feel that heavy feeling?

Hmm, I don’t really think I “decide,” it just kind of happens. Sometimes if things feel too overwhelming, I just shut down and avoid everything—like, I’ll ignore texts or just zone out. Other times, I get this jittery energy and start cleaning or fixing things, like I’m trying to make up for messing up. It’s weird, ’cause even when I do all that, I still feel unsettled afterward—oh, and then I start worrying that people will notice I’m acting off.
When you notice people might be picking up on you feeling off or unsettled, what goes through your mind about how they might be seeing you?

I immediately start worrying they’ll think I’m too much or just annoying. Like, maybe they’ll get tired of me being anxious or weird and just decide to leave. Sometimes I wonder if I should just pretend I’m fine, so nobody gets fed up with me. Actually, the other day I caught myself smiling and joking around at dinner even though I wanted to just disappear—oh, and that restaurant had the worst lighting, it made my food look so strange!

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has a long-standing history of feeling undervalued at work, often doubting their contributions despite positive feedback. They grew up in an environment where achievements were rarely acknowledged, reinforcing feelings of inadequacy.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t please others, they will reject me. I must always put others’ needs before my own to be accepted. Making mistakes means I am a failure and not good enough.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient often goes out of their way to agree with colleagues and takes on extra tasks to avoid conflict. They avoid speaking up or asserting their own needs, fearing negative judgment or rejection.
ccd.situation: Facing tight deadlines and high expectations at work, the patient feels overwhelmed and worries they won’t meet others’ standards.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to mess this up and everyone will see I’m useless.” “If I don’t do everything perfectly, I’ll be a disappointment.” “I’m just a waste of space here.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and shame dominate the patient’s experience, often accompanied by a deep sense of hopelessness.
ccd.behaviors: The patient withdraws from team interactions, stays late to overwork, and avoids asking for help even when struggling, reinforcing their isolation and exhaustion.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been a little busy, but nothing I can’t handle. I just try to keep up and not let anyone down. Sorry, I don’t mean to complain—it’s all fine, really.
It sounds like you’re managing a lot and trying hard not to let others down; what goes through your mind when you feel that pressure to keep up?

Well, I guess I just worry about making mistakes, you know? Like, if I don’t do everything just right, people might think less of me. Sometimes I think maybe I’m not doing enough, even when I try really hard. Sorry, I know that probably sounds silly.
That worry about making mistakes and how others might see you sounds really important—what feelings come up for you when you notice those worries?

Um, I don’t know, I guess I feel kind of anxious, maybe even a bit embarrassed. Sometimes I get this heavy feeling, like I’m letting people down if I’m not perfect. I try not to show it, though—I don’t want to cause any trouble or be a burden. Sorry, I probably shouldn’t even be bringing this up.
Thank you for sharing that; feeling anxious and wanting to avoid being a burden makes a lot of sense. When you notice that heavy feeling or anxiety, how do you usually respond or cope with it?

Oh, um, I mostly just try to work harder and make sure everything gets done—like, I’ll stay late or double-check things over and over. I don’t really like to ask for help, since I don’t want people to think I can’t handle it. Usually, I just keep quiet about how I’m feeling. It seems simpler that way, I guess.

It sounds like you often push yourself harder and avoid asking for help to keep things under control; how do you feel afterward, once you’ve been working so intensely or keeping those feelings inside?

Honestly, I just feel really tired a lot of the time. Even when things go okay, I still wonder if I did enough or if people noticed I was struggling. Sometimes I feel kind of alone, but I don’t want to bother anyone with that. I just try to move on and hope next time will be easier.
Feeling tired and alone despite your efforts sounds really tough; what would it be like for you to share some of these feelings with someone you trust?

Oh, um, I’m not really sure—I don’t do that very often. I guess I worry they might think I’m just complaining or overreacting. I’d hate for anyone to think less of me or feel burdened by what I’m saying. It just feels safer to keep it to myself, I think.

ccd.relevant_history: I have often felt rejected by people I cared about, especially in past relationships where I was ignored or dismissed. Growing up, I rarely received positive attention or affection, which made me feel invisible and unwanted.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to be myself, people won’t like me. I have to make others happy to be accepted. If someone truly knew me, they would leave.
ccd.coping_strategies: I often agree with whatever others want, even if it’s uncomfortable for me, to avoid conflict or rejection. I avoid sharing my true feelings because I fear being judged or abandoned.
ccd.situation: I am talking with my partner about plans for the weekend, and I worry they might not want to spend time with me or might get annoyed if I suggest something different.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They probably don’t really want to be with me.” “If I say what I want, they’ll think I’m selfish.” “I’m going to mess this up and they’ll leave.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and feeling lonely. I also feel a deep sense of shame and fear of being abandoned.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet and agree with whatever my partner says, even if it means sacrificing my own desires. I avoid bringing up my needs or concerns to keep the peace.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting, just the usual stuff. I’ve just been trying to keep busy, you know. I hope that’s alright.
It sounds like staying busy has been important to you recently; what kinds of things have you been keeping yourself busy with?

Oh, mostly work things and a bit of cleaning around the house. I spent some time helping a friend move, too—I just wanted to be useful. I guess I try to help out wherever I can. I don’t know, I just like feeling needed, I suppose.
Feeling needed seems meaningful to you—what goes through your mind when you’re helping others or being useful?

Um, I guess I just feel... safer, maybe? Like, if I’m doing something for someone, they’ll be happy with me and not get upset. I worry a bit that if I don’t help, people might not want me around. I just want to make sure I don’t mess things up.
That sounds like a lot of pressure to put on yourself; when you imagine not helping or disappointing others, what feelings or thoughts come up for you?

Yeah, it definitely feels like a lot sometimes. If I think about not helping or letting someone down, I get really anxious. I start thinking they’ll be annoyed or… maybe just not want me around anymore. I guess I get kind of scared that people will leave.
Thank you for sharing that—it sounds really tough. When those anxious thoughts about people leaving come up, how do you usually respond or try to cope with them?

Um, I usually just try harder to do what they want, or I stay really quiet so I don’t bother anyone. Sometimes I just keep my own opinions to myself so I don’t risk making anyone upset. I guess I just try to blend in and not cause any trouble. It feels easier that way, even if it’s a bit lonely.
It sounds like blending in helps you avoid conflict but can leave you feeling isolated; when you hold back your opinions, what do you notice happens to how you feel about yourself?

I guess I feel kind of small, or like I don't really matter that much. Sometimes I feel a little… empty, I guess, like I’m just there to make everyone else comfortable. It's hard not to think maybe I'm just not that interesting or important. But I don’t want to bother anyone, so I just keep quiet.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has struggled with money management since early adulthood, often feeling overwhelmed by bills and debt. They grew up in a household where financial instability was common, which reinforced feelings of dependence and inadequacy.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don't rely on others, I won’t get what I need. Asking for help means I’m weak or a burden. I have to be careful with money because I can’t trust myself to handle it well.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid looking at bills or financial statements, hoping problems will resolve on their own. They sometimes ask family members for money reluctantly, feeling guilty afterward. They also isolate themselves when stressed about finances.
ccd.situation: The patient received a reminder about an overdue credit card payment and is worried about how to cover the amount due this month.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m always messing things up." "I can’t handle this on my own." "People will think I’m a failure if I ask for help."
ccd.emotions: Anxious, ashamed, helpless, and frustrated.
ccd.behaviors: Avoids opening bills, delays making payment arrangements, withdraws from friends and family, and procrastinates on budgeting or seeking financial advice.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been kind of stressful. I got this reminder about a credit card payment that’s past due. I guess I’ve just been trying not to think about it too much. I’ve been a bit on edge because of it.
What thoughts go through your mind when you see that reminder about the past due payment?

Mostly I just think, “Here we go again, I screwed up.” I worry that I won’t be able to figure it out on my own. Sometimes I think maybe I should ask someone for help, but I really don’t want to bother anyone. It just makes me feel like I can’t handle basic stuff.
When you notice those thoughts like “Here we go again, I screwed up,” how does that affect what you do next or how you feel physically?

I just feel tense, like my chest tightens up and my stomach drops. Usually I try to distract myself—watch TV or scroll on my phone—so I don’t have to think about it. Sometimes I leave the bill unopened on the table for days. I end up feeling stuck and more stressed the longer I avoid it.
What do you think might happen if you opened the bill and took a small step toward dealing with it?

Honestly, I feel like I’d just get overwhelmed. I worry I’ll see something worse than I expected and panic. Part of me thinks maybe I’ll mess things up even more if I try to fix it. It just seems safer to put it off, even though I know that probably makes it worse.
Have you faced a similar situation before where you took a small step despite feeling overwhelmed, and how did that turn out?

Yeah, I guess there were times when I finally forced myself to call and ask for a payment plan or something. It was scary, but sometimes it worked out better than I thought. Still, I always felt pretty anxious and embarrassed when I had to do it. Even if it helped, I just remember dreading the whole thing.
What do you tell yourself about feeling anxious and embarrassed when you reach out for help in those moments?

I usually tell myself that I shouldn’t need to ask for help, that I should be able to handle it on my own. It makes me feel like I’m failing at something everyone else can do. I worry people will think less of me, or that I’m just being a burden. Mostly I just want to hide it and keep it to myself.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored or dismissed when trying to express myself, which made me believe that my voice didn’t matter and that I couldn’t influence what happened around me. Social situations have frequently left me feeling overwhelmed and unsure of how to respond, reinforcing that sense of being out of control.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to speak up, I’ll end up saying the wrong thing or making things worse. It’s safer to stay quiet because I don’t trust myself to handle social interactions well. Trying to control what others think or do is pointless since I have no real power in the situation.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid speaking up or sharing my thoughts in group settings, often changing the subject or diverting attention to avoid conflict. Sometimes I withdraw from social gatherings early or keep my responses very brief to minimize the chance of feeling out of control.
ccd.situation: At a small work meeting, when asked for my opinion on a project plan, I feel pressured and unsure about how to respond without causing problems or seeming incompetent.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to mess up and everyone will think I’m clueless.” “I can’t handle this; I’m just out of control.” “Maybe if I change the topic, they won’t notice I’m lost.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, embarrassment, and helplessness flood in, making me feel small and overwhelmed. There’s also a sense of frustration with myself for not being able to manage the situation better.
ccd.behaviors: I interrupt myself or shift the conversation to something unrelated, avoid eye contact, and sometimes excuse myself from the meeting early to escape the discomfort.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um... it’s been okay, I guess. Work was kind of busy, which sometimes makes it easier to just keep my head down and not get too involved with anyone, you know? Oh, actually, the coffee machine at work broke on Tuesday, so there was this whole thing where everyone was joking around about it, and I just sort of stood there not really knowing what to say. Anyway, it’s just been... yeah, just kind of a normal week, I suppose.
When you noticed yourself standing there without knowing what to say during that moment with your coworkers, what thoughts were going through your mind?

Oh, um, I mostly just kept thinking that I’d probably say something weird if I tried to join in, so it was better to just smile and nod. Actually, it reminded me a bit of school days, when there’d be a group talking and I’d just kind of hover at the edge because I never really knew how to jump in without feeling awkward. I guess, in that moment, part of me was hoping someone would notice I was there and maybe bring me in, but also kind of relieved that no one did. Oh, and the coffee thing—someone made a joke about instant coffee being the end of civilization, which, I don’t know, I just wasn’t sure how to react to that.
When you think about those moments from school and now at work, what feelings come up for you when you’re on the edge of the group but not really part of the conversation?

Hmm, I guess I feel sort of...left out, maybe? Like there’s this wall between me and everyone else and I can’t quite figure out how to get through it. It’s kind of embarrassing, honestly, being there but not, and sometimes I get frustrated with myself because I feel like I should know how to act by now. Oh, and sometimes I just end up focusing on something else, like scrolling through my phone or pretending to check an email, so I don’t seem so awkward.
When you notice yourself focusing on your phone or pretending to check an email in those moments, what do you think that helps you avoid feeling or experiencing?

I guess it helps me avoid, um, feeling like people are watching me or judging me for not saying anything. It’s easier to look busy than to stand there not knowing what to do with myself. Actually, sometimes I worry people think I’m rude or uninterested, but it just feels safer to not have to try and come up with something to say. Oh, like last week, I accidentally replied “you too” when someone said “good luck on your presentation,” and I felt so embarrassed—I just wanted to disappear after that.
What do you imagine might happen if you tried to join in the conversation, even if you felt unsure or thought you might say something awkward?

Uh, I think I’d probably just blurt something out that doesn’t really fit, and everyone would go quiet or look at me funny. Or, sometimes I worry I’ll interrupt someone or say the wrong thing and mess up the flow, and then people might think I’m clueless or just...weird. I mean, I know sometimes people say awkward things and it’s fine, but for me it just feels like everyone would notice and remember it. Oh, and then I’d probably spend the rest of the day replaying what I said in my head, cringing about it even hours later.
When you catch yourself replaying those moments and feeling cringe afterward, what do you usually say to yourself about the situation or about you?

Um, I usually just think, “Why did I say that?” or “I should’ve just kept quiet.” It’s like I start picking apart every word and convincing myself that I looked totally out of place or incompetent. Sometimes I tell myself that I just can’t handle these things like other people can. Actually, there was one time I left a meeting early because I couldn’t stop thinking everyone was judging me for stumbling over my words.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has struggled with feelings of inadequacy and low self-esteem since childhood, often feeling overlooked and unappreciated by family and peers. Past experiences of failure and criticism have reinforced a negative self-view, making it difficult to pursue personal goals or take risks.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to improve or grow, I will just fail and prove how worthless I am. Nobody really values me or wants me to succeed. It’s safer not to bother trying because I’m bound to waste time and effort.

Um, I just... I worry that if I really say what’s on my mind, people might get annoyed or think I’m being difficult. I don’t want anyone to feel uncomfortable because of me. It just feels safer to keep things to myself, you know? I guess I’m kind of used to it by now.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

I just keep thinking that if I mess something up or can’t handle it, everyone will notice. Like, they’ll think less of me or not want me on their team anymore. Sometimes I get this feeling that I have to prove myself all the time or I’ll be left out. It’s kind of exhausting.

Usually I just double down and work harder. I try not to ask for help, even if I’m struggling, because I don’t want anyone to think I can’t handle it. Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting, just the usual stuff. I’m probably overthinking, but a friend seemed a little distant when we talked, so I’ve been worried I did something wrong. But I’m sure it’s nothing big—I’m probably just being sensitive.
When you notice yourself thinking that your friend is distant, what kinds of thoughts usually go through your mind?

Oh, um… I guess I start wondering if I upset them somehow. I worry that maybe I said something wrong, or maybe I just wasn’t interesting enough. It’s silly, but I sometimes think maybe they just don’t want to be around me anymore. I try not to make a big deal out of it, though—I know people have their own stuff going on.
What feelings come up for you when you have those thoughts about upsetting your friend or not being interesting enough?

I mostly just feel kind of anxious, I guess. A bit sad, too—like a heavy feeling in my chest. I start worrying that maybe I’ll end up alone. But I try to shake it off and just hope things will get better if I’m careful.
When you try to shake off those feelings and hope things get better, what do you usually do or tell yourself to help manage the anxiety and sadness?

Um, I usually tell myself that I’m probably just being dramatic and that it’s not a big deal. I try to act really normal and agreeable around my friend, so they don’t get annoyed with me. Sometimes I apologize, even if I’m not sure what for, just to be safe. I mostly just try not to cause any trouble or draw attention to how I’m feeling.
How do you feel inside when you apologize or act agreeable just to avoid causing trouble or drawing attention to your feelings?

Honestly, I guess I feel kind of small, like my own feelings don’t really matter much. It’s like I’m sort of invisible, but at least there’s less chance of upsetting anyone. Sometimes I feel a bit lonely because I’m not really saying what I actually think. But I’d rather keep things smooth than risk someone getting mad or pulling away from me.
What do you think might happen if you shared more of your true feelings with your friend instead of trying to keep things smooth?

I don’t know, I guess I worry they might think I’m too much, or that I’m needy or annoying. I’m kind of scared they’d pull away or maybe even stop wanting to talk to me. I feel like if I say what’s really on my mind, it might push people away. I just don’t want to risk losing them.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated financial setbacks and feels unsupported by others during these times, which has reinforced feelings of being unwanted and undesirable. Past relationships and social interactions have also contributed to a sense of isolation and low self-worth.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "I have to be perfect to be accepted." "If I can't manage my finances well, people will reject me." "No one wants to be around someone who struggles or causes problems."
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid discussing financial issues with friends or family, withdraws socially, and sometimes distracts themselves with excessive work or shopping to escape feelings of inadequacy. They may also ruminate silently about their perceived failures.
ccd.situation: Facing mounting bills and debt, the patient worries about how others will judge them and fears being seen as a burden or failure.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I'm such a failure for not having money." "People probably think I'm useless and don't want me around." "I'll never be good enough to be cared for."
ccd.emotions: Shame, sadness, anxiety, and loneliness.
ccd.behaviors: Withdrawing from social contact, avoiding conversations about money, procrastinating on addressing financial problems, and sometimes engaging in impulsive spending to cope with distress.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Some things have been stressful, but I’m managing. Work kept me busy, which is probably a good thing. I haven’t really talked to many people outside of work, though.
When you notice yourself feeling stressed, what kinds of thoughts tend to go through your mind?

I just keep thinking that I should have things together by now. I worry people are judging me for not having my life sorted out, especially with money. Sometimes I wonder if I’m just disappointing everyone. It sort of loops in my head.
When those thoughts about not having your life together or disappointing others come up, how do they affect what you do or how you feel in the moment?

I usually just want to hide or avoid people when I feel like that. I get this heavy feeling, kind of embarrassed, and I just don’t want to talk about anything real with anyone. Sometimes I distract myself with work or just scroll on my phone. It feels easier than dealing with it.
What do you notice happens to your stress or mood after you’ve been avoiding or distracting yourself in those ways?

Honestly, it doesn’t really help in the long run. The stress just comes back, and sometimes it feels even worse because I know I haven’t dealt with anything. I end up feeling pretty lonely too. It’s like I’m stuck in this cycle.
Can you think of a recent situation where this cycle felt especially strong—what was happening, and what went through your mind at that time?

Yeah, actually, last week I got a bunch of bills I wasn’t expecting. I just felt overwhelmed, like I’d messed up again. My mind went straight to, “Of course this would happen to me,” and “I can’t tell anyone because they’ll just see me as a burden.” I didn’t really talk to anyone about it and just tried to ignore it for a while.
When you had those thoughts like “Of course this would happen to me,” what feelings did that bring up for you in that moment?

Mostly I just felt ashamed, honestly. There was this heavy sadness, too, like I’d let myself down again. I also felt pretty anxious, worrying about what’s going to happen next. It made me want to disappear for a while.

ccd.relevant_history: I often feel like I’m a burden to others and that people expect too much from me. Growing up, I didn’t get much support when I asked for help, so now I hesitate to rely on others even when I really need to.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I ask for too much support, people will get annoyed or leave me. I have to manage on my own because depending on others just makes me weak or needy. It’s safer to keep my needs to myself.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to avoid sharing my feelings or asking for help because I’m afraid of being rejected. Sometimes I push people away before they can get too close, just to protect myself from feeling helpless.
ccd.situation: When I’m at a social gathering and I feel like I need someone to talk to or lean on, but I worry that asking for support will make me seem needy or weak.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m bothering everyone with my problems.” “If I show how much I need help, they’ll think less of me.” “I have to handle this alone or I’ll be a burden.”
ccd.emotions: Anxious, ashamed, lonely, and a bit hopeless.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet and keep to myself, avoiding conversations that might reveal my struggles. I also cancel plans or leave early to avoid feeling exposed or needy in front of others.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been kind of rough, honestly. I’ve just been feeling a bit off and keeping to myself more than usual. Nothing really big happened, I guess—I just felt kind of out of place a lot.
When you say you’ve been feeling “off” and out of place, what kinds of thoughts have been going through your mind in those moments?

I don’t know, it’s like... I walk into a room and I just feel like I shouldn’t really be there. I worry that people are probably annoyed by me or think I’m weird for being so quiet. Sometimes I catch myself thinking it’d be better if I just kept out of everyone’s way.
When you notice those thoughts about people being annoyed or thinking you’re weird, how does that affect the way you behave or interact with others?

Honestly, I kind of just go quiet and try not to draw attention to myself. I’ll find a reason to leave early or stick to the edges of the group. It feels safer that way, like at least I’m not bothering anyone. Sometimes I just avoid making plans in the first place.
What do you notice happens to your feelings or mood after you’ve kept to yourself or avoided social situations like that?

I usually end up feeling kind of lonely, to be honest. At first it feels like a relief, but then I just feel left out or like I’m missing something. Sometimes I get down on myself for not even trying. It’s like I can’t win either way.
When you find yourself feeling lonely or down after avoiding social situations, what do you tell yourself about those feelings or about yourself in those moments?

I guess I just think that it’s my own fault for not fitting in better. I tell myself that I should be able to handle it and not need anyone else. Sometimes I feel stupid for wanting support in the first place. It’s like I’m always too much or not enough.
That sounds really tough to carry those thoughts. When you think about being “too much” or “not enough,” what evidence do you notice that supports or challenges those beliefs?

I mean, sometimes people seem tired or distracted when I talk to them, so I figure I must be annoying. Or if I try to open up and it gets awkward, I just assume I pushed it too far. But I guess there have been times when someone listened or reached out after I was quiet for a while, though I usually brush that off and think they’re just being polite. It’s hard to actually believe I’m not a burden.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like no one truly cares about me, especially when money gets tight. In past relationships and jobs, whenever things got difficult financially, I was often left alone or blamed, which made me feel abandoned and unworthy.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I struggle with money, people will leave me because I’m not lovable or important enough to stick around. I have to be perfect or provide something valuable to keep others interested in me.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to avoid talking about financial problems to prevent others from rejecting me. Sometimes I overwork or spend money to gain temporary approval, but I also isolate myself when I feel overwhelmed.
ccd.situation: I just found out I might not get a bonus this year, and I’m worried I won’t have enough to cover my expenses.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They’re going to see I can’t handle this, and they’ll leave me like always.” “I’m just a burden; no one wants to be around someone who can’t manage their money.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and a deep feeling of loneliness and fear of being abandoned.
ccd.behaviors: I withdraw from my partner and friends, avoid discussing money, and sometimes overwork myself to prove my worth.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Honestly? Not great. I just found out at work that my bonus probably isn’t happening this year, and it’s stressing me out. I don’t really want to talk about it, though—I doubt there’s anything you can say that’ll help. It just feels pointless.
That sounds really frustrating and disappointing. When you think about the bonus not coming through, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind?

I mean, what do you think goes through my mind? That I screwed up somewhere, or that everyone’s going to see I can’t handle my own life. It’s like, whenever money gets tight, people just…lose patience with me. I’m tired of feeling like I’m on thin ice all the time.

It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure and fear about how others might judge you. When you notice these thoughts about messing up or being judged, how do they affect the way you feel physically or emotionally?

I just get this knot in my stomach, you know? Like I can’t relax—I keep waiting for someone to get mad or just walk away from me. It’s exhausting, honestly. I end up feeling really alone, even when people are around.

That feeling of being on edge and alone sounds really tough. When you notice that knot in your stomach and the exhaustion, what do you usually do next—do you try to distract yourself, talk to someone, or maybe something else?

I usually just shut down. I stop talking to people, even my partner, because I don’t want them to see me like this or think I’m a mess. Sometimes I throw myself into work, but it doesn’t actually help. And no, I don’t want to talk about it with anyone—what’s the point?

I hear that opening up feels risky and maybe even pointless right now. When you shut down and pull away from others, what do you notice happens to those feelings inside—do they change or stay the same?
They just get worse, honestly. The more I keep to myself, the heavier everything feels. But what am I supposed to do—burden people with my problems? I already feel like I’m too much sometimes.

It sounds like you’re worried about being a burden, yet isolating makes things feel heavier. Have there been times when you shared something difficult with someone and it ended up going better than you expected?

Maybe once or twice, but it never lasts. People say they care at first, but then they get tired of hearing about it or just disappear when things aren’t easy. I’ve learned not to expect much. It just hurts less that way.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated financial instability and feels isolated due to lack of support from family or friends. Past relationships have ended with feelings of abandonment, reinforcing a sense of loneliness and unworthiness.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I am struggling financially, people will see me as a failure and reject me. Being alone means I am unlovable and destined to stay that way. I must hide my problems to avoid disappointing others.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to withdraw socially, avoids discussing financial issues, and works excessively to try to prove self-worth. They may also ruminate on negative thoughts and avoid seeking help or advice.
ccd.situation: Facing mounting bills and debt, the patient worries about how this financial strain will impact their relationships and fears being left alone because of it.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to lose everyone because I can’t support myself.” “No one will want to be with someone this broken.” “I’m just meant to be alone forever.”
ccd.emotions: Feeling hopeless, anxious, sad, and deeply lonely.
ccd.behaviors: Withdrawing from friends and family, avoiding conversations about money, neglecting self-care, and isolating themselves to avoid perceived judgment or rejection.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
I mean, it's been alright, I guess. Nothing special. Just working a lot, trying to keep myself busy. Honestly, I'd rather not get into it right now.
I appreciate you sharing that. When you say you’d rather not get into it right now, can you tell me what makes it hard to talk about at the moment?

I don't know, it just feels pointless sometimes. Like, talking about it won't actually change anything, right? I’m tired of feeling like I’m complaining or burdening people. I just deal with stuff on my own—it's easier that way.
It sounds like you’ve been carrying a lot on your own and feeling isolated. When those feelings come up, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind about asking for support or sharing with others?

Honestly, I just don't see the point. If I do talk about what's going on, people either judge me or they just don't get it. It feels like nobody really wants to hear about my problems anyway. So, I just keep it to myself and try to handle it on my own.
That sounds really tough to feel misunderstood or judged when you reach out. Can you tell me about a recent time when you felt this way—what was happening, and what were you thinking in that moment?

I guess… last week, my phone kept buzzing with missed calls from my sister, and I just let it ring. She always asks how things are, but I know she doesn’t really want to hear if things aren’t going well. I just thought, “Why bother? She won’t get it, and it’ll just make things weird.” So I ignored her and just tried to distract myself with work.
That’s a powerful example. When you thought “Why bother?” in that moment, how did that thought affect how you felt and what you chose to do next?

I just felt even more alone, honestly. Like, what's the point of reaching out if it always ends up the same way? It made me want to just shut everyone out and focus on surviving the week. I didn’t even text her back—I just buried myself in my job.
Feeling alone and wanting to shut others out sounds really painful. When you say you focused on surviving the week, what helped you get through those days despite feeling that way?

I just kept my head down at work, did extra hours, anything to keep busy. It’s easier to lose track of time that way and not think too much. Honestly, I didn’t really do anything for myself—I just tried to avoid feeling anything at all. That’s pretty much it.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like I had to prove myself at work, but no matter how hard I try, I worry I’m not good enough. Past experiences of criticism and failure have made me doubt my abilities and feel like I’m broken.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m under pressure and don’t perform perfectly, it means I’m a failure. I have to avoid making mistakes because that would show I’m defective. Others will think less of me if I don’t meet high expectations.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to work longer hours to compensate and avoid asking for help. Sometimes I shut down or avoid tasks that feel overwhelming to protect myself from failing.
ccd.situation: My supervisor gave me a tight deadline to complete a project, and I feel overwhelmed by the amount of work and pressure to get everything right.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I can’t do this on time.” “If I mess up, everyone will see I’m not capable.” “I’m just not good enough for this job.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, fear, and shame.
ccd.behaviors: I procrastinate because I’m scared to start and fail, or I work obsessively trying to control every detail, which makes me exhausted and less effective.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

It’s been pretty stressful, honestly. Work’s just been a lot—my boss gave me this big project with barely any time to do it. I’ve been trying to keep up, but I feel like I’m already behind. I guess I just feel kind of overwhelmed.
When you think about the project and feeling behind, what specific thoughts are going through your mind?

I keep thinking I’m not going to finish it in time, or if I do, it won’t be good enough. Sometimes I wonder if everyone else is handling things better than me. I worry my boss will be disappointed. I just feel like I’m not measuring up.
What do you notice happens to your feelings or behavior when you have those thoughts about not measuring up or disappointing your boss?

When I start thinking like that, I get really anxious—my chest feels tight and I can't focus. Sometimes I end up just staring at the screen, unable to actually start working. Other times I work late into the night, double-checking everything over and over. Either way, I just feel exhausted.
Can you tell me about a time recently when you were able to make some progress on the project despite feeling anxious? What was different then?

Yeah, I guess there was one night last week when I managed to get a chunk of it done. I think I was just so tired of worrying that I forced myself to start, even though I was still nervous. Once I got into it, I realized I actually knew what I was doing, at least for that part. It still wasn’t easy, but finishing something made me feel a little better, even if it didn’t last long.
That sounds like an important moment. What do you think helped you push through the worry and start working that night?

I think I got fed up with putting it off and feeling stuck. I told myself I just had to get something—anything—done, even if it wasn’t perfect. Maybe it was a mix of frustration and just not wanting to fall even further behind. Honestly, I was desperate to prove to myself I could do at least part of it.
When you told yourself that it didn’t have to be perfect and just focused on getting something done, how did that change the way you felt or behaved afterward?

It made things feel a little less overwhelming, at least for a while. I wasn’t as frozen—I could actually start typing instead of just worrying. I still felt nervous, but it was more manageable. I guess I felt a bit of relief, like I wasn’t completely stuck anymore.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has struggled with managing money since early adulthood, often feeling overwhelmed by bills and budgeting. Past experiences of financial instability and criticism about their money skills have reinforced feelings of inadequacy. These experiences have contributed to a deep sense of incompetence regarding financial matters.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "I always mess things up when it comes to money." "If I can't handle my finances, I’m not capable of taking care of myself." "People will judge me if I show I don’t know what I’m doing."
ccd.coping_strategies: Avoiding looking at bills or bank statements, procrastinating on financial tasks, and sometimes relying on others to manage money. Occasionally, distracting themselves with shopping or entertainment to escape the stress of financial worries.
ccd.situation: The patient received their monthly bills and notices some overdue payments, which triggers doubts about their ability to manage their finances.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m such a failure; I can’t even keep track of my bills." "I’ll never get this under control." "I’m just not smart enough to handle money."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, frustration, helplessness, and shame.
ccd.behaviors: Avoiding opening bills, withdrawing from discussions about money, and delaying actions like making payments or creating a budget.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

It’s been a bit rough, honestly. I got some bills in the mail, and I’ve kind of just... set them aside for now. I know I should deal with them, but I keep putting it off. I guess I’ve just felt really anxious about it all.
When you think about opening and handling those bills, what kind of thoughts go through your mind?

Mostly that I’m just going to mess it up somehow, or that there’s probably something I forgot to pay. Sometimes I think, what’s the point—I’ll never really get on top of it anyway. It just feels kind of hopeless, like I’m always behind. And I worry that if I try to fix it, I’ll just make it worse.
When those thoughts about messing up or feeling hopeless come up, how do you usually feel in your body or emotionally?

I get this tight feeling in my chest, and my stomach feels kind of knotted up. My heart races a bit, and sometimes I feel shaky. Emotionally, it’s just... a lot of worry and stress. Sometimes I just feel sort of stuck, like there’s no way out of it.
What do you usually do when you start feeling that tightness and worry—are there specific ways you cope or respond in those moments?

Honestly, I usually just avoid it. I’ll distract myself with TV or go scroll on my phone instead of dealing with the bills. Sometimes I’ll even leave the mail unopened for days. It feels easier to just ignore it, even though I know that probably makes things worse.
When you distract yourself or avoid the bills, how do you feel afterward—does it help ease the stress, or do other feelings come up?

It helps for a little while, like a quick break from the stress. But then the worry comes back, sometimes even worse. I end up feeling guilty for not dealing with it. I just keep thinking I should be able to handle this stuff by now.
What do you think it would be like to face the bills little by little—what concerns or hopes come up when you imagine that?

I guess part of me hopes it might not be as bad as I’m expecting, or maybe I could finally get things under control. But honestly, I worry I’ll just run into more problems or realize I’ve made mistakes. I’m afraid I’ll feel stupid if I mess up again. There’s also this fear that no matter what I do, I’ll still end up overwhelmed.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

ccd.relevant_history: I've had a few close relationships that ended suddenly, and each time I felt like I was left completely on my own without warning. Growing up, I often felt overlooked and like my feelings didn’t matter to my family, which made me worry people would leave me if they really got to know me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I get too close to someone, they will eventually leave me because I’m not worth sticking around for. It’s better to expect abandonment so I’m not caught off guard or hurt too badly. People only stay if I’m constantly proving I’m not a burden.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to keep people at a distance or avoid getting too emotionally involved to protect myself. Sometimes I test others by pulling away to see if they’ll chase me, but I usually end up feeling even more alone. I also distract myself a lot to avoid thinking about being left behind.
ccd.situation: I noticed my partner didn’t respond to my texts all evening, and I started worrying that they might be losing interest or planning to leave me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They’re probably upset with me and don’t want to be with me anymore.” “I must have done something wrong again.” “I’m going to be left alone like always.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, fear of rejection, and a deep feeling of loneliness.
ccd.behaviors: I sent multiple messages asking if everything was okay and later withdrew emotionally, not sharing my feelings even when we talked. I also avoided making plans for the weekend, expecting to be let down.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

It’s been… I guess kind of up and down. Work was busy, and I had a couple late nights, which always throws me off. My partner was kind of quiet yesterday, and I kept checking my phone way too much. Oh, and I tried this new coffee place, but I don’t think I’ll go back—the coffee was weirdly salty.
When you noticed your partner being quiet yesterday, what thoughts went through your mind as you kept checking your phone?

Well, at first I just thought maybe they were caught up with something, but then I started getting this feeling that maybe they were annoyed with me or just didn’t want to talk. I kept replaying our last conversation in my head, trying to see if I said something wrong—I do that a lot. It’s kind of silly, but I even wondered if their phone was broken or something. And then… I don’t know, my mind just sort of spiraled off, and I started thinking about this time in college when a close friend suddenly ghosted me for no reason—it’s weird how that memory pops up.
That sounds really tough. When you noticed those thoughts and memories coming up, how did they affect how you felt in the moment?

Yeah, it definitely got to me. My chest felt tight, and I got really restless—I kept picking up my phone, putting it down, pacing a bit. Honestly, it just made me feel sort of… heavy, like I was bracing myself for bad news. I also started getting this urge to just distract myself completely, so I turned on a show I’ve seen a million times and zoned out for a while.
When you think back to how you reacted in that moment, what do you think you were hoping would happen or be different?

I guess I was hoping they’d just text back quickly and say everything was fine, or maybe even call me. That would’ve made it all just go away, probably. But also… I think I kind of wished I could just not care so much, you know? It’s like, sometimes I imagine what it’d be like to not get so thrown off whenever things feel uncertain.
That makes a lot of sense. When you imagine not getting so thrown off by uncertainty, what do you think might be different for you in how you handle those moments?

Hmm… I think maybe I’d just be able to shrug it off and go about my day, instead of sitting there overthinking. Maybe I wouldn’t feel the need to check my phone every two minutes or send extra messages. I mean, I see other people just… trusting things are okay, and that seems so much easier. Actually, it reminds me of my cousin—she just always assumes people like her and never seems worried about being left out, even if someone doesn’t reply right away.
What do you think helps your cousin feel that sense of trust and ease, and how might that be different from what you experience?

I’m not really sure, but maybe she just grew up with, I don’t know, more of that steady kind of support? I feel like she never had to wonder if people would be there for her, or maybe she just always felt like she belonged. For me, it’s almost like I’m constantly double-checking if it’s safe to trust people—like waiting for the other shoe to drop. Actually, now that I think about it, even when I was a kid, I kind of kept to myself a lot, just in case.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored or overlooked by family members, which made me internalize the idea that I wasn’t worthy of attention or love. Past relationships have reinforced this feeling, as partners have either rejected me or treated me as if I wasn’t enough. These experiences have deeply affected how I see myself and connect with others.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I believe that because I am unattractive, people won’t want to get close to me or care about what I have to offer. I also think that if I don’t appear perfect or appealing, I will be rejected or abandoned. These beliefs make me doubt my value in relationships and feel that I must hide my true self to be accepted.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid situations where I might be judged or rejected, like dating or social gatherings, and sometimes I withdraw emotionally to protect myself from feeling hurt. I also try to overcompensate by trying to please others or changing aspects of myself to fit what I think they want, even if it doesn’t feel authentic.
ccd.situation: I’m talking to my partner and notice they are quiet and not very responsive during our conversation. I immediately feel like something is wrong between us, and I start to question whether they still find me attractive or want to be with me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They must be losing interest in me because I’m not attractive enough.” “I’m probably going to be left alone again.” “There’s no point in trying to connect if they don’t find me lovable.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, sad, and hopeless, like I’m not good enough to be loved or wanted. There’s also a deep sense of loneliness and fear of abandonment that weighs heavily on me.
ccd.behaviors: I withdraw from the conversation, becoming quiet and distant, which makes it harder to communicate and increases the emotional gap between us. I might also ruminate on these feelings later, which makes me feel even more isolated and disconnected.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um… I guess it’s been a bit up and down. Work’s been busy, which helps distract me, but I’ve also just felt kind of… off? Like, I’ve been more withdrawn than usual, especially around my partner, and I can’t quite shake this nagging feeling that something’s wrong. I don’t really know why, it’s just been hard to feel settled.
When you notice that nagging feeling that something’s wrong, what thoughts tend to come up for you in those moments?

Well, it usually starts off kind of vague, like just this sense that I’m not really… connecting, I guess? And then I start wondering if it’s something about me—like maybe I’ve done something wrong, or I’m just not interesting enough. Sometimes I catch myself thinking things like, “Maybe they’re just bored of me,” or, “What if they don’t really want to be around me?” It’s embarrassing to admit, but those thoughts pop up a lot when things get quiet between us.
That sounds really tough. When you have those thoughts about not being interesting enough or worrying your partner might be bored, how do you usually respond or cope with those feelings?

Honestly, I tend to just shut down a bit. Like, I’ll get really quiet or distracted, and I might pull away from the conversation because I don’t want to make things more awkward. Sometimes I’ll try to overcompensate and be extra agreeable, even when I don’t feel like myself, just to keep the peace. But afterwards, I end up feeling kind of empty and frustrated with myself for not just saying how I feel.
That sounds like a difficult cycle. When you notice yourself starting to shut down or overcompensate, what do you think might happen if you tried sharing a small part of what you’re feeling instead?

I don’t know… I guess part of me worries that if I say what I’m really feeling, it’ll just confirm that I’m too much, or too needy, or that I’ll drive them further away. I always imagine them reacting badly, or just not understanding, and then I’ll feel even more alone. It just feels safer to keep things to myself, even though I know that probably makes things worse. Honestly, the idea of being that vulnerable is kind of terrifying.

It makes a lot of sense that vulnerability feels scary when you fear being misunderstood or rejected; what do you think might be one small, manageable step you could try to test out sharing your feelings with your partner?

Maybe I could just say something simple, like, “I’m feeling a little off today,” instead of trying to hide it completely. I guess it doesn’t have to be this big, dramatic thing—I could just test the waters and see how they respond. Even that feels a bit daunting, but it sounds less risky than spilling everything all at once. I’m not sure how they’d react, but I suppose I won’t really know unless I try.
That sounds like a thoughtful and gentle way to start; how do you think you might prepare yourself emotionally before sharing that small statement with your partner?

I think I’d need to take a few moments to calm myself down first—maybe just focus on breathing or remind myself that it’s okay to have feelings. I’d probably have to pep-talk myself a little, like telling myself it’s not the end of the world if things feel awkward. Part of me would want to rehearse what I’m going to say so I don’t freeze up or back out last minute. Even just imagining it makes me nervous, but I do want to try, because keeping everything inside just feels so heavy.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and criticized by my family, which made me believe I was never good enough. Over time, these feelings deepened, especially during times when I struggled to meet expectations at school and work, reinforcing a sense of being a burden.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t prove my worth to others, I am useless and invisible. People would be better off without me because I only cause disappointment. Trying to change or grow is pointless since I will always fail and let everyone down.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid new challenges or opportunities for growth because I’m afraid of failing and confirming my worst beliefs. Sometimes I isolate myself or withdraw from social situations to avoid burdening others or facing judgment.
ccd.situation: I was offered a chance to take on a new project at work that could help me develop new skills and advance my career, but I hesitated and felt overwhelmed by doubt about my ability to succeed.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to mess this up like I always do.” “I don’t deserve to be trusted with this responsibility.” “If I fail, it will just prove that I’m worthless and should give up.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, ashamed, and hopeless. There’s a heavy sadness that makes me want to shrink away and not try at all.
ccd.behaviors: I procrastinate and avoid starting the project, sometimes making excuses to my supervisor. I also withdraw from colleagues and spend more time alone, which only deepens my feelings of isolation and worthlessness.

messages.content: How has your week been so far? Can you tell me about any moments that stood out to you?

Um, my week has been… I don’t know, kind of up and down, I guess? Nothing too dramatic happened, really. I mean, work was the usual, though something did come up—my manager asked if I wanted to try helping out on this new project, but I haven’t said yes or no yet. I’ve mostly just felt… sort of tired, I suppose.
When you think about the new project your manager mentioned, what thoughts or feelings come up for you?

Well, honestly, I just feel really uneasy about it. It’s like, the minute she brought it up, my mind started racing with all these worries about what could go wrong. I just kept thinking, what if I can’t handle it, or I make a complete mess of things? I can’t really shake this kind of nervous, heavy feeling whenever I think about it.
What do you notice about how your body feels or what you do when those worries and that nervous feeling come up?

Uh, I guess I kind of tense up, like my shoulders get really tight and I feel this knot in my stomach. Sometimes my heart beats a little faster, and I just want to hide somewhere, honestly. I notice that I start putting things off, like I’ll find reasons to stay busy with other stuff so I don’t have to think about the project. I also end up avoiding people at work more than usual, like I’ll skip lunch with colleagues or keep my office door closed.

It sounds like those physical sensations and avoidance behaviors are pretty strong—when you notice yourself avoiding others or procrastinating, what thoughts tend to go through your mind in that moment?

Yeah, it’s pretty hard to ignore, honestly. Most of the time, I just think I’m going to let everyone down if I try, so maybe it’s better to not even start. I worry that if I talk to people or let them see how stressed I am, they’ll think I’m not capable or that I don’t belong there. It’s like there’s this voice in my head pointing out that I’m just going to prove everyone right if I mess up.
That inner voice sounds quite critical. Can you remember a time recently when you challenged or questioned that voice, or when you felt differently about your ability to handle a challenge?

Um… I guess, to be honest, it’s really rare for me to feel differently. I can’t really remember a time lately where I’ve actually challenged that voice in my head or believed I could handle things. Usually, I just kind of give in to it—like, I don’t even try to argue with it because it feels so convincing. Maybe there was a small moment where I thought, “Maybe I could try,” but it faded pretty fast.
When that small thought of “Maybe I could try” came up, what was happening around you or inside you that made it feel possible, even if only briefly?

I think it was just right after my manager mentioned the project—she sounded, I don’t know, kind of positive about me doing it, which caught me off guard. For a split second, I wondered if maybe she actually thought I was capable, and that maybe I wasn’t a total screw-up. I remember feeling a tiny bit lighter, like maybe there was a chance I could manage it. But then all the old doubts just hit me again, and it felt safer to back away from that possibility.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced several difficult relationships and setbacks in their personal and professional life, often feeling powerless to change their circumstances. They grew up in an environment where their opinions were dismissed, reinforcing a sense of helplessness.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: “I don't have control over my life,” “Others take advantage of me,” and “If I try to stand up for myself, it won't make a difference.” These beliefs make the patient expect failure or mistreatment in new situations.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid confrontation and new challenges, often withdrawing or staying silent to prevent further hurt. They may also seek reassurance from others but feel frustrated when it doesn’t help.

ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m just going to mess this up like always,” “No one will support me,” and “I’m stuck being a victim of my circumstances.”
ccd.emotions: Feelings of sadness, frustration, anxiety, and a sense of hopelessness.
ccd.behaviors: Avoiding trying new activities, withdrawing from social opportunities, and giving up quickly when faced with challenges.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting happened. I mostly just kept to myself and tried to get through work. Some days felt kind of heavy, but that’s pretty normal for me.
When you say some days felt heavy, what kinds of thoughts or feelings were coming up for you in those moments?

I don’t know, it’s like… I just get this feeling that there’s no point trying too hard. Sometimes I catch myself thinking, “Why bother? Things never really work out for me anyway.” I guess I feel kind of tired and stuck.
That sounds really tough. When you notice those “Why bother?” thoughts, what do you usually do next—do you find yourself acting in a certain way or feeling something specific?

Yeah, I usually just kind of shut down. I stop whatever I was trying to do and maybe distract myself with TV or something. It’s easier than pushing through. I don’t really talk to anyone about it either—I just keep it to myself.
When you think about pushing through instead of shutting down, what do you imagine might happen if you tried to do that?

Honestly, I just picture myself messing it up or looking stupid. Or maybe people wouldn’t care anyway, so it feels pointless. I guess I sort of expect something to go wrong, so I avoid it. It just feels safer that way.
That expectation sounds really painful. Can you recall a recent time when you tried something despite feeling that way—what happened then?

Um, I tried joining this online group for a hobby I was interested in. I posted something, but no one really responded. That kind of confirmed what I thought would happen, so I just stopped participating. After that, I felt even more like it’s not worth putting myself out there.
That sounds really discouraging. When you remember posting in the group, was there any part of that experience that felt positive or gave you some hope, even if it was small?

I guess, maybe for a minute, I felt sort of proud that I actually tried. It took a lot for me to hit “send.” But that feeling went away pretty quick when there was no response. Mostly, I just felt embarrassed about putting myself out there.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… I guess it’s been okay. Just the usual stuff, nothing special. Not much to talk about, really.
When you say "just the usual stuff," what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you during your day-to-day activities?
I don’t really know… I mostly just try to get through the day. Sometimes I feel kind of on edge, I guess. Like I’m just hoping nothing goes wrong.
What kinds of situations or events tend to make you feel on edge or worried that something might go wrong?

Uh, probably when I have to be around people or speak up. Stuff like group meetings or, I don’t know, even just talking in class or at work. I get nervous that I’ll say something wrong or mess things up. It’s easier to just keep quiet.
When you imagine speaking up or participating in those situations, what specific thoughts go through your mind about what might happen?

I usually just think… people won’t like what I say, or maybe they’ll get annoyed. Sometimes I worry they’ll think there’s something wrong with me. Or that I’ll make things uncomfortable for everyone. It feels safer not to risk it.
How do these thoughts about others’ reactions affect how you feel in your body or your mood when you’re in those situations?
Um… I get tense, I guess. My stomach feels tight, and I just feel really awkward and embarrassed. Sometimes I get kind of sad, too. Mostly I just want to disappear.
When you notice these feelings of tension and wanting to disappear, how do you usually respond or cope in the moment?
I usually just stay quiet and try not to draw attention to myself. Sometimes I pretend to be busy or look at my phone. I kind of shut down, I guess. I just wait for it to be over.
