ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m not useful or productive, then I am a failure. People will reject me if I show my true feelings or ask for help. I must hide my weaknesses to avoid being judged or abandoned.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It's been alright, I guess. Nothing major happened. I went to a family thing over the weekend, but I just kind of stuck to myself. I don’t know, it’s just the usual.
When you say you stuck to yourself at the family event, what kinds of thoughts or feelings were going through your mind in that moment?

I just felt kind of out of place, honestly. Like, I was there, but it didn’t really matter if I said anything or not. Sometimes I’d think, “Why am I even here?” I mostly felt like no one would notice if I left early.
That sounds really tough. When those thoughts like “Why am I even here?” come up, what emotions do you notice in your body or mind?

I just feel heavy inside, like there’s this weight in my chest. It’s sort of empty but also hurts at the same time. Mostly, I just feel sad and tired. Sometimes I get this weird feeling that I want to disappear.
Thank you for sharing that—it sounds really painful. When you notice that heaviness and those feelings of wanting to disappear, what do you usually do or how do you respond to those feelings?

Usually, I just go quiet and try not to draw attention to myself. If it gets too much, I’ll find a reason to leave early. I keep everything to myself because I don’t want to bother anyone or make things awkward. Afterward, I just replay everything in my head and wonder what’s wrong with me.
It seems like you’re being really hard on yourself afterward. When you replay those moments and ask, “What’s wrong with me?” what beliefs or messages do you notice coming up about yourself?

I usually just think that I don’t have anything worthwhile to offer. Like, I’m just taking up space and nobody really cares what I think anyway. Sometimes it feels like I’m just sort of… unnecessary, I guess. I end up feeling pretty ashamed for even being there at all.
That sounds incredibly painful to carry. When you have those thoughts about not being worthwhile or unnecessary, do you find any evidence that supports or challenges those beliefs?

Honestly, it’s hard to find anything that challenges those thoughts. Most of the time, it feels like people don’t notice me anyway, so it just kind of adds up in my mind. Even when I try to say something or help out, it feels like it goes ignored. I can’t really think of times when I actually felt seen or important, at least not lately.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and powerless in my family, as if my opinions didn’t matter and I had little control over what happened at home. Conflicts rarely got resolved, leaving me feeling stuck and unheard.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I speak up, I’ll just be ignored or blamed, so it’s safer to stay quiet. No matter what I do, I won’t be able to change how things are in my family.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to withdraw from family conversations and avoid sharing my feelings to prevent further conflict. Sometimes, I try to please others quietly, hoping things will improve without me having to say much.
ccd.situation: During family gatherings, when disagreements come up, I feel like my voice doesn’t matter and that I’m just caught in the middle without any way to make things better.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “There’s nothing I can do to fix this.” “I’m just a victim here.” “If I say something, it won’t change anything and might make things worse.”
ccd.emotions: I feel helpless, sad, and frustrated. There’s also a sense of loneliness because I don’t feel understood or supported.
ccd.behaviors: I tend to stay silent and keep my distance emotionally, avoiding involvement in family issues. Sometimes I isolate myself to protect from feeling overwhelmed.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Nothing much happened. I just went to work and came home. Pretty normal week.
When you say "pretty normal," what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you during your typical workday?

Uh, I don’t really think much about it, to be honest. Mostly I just try to get through the day and not draw too much attention. Sometimes I feel a bit tired, but I just keep my head down. Nothing major, really.
When you notice that feeling of tiredness during the day, what do you usually say to yourself about it or the situation?
I don’t know... I just kind of tell myself it’s whatever, like, just get through it. Sometimes I think it’s not worth making a big deal out of things. I just try to ignore it and move on.
What do you imagine might happen if you allowed yourself to pay more attention to those feelings instead of pushing them aside?
I’m not sure. I guess I feel like it wouldn’t really change anything if I did. Maybe I’d just end up feeling worse or overthinking stuff. It seems easier to just leave it alone.
When you think about the possibility of feeling worse or overthinking, what specific worries or concerns come to mind?

I guess… I worry that talking about it or focusing on it would just make things more complicated. Maybe people would get annoyed or think I’m causing problems. I don’t really want to deal with that. It just seems safer not to say much.
Have there been times in the past when you did share your feelings or concerns—how did that go for you?
Yeah, a couple of times. It didn’t really go well. I felt like people just brushed it off or didn’t really listen. Sometimes it felt like I made things worse by bringing it up.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

ccd.relevant_history: I've always felt like I didn’t quite fit in with my family or friends growing up, and that feeling only got worse after a few painful breakups. Over time, these experiences made me doubt that anyone could ever truly care for me or want to stay by my side.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I often tell myself that if I try to get close to someone, they will eventually leave or reject me because deep down, I believe I’m not worth loving. I also feel like being alone is my destiny, so it’s better to expect isolation than to risk getting hurt.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid opening up to people or showing my true feelings because I’m scared they’ll see the “real” me and walk away. Sometimes, I push others away before they have a chance to leave, thinking it’s safer that way, even though it makes me feel lonelier.
ccd.situation: I’m in a new relationship, and my partner hasn’t responded to my message all evening. I start wondering if they’ve lost interest or if I’ve done something wrong.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: They don’t really want to be with me; I’m just a burden. I always end up alone because I’m not lovable. Maybe I should just stop trying so hard because it’s pointless.
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, sad, and hopeless, like I’m sinking into a pit of loneliness that I can’t escape from.
ccd.behaviors: I withdraw and stop texting back, avoiding any further contact. Sometimes I ruminate over what I did wrong, which makes me feel even worse and less motivated to reach out again.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been… I guess kind of up and down. Work kept me busy, but I just kept thinking a lot, maybe too much. Some days are fine, but then little things get to me, you know? Like, I find myself overthinking stuff, especially when I’m by myself.
When you notice yourself overthinking, what kinds of thoughts usually come up for you?

Honestly, it’s usually about my relationship, or just people in general. Like, if someone doesn’t text me back right away, I start wondering if I said something wrong, or if they’re upset with me for some reason. I know it sounds silly, but sometimes I really can’t help it. I just keep replaying little moments in my head, trying to figure out what I did wrong.
That sounds really tough. When you replay those moments, what feelings or physical sensations do you notice in your body?

Yeah, it’s pretty exhausting. I get this heavy feeling in my chest, like I can’t breathe properly or something’s weighing me down. My stomach gets knotted up too, almost like I’m dreading something bad is about to happen. Sometimes I feel really restless and jittery, like I can’t sit still.
Those sensations sound very uncomfortable. When you start to feel this way, what do you usually do to try to cope or manage those feelings?

Um, I guess I mostly just shut down. I stop messaging people or answering calls, even if part of me wants to reach out. I put my phone away and just kind of retreat into myself, maybe distract myself with TV or scrolling, but nothing really helps. After a while, I just end up feeling even lonelier.

It sounds like withdrawing might feel safe but also leaves you feeling more isolated. Have you noticed any times when you’ve responded differently in those moments, or ways that helped you feel a bit better?

Hmm… that’s a good question. I mean, sometimes if I’m really upset, I’ll try journaling or go for a walk, but it doesn’t always make the thoughts go away. There have been times when I’ve pushed myself to text a friend, but I always worry afterward if I said too much or bothered them. Honestly, it’s pretty rare for me to reach out first—most of the time I just keep it all to myself.
It sounds like reaching out can be both helpful and stressful for you. When you worry about bothering others after reaching out, what specific thoughts go through your mind?

Yeah, it’s like this back-and-forth in my head. I’ll think, “What if they’re just being polite and don’t actually want to talk to me?” Or, “Maybe they’ll think I’m too needy or annoying for sharing how I feel.” Sometimes I even wonder if I’m a burden and they’d be happier if I didn’t message them at all. I end up overanalyzing every little thing I said and feel kind of embarrassed afterward.

ccd.relevant_history: Throughout my life, I have often faced challenges where I felt unsupported and unable to change my circumstances, leading me to believe that no matter what I do, I won’t be able to improve my situation. Experiences of repeated setbacks and feeling overwhelmed have reinforced this sense of helplessness. These moments have made me doubt my ability to influence my personal growth or take control of my future.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I often think that trying hard won’t make a difference because things are just out of my control. I believe that if I fail once, it means I’m just not capable of succeeding. I also feel that asking for help is a sign that I’m weak or destined to remain stuck where I am.
ccd.coping_strategies: When I feel overwhelmed, I tend to avoid facing my problems and distract myself with activities that don’t require much effort. Sometimes I withdraw from people because I’m afraid they’ll see me as incompetent. I might also procrastinate or give up quickly because it feels safer than risking failure.
ccd.situation: I’m trying to learn a new skill to improve my career prospects, but I keep feeling like I’m not making any progress. Despite putting in some effort, I catch myself thinking that I’ll never be good enough and that it’s pointless to keep trying.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m just no good at this.” “Why bother? I’ll never get it right.” “Everyone else is better than me, so I might as well give up.” “If I ask for help, people will think I’m incapable.”
ccd.emotions: I feel frustrated, discouraged, and overwhelmed. There’s also a deep sadness that comes from feeling stuck and powerless. Sometimes I feel anxious about the future because I don’t see a way forward.
ccd.behaviors: I tend to avoid practicing or working on the skill because it feels too hard and I’m afraid of failing. I might withdraw from opportunities to learn or connect with others who could support me. Often, I procrastinate and distract myself instead of facing the challenge head-on.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, I guess it’s been a bit up and down. I’ve been trying to work on this new skill for my job, but to be honest, it’s kind of draining. Sometimes I feel like I’m just spinning my wheels and not getting anywhere. It’s been a little frustrating, I guess.
What thoughts go through your mind when you notice yourself feeling like you’re spinning your wheels and not making progress?

Well, a lot goes through my head honestly. Mostly, it’s stuff like, “Why is this so hard for me?” or like, “Maybe I’m just not cut out for this.” Sometimes I catch myself thinking, “Other people probably pick this up way faster.” And then I start wondering if it’s even worth it to keep trying.
When you have those thoughts about not being cut out for this or others picking it up faster, how do those thoughts affect your motivation or the way you approach practicing the skill?

Yeah, I think those thoughts sort of zap my motivation, honestly. Like, the more I focus on them, the less I want to even bother trying. Sometimes I’ll just sit and stare at what I’m supposed to do, and then I end up doing something else completely—like scrolling on my phone or just zoning out. It almost feels easier to avoid it than to keep feeling like I’m failing at it.
When you find yourself avoiding the task and doing something else instead, what emotions or physical sensations do you notice in those moments?

Um, I think I mostly feel this mix of frustration and guilt. There’s this heaviness in my chest, like a tightness, and sometimes I feel kind of restless, like I can’t sit still but also can’t get started. It’s like my mind just keeps circling around how I should be doing better. Underneath all that, there’s this kind of sadness that creeps in, too.
That sounds really tough to experience. When you notice that heaviness and restlessness, what do you usually do next—do you stay with those feelings, or try to change them somehow?

Most of the time, I just try to distract myself so I don’t have to sit with it. I’ll put on some music or watch videos, anything that helps me not think about how stuck I feel. Sometimes I just end up lying on my bed, scrolling through my phone without really paying attention to anything. I guess I don’t really try to deal with the feelings—I just sort of hope they’ll go away on their own.
When you distract yourself in those moments, does it help you feel better in the long run, or do the feelings tend to come back afterward?

Honestly, it only helps for a little while. As soon as I stop distracting myself, all those feelings kind of rush back in, sometimes even stronger than before. I start thinking about how much time I’ve wasted or how I’m falling behind, and then I feel even worse. It’s like a cycle I can’t really break out of.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like I can’t do things right, especially when trying to improve myself. Past experiences of failure or criticism have made me doubt my abilities and worth. This has made me hesitant to take on new challenges or pursue personal growth.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to change, I will just mess things up or disappoint others. People won’t accept me if they see my flaws. It’s safer to keep things as they are because I’m not capable of handling more.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid situations where I might fail or be judged. I keep my thoughts to myself and don’t share my goals or struggles with others. Sometimes I distract myself with routines or small tasks to avoid facing bigger issues.
ccd.situation: I want to start learning a new skill or hobby to grow personally, but I feel uncertain and hold back because I’m afraid I won’t be good enough or I’ll embarrass myself.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: I’m going to fail no matter what. People will think I’m a fool. I’m just not cut out for this kind of change.
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, self-doubt, sadness, and a sense of defeat.
ccd.behaviors: Avoiding signing up for classes or practicing new skills. Keeping my feelings and goals private. Sticking to familiar routines instead of trying new things.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting. Just the usual stuff—work, errands. Not much else going on.

I don’t know… I guess I mostly just try to get through the day. Sometimes I feel kind of… tired, I guess, or like I wish things were different. Not sure how to describe it. It’s not really anything special, just… normal, I suppose.

I guess… sometimes I just feel like I’m stuck. Like, I see other people doing things and I wonder if I should try something new too, but I’m not sure it’s worth it. I worry I’ll just mess it up, honestly. It’s easier to just keep things the way they are.
What do you imagine might happen if you did try something new, and how do those thoughts affect what you decide to do?

Um… I guess I imagine it wouldn’t go well. Maybe I’d make a fool of myself, or people would see that I’m not really good at it. It’s kind of embarrassing to think about. So I usually just don’t bother trying.
When you think about making a fool of yourself or being seen as not good enough, how does that make you feel in your body or emotionally?
I feel tense, I guess. My stomach kind of knots up, and I just want to disappear. It makes me feel… small, I suppose. Sometimes I just get really down on myself.
When those feelings of tension and wanting to disappear come up, what do you usually do to cope or respond in that moment?
Um, I usually just distract myself somehow. Maybe I do chores or scroll on my phone. Sometimes I just keep quiet and try not to think about it. I don’t really talk to anyone about it.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like people don’t really see me or want to be around me, especially when I was younger. I had a few close friends, but I usually felt like the odd one out and worried that others thought I was ugly or not worth their time.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t look good, people won’t like me or want to be close to me. I have to try hard to be noticed or accepted, but even then, I’m probably not enough. It’s safer not to get too close to others because I’ll just get rejected.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid social events or keep interactions short because I’m afraid people will judge how I look. Sometimes I try to distract myself with work or hobbies so I don’t have to think about feeling unattractive. Other times, I seek reassurance from others, but it doesn’t last long.
ccd.situation: I’m at a casual get-together with friends, and someone makes a comment about how others are dressed or look. I notice people glancing around and laughing, and I feel like all eyes are on me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: They’re probably thinking I look bad or out of place. I bet they don’t want me here. I must be so unattractive that no one wants to include me.
ccd.emotions: I feel embarrassed, lonely, and anxious. There’s a heavy sadness that I’m not good enough to be liked or accepted.
ccd.behaviors: I withdraw from the group, stay quiet, and avoid eye contact. I might leave early or find a way to be alone to hide how uncomfortable I feel.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Nothing really special happened. I hung out with some friends over the weekend, but I didn’t stay long. I just felt kind of off, so I left early.
When you say you felt “kind of off,” can you tell me more about what you were thinking or feeling in that moment?

I just felt out of place, like I didn’t really fit in. I kept thinking everyone else seemed comfortable and like they belonged, and I just… didn’t. Honestly, it made me want to disappear. I felt really self-conscious.
What do you think was going through your mind about yourself when you noticed those feelings of not fitting in?

I kept worrying that people were looking at me and thinking I didn’t measure up. Like maybe I looked weird, or that I just wasn’t as put-together as everyone else. I guess I was thinking I wasn’t good enough to be there with them. It’s hard not to feel like the odd one out.
When you notice those thoughts about not measuring up, how do they affect what you do next or how you behave in the situation?

Usually, I get really quiet and try not to draw attention to myself. I avoid eye contact and kind of hope no one talks to me. Sometimes I’ll make up a reason to leave early. It just feels easier to be alone than to stay and feel so uncomfortable.
What do you think might happen if you stayed a bit longer and tried to engage, even just a little, despite feeling uncomfortable?

I don’t know. Part of me thinks it would just make things worse—I’d say something awkward or look stupid, and people would notice. Or maybe they’d wonder why I’m even there. I guess I worry I’d just end up feeling even more out of place.
Have you had any past experiences where you stayed in a social situation despite feeling awkward, and how did those moments turn out?

Yeah, there have been a few times I tried to stick it out. Sometimes it was okay, like nothing horrible happened, but I still felt tense the whole time. Other times, I thought people seemed bored talking to me or just didn’t really want me there. I usually end up replaying everything in my head afterward and feeling like I messed up somehow.

ccd.relevant_history: I've always felt like people don't really notice me or find me interesting. Growing up, I rarely got compliments and often felt invisible in social settings, which made me believe I wasn’t attractive or worth attention.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don't look good or stand out, people won't like me. I have to be physically appealing to be accepted or valued by others.
ccd.coping_strategies: I usually avoid social events or keep to myself because I expect rejection. Sometimes I try to dress very carefully or act overly nice to get people to like me.
ccd.situation: At a small gathering with coworkers, I notice people chatting and laughing but no one seems to include me in the conversation.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I'm ugly and nobody wants to talk to me.” “They probably don’t like me because I’m not attractive.”
ccd.emotions: I feel lonely, sad, and anxious. There’s also a sense of shame and insecurity.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet and avoid joining in. I might even leave early or find a way to be alone during the event.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Work’s been pretty normal. I went to a small get-together with some coworkers last night, but I didn’t really stay long. I just felt kind of out of place there.
What kinds of thoughts were going through your mind while you were at the get-together?

Mostly I was thinking that no one would really notice if I was there or not. It felt like everyone else was having fun and connecting, and I was just sort of… on the sidelines. I guess I started wondering if there was something about me that just makes me forgettable.
When you notice those thoughts about being forgettable or on the sidelines, how do they make you feel emotionally in that moment?

I usually feel pretty down—like sad and kind of embarrassed. There’s this tight, anxious feeling in my chest. Sometimes I just want to leave so I don’t have to keep feeling that way around other people.
How do you usually respond or act when you notice those feelings and thoughts in social situations like that?

I tend to go quiet and keep to myself. If there’s a way to step outside or leave early without drawing attention, I’ll do that. Mostly, I just try not to be noticed—I don’t want anyone to see that I’m uncomfortable.
Have you noticed any times when you’ve stayed in a social situation despite those uncomfortable feelings? If so, what helped you stay?

Yeah, I guess there have been a few times. Usually, it’s when someone actually comes over and talks to me first, or if I’m with someone I know really well. That makes it a bit easier to stick around, even if I still feel awkward. Having someone include me helps, even if the feeling doesn’t totally go away.
What do you think that experience of being included by someone else says about your thoughts that you’re forgettable or on the sidelines?

I don’t know, I guess it kind of throws me off. Part of me wants to believe maybe I’m not as invisible as I think, but it’s hard to trust that feeling. Most of the time, I just assume people are being polite. It doesn’t really make the other thoughts go away—at least not for long.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and rejected by close family members, which left me with a deep sense of not being truly cared for. Past relationships have reinforced this feeling, as partners have left or distanced themselves unexpectedly, making me fear that abandonment is inevitable.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I believe that if I get too close to someone, they will eventually leave me because I'm not worthy of lasting love or attention. I also think that showing my true feelings will push others away, so I have to keep parts of myself hidden to avoid being left alone.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to stay emotionally distant or overly accommodating to prevent others from getting upset with me, hoping this will keep them around. At times, I might push people away first to avoid the pain of being abandoned unexpectedly.
ccd.situation: When my partner doesn’t respond to my messages for a few hours, I immediately worry that they are losing interest or planning to leave me. This uncertainty triggers a flood of anxious thoughts and feelings.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They probably don’t care about me anymore.” “I must have done something wrong to make them pull away.” “I’m going to be left alone again, just like always.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, sad, and hopeless, like there’s a heavy weight in my chest that won’t lift. Sometimes I get irritable or desperate, afraid that if I don’t act, I’ll lose this connection.
ccd.behaviors: I end up sending multiple texts to check in or apologize repeatedly, trying to get reassurance. At other times, I withdraw and avoid contacting them altogether, fearing that pushing too hard will confirm my worst fears.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um… I guess it’s been okay, but honestly, kind of up and down. Work was stressful and I’ve felt kind of on edge, just sort of tense for no real reason I can put my finger on. My sleep hasn’t been great either. I don’t know, I just feel a little unsettled, I guess.
When you notice that tension or feeling on edge, what kinds of thoughts tend to go through your mind?

Well, sometimes it just feels like something’s about to go wrong, even if nothing’s actually happening. I catch myself wondering if I upset someone or if I did something wrong without realizing it. Like, even with my partner, if they’re quiet or distracted, I start thinking maybe I annoyed them, or they’re mad at me. It’s like this constant worry in the back of my head that I can’t really shake off.
When those worries about upsetting someone come up, how do you usually respond or cope with them in the moment?

Um, well, I guess it depends on the situation, but a lot of times I’ll just start replaying things in my head, trying to figure out if I said something weird or if I should have acted differently. Sometimes I’ll try to check in with them—like, I’ll send a text just to see if they’re okay, or maybe say sorry even if I’m not sure what for. Other times, I kind of pull back and keep to myself, because I figure if I just stay quiet, maybe I won’t make it worse. It’s exhausting, honestly.
When you replay those moments or reach out to check in, how do you feel afterward? Does it usually help ease the worry, or does it tend to stay the same or get worse?

It’s kind of a mixed bag, honestly. Sometimes, if they respond quickly and seem normal, I’ll feel better for a little while, like this wave of relief. But more often than not, the worry just sort of comes back again, maybe even a little stronger, because I start feeling embarrassed for reaching out too much or wonder if I’m being annoying. Then I’ll sit there second-guessing myself—like, was that too much? Should I just leave them alone? It’s this weird cycle that doesn’t really let up.
That sounds really challenging. When you notice those thoughts about being annoying or second-guessing yourself, what do you say to yourself or believe about those feelings?

Honestly, I end up telling myself that I probably am being too much, or that I’m just making things worse by not being able to let things go. I get frustrated with myself, like, “why can’t I just relax and trust that things are fine?” But then another part of me just feels… I don’t know, scared? Like if I don’t check in, I’m taking this big risk and something bad might happen—like they’ll get sick of me or pull away. So I guess I mostly end up feeling stuck, not really believing that I’m handling things the right way, no matter what I do.

It sounds like there's a part of you that worries about the consequences of not checking in, and another part that criticizes you for doing it too much; how do you think these two parts affect how you feel day to day?

Yeah, it’s honestly exhausting. I feel like I’m constantly caught in the middle—never quite comfortable, because if I reach out, I worry I’m smothering them, but if I don’t, I’m anxious that I’m pushing them away or missing something. It makes just regular days feel heavier, like I’m always on alert or bracing for something to go wrong. Most of the time, I just feel tired and sort of sad, like it’ll never really get easier.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like I need to work extra hard to be accepted, especially at work where I worry people don’t really want me around. Growing up, I often felt overlooked or ignored by family members, which made me think I wasn’t worth much. These feelings have stuck with me and come up a lot when I face pressure or criticism.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t keep everyone happy and avoid conflict, people will like me and want me around. Showing my true feelings or standing up for myself will push others away because I’m basically unlovable. I have to be perfect and always agreeable to avoid being rejected.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to please my coworkers and bosses by saying yes to everything, even when I’m overwhelmed. I avoid sharing my honest opinions or feelings to keep the peace and prevent anyone from getting upset with me. Sometimes I overwork myself to prove I’m valuable and deserving of attention.
ccd.situation: At work, my manager asks me to take on a high-pressure project with a tight deadline, and I worry I won’t be able to handle it without disappointing everyone. I feel like if I say no or express concerns, they’ll think I’m weak or don’t belong on the team.
ccd.automatic_thoughts:
“If I mess this up, they won’t want me here anymore.”  
“They probably already think I’m not good enough.”  
“I can’t say no; I have to make them happy no matter what.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, fear of rejection, and sadness. I feel overwhelmed and like I’m constantly on edge, afraid of being found out as a failure.
ccd.behaviors: I say yes to the project without expressing my concerns and push myself to work late hours. I avoid asking for help or admitting I’m struggling. I try extra hard to be agreeable and keep my thoughts to myself, even when I’m exhausted.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um, it’s been… okay, I guess. Work’s been a bit busy, but nothing I can’t handle. I’ve been trying to keep up with everything and not let anyone down. Honestly, I don’t want to complain or anything—I know everyone has a lot going on.
What kinds of thoughts go through your mind when you feel the pressure to keep up and not let anyone down?

Um, I guess I just worry a lot about what everyone thinks of me. I keep thinking that if I don’t do a good job or if I say the wrong thing, people might not want me around. Sometimes I just feel like I have to prove that I belong, you know? I don’t want to make a fuss, so I just try to do whatever’s asked of me.
When you notice those worries about what others think or needing to prove you belong, how does that affect the way you feel emotionally or physically in the moment?

Oh, um… I get really nervous, honestly. My heart races and I feel kind of tense, like I can’t relax. Sometimes I feel a bit sad too, but I try not to show it because I don’t want anyone to think I’m not coping. I just do my best to push through.
What do you usually do or tell yourself when you start feeling that nervousness and sadness, especially when you want to keep pushing through?

I usually just tell myself to keep going and not make a big deal out of it. Like, I try to remind myself that other people probably handle things better, so I should too. I don’t want to bother anyone or seem like I can’t handle my work. So, I just try to focus on doing a good job and hope that’s enough.
What do you think happens to those feelings of nervousness and sadness when you keep pushing through and tell yourself to just handle it quietly?

Hmm… I guess they don’t really go away, if I’m being honest. It’s like they just stay in the background, and sometimes they get even stronger if things get more stressful. But I feel like I have to just deal with it because I don’t want to cause any trouble or seem like I’m not strong enough. It’s kind of tiring, but I don’t really know what else to do.
When those feelings get stronger and more tiring, what do you notice about how it affects your energy or motivation to keep going?

Yeah, it definitely makes me feel more worn out. Sometimes I get so tired that it’s hard to focus or feel excited about anything, even outside of work. But I usually just push myself anyway, because I don’t want anyone to notice I’m struggling. I think I just worry people wouldn’t understand, so I try to act like everything’s fine.

core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt criticized and blamed by my family, which made me believe I was inherently bad. Over time, I started to see myself as dangerous and toxic, pushing others away to avoid causing harm.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If people get close to me, they’ll get hurt or disappointed because there’s something wrong with me deep down. I have to keep my distance or hide parts of myself to protect others and avoid rejection.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid social gatherings and keep conversations superficial to prevent others from finding out how bad I really am. Sometimes I isolate myself or shut down emotionally so I don’t have to face judgment or conflict.
ccd.situation: I was invited to a friend’s birthday party, but as the day approached, I started feeling anxious about being around others and feared I’d say or do something awful.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “What if I say something terrible and ruin the night? They’ll see how toxic I am and won’t want to be around me anymore.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, ashamed, and isolated, with a heavy weight of fear that I’m going to mess everything up.
ccd.behaviors: I canceled my plans at the last minute and spent the evening alone, convincing myself it was safer not to risk being rejected.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been okay, I guess. I mean, work was a bit stressful, but that’s pretty normal, right? Oh, actually, have you ever noticed how loud the birds are in the mornings lately? I swear, there’s this one that sits right outside my window and just goes off at like 5am.
It sounds like the noise from the birds is catching your attention—how do you usually feel or react when they start making noise so early?

Oh, I usually wake up before my alarm and just sort of lay there, trying to ignore it. Sometimes I feel kind of annoyed, but mostly I just end up thinking about the day ahead and all the stuff I have to deal with. It’s funny, because everyone else seems to just sleep through it or not care. Did you ever have a neighbor who did renovations really early in the morning? That’s way worse, honestly.
When you notice yourself thinking about the day ahead during those early mornings, what kinds of thoughts or worries tend to come up for you?

Uh, I don’t know, it’s kind of random sometimes. Like, sometimes I’ll worry about stuff at work or if I forgot to send an email, but other times it’s just… I don’t know, I start thinking about all these little things I could mess up. Oh, and then I’ll remember something embarrassing I said years ago, and that just spirals. You ever get those memories that just pop up out of nowhere and make you cringe?
Those random worries and memories sound pretty distracting—when those embarrassing memories come up, what do you usually do or tell yourself in response?

Honestly, I mostly just try to shove them away, like, “Ugh, not this again,” and I’ll maybe turn on some music or scroll on my phone to get my mind off it. Sometimes it feels like I can’t escape my own brain, though. I start thinking, why did I even say that, or what’s wrong with me? Anyway, sometimes I wonder if people remember that stuff about me, but I guess everybody has embarrassing moments, right?
It sounds like those thoughts can be pretty persistent and hard to shake—when you catch yourself thinking, “What’s wrong with me?” how does that affect your mood or what you do next?

Yeah, it kind of just makes everything heavier, I guess. Like, I’ll feel this sort of pit in my stomach, and then I don’t really want to talk to anyone for a while. Sometimes I start replaying different situations in my head, trying to figure out if I messed something up without even realizing it. Oh, and then I usually just distract myself, like watching videos or playing games—anything to not think about it.
When you replay those situations and try to figure out if you messed up, what kinds of conclusions do you tend to come to about yourself or what happened?

Um, most of the time I end up thinking I said something wrong or that I probably came across as weird or annoying. It’s like, even if people didn’t react badly, I just assume I did something off. I guess I usually convince myself it’s safer to just keep quiet or avoid saying much next time. Oh, actually, the other day I was at lunch with coworkers and barely spoke because I didn’t want to accidentally say something stupid—so, yeah, I guess it definitely affects me.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, the patient often felt powerless in their family, with decisions made without their input and frequent conflicts that they couldn't influence. This has led to a deep-seated feeling of being unable to change their circumstances or assert control in family interactions.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I try to assert myself, things will only get worse." "I have no power to influence what happens in my family." "It’s safer to just go along and avoid making waves."
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to withdraw during family disagreements, avoids expressing opinions, and sometimes appeases others to prevent conflict. They may also ruminate silently, feeling overwhelmed but not speaking up.
ccd.situation: During a recent family gathering, the patient felt overwhelmed as relatives argued about plans and decisions, leaving them feeling sidelined and ignored.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I'm completely out of control here." "No one is listening to me." "If I say something, it will just make things worse."
ccd.emotions: Frustration, sadness, helplessness, and anxiety.
ccd.behaviors: The patient stayed quiet, avoided eye contact, and physically distanced themselves from the group, later feeling isolated and resigned.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Honestly? Not great. There was this family thing over the weekend, and, I don’t know, it was just more of the same—people arguing, no one really paying attention to what I wanted. I just… kind of drifted through it. It’s exhausting.
When you say you "drifted through it," what was going through your mind in those moments?

I don’t know... It just felt like there was no point in even trying to get involved. Like, everyone was so loud and set on what they wanted, and I felt like I’d just make things worse if I spoke up. So I just kept thinking, "Why bother?" and waited for it to be over.
What emotions did you notice in yourself while you were thinking, "Why bother?" during the family gathering?

Uh… I guess I felt really frustrated, honestly. And maybe a little sad, too, just sitting there while everyone else steamrolled over me. It’s hard not to feel kind of helpless when it happens over and over. Sometimes I wonder if it’ll ever be any different.
When you imagine the possibility of it being different, what do you think might need to change for you to feel less helpless in these situations?

I don’t even know if anything could change at this point. It just seems like my family is always going to be this way, and I’m always going to be the one who gets ignored. Maybe if people actually cared about what I thought, but… that’s probably not going to happen. I just try to get through it.
What do you notice about yourself or your thoughts when you tell yourself that people probably won’t care about what you think?

I just feel smaller, I guess. Like, what's the point in speaking up if it won't matter? It kind of makes me angry too, honestly—like no matter what I do, I’m stuck in the same spot. It’s just this mix of being pissed off and totally worn out.
When you feel that mix of anger and being worn out, how do you usually respond or cope in those moments?

I usually just shut down. I pull back and stop talking, or I find some excuse to leave the room. Sometimes I just sit there and zone out until it’s over. It’s like, I’d rather disappear than deal with it.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt overwhelmed at work, especially when deadlines pile up and I don’t have full control over my tasks. Growing up, I was taught to avoid conflict and make others happy, which sometimes made me feel powerless in stressful situations.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t keep everyone happy, they’ll be upset with me, and I’ll be left alone to deal with the pressure. I can’t stand up for myself because it will only make things worse, so it’s safer to just go along with what others want.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to agree with whatever my boss or colleagues say, even if it stresses me out, to avoid conflict. I often put others’ needs before my own and avoid speaking up about my workload, hoping the pressure will eventually ease on its own.
ccd.situation: At work, when the pressure builds up with tight deadlines and high expectations, I feel like I have no control and just have to accept whatever comes my way.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m stuck in this mess and there’s nothing I can do.” “If I say no, they’ll think I’m weak or incapable.” “I’m just a victim of the situation and can’t change it.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, helplessness, frustration, and sadness.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid asserting myself or setting boundaries, keep working longer hours to try to keep up, and withdraw emotionally from colleagues to avoid conflict.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Oh, um, it's been okay, I guess. Work’s been a bit busy, but nothing I can’t handle. I just try to keep up and not cause any problems. But yeah, I’m fine, really.
When you say you try not to cause any problems at work, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind in those moments?

Well, I just worry that if I speak up or say something’s too much, people might get annoyed with me. I guess I just think it’s better to stay quiet and go along with whatever’s asked. I don’t want to be the person making things difficult for everyone else. It just feels safer that way, you know?
That sounds like it feels pretty stressful—when you think about staying quiet to avoid upsetting others, what emotions tend to come up for you?

Yeah, sometimes I do feel a bit anxious about it. I get this sort of knot in my stomach, like I’m always on edge, worried I’ll mess things up. I also feel frustrated with myself afterwards for not saying anything. But mostly, I just try to push those feelings aside and keep going.
When you notice that knot in your stomach and feel on edge, what do you usually do in those moments—how do you respond or cope?

Honestly, I usually just keep my head down and try to work harder. I’ll stay late if I have to, just to make sure everything gets done and nobody’s disappointed. Sometimes I just sort of keep to myself and avoid talking to people too much. I guess I hope the stress will go away if I just push through it.
It sounds like you put a lot of pressure on yourself to fix things by working harder—what do you think might happen if you tried speaking up about your workload or how you’re feeling?

Oh, um, I don’t know... I guess I worry people might think I’m not capable or that I can’t handle my job. I’m afraid they’d be upset or disappointed in me, and I really don’t want that. It just feels risky—like maybe things would get even worse if I complained. I just don’t want to make a fuss or be seen as a problem.
When you imagine those people being upset or disappointed if you spoke up, what does that mean to you about yourself or your value at work?

Hmm, I guess it makes me feel like I’m letting everyone down. Like I’m not good enough or I’m just a burden. If people are unhappy with me, it kind of feels like I don’t really belong, or they’d be better off without me causing trouble. I try not to think about that too much, but it does cross my mind.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt that no matter how hard I try, I can’t control what happens at work. Past experiences where my efforts went unnoticed or were overridden by others have made me feel small and powerless. This has led me to avoid speaking up or asserting myself in stressful situations.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t please others and avoid conflict, they won’t reject or criticize me. Showing weakness means I’m vulnerable and could be taken advantage of. I have to keep the peace to avoid feeling helpless or exposed.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to agree quickly with others, even if I disagree, to avoid tension. I often stay silent when I’m upset or overwhelmed, hoping the situation will blow over. I try to work harder and longer to prove my worth, even when I’m exhausted.
ccd.situation: At work, I’m given a tight deadline on a project that feels overwhelming, and my supervisor adds last-minute changes. I worry I won’t be able to meet expectations and fear others will see me as weak.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I can’t handle this; I’m going to mess up.” “If I say no or ask for help, they’ll think I’m useless.” “I’m just not strong enough to deal with this pressure.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, helplessness, frustration, and a deep sense of vulnerability.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid asking for support and push myself to keep going, even when I feel overwhelmed. I agree to unrealistic demands and suppress my own needs to keep others happy. I withdraw emotionally and sometimes procrastinate to avoid the stress.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s just been a little busy, but nothing I can’t handle. I’ve just tried to keep up with what everyone needs from me. Sorry, I know other people have it worse, so I shouldn’t complain.
It sounds like you’ve been managing a lot at work—what kinds of thoughts go through your mind when you feel like you need to keep up with everyone’s needs?

Well, I just… I guess I worry a bit about letting people down if I can’t keep up. I don’t want anyone to think I’m not trying hard enough. Sometimes I find myself thinking I should be able to handle it all. Sorry, I know that probably sounds silly.
That worry about letting people down sounds important to you; when you think you should be able to handle everything, what feelings or physical sensations do you notice in those moments?

Oh, um, I guess I get this sort of tight feeling in my chest, and my stomach feels weird—like it’s all knotted up. I feel kind of anxious, and sometimes my hands get a bit shaky. I just really don’t want to cause any problems for anyone. Sorry, I’m probably making a bigger deal out of it than it really is.
That sounds really uncomfortable. When you notice those anxious feelings and physical sensations, what do you usually do or how do you respond to try to cope with them?

Honestly, I just try to keep working and focus on what people need from me. I usually don’t say anything and just hope it gets better if I push through. Sometimes I tell myself it’ll be fine as long as I don’t draw attention to how I’m feeling. I’d rather not bother anyone with my stuff, you know?
It sounds like you often push through without sharing how you’re feeling; what do you imagine might happen if you did talk to someone about the stress you’re experiencing?

Um, I’m not really sure… I guess I worry they might think I’m not cut out for the job, or that I’m just making excuses. Maybe they’d get frustrated with me, or see me as weak. I just really wouldn’t want to let anyone down or be a burden. I don’t think I’d know how to even bring it up without sounding like I’m complaining.
That fear of being seen as weak or a burden sounds really tough; have there been times when you shared how you felt and it went differently than you expected?

Hmm… I’m trying to think. I don’t really talk about this stuff much, to be honest. I guess maybe once or twice I mentioned feeling tired to a coworker, and they just kind of brushed it off or changed the subject. So, I usually decide it’s better not to say anything.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated feelings of rejection and neglect in past relationships, leading to a deep sense of not being valued. They often felt overshadowed by others and learned to put others’ needs before their own to gain approval.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t please others, they will leave me or think less of me. My worth depends on how much I can do for others. Showing my true feelings might push people away.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to agree with others even when they disagree, avoids expressing their own needs, and overcommits to help others to prevent conflict or rejection. They suppress negative emotions to maintain harmony.
ccd.situation: In a recent argument with a close friend, the patient felt criticized and worried that the friend was upset with them for not being supportive enough.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I'm such a failure for not being there like I should. They probably think I’m useless and don’t care. I’m just a burden to everyone."
ccd.emotions: Feelings of sadness, anxiety, and shame arise, accompanied by a deep sense of loneliness and fear of abandonment.
ccd.behaviors: The patient withdraws emotionally, apologizes excessively, and tries to make up for perceived shortcomings by overhelping or avoiding future conflicts altogether.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting, just the usual stuff. I’ve just been keeping busy with work and trying to make sure everyone’s happy. Sorry, I don’t have much to report.
It sounds like you’ve been focusing a lot on others—how do you feel when you’re trying to make sure everyone else is happy?

I mean, I guess it feels good when people are happy with me, like I’m doing something right. Sometimes it’s a little tiring, but I don’t really mind. I just want things to be okay and for people not to get upset. It’s easier that way, I think.
What goes through your mind when you notice that someone might be upset or unhappy with you?

Um, I usually start worrying that I did something wrong. I feel really anxious, like maybe I messed up somehow. I just want to fix things as quickly as possible so everything’s okay again. I don't want anyone to feel bad because of me.
When you start worrying you might have done something wrong, what kind of thoughts or images come to mind about what might happen next?

I guess I picture them being disappointed in me or maybe even pulling away. Like, maybe they’ll start ignoring me or not want to talk to me as much. I start thinking that they’d be better off without me around, honestly. I just keep replaying what I did wrong in my head.
That sounds really painful—when those thoughts come up, how do you usually respond to them?

Um, I usually just feel really bad and try to apologize a lot, even if I’m not totally sure what I did. And then I might try to do something nice for them, just to make up for it. I kind of end up avoiding talking about how I feel because I don’t want to make things worse. I just focus on making sure they’re okay.
When you avoid talking about how you feel, what do you notice happens inside you emotionally or physically?

Um, I guess I start to feel kind of heavy inside, like there’s this knot in my stomach. Sometimes I feel really alone, even when people are around. It’s hard to relax, and I get kind of anxious and restless. But I don’t really know how to say any of that out loud.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like I have to work extra hard to be accepted by others, especially at work. Growing up, I was often told I wasn’t good enough, so I developed a habit of trying to please people to avoid rejection.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t make everyone happy, they won’t like me. I have to put others’ needs before my own to be accepted. Showing my true feelings might push people away.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to agree with everything my coworkers say, even if I disagree, just to avoid conflict. I often take on extra tasks to prove my value, even when I’m overwhelmed. I avoid speaking up about my own needs or opinions.
ccd.situation: At work, my manager gives me a tight deadline and asks for extra help on a project. I immediately worry I won’t be liked if I say no or ask for support.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I don’t say yes, they’ll think I’m unreliable.” “I have to do this perfectly or they won’t care about me.” “I’m just a burden to everyone.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, fear of rejection, sadness, feeling overwhelmed, and a deep sense of loneliness.
ccd.behaviors: I stay late working to finish the tasks, even if it means neglecting my own needs. I avoid asking for help or expressing how stressed I feel. I smile and agree with others, hiding my true feelings.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um, it’s been alright, I guess. Work has been kind of busy, but that’s normal. I just try to keep up and do my best so things don’t get too overwhelming. But overall, I think I’m doing fine, thank you for asking.
When you think about the times at work that feel overwhelming, what kinds of thoughts usually go through your mind?

Hmm… I guess I mostly just worry about not letting anyone down. I don’t want people to think I’m not trying hard enough. Sometimes I wonder if everyone else is handling things better than I am. I just really hope I’m doing enough, you know?
That sounds like a lot of pressure. When you notice those worries about not doing enough or comparing yourself to others, how do you usually respond or cope in those moments?

Oh, um, I guess I just try to work even harder, so nobody has a reason to be upset with me. I’ll usually say yes to whatever is asked, even if I’m already busy. I don’t really want to make a fuss or let anyone down. Most of the time, I just focus on getting everything done, even if it means staying late or skipping breaks.
It sounds like you put a lot on yourself to meet others’ expectations. How do you feel physically and emotionally after pushing yourself to work late and skip breaks?

Yeah, I guess I do feel kind of tired a lot of the time. Sometimes I get this tight feeling in my chest, and I feel pretty drained when I finally get home. I just try not to think about it too much. Emotionally…I think I just feel kind of empty or sad sometimes, but I usually brush it off and tell myself it’s not a big deal.
When those feelings of emptiness or sadness come up, what do you notice about the thoughts or messages you tell yourself that lead you to brush them off?

Hmm… I guess I just tell myself not to make a big deal out of things, because everyone else seems to handle it fine. I worry that if I complain, people might get annoyed or think less of me. I try to remind myself to just keep going and not bother anyone. Honestly, I’m not even sure my feelings are really important compared to what everyone else needs.
That sounds really tough to carry on like that. How do you think it would feel or what might happen if you allowed yourself to acknowledge those feelings more openly instead of pushing them aside?

Um, I’m not really sure. I guess part of me is worried that if I did, I might seem selfish or like I’m just making problems for others. I don’t want people to think I’m difficult or too much to handle. I guess I just worry it might push people away if I’m honest about how I feel.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient often feels overwhelmed by daily challenges and has a history of feeling powerless in family and work situations. Past experiences of being dismissed or ignored have reinforced a sense of victimhood and helplessness.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "I have to keep everyone happy to avoid conflict." "If I don’t please others, they will reject me or things will get worse." "I can’t stand up for myself because it will only make things harder."
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to agree with others even when uncomfortable, avoids expressing personal needs, and tries to anticipate and prevent conflict by over-accommodating. They may withdraw emotionally when feeling overwhelmed.
ccd.situation: At work, the patient is asked to take on extra tasks despite already feeling overloaded, and they worry about disappointing their boss or coworkers if they say no.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "If I say no, they’ll think I’m useless and I’ll lose their support." "I’m stuck and can’t do anything about this." "I’m just a victim in this situation."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, helplessness, frustration, and sadness.
ccd.behaviors: The patient agrees to take on the extra tasks, suppresses their own needs, and may later feel exhausted or resentful but avoids addressing the issue directly.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been a little busy, but nothing I can’t handle. I just try to keep up with everything and not let anyone down. Honestly, I’m just glad to be here and talk.
When you say you try not to let anyone down, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind in those moments?

Oh, um… I guess I just worry that if I mess up or say no to something, people will be disappointed in me. I really don’t want to cause any problems or make things harder for anyone. Sometimes I catch myself thinking, “What if they get upset with me?” So, I just try to do whatever’s asked, even if it’s a lot.
What feelings do you notice in your body or mood when you have those worries about disappointing others?

Um, I guess I feel pretty tense, like my shoulders get tight and my stomach feels a bit off. Sometimes I feel sort of anxious, like I have to keep moving or fix things. And I guess there’s kind of this heaviness, almost like I’m weighed down. It’s just… uncomfortable.
When you notice that tension and heaviness, what do you usually do next? Do you try to take a break, push through, or something else?

Usually, I just push through and keep doing what needs to be done. I don’t really take breaks, even when I probably should. I just tell myself it’ll be fine and try not to think about it too much. I guess I worry that if I stop or speak up, I’ll make things worse.
Have there been times when pushing through has helped, and other times when it might have made things harder for you?

Yeah, I think sometimes it does help, like people seem happy with me and I get things done. But other times, I just end up really tired or kind of frustrated afterwards. I don’t usually say anything, though—I just sort of keep it to myself. I don’t want to seem difficult or ungrateful.
What do you imagine might happen if you did express your feelings or limits to others instead of keeping them to yourself?

Um, I guess I worry that people might get upset or think I’m being selfish. Maybe they’d be disappointed in me, or not want to rely on me anymore. I just… I don’t know, I’m afraid it would change how they see me. It just feels safer to keep quiet.

ccd.relevant_history: I've always felt like no matter how hard I work, I can't get ahead. In previous jobs, I struggled with feeling stuck and not in control, which made me anxious and discouraged. This history makes it hard to believe things can change now.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to change my situation, I'll just fail or make things worse. Asking for help means I’m weak and will be judged. There’s no way out of this pressure, so I have to just keep pushing through alone.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid talking about my struggles and keep everything bottled up. Sometimes I procrastinate because I feel overwhelmed. Other times, I work late to avoid facing the feeling of being trapped.
ccd.situation: At work, I have constant deadlines and high expectations from my boss. The workload feels endless, and I don’t see a clear way to manage everything or get support.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I can’t handle all this.” “I’m stuck and there’s no escape.” “If I ask for help, I’ll just look incompetent.” “I’m going to fail no matter what.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, overwhelmed, and hopeless. There’s also a sense of frustration and sadness about not being able to change things.
ccd.behaviors: I withdraw from colleagues and avoid raising concerns. I push myself to work longer hours but end up feeling drained and less effective. Sometimes I procrastinate, which makes the pressure worse.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been... okay, I guess. Work has been really busy, just a lot going on and not much time to breathe. I’ve mostly just tried to get through it. Nothing major happened, just the usual stress.
When you say you’ve been trying to get through it, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind during those busy moments at work?

Honestly, I just keep thinking that there’s too much to do and not enough time. Sometimes I wonder if I’m even keeping up or if I’m missing something important. It’s hard not to feel like I’m just barely staying afloat. I don’t really let myself think much past that—I just try to plow ahead.
What do you notice happens to your mood or energy when you have those thoughts about not keeping up or missing something important?

I feel pretty drained, to be honest. It’s like this heavy feeling settles in and I get tense or on edge. My motivation drops, and I just want to zone out or avoid everything for a bit. But then I feel guilty about that, so I push myself to keep working.
When you notice that heavy, tense feeling and the urge to zone out, what do you usually do in those moments?

Sometimes I just stare at my screen and end up doing nothing for a while. Other times, I force myself to keep working, even if I’m totally wiped out. I try not to let anyone see that I’m struggling, so I just keep it to myself. I guess I just kind of shut down a bit.
What do you think might happen if you allowed yourself to take a short break or share how you’re feeling with someone at work?

I honestly worry people would think I can’t handle my job if I said anything. Taking a break feels risky, like I’ll fall even further behind or look lazy. I don’t really trust that it’d make things better. Mostly, I just feel like I have to deal with it on my own.
When you tell yourself that you have to deal with it on your own, how does that belief affect the way you handle your stress and workload?

It makes everything feel heavier, I guess. I don’t really look for support or try to change anything—I just push through and hope I make it to the end of the day. Sometimes I end up working later than I should or avoiding things until they pile up. It just feels like there’s no other option.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt like I couldn’t meet expectations at school or in social groups, which made me doubt my abilities. Friends and family sometimes overlooked my contributions, reinforcing the idea that I wasn’t capable. These experiences have made me expect failure or rejection in social interactions.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to speak up or take the lead, I’ll just mess things up or say something stupid. People won’t respect me if I show my true self because I’m not good enough. It’s safer to stay quiet and avoid drawing attention to my incompetence.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid speaking in groups or sharing my opinions, hoping no one will notice me. When I do engage, I sometimes go off-topic or ramble because I’m anxious and trying to fill the silence. I also rehearse conversations in my head to prepare, but I often feel overwhelmed when things don’t go as planned.
ccd.situation: At a small gathering with coworkers, I was asked to share my thoughts about a project we were all working on. I felt the pressure to say something meaningful but was afraid of sounding foolish or being judged.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “Oh no, what if I say something dumb? Everyone will think I don’t know what I’m talking about.” “I’m probably the only one who hasn’t figured this out.” “I should just stay quiet before I embarrass myself.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, embarrassment, self-doubt, and a sinking feeling of inadequacy.
ccd.behaviors: I stayed mostly silent during the discussion and changed the subject when I was finally asked for input. Later, I avoided eye contact and replayed the moment in my head, feeling more convinced that I’m incompetent.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work has been a little stressful, actually… there was this meeting where we had to share updates. Oh! And my neighbor’s dog got out again and I ended up helping catch it—which was kind of a weird distraction. Anyway, I guess overall it’s just been one of those weeks where things feel a bit off.
When you say things feel a bit off, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind when you notice that feeling?

Well, I guess I start thinking that maybe I’m not handling things as well as other people would. Like during that meeting, I kept worrying that whatever I said would sound dumb, so I sort of clammed up. Then afterward, I just kept replaying everything in my head. Actually, that reminds me—last year at my cousin’s birthday, something similar happened and I ended up spilling coffee on myself right when everyone was paying attention, so it kind of feels like a pattern or something.
What emotions do you notice when you think about those moments during the meeting or the birthday party?

Um, I guess mostly I feel embarrassed. Sometimes it’s almost like this heavy, sort of sinking feeling in my stomach. I get anxious too, and then it just kind of lingers after, like I can’t shake it off. Oh, and then I end up feeling bad for days—kind of like when I forgot my lines in the school play in sixth grade and thought everyone would remember it forever.
When you notice that sinking feeling or anxiety, what do you usually do or avoid doing in response?

I usually just try to keep quiet and fade into the background, honestly. If someone tries to get me to talk, I might just say something really quick or change the subject. Sometimes I’ll rehearse what I want to say over and over in my head, but then I get flustered if it doesn’t come out right. Oh, and later I usually replay everything in my mind, like I’m rewatching a movie I really hated the first time.
How do you think these ways of coping affect how you feel afterward or how those situations turn out?

I guess it makes things worse, to be honest. Like, I feel even more awkward after because I didn’t say what I wanted to, or I feel like people notice that I’m acting weird. It’s kind of like when you’re at a family dinner and you know you should say something, but then you don’t, and everyone just keeps talking over you. Then later, I just keep thinking about how I probably seemed clueless or like I didn’t belong there at all.
What do you imagine would happen if you tried speaking up more in those moments, even if you felt a bit nervous or unsure?

Um, I don’t know… I worry that I’d say something totally off or just mess it up somehow. Like, maybe people would look at me funny or just ignore what I said, which would feel pretty awful. Honestly, even when I think about saying something, my mind kind of goes blank, or I start rambling and get off track—like I did once during a team lunch when I ended up talking about my old cat for no reason. So I guess I just imagine it not going well and feeling even more embarrassed than if I’d stayed quiet.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has a longstanding fear of being left alone, which seems to stem from early experiences of inconsistent caregiving. Financial stress has recently heightened feelings of insecurity and vulnerability in relationships.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I struggle financially, people will see me as weak and reject me. I must not show my difficulties or others will leave me. It’s safer to keep my distance so I don’t get hurt.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid asking for help and hides financial worries from close ones. They often withdraw emotionally and minimize their needs to prevent potential rejection.
ccd.situation: The patient is worried about upcoming bills and feels unsure about discussing money problems with their partner or friends.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I tell them I’m having trouble, they’ll think less of me and eventually leave. I’m just not worth sticking around for.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and a deep sense of loneliness.
ccd.behaviors: Withdrawing from conversations about money, avoiding reaching out for support, and isolating themselves to avoid potential rejection.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting. Just… a bit tired, maybe.
When you notice feeling tired, what kinds of thoughts usually go through your mind?
I don’t know, really. I guess I just feel like it’s hard to keep up sometimes. Maybe I worry I’m not doing enough. It’s just kind of… there in the background.
When you think about not doing enough, what do you imagine might happen if that were true?
I don’t know… maybe people would get frustrated with me or something. Or… just sort of drift away. It’s probably silly. I just try not to think about it too much.
That sounds really tough to carry around. When these worries come up, how do you usually respond or cope with them?
I usually just keep it to myself. I don’t really talk about it with anyone. I just kind of… wait for it to pass, I guess. Sometimes I just distract myself with other things.
When you distract yourself, do you find that it helps you feel better right away, or do the worries tend to come back afterward?
It helps for a little while, I suppose. But the worries usually come back later. Sometimes even stronger. I just try to keep busy so I don’t think about it too much.
When those worries come back stronger, what do you notice happens to your mood or behavior at those times?
I get pretty down, I guess. I don’t really feel like talking to anyone. I kind of pull back and just stay by myself. It’s easier than trying to explain how I’m feeling.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like I let people down, especially in my close relationships. Growing up, I was frequently criticized and told I wasn’t good enough, which made me internalize a sense of failure. These feelings have carried into my adult relationships, where I worry that I’m always disappointing others.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t succeed or meet expectations, it means I’m worthless and unlovable. People will reject me if I show any weakness or make mistakes. I must constantly prove myself or I’ll be abandoned or ridiculed.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid deep conversations or sharing my feelings because I fear being judged or rejected. Sometimes I overwork myself trying to be perfect or overly helpful, hoping to earn approval. Other times, I withdraw completely, feeling there’s no point in trying.
ccd.situation: My partner pointed out that I seemed distant and asked if something was wrong during a recent conversation. I felt overwhelmed and unsure how to respond.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: I thought, "I’m such a failure for not being able to open up. They’re probably disappointed in me and will think I don’t care." I also worried, "Maybe they’ll leave me if I can’t be better."
ccd.emotions: I felt anxious, ashamed, and deeply sad. There was also a strong sense of loneliness and fear of rejection.
ccd.behaviors: I shut down and stopped talking, withdrawing emotionally from the conversation. Later, I avoided seeing my partner and kept to myself, reinforcing the distance between us.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing really out of the ordinary happened, but I’ve just felt… kind of heavy, I suppose. I’ve been a bit tired and maybe more distracted than usual. I don’t know, it’s hard to put my finger on it exactly.
When you notice that heaviness or tiredness, what kinds of thoughts tend to go through your mind in those moments?

I don’t know, it’s like… I keep thinking I should be doing better than I am. I feel like I’m letting people down, even if I can’t point to anything specific. Sometimes I just wonder if people are getting fed up with me being like this, you know? I guess I can’t shake the feeling that I’m… disappointing somehow.
That sounds really tough. When you think you’re letting people down or disappointing them, what do you usually imagine might happen as a result?

I mean, I guess I just picture them getting tired of me, or thinking I’m too much trouble to deal with. Sometimes I worry they’ll just sort of… give up on me, or decide to keep their distance. It’s almost like I expect people to eventually walk away if I don’t keep up or if I make mistakes. I know that sounds kind of dramatic, but that’s what goes through my head a lot of the time.
It makes sense that those worries would feel heavy on you. When those thoughts about people walking away come up, how do you usually respond or cope with them in the moment?

Um, I tend to just shut down, honestly. I’ll pull back and try not to say much, or sometimes I’ll even avoid people altogether, just because it feels safer. It’s like if I keep quiet or keep my distance, maybe I won’t mess things up even more. But then I end up feeling lonely, which I know doesn’t help, but it’s really hard to break out of that cycle.

It sounds like pulling back is a way to protect yourself, even if it feels isolating afterward. Have there been times when you reached out or stayed connected despite those fears—what was that like for you?

Hmm, I guess there have been a few times when I tried to push through and talk to someone, even though I was scared. Honestly, it was really uncomfortable—I was worried the whole time that I was saying the wrong thing or coming across as needy. Sometimes the other person was understanding, and it was a bit of a relief, but other times I just felt exposed or awkward after. It’s like, even when things go okay, I still end up second-guessing myself for days.

That’s really brave of you to keep trying, even with those uncomfortable feelings. What do you think might happen if you allowed yourself to be a bit more forgiving or gentle when you notice those moments of second-guessing?

I don’t know… part of me feels like if I’m not hard on myself, I’ll just get lazy or mess up even more. I worry that letting myself off the hook means I won’t improve, or that people will just get annoyed with me for not trying hard enough. But I guess there’s also a small part of me that wonders what it would be like to not feel so tense all the time. I’m just not sure how to even start doing that, or if it would actually help.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

ccd.relevant_history: I've often felt like I don't really fit in with others, especially during social events. Growing up, I was overlooked and sometimes teased, which made me believe that people don’t truly want me around. These experiences have made me expect loneliness as something inevitable.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I get close to people, they will eventually reject me. Trying to connect is risky because it usually ends in disappointment. It’s safer to keep my distance since that way I won’t get hurt as much.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid social gatherings or keep conversations very surface-level to prevent deeper connections. Sometimes I distract myself with work or hobbies to not feel the pain of being alone. I also find myself changing the topic quickly when conversations start to feel personal or emotional.
ccd.situation: At a party or group setting where people are talking and laughing, I feel like an outsider, unsure if anyone really wants me there.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one here really likes me.” “I’m just going to end up alone anyway.” “If I say something, I’ll probably embarrass myself or be ignored.” “Why bother trying to fit in when it never works out?”
ccd.emotions: Loneliness, sadness, anxiety, and a sense of hopelessness about forming meaningful connections.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet, avoid deep conversations, sometimes leave early, or avoid social events altogether. When I do engage, I often change the subject abruptly to avoid showing vulnerability.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um, it’s been alright, I guess. Work has been kind of busy—nothing too dramatic, just the usual meetings and deadlines. I did end up going to this thing at my friend’s place over the weekend, which was… well, I don’t know, it was fine. Actually, I tried this new dessert that had, like, way too much cinnamon, and it totally reminded me of this one time in high school when my friend dared me to eat a spoonful of cinnamon—it was such a mess, I couldn’t stop coughing for like, five minutes!
That cinnamon memory sounds pretty intense! When you think back to that high school moment, what feelings or thoughts come up for you?

Yeah, I mean… it’s kind of funny now, but at the time, it was super embarrassing. Everyone was laughing, and I just wanted to disappear. I guess moments like that stick with you, you know? Makes me think twice about doing silly things in front of people.
It sounds like that embarrassment really stayed with you. When you find yourself thinking about that moment now, how does it affect what you choose to do or say in social situations?

Yeah, I guess I just… try not to draw too much attention to myself. Like, sometimes I’ll want to say something or join in, but then I remember how awkward it felt back then. So I end up just listening or maybe just talking about random things—like, I’ll bring up something about work or movies instead of anything personal.
