Oh, speaking of movies, did you see they’re making another sequel to that superhero franchise? I always wonder how they keep coming up with new villains.

It sounds like you sometimes hold back in conversations to avoid feeling awkward again. When you stay on safer topics like work or movies, how do you feel inside—do you notice any change in your mood or sense of connection?

Hmm, I guess I just feel kind of...neutral? Like, I don’t feel as anxious, but I don’t really feel close to anyone either. It’s sort of like being in the same room as everyone else, but not really being part of things, if that makes sense. Oh, and sometimes I get distracted thinking about other stuff I have to do—like, I’ll remember I need to pick up groceries or reply to an email, and then I’m not really focused on the conversation at all.
That sense of being present but not fully connected sounds tough. When you notice your mind drifting to those other tasks during conversations, what do you think that experience is like for you?

Yeah, it’s kind of weird—it’s like I’m there, but not really there, if that makes sense. Sometimes I’ll catch myself nodding along but not really hearing what people are saying. I guess it feels a bit lonely, honestly, even if there are people all around. Oh, and I end up worrying that if someone noticed, they’d think I was being rude or uninterested, which isn’t what I want at all.
It sounds like that feeling of disconnect brings up worry about how others might perceive you. When those worries come up, what do you typically tell yourself about the situation or about yourself?

Uh… I usually just think, “Well, here we go again, I’m messing this up.” Or like, “They probably think I’m weird or don’t want to talk to me.” Sometimes I try to remind myself to just act normal, but then I get worried that I don’t even know what “normal” looks like. And then I might just get quiet or look at my phone—oh, which reminds me, I never replied to my cousin about that family thing next month.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like I don’t deserve good things or respect from others, especially after some past mistakes where I hurt people without meaning to. Growing up, I was criticized a lot for being selfish or dishonest, which made me believe deep down that I’m a bad person.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m not perfect or always doing the right thing, then I must be immoral and untrustworthy. People will reject me or think less of me if they see the real me. I have to hide my flaws or avoid close relationships to protect myself.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to change the subject when conversations get too personal or emotional to avoid revealing too much. Sometimes I push others away or stay quiet because I’m afraid they’ll find out how “bad” I am. I also try to distract myself with work or hobbies to not think about these feelings.
ccd.situation: I’m at a small gathering with coworkers, and the topic turns to personal values and mistakes. When someone asks about a time I felt proud of myself, I struggle to share and start talking about unrelated topics instead.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I say anything, they’ll think I’m a terrible person.” “I don’t belong here because I’m not a good person.” “If they knew the truth about me, they’d want nothing to do with me.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, ashamed, and isolated. There’s a heavy weight of guilt and fear that others will judge me harshly.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid answering questions directly and steer the conversation away from myself. I keep my distance physically and emotionally, and sometimes I leave the gathering early to escape the discomfort.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work has been kind of busy—we had this meeting about deadlines, and someone brought in these homemade cookies, which was nice. I tried a new recipe at home too, for banana bread, but it didn’t come out quite right. Anyway, nothing too out of the ordinary.
What was going through your mind when the banana bread didn’t turn out the way you hoped?

Oh, well, I was a little frustrated, I guess. I kept thinking I must have messed something up with the measurements or maybe the oven temperature wasn’t right. Actually, it reminded me of this time in school when I tried to help with a bake sale and ended up burning the brownies—everyone joked about it for weeks. So, I just kind of shrugged it off and cleaned up the kitchen.
When you remembered that school bake sale experience, how did it make you feel about trying again this time?

Honestly, I felt a little embarrassed, even though nobody was there to see it this time. It’s funny, but messing up something small like banana bread just brings back that old feeling, like I just can’t get things right sometimes. I almost didn’t want to try baking again, but then I got distracted looking for some new music to listen to instead. Speaking of music, have you ever heard that new album by The National?
It sounds like that feeling of embarrassment from the past still sticks with you when things don’t go as planned. What do you think that feeling tells you about yourself when it comes up?

Yeah, I guess it creeps up a lot when I mess things up, even little things. I start thinking that maybe I’m just not the kind of person who gets things right, you know? Like, other people can laugh off mistakes, but for me it just sort of confirms what I already worry about. Sorry, I keep rambling—oh, and by the way, I did end up finding a really good playlist, so at least my evening wasn’t a total loss.

That sounds really tough, feeling like mistakes confirm something negative about yourself. When you notice those thoughts that you’re “not the kind of person who gets things right,” how do you usually respond to them?

Um, usually I just try to brush it off or distract myself with something else—like music, or I’ll scroll through my phone or start organizing something random. Sometimes I just get really quiet and avoid talking to anyone, even if someone texts me. It’s easier to just avoid thinking about it, honestly. Oh, and I remembered—my friend asked if I wanted to join a trivia night, but I haven’t replied yet.
It sounds like distraction and withdrawal are ways you cope with those tough thoughts. When you think about joining the trivia night, what feelings or thoughts come up for you?

Oh, well, I mean, part of me thinks it could be fun, but then I get worried I’ll just say something dumb or mess up an answer and everyone will notice. I imagine people rolling their eyes or thinking I don’t belong there. So I keep putting off responding because it just feels easier to avoid it than risk feeling awkward in front of everyone. Actually, it reminds me of when I used to play board games with my cousins and I’d always get really quiet if I made a mistake.

ccd.relevant_history: I often feel powerless in social situations because I’ve experienced times when others took advantage of me or ignored my needs. Growing up, I was told to just accept things as they were, which made me believe I couldn’t change what happened around me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t give in or stay quiet, something bad will happen to me. People are likely to hurt or reject me if I stand up for myself. I have to accept things the way they are because I can’t control other people.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid speaking up or sharing my opinions in group settings. Sometimes I withdraw or stay silent to keep the peace, even when I feel upset. I also try to please others to avoid conflict or rejection.
ccd.situation: I’m in a group conversation, and someone interrupts me or disagrees strongly with what I say.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I can’t do anything about this.” “They’re going to think I’m weak or stupid.” “If I speak up, I’ll just make things worse for myself.”
ccd.emotions: Helplessness, frustration, anxiety, and sadness.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet and don’t share my thoughts. I avoid eye contact and try to make myself small. Afterwards, I ruminate about what I should have said but feel like it’s too late.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

It’s been alright, I guess. Work was a bit stressful—there were a couple of meetings where I just kind of kept quiet. Nothing major happened, but I felt a bit drained after. I don’t know, some days just feel heavier than others.
When you say you felt drained after the meetings, what kinds of thoughts were going through your mind during or after those times?

During the meetings, I kept thinking I should probably say something, but I just… couldn’t. Afterwards, I kept replaying things in my head, wondering if people noticed I didn’t speak up. Part of me felt like, “Well, it’s not like it would’ve mattered anyway.” I guess I also worried that people might think less of me.
That sounds tough. When you notice those thoughts about people thinking less of you, how does that affect the way you feel in your body or your emotions?

Yeah, it’s not a great feeling. I usually get this sort of tightness in my chest, and I start feeling really small—like I want to disappear. My heart beats faster, and I get anxious. Sometimes I just feel kind of sad or hopeless afterwards.
Thank you for sharing that. When you notice those physical sensations and feelings of wanting to disappear, what do you usually do next? How do you respond or cope in those moments?

Honestly, I mostly just keep quiet and try to avoid drawing attention to myself. I’ll look down or pretend to be busy with something else. If I can, I just wait for the meeting to end so I can leave. Then later, I spend a lot of time thinking about what I should’ve said or done differently.
It sounds like those moments are really uncomfortable for you. When you reflect later on what you should’ve said or done differently, what kinds of things do you tell yourself?

I usually tell myself that I messed up, or that I should’ve just spoken up like everyone else. Sometimes I think, “Why can’t I just be more confident?” Or, “People probably think I’m useless.” I get frustrated with myself for not doing anything.

Those thoughts sound really critical and painful. When you catch yourself thinking “I messed up” or “People probably think I’m useless,” how much do you believe those thoughts on a scale from 0 to 100?

It honestly feels like… almost 100 sometimes. In the moment, it’s hard to imagine that those thoughts aren’t true. I just kind of accept them without really questioning it. It’s like that’s just how things are.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like I don’t really belong or matter to others, especially in social situations where I’m ignored or overlooked. Growing up, I rarely got much attention or affection, which made me feel unwanted and like there was something wrong with me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I show my true self, people will reject me because I’m not good enough. I have to hide my needs or else I’ll be seen as a burden. Being liked means bending over backwards and never causing problems.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid reaching out to people or sharing my feelings because I’m afraid of being pushed away. Sometimes I put on a cheerful front, even when I feel sad inside, to make sure no one gets upset with me.
ccd.situation: I was at a small gathering with friends, but I noticed they didn’t really include me in the conversations or invite me to join activities. I felt invisible and like I didn’t matter to them.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They don’t want me here; I’m just in the way.” “I’m such a disappointment, no one really likes me.” “If I speak up, they’ll probably get annoyed and push me away.”
ccd.emotions: I felt lonely, rejected, and anxious. There was a deep sadness mixed with a sense of hopelessness that maybe I’ll always be on the outside looking in.
ccd.behaviors: I stayed quiet and withdrew from the group, avoiding eye contact and not trying to join in. Later, I isolated myself at home, feeling too down to reach out or do things I usually enjoy.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been alright, I guess. Work’s been kind of busy, and I haven’t really done much else. Oh, actually, I tried this new coffee place near my apartment—the barista messed up my order, but it’s fine, that sort of thing always happens to me. Sorry, was I supposed to just talk about my week?
It sounds like the coffee order mix-up was frustrating but also something you expect to happen—what thoughts went through your mind when that happened?

Oh, I don’t know, I guess I just thought, “Of course this would happen to me.” Stuff like that always seems to happen, you know? Honestly, I didn’t even bother correcting them, I just took the drink and left. It’s funny—I remembered this time in school when I got assigned the wrong locker and didn’t say anything then either... I guess I just don’t like making a fuss.
That feeling of “of course this would happen to me” seems important—what do you think it means about you or your experiences when that thought comes up?

Um… I guess it just feels like I’m not really meant to be noticed, or like my preferences don’t matter much. It sort of reminds me of family dinners growing up, when I’d ask for something and everyone would just ignore me or talk over me. Sorry, this is a bit all over the place—I just… it’s not a big deal or anything, I’m just used to letting things slide. Sometimes I wonder if it’s just easier not to expect much.
It sounds like feeling unnoticed or ignored has been a recurring experience for you—how do you usually feel inside when you let things slide instead of speaking up?

Um, I guess most of the time I just feel kind of small, like it’s safer to stay in the background. Sometimes I feel a little sad, or even frustrated with myself afterwards for not saying anything. But then I worry that if I did speak up, I’d just annoy people or make things awkward. It’s weird—I pretend I don’t care, but deep down it does sting a bit.
That’s a lot to carry—when you notice those feelings of sadness or frustration afterward, what do you tell yourself about why you didn’t speak up?

I usually just tell myself that it wasn’t worth the trouble, or that I probably would’ve messed it up anyway. Sometimes I think maybe people will like me more if I just go along with things quietly. Honestly, I don’t know—I just kind of convince myself that my needs aren’t that important. Actually, now that I think about it, I tend to replay the whole thing in my head later, wishing I’d done it differently, even though I know I probably wouldn’t.
When you replay those moments and wish you’d done things differently, what do you imagine saying or doing instead?

Um… well, sometimes I picture myself just asking for what I want, like telling the barista, “Hey, this isn’t what I ordered,” or speaking up in a group if I have something to add. Or maybe just being more relaxed and joining in without second-guessing everything I say. But then I always imagine people rolling their eyes or thinking I’m being difficult, so I end up just… not doing it. It’s kind of embarrassing to admit, but I worry a lot about coming across as annoying.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up in a household where criticism was frequent and support was scarce, I often felt overlooked and powerless to change how my family treated me. These experiences made me believe that I wasn’t good enough and that nothing I did could make a difference.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I show my true self, others will reject me because I am flawed. I must keep my feelings to myself to avoid burdening others or being seen as weak.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to stay quiet during family conflicts and avoid expressing my needs, hoping to keep the peace even if it means feeling isolated. Sometimes I withdraw completely to protect myself from further hurt.
ccd.situation: During a recent family gathering, when my opinion was dismissed and my feelings ignored, I felt invisible and powerless to change the situation.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one cares what I think. I’m just a burden to them. I’m broken and there’s nothing I can do to fix this.”
ccd.emotions: I feel sad, worthless, and anxious, like I’m trapped and alone in this family dynamic.
ccd.behaviors: I clam up and avoid speaking up, keeping my distance emotionally and physically to avoid further rejection or disappointment.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um... it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing much happened. Just busy with work and stuff. Nothing really to talk about, honestly.
When you say "nothing really to talk about," what thoughts or feelings come up for you in that moment?
I don’t know… I just don’t think it matters much. I guess I just feel like it’s not important. Other people have bigger problems, so… yeah. I don’t really want to get into it.
It sounds like you might be comparing your experiences to others’. What impact does thinking your problems are less important have on how you feel or what you do?

I don’t know, I just… keep things to myself most of the time. It feels easier that way. Sometimes I just feel kind of… alone, I guess, but I don’t want to bother anyone. So I just try not to think about it too much.
When you notice that feeling of loneliness, what do you usually tell yourself about reaching out or sharing with someone?
Honestly, I just sort of convince myself there’s no point. Like, I doubt anyone would really care or understand. I usually just end up thinking it’s better to keep quiet. It's just... safer, I guess.
What do you think might happen if you did decide to share a little of what you're feeling with someone you trust?

I don’t know… I guess I worry they’d just brush it off or not really get it. Maybe they’d think I’m being dramatic or too sensitive. It’s happened before, so I just… don’t really expect much. I’d probably just feel worse if they didn’t take me seriously.
When those past experiences come to mind, how do they influence what you believe about yourself and your needs?

Um… I guess it just makes me think maybe I’m the problem. Like, maybe I’m just too much or too needy or something. It’s hard not to feel like there’s something wrong with me. So I kind of just stop asking for anything.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve had several relationships where I felt like I was always the one trying harder, and eventually, I was left feeling ignored or rejected. Growing up, I often felt overlooked by my family and was told I wasn’t good enough, which stuck with me deeply.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I get close to someone, they will eventually leave me or reject me. I have to be careful about how much I show who I really am because if I’m too open, I’ll be hurt. People only accept me on their terms, and I can’t trust that acceptance to last.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to keep my distance emotionally or avoid getting too attached to protect myself. Sometimes I overanalyze every little thing they say or do to catch signs of rejection early. Other times, I try to please others excessively, hoping it will keep them from leaving.
ccd.situation: I’m talking with my partner and notice they haven’t replied to my last message for several hours. I start feeling anxious and wonder if they are losing interest or planning to end things.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: They must be upset with me or don’t want to be with me anymore. I’m probably annoying or not good enough for them. This is exactly what always happens—I’m bound to be rejected again.
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, sad, and hopeless. There’s a knot of fear in my stomach, and I feel a deep ache of loneliness creeping in.
ccd.behaviors: I send multiple follow-up messages, trying to get reassurance. If I don’t get a quick response, I withdraw emotionally or get irritable, pushing them away before they can leave me first.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Uh, it’s been… I guess a bit up and down. Work’s been kind of hectic, but that’s nothing new. Actually, you know what’s weird? My neighbor’s cat has been sitting outside my window all week—I’m not sure if it wants something or if it’s just lost, but I keep worrying about it for some reason.
That sounds like a mix of stress from work and this unexpected worry about the cat; what kinds of thoughts go through your mind when you see the cat outside your window?

Yeah, I mean, I keep wondering if someone’s forgotten about it, or if it’s just lonely—I guess I start thinking maybe no one’s really paying attention to it. It’s weird, but sometimes I feel like I can relate. Oh, and I keep getting sidetracked watching it instead of working, which I guess isn’t great for my productivity. Anyway, sorry, I’m rambling—what was the question again?
No need to apologize—what you’re noticing sounds important. When you think about relating to the cat’s situation, what feelings come up for you?

Um… I guess it just feels kind of sad, like… I don’t know, like it’s just waiting for someone, and maybe no one’s coming. It reminds me of times I’ve felt sort of left out or forgotten. But, you know, sometimes I just try to brush it off and distract myself—like, I’ll clean something or scroll on my phone. Oh, actually, that reminds me—did I tell you about the time my friend forgot my birthday last year? That was kind of a weird one.
It sounds like those feelings of being left out or forgotten are really meaningful to you; when your friend forgot your birthday, what thoughts or emotions did that bring up?

Yeah, that was a rough day. I remember just feeling kind of invisible, like maybe I didn’t matter as much as I thought. I kept telling myself it was probably just a mistake, but honestly, it stung way more than I wanted to admit. Actually, it made me think about other times—like with my family growing up—where I felt like I just slipped through the cracks, you know?

That sounds really painful to carry those feelings from both past and present; when you notice yourself thinking you might not matter, what impact does that have on how you feel or what you do afterward?

Uh… yeah, it kind of sinks in and I just get this heavy feeling, like, what’s even the point in reaching out sometimes? I usually end up shutting down a little, or I’ll try super hard to be extra nice or helpful, hoping maybe someone notices. Sometimes I just zone out and watch TV or scroll online to distract myself. Oh, this is kind of random, but I saw this documentary the other day about people who live totally off the grid—it made me wonder if they ever feel this way, or if it’s easier when you don’t expect much from others.

It seems like those feelings lead you to withdraw or try to get attention through being extra helpful; when you find yourself zoning out or distracting, what do you hope will happen or change for you?

I guess I’m just hoping the feelings will fade if I don’t think about them too much. I tell myself maybe things will look better after a while or that I’ll just forget about whatever’s bugging me. Sometimes I hope someone will notice and reach out, but usually, I just end up feeling kind of numb. Oh, and sometimes I even start worrying that I’m just being too sensitive, you know?

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve struggled with setbacks in school and work, and people around me often expect me to mess things up. This has made me feel like no matter what I do, I’m just not good enough. I’ve also faced criticism from family that made me doubt my abilities.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try something new, I will probably fail and embarrass myself. I have to avoid taking risks because I’m just not capable like others. People will judge me as a loser if I don’t succeed right away.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid challenges or opportunities that could help me grow because I’m afraid of failing. Sometimes I procrastinate or give up quickly to protect myself from feeling incompetent. I also compare myself to others and put myself down quietly.
ccd.situation: I was offered a chance to lead a project at work, but I hesitated and doubted I could handle it successfully. I felt stuck because I wanted to grow but feared making mistakes that would prove I’m a failure.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I take this on, I’m going to mess it up and everyone will see I’m useless.” “I’m just not cut out for this kind of responsibility.” “I’ll never be as good as the others.”
ccd.emotions: Anxious, discouraged, ashamed, and hopeless.
ccd.behaviors: I avoided volunteering for the project and stayed silent during meetings. I withdrew from colleagues and stopped putting effort into tasks that might highlight my weaknesses.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Work has been a bit stressful. I just tried to keep my head down and not attract too much attention. Nothing too exciting happened.
When you say you tried to keep your head down at work, what thoughts were going through your mind in those moments?

I just felt like if I stood out, I’d probably mess something up. I kept thinking it’s safer if people don’t notice me too much. Honestly, I worry I’ll embarrass myself if I try to do more. So I’d rather just blend in and not take any risks.
That sounds like a tough spot to be in. What feelings come up for you when you think about standing out or taking more risks at work?

Mostly I just feel really anxious. There’s this knot in my stomach, and I get kind of tense thinking about it. Sometimes I feel embarrassed, even before anything happens. It makes me want to avoid those situations altogether.
It sounds like those feelings are quite intense. Can you tell me about a recent situation at work where you felt that anxiety and embarrassment—what happened, and what did you do in response?

Yeah, actually, last week my manager asked if anyone wanted to lead a new project. My first instinct was to just stay quiet and hope she wouldn’t look at me. I felt my heart racing and just kept my eyes down while others spoke up. I didn’t volunteer for it, even though part of me was kind of interested.
When you think back on that moment, what thoughts were running through your mind about what might happen if you did volunteer to lead the project?

I just kept thinking I’d end up screwing it up somehow. Like, if I took the lead and made a mistake, everyone would notice and judge me for it. I imagined people thinking I wasn’t cut out for it. The idea of disappointing everyone really freaked me out.
That sounds really overwhelming. How do those thoughts about making mistakes and being judged affect what you do next in situations like this?

Honestly, they make me want to avoid trying at all. I usually just stay quiet or let others take the lead so I don’t risk messing up. Sometimes I even start convincing myself I wasn’t interested in the first place, just so I don’t feel bad about it. Afterward, I feel kind of disappointed in myself, but I guess I’d rather feel that than totally humiliated.

ccd.relevant_history: I've often felt like I can't manage on my own, especially in close relationships. Growing up, I depended a lot on my family for support, and whenever I tried to assert myself, I was told I was too sensitive or needy. These experiences have left me feeling unsure about my ability to handle things independently.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don't get constant reassurance from others, I will feel abandoned or unworthy. People won’t want to be around me if I show too much neediness, so I have to keep asking for help to feel safe. My worth as a person depends on how much others are willing to support me.
ccd.coping_strategies: I often reach out repeatedly to friends or partners, hoping they’ll respond quickly and show they care. Sometimes I hold back my true feelings to avoid being seen as a burden, but other times I over-express my needs to try to get reassurance. I also find myself avoiding conflicts because I’m scared it will push people away.
ccd.situation: I’m texting my partner and don’t get a reply for a while, so I start to worry that they’re upset with me or losing interest. I feel increasingly anxious and unsure about whether they still care.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: They must be annoyed with me or don’t want to talk right now. Maybe I’m being too needy and that’s why they’re not answering. I’m probably bothering them and they might stop caring if I keep reaching out.
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, rejected, and lonely. There’s also a deep sadness and fear of being abandoned or left alone.
ccd.behaviors: I send multiple messages asking if everything is okay, hoping to get reassurance. When I don’t get an immediate response, I start to withdraw emotionally, feeling distant and hurt inside, even if I don’t show it outwardly.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, I guess it’s been okay, nothing too out of the ordinary. Work’s been kind of busy, and I’ve just been trying to keep up with things. I did have a bit of a rough patch with my partner this week, but I’m not sure if it’s even a big deal. I guess I’ve just been feeling a little on edge, but I’m not really sure why.
When you noticed feeling on edge, what thoughts were going through your mind at that moment?

Well, I remember one night I texted my partner and they didn’t reply for a while. I started thinking maybe I’d done something wrong, or maybe they were annoyed with me and just didn’t want to talk. Honestly, I couldn’t stop checking my phone and kept wondering if I should send another message, just to see if everything was okay. I know it sounds kind of silly, but I couldn’t help overthinking it.
What do you think would happen if you didn’t check your phone or send another message right away?

Honestly, I think I’d just get more and more anxious, like my mind would keep racing with all these worst-case scenarios. I’d probably start to convince myself that they were ignoring me on purpose, or that something was wrong between us. The silence feels really loud, if that makes sense, and it just gets harder and harder to focus on anything else. I guess I worry that if I don’t reach out, they’ll just forget about me or decide I’m not that important.
When you notice those worst-case scenario thoughts starting, how do you usually respond to them?

Usually, I try to distract myself at first—like, I’ll scroll through social media or watch something on Netflix, but it never really works for long. After a while, I usually end up sending another message, even if I know I probably shouldn’t. It’s almost like I need to get some sort of answer just to calm myself down. And then, if I still don’t hear back, I start feeling really upset and just want to curl up and be alone.
What do you tell yourself about needing to get an answer quickly—what meaning do you give to that silence?

I guess, in my head, the silence starts to feel like rejection, even though I know that might not be true. I start telling myself things like, “If they really cared, they’d answer right away,” or “I must have done something wrong.” It becomes really hard not to take it personally, even if they’ve just gotten busy or distracted. It just feels like if I don’t hear from them, maybe I don’t matter that much to them after all.
When you notice those thoughts about rejection, what feelings come up in your body or mood?

I usually get this tight, kind of sinking feeling in my stomach, and my chest feels heavy. It’s like a mix of anxiety and this kind of deep sadness that just sits with me. I start to feel really alone, even if I’m surrounded by other people. Sometimes I just want to disappear or hide until the feeling goes away.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and misunderstood by my family, which made me believe that I wasn’t good enough or worthy of attention. Past relationships have reinforced this feeling, as I frequently experienced rejection or criticism that left me doubting myself deeply.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I open up to others or express my needs, they will see my flaws and leave me. I have to keep my feelings hidden because showing vulnerability means I’m weak and will be abandoned. It’s safer to expect disappointment than to hope for acceptance.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid sharing my true feelings or asking for support, trying to manage everything on my own even when it’s overwhelming. Sometimes, I withdraw from social interactions or stay silent during conflicts to prevent others from seeing my perceived defects.
ccd.situation: During an argument with my partner, I felt like I was being blamed for everything going wrong, and I worried that this would confirm the idea that I’m fundamentally broken and unlovable.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They think I’m useless and can’t do anything right.” “If they really knew me, they’d leave me.” “I’m just a burden and not worth their time or love.”
ccd.emotions: I felt overwhelmed by sadness and anxiety, mixed with a deep sense of shame and loneliness. There was also a lingering fear that things would never get better and I’d always be this way.
ccd.behaviors: I shut down emotionally and stopped trying to explain myself, withdrawing physically and mentally from the conversation. I avoided reaching out for reassurance or help, convincing myself I had to handle the pain alone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um… I guess it’s been kind of up and down. Work was busy, and I tried to keep myself focused, but I kept finding my mind wandering back to this argument I had with my partner a few days ago. It’s just been hard to shake off, like I keep replaying what happened and wondering what I could have done differently. I don’t know, I just feel sort of heavy this week.
When you find yourself replaying the argument, what kinds of thoughts or feelings usually come up for you?

Honestly, it’s like this constant loop in my head, and I start questioning everything I said or did. I worry that maybe I really did mess up or that I’m somehow to blame for everything going wrong. It makes me feel this crushing mix of sadness, and I guess a lot of anxiety too, like I’m bracing for things to fall apart. Sometimes I just end up feeling really alone, even if my partner is still around.
That sounds really tough to carry around. When you notice those feelings of sadness and anxiety, what do you usually do next—do you try to distract yourself, talk to someone, or something else?

Um, most of the time I just… keep it to myself, I guess. I don’t really reach out or talk about it, even when I probably should. I usually just sort of withdraw and try to act like everything’s normal, even if inside it doesn’t feel that way at all. Honestly, sometimes I just end up overthinking for hours and hoping it’ll pass on its own.
What do you think makes it hard for you to share these feelings with your partner or someone else?

I don’t know, it just feels really risky to let someone see how much things are bothering me. I always worry that if I actually say what’s on my mind, they’ll just think I’m too much to handle or that I’m being dramatic. There’s a part of me that’s scared they might get frustrated or even leave if I show too much weakness. It just seems safer to keep it all hidden, even though I know that’s probably not good for me.
It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of fear about how others might respond. When you imagine opening up to your partner, what’s the most worrying thing you think could happen?

I guess the thing that scares me most is that they’ll finally see what’s really wrong with me and decide they don’t want to deal with it—or with me. Like, if I let them in, they’ll just confirm everything I’m afraid of and maybe walk away for good. I worry that I’ll end up alone because I couldn’t hide the things I’m ashamed of. Just the thought of that makes it feel almost impossible to take the risk of being honest.
That fear of rejection sounds very powerful. Have there been times in the past when you’ve shared something vulnerable and the outcome was different from what you feared?

Um, I’m trying to think… I guess there have been a few moments, maybe with friends, where I shared something personal and they were actually supportive. But even then, it’s like I’m always waiting for the moment when they’ll change their mind or get tired of me. It’s hard to trust that the acceptance is real or that it’s going to last. Sometimes I almost feel guilty for needing support at all, like I’m asking for too much.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like I don’t have control in social situations, especially when others seem confident or assertive. Growing up, I was overlooked and sometimes dismissed, which made me believe that I’m not strong enough to handle challenges or speak up.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to assert myself, I’ll just make things worse or be rejected. People will see me as weak or incapable, so it’s safer to stay quiet and avoid drawing attention. I don’t have the skills or strength to influence how others treat me.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid speaking up or sharing my opinions in groups, hoping that staying quiet will keep me safe. Sometimes I withdraw or agree with others even if I don’t want to, just to prevent conflict or rejection.
ccd.situation: I’m at a social gathering where people are talking and joking, and I want to join in but feel unsure about what to say or if I’ll be accepted.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one wants to hear what I have to say.” “If I say something, they’ll think I’m stupid or weak.” “I’m just going to embarrass myself if I try.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and a sense of helplessness wash over me. I feel small, vulnerable, and isolated even though I’m surrounded by people.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet, avoid eye contact, and keep to myself. I might leave the room or the gathering early to escape the discomfort.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Nothing too exciting happened. I went to a couple of social things, but I mostly kept to myself. I don’t know, I just feel kind of drained after stuff like that.
When you say you feel drained after social events, what thoughts go through your mind in those moments?

Honestly, I keep wondering if I said something wrong or if people thought I was awkward. I worry a lot about how I came across, even if I barely spoke. It just feels easier to sort of fade into the background. Sometimes I leave thinking people probably didn't even notice I was there.
What emotions do you notice in yourself when you have those thoughts about how others might have perceived you?

I feel anxious, for sure. Sometimes there’s this heavy kind of sadness too, like I’m missing out or just not connecting. I also get embarrassed, even if nothing really happened. It makes me want to just avoid those situations altogether.
When you imagine avoiding social situations, what do you think might happen both in the short term and over time?

In the short term, I guess I feel some relief—like I can relax and not worry so much. But after a while, it gets kind of lonely, and I start feeling like I’m on the outside looking in. I worry that if I keep doing this, I’ll just get more and more distant from people. Sometimes I wonder if I’ll ever really fit in anywhere.
Can you recall a recent social experience where you felt more comfortable or connected than usual? What was different about that situation?

Um… there was this one time at work when I ended up talking one-on-one with a coworker during a break. It was a lot easier than being in a group. I didn’t feel as nervous, and the conversation just kind of flowed. I guess I didn’t feel like I was being watched or judged as much.
What thoughts were running through your mind during that one-on-one conversation compared to when you’re in a group setting?

I remember thinking, “This isn’t so bad, it’s just the two of us.” I wasn’t overthinking every word like I usually do in a group. I felt like I could just be myself without worrying too much. In groups, though, it's more like, “Don’t say anything dumb. Don’t draw attention.”

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I always felt like I wasn’t good enough, especially when my family struggled financially. I was often told I didn’t measure up, which made me believe that I’m flawed and destined to fail. These early experiences have made me cautious and withdrawn when dealing with money matters.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I can’t manage my finances perfectly, it means there’s something wrong with me. Asking for help will just show others how defective I am. I must handle my problems on my own, or else I’ll be seen as weak and incapable.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid looking at bills or talking about money because it makes me anxious and vulnerable. I keep my financial worries to myself and try to suppress any distressing thoughts, hoping they’ll go away. Sometimes I procrastinate on making decisions, which only makes things worse.
ccd.situation: I just received my credit card statement, and the balance is higher than I expected. I’m worried I won’t be able to pay it off this month.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m terrible with money. I’m never going to get this under control.” “If I can’t fix this, it means I’m broken.” “People will think I’m a failure if they find out.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, ashamed, and overwhelmed. There’s a heavy sense of hopelessness weighing me down.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid opening my statements and delay making a budget. I isolate myself from friends and family to hide my struggles. Sometimes I distract myself with other activities to avoid thinking about the problem.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Just the usual stuff. Nothing really exciting. I’ve just been keeping to myself mostly.
When you say you’ve been keeping to yourself, what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you in those moments?
I don’t know, I just feel kind of tired. Sometimes it’s easier to be alone. I guess there’s just a lot on my mind. It’s hard to explain.
What are some of the thoughts or worries that have been on your mind lately?
Um… I guess I’ve been thinking about bills and money stuff. Things just feel a bit overwhelming sometimes. I worry I’m not handling things the way I should. It makes me feel kind of stuck.
When you notice yourself feeling overwhelmed or stuck about money, what do you usually tell yourself in those moments?
I usually just think I should’ve done better. Like, I messed up somewhere. Sometimes I think maybe I just don’t know what I’m doing. I try not to think about it too much though.
When you notice those thoughts about messing up or not knowing what you’re doing, how do they affect what you do next?
Honestly, I just kind of shut down. I avoid dealing with it and push it off for later. I know that doesn’t help, but I can’t help it. It just feels easier to ignore it for a while.
What do you think might happen if you tried to face those money worries little by little instead of avoiding them?

I don’t know… I guess I’d be really anxious. Part of me thinks I’d just mess things up even more. It’s hard not to feel like I’ll just prove I can’t handle it. I worry people would see that I’m not good at this.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt overwhelmed at work, especially when deadlines are tight or when I’m given a lot of responsibility. In the past, I’ve struggled with feeling like I can’t keep up, which makes me doubt myself.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t handle everything perfectly, it means I’m weak and can’t be trusted. Asking for help shows I’m not capable, and if I mess up, people will think less of me.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to push through and do everything on my own, even when I'm exhausted. Sometimes I avoid speaking up or asking for support because I’m afraid it will make me look incompetent.
ccd.situation: I have a big project deadline coming up at work, and I’m already feeling the pressure to perform perfectly and not let anyone down.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to mess this up because I’m not strong enough.” “If I can’t handle this, everyone will see that I’m powerless.” “I shouldn’t ask for help or I’ll prove I’m weak.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, fear, and a deep sense of helplessness. I also feel ashamed and worried about being judged.
ccd.behaviors: I isolate myself and avoid talking to colleagues about my struggles. I overwork myself, trying to control everything, but sometimes I end up procrastinating because I feel so overwhelmed.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been kind of rough, honestly. Work has just been nonstop, and I feel like I can’t really catch a break. I’m pretty tired. I don’t know, I guess I just feel under a lot of pressure.
When you say you feel under a lot of pressure, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind in those moments?

I keep thinking I can’t afford to mess up. It’s like, if I make even a small mistake, everyone will notice and judge me for it. I get worried that I’m not doing enough, or that I’m not good enough to handle everything. Sometimes I just feel like I’m barely holding it together.
What do you usually do or how do you respond when you notice those thoughts about messing up or not being good enough?

I usually just put my head down and try to push through on my own. I don’t really tell anyone how I’m feeling—I just try to handle everything myself. Sometimes I end up working late or skipping breaks because I think I have to keep going. Other times, I get so overwhelmed that I end up doing nothing for a while, just kind of stuck.
When you find yourself stuck and doing nothing, what emotions or physical sensations tend to come up for you in those moments?

Mostly I just feel really anxious, like my chest feels tight and it’s hard to breathe properly. I get this kind of heavy feeling, almost like I can’t move. There’s also this sense of shame, like I should be able to pull myself together but I just can’t. It’s pretty exhausting, honestly.
That sounds really tough. When you notice that tightness in your chest and the sense of shame, what do you tell yourself about those feelings or about yourself?

I usually tell myself that I shouldn’t feel this way, that I need to toughen up. I worry that if I let these feelings show, people will think there’s something wrong with me. Sometimes I just feel weak and wonder why I can’t handle things like everyone else seems to. It makes me want to hide it even more.
What do you imagine might happen if you did share these feelings with someone at work or in your personal life?

Honestly, I worry they’d think I’m not up to the job, or that I can’t be relied on. I’m afraid people would see me as less capable, maybe even a burden. Part of me just expects them to judge me or lose respect for me. I don’t really want to risk that.

ccd.relevant_history: I have struggled with feeling like I can’t succeed in school or at work, which has been a consistent issue since my teenage years. Past experiences of criticism and failure have reinforced my belief that I’m not good enough. I tend to keep my feelings to myself and avoid sharing my struggles with others.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to improve or take on new challenges, I will probably fail again and feel embarrassed. It’s safer not to put myself out there because I’m likely to disappoint myself and others. People will think less of me if I show weakness or admit I’m struggling.
ccd.coping_strategies: I often withdraw from opportunities for growth and avoid taking risks. I keep my opinions to myself and rarely ask for help, trying to appear competent even when I’m unsure. Sometimes I distract myself with TV or browsing the internet to avoid facing my feelings.
ccd.situation: I was offered a chance to lead a new project at work, but I hesitated and almost declined because I doubted my ability to succeed.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to mess this up like I always do.” “Everyone will see that I’m not capable.” “It’s better if I don’t try at all.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, and sadness. I feel overwhelmed and defeated before I even start.
ccd.behaviors: I procrastinate on making decisions related to the project, avoid asking questions, and consider stepping back from the opportunity altogether.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing really out of the ordinary. Work was a bit stressful, but I managed. I don’t know, I just kept busy.
When you say work was a bit stressful, what kinds of thoughts were going through your mind during those moments?

I don’t know, I just kept thinking I had a lot to do and wasn’t sure I could handle it. Sometimes I get worried I’ll mess something up. I tried not to focus on it too much, though. Mostly just tried to get through the day.
When you notice those worries about messing something up, how do those thoughts affect how you feel in the moment?
I guess I just feel kind of tense and on edge. It makes me anxious, like my chest gets tight. Sometimes I feel sort of… discouraged. It’s hard to focus.
What do you usually do when you start feeling that tension and anxiety?
Um, I usually just try to push through it. Sometimes I’ll distract myself and check my phone or something. I don’t really talk to anyone about it. I just try to act normal and hope it passes.
When you push through or distract yourself, how effective does that feel in reducing your anxiety or tension?
It helps a little, I guess, but not for very long. The anxious feeling usually comes back. Sometimes I just end up feeling tired or kind of down. It’s like I’m just waiting for the day to be over.
When you notice that your anxiety returns or you feel tired and down by the end of the day, what do you think that means to you about your ability to handle stress?

I don’t know… maybe it just means I’m not as good at handling things as other people. Sometimes I feel like I should be better at this by now. It kind of makes me doubt myself. I wonder if I’m just not cut out for certain things.

core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I must not make mistakes or I will be rejected. If others see my true self, they will think I am dangerous and avoid me. I have to keep my feelings hidden to avoid being judged or hurt.
ccd.coping_strategies: The client tends to withdraw from social interactions at work, avoids sharing opinions, and works excessively to avoid errors. They often ruminate on perceived failures and try to control situations rigidly to prevent criticism.

ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I'm a failure and everyone thinks I'm a threat to the team." "If I mess up again, they'll hate me and I’ll be completely alone." "I’m just bad at this and don’t belong here."

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um... it's been okay, I guess. Work’s been kind of stressful, but nothing out of the ordinary. I’ve just been trying to keep up with everything. Not much else to say, really.
When you think about the stress at work, what kinds of thoughts usually come up for you in those moments?

I don't know... I just worry I’m not doing enough, or that I’ll mess something up. Sometimes I feel like people are just waiting for me to fail. Honestly, it’s hard to stop thinking about that when things get busy.
That sounds really tough. When those thoughts about not doing enough or people expecting you to fail come up, how do they affect the way you feel or act?

Yeah, it’s… it’s not great. I get pretty anxious and just want to keep my head down. I usually try not to talk much or draw attention to myself. Sometimes I end up staying late to double-check things, just in case.
It sounds like those thoughts lead you to feel anxious and maybe try to avoid drawing attention—how does staying late and double-checking affect your stress or anxiety by the end of the day?

I guess sometimes it helps a little, like I can relax for a minute if I know I didn't miss anything. But mostly I just end up exhausted. The worry doesn’t really go away—there’s always something else I could’ve done better. It just feels kind of never-ending.
That sounds really exhausting. When you notice those feelings that the worry never goes away, what do you usually do next?
I usually just keep to myself. I might go straight home and not talk to anyone. Sometimes I just replay things in my head, trying to figure out what I did wrong. I don’t really know what else to do.
When you replay those moments in your head, what kinds of conclusions or judgments do you tend to come to about yourself or the situation?

I don’t know… most of the time I just end up thinking I messed up somehow, even if I can’t really say what exactly. I start to feel like maybe I’m just the problem. It’s hard not to feel like I’m the one bringing things down for everyone else. I guess I just feel pretty lousy about myself.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced several situations where they felt powerless, such as struggling with demanding work tasks and difficult family dynamics. These repeated experiences have reinforced a deep sense of being stuck and unable to change their circumstances.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "I have to keep everyone happy to avoid conflict." "If I show my true feelings, people might reject me." "No one will help me, so I have to manage on my own."
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient often agrees with others even when they disagree, avoids expressing frustration or needs, and tries to anticipate and meet others’ expectations to prevent problems.
ccd.situation: The patient is asked to take on extra responsibilities at work while feeling overwhelmed by personal issues at home.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I can’t say no or they’ll be upset with me." "I’m stuck and there’s no way out of this." "If I mess up, everyone will think I’m useless."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, frustration, sadness, and a sense of hopelessness.
ccd.behaviors: The patient agrees to the additional tasks despite feeling overwhelmed, avoids discussing their own needs, and withdraws from social support to keep peace.

messages.content: How has your week been so far? Are there any particular moments or experiences that stand out to you?

Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work was a bit hectic, but nothing too out of the ordinary. I just tried to stay on top of everything and not fall behind. I can’t really think of anything major that happened—just the usual stuff, I suppose.
When you think about those hectic moments at work, what thoughts or feelings tend to come up for you in those situations?

Oh, um, I guess I just try to focus on getting things done and not letting anyone down. Sometimes I worry a bit about making mistakes, but I try not to dwell on it too much. Mostly I just tell myself I need to keep it together and handle things. It can get a little tiring, but I manage.
When you notice yourself worrying about making mistakes, what do you imagine might happen if a mistake did occur?

Hmm, well, I guess I just worry that people might be disappointed in me or think I’m not good enough. I don’t want to let anyone down, you know? If something goes wrong, I feel like it’ll just cause problems for everyone else. I try really hard to avoid that.
That sounds like a lot of pressure to carry. When you think about being seen as not good enough or letting others down, how does that affect what you do or how you behave at work?

Yeah, I guess I probably try a bit too hard to avoid upsetting anyone. Even if I’m really busy or stressed, I’ll still say yes if someone asks for help or gives me more to do. I just don’t want to be seen as difficult or unreliable. Sometimes I wish I could just ask for a break, but I usually don’t.
What do you notice happens inside you—thoughts, feelings, or physical sensations—when you say yes to more tasks even though you wish you could ask for a break?

Um, I usually just feel kind of tense and a bit anxious, like my chest gets tight. My mind starts racing with all the things I need to do, and I worry I might forget something or mess up. I feel sort of stuck, but I try to push those feelings aside and just keep going. I tell myself it’ll be fine if I just work harder.
When you tell yourself that working harder will make things fine, do you find that it helps calm your anxiety, or does it sometimes have another effect?

I mean, maybe it helps a little at first because I feel like I’m doing what I’m supposed to. But honestly, after a while, I just end up feeling more tired and kind of overwhelmed. It’s like no matter how hard I try, there’s always something else to handle. Sometimes I start to feel a bit hopeless about it.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

Past relationships often ended with the person feeling unsupported or rejected.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um... it's been okay, I guess. Just busy with work and things. Nothing too out of the ordinary.
What kinds of thoughts have been going through your mind when you feel busy or overwhelmed at work?
I don’t know, I just try to keep my head down and get things done. Sometimes I wonder if I’m doing enough, or if people notice when I mess up. I guess I just push through it.
When you notice those thoughts about whether you’re doing enough or if people notice mistakes, how do those thoughts make you feel?
I guess it makes me feel kind of uneasy. Like, tense or on edge, I suppose. I try not to think about it too much, but it’s hard sometimes.
When you feel that tension or unease, what do you usually do to cope with those feelings?
Mostly I just keep working, try to stay busy. I don’t really talk about it with anyone. Sometimes I’ll just stay home after work and keep to myself. It’s easier that way, I think.
How does keeping busy or staying to yourself after work affect how you feel later on?
I don’t know. It helps for a bit, but then I just end up feeling kind of tired, I guess. Sometimes a bit lonely, too. But I’m not sure what else to do.
When you notice feeling lonely or tired after trying to cope this way, what do you think might help you feel better in those moments?
I’m not really sure. Maybe talking to someone could help, but I don’t want to bother anyone. I usually just wait for the feeling to pass. Sometimes I just distract myself with TV or something.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I open up to people, they will eventually leave me. It’s safer to keep my distance to avoid getting hurt. I don’t deserve lasting friendships or love.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid social situations or keep conversations very superficial. Sometimes I push people away before they can reject me, even though it makes me feel lonely.
ccd.situation: I was invited to a friend’s party, but I felt nervous and convinced myself that nobody would want to talk to me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one here wants me around. I’m just going to be ignored or left alone again.”
ccd.emotions: Lonely, anxious, rejected, and hopeless.
ccd.behaviors: I stayed quiet in the corner, avoided joining group conversations, and left the party early.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting. I did go to this party a few days ago, but I didn’t really stay long. I just wasn’t feeling it.
What was going through your mind when you decided to leave the party early?

I just kept thinking that nobody really noticed I was there. It felt awkward standing around, trying to fit in. Honestly, I figured people wouldn’t miss me if I left. So I just made up an excuse and slipped out.
When you had the thought that people wouldn’t miss you, how did that make you feel in the moment?
It made me feel pretty down, to be honest. Kind of lonely and, I don’t know, almost invisible. It’s like I was on the outside looking in. It just made me want to leave even more.
That sounds really tough. When you think back to similar situations in the past, do you notice any patterns in your thoughts or feelings?

Yeah, I guess it does happen a lot. Whenever I’m in a group, I start to expect that I won’t really fit in or that people won’t want to talk to me. It’s sort of like I’m waiting for the moment when I’ll get left out. I tend to keep a bit of distance because of that.
When you notice yourself expecting to be left out or not fitting in, what do you usually do next—how do you respond or behave?

I usually get pretty quiet and just hang back. Sometimes I’ll check my phone or pretend to be busy so I don’t look awkward. I don’t really try to join in much. Honestly, I’ve left early more than once just to avoid that whole feeling.
What do you think might happen if you tried to join in more, even just a little, instead of keeping that distance?

I worry that it would be obvious I don’t belong, or that I’d say something stupid and people would judge me. Part of me thinks maybe they’d just ignore me anyway. It feels safer to not risk it. I just don’t want to end up embarrassed or shut out.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and rejected by people I cared about, which made me believe deep down that no one could truly love me. Experiences of repeated criticism and emotional distance from family and friends have reinforced this painful sense of being unlovable over time.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I let people get close, they will eventually see how flawed I am and leave me. I have to hide my true self to avoid being rejected or abandoned. It’s safer to expect people won’t care about me so I won’t be hurt again.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to keep others at arm’s length and avoid sharing my feelings or needs to protect myself from disappointment. Sometimes I try extra hard to please people, hoping to earn their affection, but it often feels exhausting and never quite enough.
ccd.situation: I’ve been invited to join a new group activity that could help me meet people and grow personally, but I’m hesitant and anxious about whether anyone will actually like me or want me around.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one will want to be friends with me because I’m not lovable.” “If I open up, they’ll see the real me and reject me.” “I’m better off staying quiet and invisible than risking getting hurt.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, sad, and lonely. There’s also a sense of hopelessness and self-doubt that weighs heavily on me, making it hard to feel optimistic about connecting with others.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid participating fully in the group and keep conversations superficial. I might cancel plans or find reasons not to attend, reinforcing my isolation and confirming my belief that I’m unlovable.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um… I guess it’s been okay, nothing really out of the ordinary. Work’s been kind of busy, and I haven’t really done much outside of that. I did get invited to this group thing, but I haven’t decided if I want to go or not. Honestly, I’ve just mostly kept to myself.
What goes through your mind when you think about going to that group event?

Well, honestly, I just feel pretty nervous about it. I keep thinking, like, what if I show up and no one really talks to me, or worse, they think I’m weird or something? It just feels easier to stay home and avoid that whole situation. I guess I sort of expect that I won’t really fit in, so I’m not sure it’s worth putting myself out there.
When you notice those thoughts about not fitting in or being seen as weird, how do you usually respond to yourself in those moments?

Usually, I just kind of accept it as, like, the way things are—I don't really argue with myself about it. Sometimes I’ll try to convince myself that it doesn’t matter if I go or not, or I’ll come up with reasons why I’m “too busy” anyway. There’s this voice in my head saying, “Why bother? People probably won’t care if you’re there.” I just feel sort of resigned, I guess, and I end up not really trying.
How do you feel emotionally when you tell yourself those things or decide not to go?

I don’t know… it’s a weird mix. On the one hand, there’s a little relief because I don’t have to face the anxiety of going, but mostly I just feel kind of empty and disappointed in myself. I get this heavy, sad feeling, like I’ve let myself down again. It’s lonely too, and sometimes I wonder if it’ll always just be like this.
What do you think might happen if you did decide to go to the group, even with those feelings of anxiety?

Honestly, I go back and forth about it. Part of me thinks maybe it wouldn’t be a total disaster, but a bigger part worries I’d just feel awkward and out of place the whole time. I imagine myself standing off to the side, not really knowing how to join in or what to say, and then feeling even worse if people sort of ignore me. I guess I’m scared I’d come home feeling even more convinced that I don’t really belong anywhere.
Can you recall a time when you felt awkward or out of place but then something turned out better than you expected? What happened?

Let me think… I mean, there was this one time a few years ago when I started a new job. I remember feeling super anxious the first week, like everyone already had their own groups and I was just this outsider. But after a while, there was this coworker who started chatting with me during lunch breaks, and we ended up getting along pretty well. I guess that surprised me, but it also felt kind of rare, like maybe it was just luck or something.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt controlled by others and unable to make my own choices, which made me feel stuck and powerless in social situations. I learned early on that trying to please people was the only way to avoid conflict or rejection.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t keep others happy, they will leave or get angry with me. I have to put their needs before my own or I’ll be alone and trapped. Expressing my true feelings will only cause problems.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to agree with what others want, even when it goes against my own desires. I avoid saying no and suppress my feelings to keep the peace. Sometimes I over-apologize or try to fix everyone else’s problems to feel useful.
ccd.situation: During a group conversation, I notice others making plans without asking for my input, and I feel like I have no way to voice my opinions without upsetting the group.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one cares what I want, I’m just stuck here going along with whatever they say. If I speak up, they’ll get mad at me and I’ll be left out.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and a deep sense of helplessness wash over me. I feel invisible and rejected, even though no one has said anything directly.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet and nod along, forcing a smile to avoid rocking the boat. I agree to plans I don’t want to be part of and avoid bringing up my own needs.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting, just the usual stuff. I’ve been keeping busy with work and trying to stay out of trouble. Honestly, I can’t complain.
When you say you’re just trying to stay out of trouble, what kinds of things do you mean?

Oh, it’s nothing serious—I just mean, you know, trying not to cause any issues with people around me. I like keeping things smooth and making sure everyone’s happy. Sometimes I worry I might accidentally upset someone, so I just try to go with the flow. It’s easier that way, I think.
What goes through your mind when you worry about upsetting someone?

Hmm, I guess I just start thinking that if I say the wrong thing or disagree, people might get annoyed or not want me around. I usually feel like it’s better to just keep quiet than risk it. I worry I’ll make things awkward or cause problems for everyone. I don’t want anyone to feel upset because of me.
When you imagine those situations where you might upset someone, how does your body usually respond?

Um, I guess I get kind of tense, like my shoulders go up and my stomach feels tight. My heart starts beating faster too, and I feel sort of shaky. Sometimes I just get really quiet and hope it all passes quickly. I try to smile so no one notices.
What do you usually do in those moments when you feel tense and shaky—do you notice any particular thoughts or actions that help or make things harder?

I usually just stay really quiet and try not to draw attention to myself. I might nod or agree with whatever people are saying, even if I don’t really want to. Sometimes I just keep thinking, “Don’t mess this up, just go along with it,” and that makes me feel a bit stuck. I guess it helps avoid arguments, but afterward, I feel kind of drained.
What do you think might happen if you spoke up more or shared your true thoughts in those situations?

Um, I worry people might think I’m difficult or annoying if I actually say what I want. I guess I’m scared they’d get upset or leave me out next time. I just don’t want to make things awkward for anyone. It feels safer to just agree and not make a fuss.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like I have to prove myself at work, but no matter how hard I try, I keep falling short. Past experiences of being criticized or overlooked have made me doubt my abilities a lot.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t succeed perfectly, it means I’m useless. People will think less of me if I make mistakes. I have to control everything or I’m going to fail.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to work longer hours to avoid messing up and keep everything under control. Sometimes I avoid asking for help because I don’t want to seem weak or incapable.
ccd.situation: At work, my boss gave me a tight deadline for a project that feels overwhelming, and I’m worried I won’t finish it well enough.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to mess this up like always.” “Everyone will see I’m not good enough.” “I’m such a loser for struggling with this.”
ccd.emotions: Anxious, overwhelmed, hopeless, and ashamed.
ccd.behaviors: I procrastinate because I’m scared to start, then I rush through the work and avoid talking to my team about how I’m feeling. I also isolate myself and don’t ask for help.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been pretty stressful, honestly. Work’s been piling up and I’m just trying to keep up. I feel like I can’t really catch a break lately.
When you notice that feeling of not being able to catch a break, what kinds of thoughts usually go through your mind?

I just keep thinking I’m not doing enough, or that I’m going to screw something up. Sometimes it feels like everyone else is handling things better than I am. It’s hard not to think I’m falling behind.
What do you think might happen if you did make a mistake or didn’t meet all those expectations perfectly?
I guess I worry people would notice and think less of me. Maybe they’d see that I’m not as capable as I try to seem. I just really don’t want to disappoint anyone or give them a reason to doubt me.
When you imagine others doubting your abilities, how does that make you feel inside?
It makes me feel really anxious, honestly. Kind of sick to my stomach, like I’m just waiting for someone to call me out. Sometimes I just want to hide or disappear, so I don’t have to deal with it.
When you start feeling that anxiety and wanting to hide, what do you usually do in response?

I usually just keep to myself and try to push through the work on my own. I don’t really talk to people about what’s going on. Sometimes I put things off because I get overwhelmed, and then I end up scrambling at the last minute. It’s not great, but I feel stuck in that pattern.
What do you think might happen if you tried sharing how you’re feeling with someone at work or in your life?
I don’t know… Part of me thinks they’d just see me as weak or not up to the job. I worry they’d lose respect for me. It feels safer to just keep it to myself, even if it’s hard.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like I don’t measure up compared to others, especially since I struggled in school and never really found my footing in my career. Growing up, I often heard that I wasn’t good enough, and those messages stuck with me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t succeed perfectly, it means I’m worthless. People will reject me if I show any weakness or make mistakes. I must avoid failure at all costs to be accepted and valued.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid new challenges or give up quickly because I’m afraid of failing. Sometimes I distract myself with TV or social media to escape these feelings, but then I feel guilty for wasting time.
ccd.situation: I missed a deadline at work because I was overwhelmed with tasks and doubted my ability to finish on time.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m such a failure. I can’t do anything right. Everyone must think I’m useless and lazy.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, ashamed, and hopeless, like there’s no point in trying anymore.
ccd.behaviors: I withdrew from my coworkers, avoided checking emails, and procrastinated even more, which only made things worse.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, uh, it’s been kind of a blur, honestly. Work was… busy, I guess, and I’ve just felt sort of tired all the time. I watched a lot of TV at night—probably too much, but I just couldn’t seem to get myself to do anything else. My cat keeps waking me up early, too, which doesn’t help.
When you say you felt tired all the time, what kinds of thoughts were going through your mind during those moments?

I don’t know, it’s like this constant background noise of thinking I should be doing more. But then I just end up scrolling on my phone because it’s easier than facing all that stuff I have to do. Sometimes I wonder if other people feel this way, or if it’s just me. I guess I keep thinking I’m letting people down, but it’s hard to stop.
What do you notice happens emotionally or physically when you think that you’re letting people down?

Um, I get this sort of knot in my stomach, and my heart feels like it’s racing a bit. I just feel kind of small, you know? Like I want to crawl under a blanket and disappear. Sometimes I even get headaches, but maybe that’s just from staring at my phone or computer too much.
When you notice that knot in your stomach and the urge to hide, what do you usually do next?

Honestly, I usually just… avoid stuff. I’ll put off checking emails or responding to messages because I don’t want to see what I’ve missed or what someone might say. Then I get even more anxious about it, and it just piles up. Sometimes I’ll just turn on some random show and try not to think about anything.
What do you think might happen if you tried opening one email or message instead of avoiding them?

I mean, I know logically it’s probably not the end of the world, but it still feels really scary. Like I’m bracing myself for someone to be disappointed or annoyed with me. Sometimes I tell myself I’ll just do one, but then I freeze up anyway. Oh, that reminds me—one time in high school I missed a homework deadline and the teacher called me out in front of everyone, and I just wanted to disappear.
That sounds like a really tough experience. How do you think that moment from high school might be connected to how you feel about emails and messages now?

Yeah, maybe it is connected, I don’t know. I guess when I think about opening emails, I get this same sort of dread, like I’m waiting to be called out or embarrassed again. It’s silly, because I’m an adult now, but it feels the same somehow. Actually, even thinking about it now makes my chest feel kind of tight.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated financial instability over the past few years, leading to feelings of insecurity and self-doubt. Growing up, they often felt neglected and believed they were not valued by their family. These experiences have contributed to a deep-rooted sense of being unlovable.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "I must be perfect to be accepted." "If I struggle financially, it means I am a failure." "People will reject me if they see my weaknesses." These beliefs create pressure and reinforce feelings of inadequacy.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid discussing money problems with others, keeping concerns to themselves to prevent judgment. They may overwork or take on extra jobs to prove their worth but also withdraw socially when feeling overwhelmed.
ccd.situation: Facing unexpected bills that exceed their current income, the patient worries about making ends meet and fears being seen as a burden or failure.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I'm going to mess everything up again." "No one wants to be around someone who can't manage their money." "I’m not good enough to be loved or supported."
ccd.emotions: Feelings of shame, anxiety, and sadness dominate, accompanied by a sense of loneliness and hopelessness.
ccd.behaviors: The patient isolates themselves from friends and family, avoids opening bills, and procrastinates on addressing financial issues, which perpetuates the cycle of distress.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um... it's been okay, I guess. Just work and stuff, nothing too unusual. I’ve been a bit tired lately. Not much else to say, really.
When you say you’ve been tired lately, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind when you notice that tiredness?
I don’t know. Sometimes I just feel kind of worn out, like I can’t keep up. I guess I start thinking that maybe I’m just not doing enough, or not handling things right. It’s hard to explain.
When you notice those thoughts about not doing enough or not handling things right, how do they affect your mood or energy in that moment?
Um, I usually just feel kind of down. Like, more drained, I guess. Sometimes I get a bit anxious too, like I’m letting things slip. It makes me want to just… not deal with stuff for a while.
What do you usually do when you feel that way and want to avoid dealing with things?
I just sort of keep to myself. Maybe distract myself with TV or something, or just... stay in my room. I don’t really talk to anyone about it. I know I probably should, but it’s hard.
What do you think makes it hard for you to talk to others about how you're feeling?

I don't know, I just... I guess I don't want people to think less of me. Or maybe I worry they'll get tired of hearing about my problems. Sometimes it feels like I'm just being a burden. So it's easier to just keep quiet.
When you have those worries about being a burden or people thinking less of you, what do you tell yourself about your own needs or feelings?

Um... I guess I just tell myself to deal with it on my own. Like, I should be able to handle things without bothering anyone. Sometimes it feels like my problems aren't important anyway. I don't know, it just feels safer to keep it to myself.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated challenges in managing daily responsibilities, often feeling overwhelmed and unsupported. Past experiences of failure and lack of control in various situations have reinforced a sense of helplessness over time.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "I can’t manage things well on my own." "If things go wrong, it means I’m powerless." "There’s no point in trying because I won’t succeed."
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid tasks that feel overwhelming, procrastinates, or seeks excessive reassurance from others. They may also withdraw socially or give up quickly when faced with difficulties.
ccd.situation: The patient faces a busy day with multiple errands and deadlines, feeling unsure about how to prioritize or handle everything effectively.
