ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m never going to get all this done." "I don’t know what to do, so I’ll probably mess it up." "There’s nothing I can do to make this better."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, frustration, sadness, and a sense of defeat.
ccd.behaviors: The patient procrastinates on starting tasks, avoids asking for help, and may isolate themselves to escape the pressure.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Just a lot going on and I feel kind of behind with things. I keep thinking I should be able to handle it better. I don’t know, it’s just been a bit much.
When you think about not being able to handle things as well as you’d like, what specific thoughts or worries come to mind?

I just keep thinking I’m not going to get everything done, no matter how hard I try. I worry I’ll mess something up, or forget something important. Sometimes it feels like I’m already failing before I even start. It makes me not even want to try sometimes.
When you notice those thoughts about failing or not getting things done, how do they affect what you do next?

Honestly, I kind of freeze up. I keep putting things off because starting just feels too hard. Sometimes I just distract myself with something else instead, like scrolling on my phone. Then I end up feeling worse because nothing gets done.
What do you notice is different about your mood or energy right before you start scrolling on your phone instead of tackling what you need to do?

I usually feel really tense and anxious, like there’s this pressure in my chest. Sometimes I just feel worn out before I even do anything. It’s like I’m already tired thinking about what needs to get done. Scrolling just feels easier—it’s kind of a way to escape for a bit.
When you think about the pressure in your chest and feeling worn out before starting, what do you tell yourself about those feelings or about what’s ahead?

I usually think, “This is too much for me,” or “I can’t handle this.” Sometimes I tell myself it’s pointless to even try because it’ll just go badly anyway. It’s like I’m convinced I’ll mess up no matter what. It makes it really hard to believe starting would help.
Have there been times when you faced something challenging but managed to get through it or even do better than you expected? What was different about those moments?

Yeah, I guess there have been a few times. Usually it’s when someone helps me break things down or tells me exactly what to do next. It feels less overwhelming when I don’t have to figure it all out alone. But if I’m by myself, I just get stuck again.

core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

ccd.relevant_history: I often felt blamed and rejected during my childhood, which made me believe that I was a harmful person. Over time, these experiences reinforced my feeling that I’m dangerous to others and that I don’t deserve close relationships.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If people get too close, I will hurt them or cause trouble. It’s better to stay distant because I’m not safe to be around. If I show my true self, others will see how bad I am and leave.
ccd.coping_strategies: I avoid social gatherings and keep conversations brief to prevent others from getting too close. Sometimes I push people away before they have a chance to get hurt by me. I also try to stay quiet and invisible so I don’t draw attention.
ccd.situation: At a party, someone tries to start a friendly conversation with me, asking questions about my life and interests.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They’re going to realize I’m a bad person. If I say too much, I’ll mess things up or scare them away.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, fear of rejection, and feeling isolated.
ccd.behaviors: I shut down, give short answers, and try to leave the conversation quickly. I avoid eye contact and keep my distance.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting happened. I went to work, came home, did my usual stuff. I did go to a friend’s party over the weekend, but I didn’t stay long.
When you think back to the party, what went through your mind that made you leave early?

I just felt uncomfortable being there. People wanted to talk, and I started to worry I’d say something wrong. It’s like, I didn’t want to mess anything up or make anyone feel weird. So I figured it was safer to just head home.
What kind of thoughts were running through your head about what might happen if you said something wrong?

I kept thinking that if I talked too much, people would notice something was off about me. Like, maybe they’d realize I’m not a good person to be around. I was worried I’d ruin the mood or make someone feel uncomfortable. I just didn’t want to cause any problems.
When you notice these thoughts about not being a good person or ruining the mood, how do those thoughts affect how you feel in the moment?

I feel really anxious, kind of tense all over. There’s a lot of shame too, like I don’t belong there. It makes me want to just disappear, honestly. I get scared that people will see me the way I see myself.
What do you usually do when you start to feel that anxious and shameful in social situations?

I usually get quiet and try not to draw attention to myself. I keep my answers short if someone talks to me. Sometimes I make an excuse to leave early. I just want to get out of there before things get worse.
Have you noticed if these ways of coping—like staying quiet or leaving early—change how you feel afterward or affect your relationships with others?

Yeah, I guess after I leave, I feel kind of relieved at first. But then I start to feel lonely and guilty, like I missed out or let people down. Sometimes I worry that people notice I pull away and think I’m rude or just don’t care. It makes it harder to get close to anyone.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced ongoing high demands at work, often feeling overwhelmed by tight deadlines and conflicting expectations. Past attempts to manage workload independently have led to feelings of frustration and failure, reinforcing a sense of helplessness.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I can’t control everything, then things will fall apart. I have to keep pushing myself to avoid failure. Asking for help means I’m weak or incapable.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to withdraw and avoid discussing workload issues, trying to handle problems alone. They may also procrastinate on tasks they feel unable to manage, leading to last-minute stress.
ccd.situation: The patient is facing a critical project deadline with little support and multiple interruptions, making it difficult to focus and complete tasks efficiently.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m completely out of control here." "If I don’t fix this, everything will collapse." "I’m the only one who can handle this, but I’m failing."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, frustration, helplessness, and a sense of being overwhelmed dominate the patient’s emotional experience in the situation.
ccd.behaviors: The patient isolates themselves, working longer hours but with decreasing productivity, and avoids reaching out to colleagues or supervisors for assistance.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Work’s just been kind of busy. Nothing out of the ordinary.
When you say work has been busy, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind during those busy moments?
I just keep thinking there’s too much to do and not enough time. Sometimes I worry I’ll miss something important. Mostly, I’m just trying to keep up.
When you notice those worries about missing something important, how do you usually respond or cope in the moment?

I usually try to double-check everything, even if it takes longer. Sometimes I end up staying late, just to make sure things are done right. I don’t really talk to anyone about it—I just try to handle it myself.
How do you feel emotionally when you’re staying late and double-checking everything on your own?
I guess I feel kind of stressed, and tired too. It’s frustrating when it seems like no matter how much I do, it’s never enough. Sometimes I just feel really drained.
When those feelings of stress and frustration come up, what do you tell yourself about your ability to manage the workload?
Honestly, I just think maybe I’m not handling things as well as I should. I end up questioning if I can really keep up. Sometimes it feels like everything could fall apart if I miss something.
What evidence do you notice that supports or challenges the idea that things might fall apart if you miss something?

Well, I guess there have been times when something slipped through and it caused problems later on. That sticks with me. But… sometimes, things actually turn out alright even if I don’t catch every little detail. I don’t know—it just feels risky to let things go.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored or rejected by my family and peers, which led me to believe that I wasn’t worthy of love or attention. Over time, these feelings became deeply ingrained, shaping how I see myself and interact with others.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to be myself or express my true feelings, people will reject or dislike me. To be accepted, I must always put others’ needs before my own and avoid causing any conflict or disappointment.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to agree with others even when I disagree, avoid sharing my opinions, and go out of my way to please people to gain their approval. Sometimes, I suppress my own needs to keep relationships intact.
ccd.situation: A friend cancels plans at the last minute, leaving me feeling left out and uncertain about our friendship.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They probably don’t really want to spend time with me. I must have done something wrong or upset them somehow.”
ccd.emotions: Sadness, anxiety, and a deep sense of rejection.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid reaching out to my friend again, try to keep busy to distract myself, and later, I might overcompensate by being extra accommodating to others to feel accepted.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um, it’s been alright, I guess. Nothing too exciting, just the usual stuff. I did have some plans with a friend, but, uh, they ended up falling through. But it’s okay, I understand people get busy!
When you found out your plans fell through, what went through your mind in that moment?

Oh, well, honestly, I just figured... maybe they had something more important come up, or maybe I did something wrong and didn’t realize it. I mean, I know people get busy, but I couldn’t help thinking maybe they just didn’t really want to see me after all. I tried not to dwell on it, though, since it’s probably nothing. I just didn’t want to be a bother or make it a bigger deal than it is.
That sounds like a mix of different thoughts—do you notice any particular feelings that come up when you think about those possibilities?

Yeah, I guess I felt kind of sad, and maybe a bit anxious. Like, I just kept worrying that I’d upset them somehow, even though they didn’t say anything like that. Honestly, it made me feel a little... I don’t know, left out, I suppose. But I try not to let it show, you know?
When you say you try not to let it show, what do you usually do or how do you cope with those feelings inside?

Um, I usually just keep myself busy—like, I’ll find something to do around the house or maybe message someone else to check in. Sometimes I just tell myself it’s no big deal and push the feelings aside. I don’t really talk about it with anyone, I guess. I’d rather not cause any trouble or seem needy.
It sounds like you’re managing a lot on your own; when you push those feelings aside, what happens inside you afterward—do the feelings change or stay the same?

Yeah, I mean, sometimes if I distract myself enough, the feelings kind of fade for a bit. But honestly, they usually come back later, maybe even stronger. I’ll catch myself thinking about it again when I’m alone. It just sort of lingers in the background, you know?
When those feelings come back later, what kinds of thoughts or worries do you notice running through your mind then?

Um, I start wondering if maybe I’m just not that interesting, or if there’s something about me people don’t really like. I think about whether I said or did the wrong thing without realizing it. Sometimes I worry that, maybe, I just don’t fit in as much as I hoped. I try not to focus on those thoughts, but they’re kind of hard to shake off.

ccd.relevant_history: Throughout my life, I’ve often felt like I don’t deserve love or respect, especially in close relationships. Past experiences where I was criticized or rejected have reinforced this feeling that I’m fundamentally flawed and immoral. These early messages have shaped how I see myself and how I expect others to treat me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I believe that because I am immoral, others will eventually see me as bad or unworthy and leave me. I also think that if I don’t constantly prove I’m a good person, I will be rejected or blamed. This makes me very cautious and fearful in my relationships, always doubting my intentions and actions.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid sharing my true feelings or needs because I fear they will reveal my “immorality” and push others away. Sometimes I people-please or over-apologize to try to keep others close, even if it means sacrificing my own well-being. When I feel overwhelmed, I might withdraw emotionally or physically to protect myself from potential rejection.
ccd.situation: When my partner points out something I did that upset them, I immediately feel like I’ve done something terribly wrong and that they must now think I’m a bad person. I fear this will lead to them leaving me or thinking less of me forever.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m such a terrible person. I can’t believe I hurt them again. They must think I’m immoral and disgusting.” “If I don’t fix this right now, they’ll leave me for good.” “I don’t deserve their love or forgiveness.”
ccd.emotions: I feel ashamed, anxious, and desperate. There’s a deep sense of sadness and loneliness because I believe I’m fundamentally unlovable. At the same time, I feel a pressing fear of abandonment and rejection.
ccd.behaviors: I immediately try to apologize excessively and take all the blame, even if it’s not entirely my fault. I might overcompensate by doing things to please my partner or avoid conflict at all costs. Sometimes, if I feel too overwhelmed, I shut down and become distant, hoping to avoid further criticism.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um… it’s been kind of up and down, I guess. Work’s been stressful, and I had a small argument with my partner a couple days ago, which is still kind of bothering me. Honestly, I keep replaying it in my head and worrying if I handled things wrong. Other than that, things have just felt a bit heavy, I suppose.
When you think back to that argument, what thoughts or images come to mind most strongly?

Well, I just keep picturing the look on their face when I upset them—it’s kind of burned into my memory. I remember thinking, “Oh no, I messed up again,” and then this wave of guilt just hit me really hard. I kept worrying that they were seeing me differently, like maybe they were starting to question who I am as a person. It’s hard not to get stuck on that feeling that I’ve disappointed them somehow.
That sounds really tough to carry around. When you notice those thoughts about disappointing your partner, how do they affect what you do next or how you feel emotionally?

Yeah, it is tough—it’s like I can’t shake it off, even hours or days later. I start feeling really anxious, like I have to do something immediately to fix things or make up for what happened, even if it wasn’t that big of a deal. Sometimes I’ll go overboard apologizing or trying to do things for them, just to prove that I care and I’m not a bad person. But at the same time, there’s this heavy feeling of shame, like I just want to hide or avoid them so I don’t make things worse.
When you notice that urge to apologize or fix things right away, what do you tell yourself about what might happen if you don’t act that way?

Um, I guess I immediately start thinking that if I don’t do something—like apologize or make it up to them—they’ll end up resenting me or maybe even leaving me. It feels almost impossible to just let things be; I worry they’ll see me as selfish or uncaring if I don’t act quickly. There’s this fear in the back of my mind that if I don’t fix it, it means I’m actually a bad person. I sort of imagine them pulling away from me for good, and that’s just… really scary.
It sounds like there’s a lot of fear tied to what might happen if things don’t get fixed right away—how do you usually cope with that fear when it feels overwhelming?

When it gets really overwhelming, I kind of freeze up and go quiet—I’ll either shut down and just keep to myself, or I’ll keep replaying everything in my mind, trying to figure out what I did wrong. Sometimes I try to distract myself by focusing on chores or work, just to avoid thinking about it. Other times, I end up texting my partner again, apologizing or asking if we’re okay, even if I’ve already done it before. It’s like I can’t settle until I get reassurance that things aren’t falling apart.
When you reach out for reassurance from your partner, how do you feel afterward—does it help ease your worries or does it sometimes bring up new feelings?

I wish I could say it always helps, but honestly, it’s kind of a mixed bag. Sometimes, if my partner reassures me, I feel a little better for a while—like, there’s some relief. But a lot of times, I end up doubting whether they really mean it, or I worry that I’m just being annoying or needy by asking. So even after I reach out, there’s often still this nagging anxiety or guilt, and I start thinking maybe I shouldn’t have said anything at all.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has a history of struggling with money management and often feels overwhelmed by financial responsibilities. Past experiences of criticism about their spending and job instability have reinforced feelings of inadequacy.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I’m not good at handling money. If I can’t keep my finances in order, it means I’m a failure. People will think less of me if I’m struggling financially.
ccd.coping_strategies: Avoiding looking at bills or bank statements, procrastinating on budgeting or financial planning, and withdrawing from discussions about money to avoid feeling shame.
ccd.situation: Receiving a notice about an overdue bill and realizing there isn’t enough money to pay it on time.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m such a waste, I can’t do anything right. I’m never going to get ahead. Why even try when I always mess up?”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, hopelessness, and a deep sense of sadness.
ccd.behaviors: Ignoring the bill, avoiding phone calls from creditors, isolating from friends and family, and feeling stuck without taking any action to improve the situation.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Uh, it’s been kinda rough, honestly. I got a notice in the mail about a bill I forgot to pay, so that’s been hanging over me. Just feeling a bit off, I guess.
When you think about that unpaid bill, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind?
Mostly just that I messed up again. Like, why can’t I just get it together? It makes me feel like I’m just not good at this stuff.
When you tell yourself “I’m just not good at this,” how does that affect how you feel in the moment?
It makes me feel pretty awful, honestly. I start to get really anxious and embarrassed. Sometimes I feel like I just want to give up because it seems pointless.
What do you usually do when those feelings of anxiety and wanting to give up come up?

I usually just try to ignore everything. Like, I’ll avoid looking at my emails or the mail for a while. Sometimes I just distract myself with TV or my phone so I don’t have to think about it. It’s easier than dealing with it, even though I know it just piles up.
What do you notice happens after you’ve been avoiding those things for a while?

Honestly, it just gets worse. The bills keep coming, and I start to feel even more stressed. I get kind of stuck, and then it feels impossible to know where to even start fixing things. I end up just feeling more alone with it all.
When you say it feels impossible to know where to start, can you think of a smaller step that might feel manageable to take first?

I guess maybe I could just open one bill and see what it says. Even that feels pretty hard, though. Sometimes I think about making a list, but I get overwhelmed before I even start. Maybe just picking one thing and looking at it wouldn’t be so bad.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, they often felt ignored or rejected by family members, which led to deep feelings of inadequacy and a sense that they were not worthy of love. These early experiences shaped their view that people don’t truly care about them.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t make others happy, they will leave me. I must always be agreeable to keep people close. If I show my true feelings, others won’t accept me.
ccd.coping_strategies: They tend to agree with others even when it conflicts with their own feelings or needs, avoid expressing disagreement, and constantly seek reassurance to feel accepted.
ccd.situation: At a small gathering with friends, they notice a moment of silence and worry that they have done something wrong or that people don’t like them.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They must be annoyed with me. I’m probably boring and no one wants me here. If I say something, they might get upset.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, feeling lonely and rejected.
ccd.behaviors: They smile nervously, try to change the subject to something they think others will like, and avoid sharing their real opinions or feelings.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting, just the usual stuff. I saw some friends, which was nice. Sorry, I feel like I’m rambling—how are you?
Thanks for sharing. When you think about seeing your friends, what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you?

Oh, uh… I mean, I like seeing them, of course. Sometimes I worry a bit before hanging out, just about, you know, saying the right things and not being awkward. But overall, I guess it’s fine. I just hope I don’t come off as weird or anything.
That sounds like a common concern. When you notice those worries about being awkward or coming off a certain way, what do you usually tell yourself in those moments?

Um, I usually just try to brush it off, like, "It’s not a big deal." But honestly, part of me keeps thinking, “Don’t mess this up, just go along with what everyone else wants.” I guess I end up just trying really hard to fit in so no one gets upset with me. It’s kind of automatic at this point.
It sounds like there’s a lot of pressure you put on yourself to fit in. How does trying to go along with what others want affect how you feel during or after those social interactions?

Yeah, I guess it does feel like pressure sometimes. During the moment, I just focus on making sure everyone else is comfortable, but afterward I feel kind of tired. Sometimes I wonder if people actually like me or if they just put up with me because I go along with things. It’s a little lonely, honestly.
That sounds really tough. When you notice those feelings of loneliness and wondering if people genuinely like you, what do you think might be going through your mind about yourself?

Um… I don’t know, maybe that I’m just not that interesting or good enough for people to really want me around. Sometimes I feel like if I wasn’t trying so hard, maybe they wouldn’t even notice if I was there or not. It’s kind of embarrassing to admit. I just don’t want to be a burden to anyone.
Thank you for being so open about that. When those thoughts about not being good enough or being a burden come up, how do they influence what you do or say in your social situations?

I guess I just try even harder to please everyone, like agreeing with whatever people say or not really bringing up my own opinions. I worry that if I disagree or ask for something, I’ll just annoy them. So I mostly just keep things light and try to make everyone else happy. Afterwards, I usually feel a bit drained, but at least no one seemed upset.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like no one really wants to be close to me, especially in past relationships where I was left or ignored. Growing up, I didn’t get much attention or affection from my family, which made me feel invisible and unwanted.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I show who I really am, people will reject me. I have to keep my distance or I won’t be accepted. Nobody will choose me because I’m not good enough or interesting enough to hold their attention.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid getting too close to people, often making jokes or changing the subject when conversations get personal. Sometimes I withdraw or push others away before they can leave me first.
ccd.situation: I’m talking with my partner about our plans for the weekend, and they mention wanting to spend time with friends instead. I immediately worry they don’t want to be with me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: They probably want to be with other people because I’m boring or annoying. I’m just a backup plan when they don’t have anyone better. I’m not important enough to be their priority.
ccd.emotions: I feel sad, anxious, and a little hopeless. There’s a deep ache of loneliness and fear that I’ll be rejected or left alone again.
ccd.behaviors: I become quiet and withdrawn, maybe giving short answers or avoiding further conversation. Later, I might check their social media obsessively or ruminate about what I did wrong.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, uh, it’s been… alright, I guess. Work was a bit hectic, felt like everyone needed something from me at the same time, which is kind of normal, I suppose. Oh, actually, I tried this new coffee place near my apartment—have you ever had one of those really over-the-top lattes with all the foam art and sprinkles? It was… well, a nice distraction, I guess.
It sounds like the busy day at work felt a bit overwhelming, but you found a little moment of enjoyment with that latte—what were you thinking or feeling when you were at the coffee place?

Yeah, I mean… sitting there, I guess I was trying to just relax for a second. Honestly, though, looking around at everyone else chatting with their friends or whatever, I kinda felt out of place. Sometimes I wonder if people notice when you’re alone at places like that, or if they just think you’re weird. Oh, but the barista drew this little heart in the foam, so that was kind of nice, I guess.
When you noticed those feelings of being out of place or worrying about what others might think, what thoughts went through your mind in that moment?

Um, I think I started wondering if people were judging me for sitting by myself. Like, you know, maybe they assume I don’t have anyone to meet or talk to, or that I’m just kind of awkward. Sometimes I get caught up in that—like, imagining they’re making up stories about me in their heads. Actually, I remembered this time in high school when I ate lunch alone for a whole semester and I always thought people were whispering about me, but maybe they weren’t—I don’t really know.
That memory sounds really vivid—when you think back to those times in high school and those worries about what others thought, how did that affect how you felt and acted back then?

Yeah, it’s weird, it still feels kind of fresh sometimes. I remember just kind of zoning out during lunch, pretending to read or scroll on my phone so I didn’t have to look up and see if anyone was looking at me. I’d try really hard not to draw any attention, you know? Actually, I sometimes skipped lunch altogether and just hung out in the library so I wouldn’t have to deal with it.

It sounds like those moments brought quite a bit of discomfort and led you to avoid certain situations; how do you think those experiences might be influencing how you feel or behave now in similar social settings?

I guess I still do some of the same things—I mean, like, I’ll keep to myself or leave early if I’m uncomfortable. I definitely overthink if people actually want me around, so I end up holding back or making jokes to change the subject if things get personal. It’s easier to just not be noticed sometimes. Oh, also, I still find excuses not to go out with groups, like saying I’m tired from work or something.
When you choose to hold back or avoid going out with groups, what do you imagine might happen if you allowed yourself to be more present or open in those moments?

Honestly, I worry people would find me awkward or boring, or just not that fun to be around. Sometimes I think they’d end up ignoring me or maybe just wish I hadn’t come at all. It’s like—if I let myself actually be there, I might mess something up or say something weird. Oh, and then afterward I’d probably just overthink everything I said or did, which is exhausting.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced several financial setbacks over the past few years, including job instability and unexpected expenses. These events have reinforced a sense of vulnerability and lack of control in their financial life. They tend to avoid discussing money issues with others, which contributes to feelings of isolation.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to manage my finances, I will fail and make things worse. Asking for help means admitting I am weak. I must handle this on my own or no one else will.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient often withdraws from financial discussions and avoids budgeting or reviewing bills. They may procrastinate on paying bills or seeking financial advice, hoping problems will resolve on their own. Occasionally, they distract themselves with non-financial activities to escape worry.
ccd.situation: Facing a mounting pile of unpaid bills and dwindling savings, the patient worries about making ends meet this month. They feel overwhelmed by the pressure to find a solution and fear the consequences of falling behind.
ccd.automatic_thoughts:
"I just can’t handle this. I’m going to mess everything up."  
"No one can help me; I have to deal with this alone."  
"If I ask for advice, it will show how weak I am."
ccd.emotions: The patient feels anxious, helpless, and ashamed. There is also an underlying sense of frustration mixed with hopelessness about their financial situation.
ccd.behaviors: They avoid opening bills or checking their bank account and delay seeking support or financial counseling. This avoidance leads to increased stress and a worsening financial position, reinforcing their negative beliefs.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it’s been okay, I guess. Just the usual stuff. Nothing too exciting. I’m just trying to get through the week.
What kinds of thoughts go through your mind when you’re just trying to get through the week?
I don’t know, it’s kind of hard to say. Just… I guess I feel a bit on edge sometimes. Mostly I try not to think about things too much. It’s easier not to, honestly.
When you say you feel on edge, can you describe what’s happening in your body or mind at those moments?

Um, I guess I just feel tense. My stomach gets tight and I get kind of restless. My mind races a bit, but I usually just try to distract myself. It’s uncomfortable, but I don’t really know what to do about it.
When your mind starts racing, what kinds of thoughts or worries tend to come up?

Usually, I just start thinking about all the stuff I need to deal with, you know… like bills or things I haven’t gotten to yet. I worry that I’m letting things pile up. Sometimes I wonder if I’m ever going to catch up. It’s just a lot, and I don’t really want to think about it.
How do you usually respond or act when those worries about catching up start to feel overwhelming?

I kind of just avoid it, honestly. I’ll do something else, like watch TV or go for a walk. Sometimes I just ignore the mail or my emails for a while. I know it’s probably not helping, but it feels easier in the moment.
When you avoid those tasks, how do you feel afterward—both emotionally and physically?

Uh… I feel kind of guilty, I guess. And more stressed, because I know the stuff is still there waiting. Sometimes I get this heavy feeling, like it’s hanging over me. Physically, I just feel kind of tired or drained.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored by my family and was rarely praised or shown affection. I was frequently left out of group activities and sometimes teased for being shy, which made me feel like I didn’t belong or wasn’t wanted. These experiences have stuck with me and shape how I see myself now.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I put myself out there, people will reject me because I’m not interesting or likable. I must be perfect or funny enough to be accepted, otherwise, I’m invisible or a burden. People only like me when I meet their expectations, but deep down, I’m unwanted.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid social gatherings or keep conversations very brief to minimize the chance of rejection. Sometimes I change the subject abruptly or go off on tangents to distract from uncomfortable feelings. I also replay social interactions in my head, criticizing myself for not being good enough.
ccd.situation: At a small party, I try to join a group conversation, but I notice people aren’t really including me or making eye contact. I feel like I’m on the outside looking in, unsure if they want me there or if I’m just taking up space.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They probably don’t want me here.” “I’m boring; no one cares what I say.” “I’m going to say something stupid and embarrass myself.” “Why would anyone want to be around me?”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, lonely, and sad. There’s also a deep sense of shame and worthlessness that makes me want to disappear.
ccd.behaviors: I withdraw from the group, speak very little, and eventually find an excuse to leave early. I avoid initiating conversations and keep my head down, hoping not to draw attention.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Uh, it’s been okay, I guess. Work was really busy—I had to stay late a couple nights, so I’m pretty tired. I did see this interesting documentary about whales last night though, which was kind of cool. Oh, and my neighbor’s dog keeps barking, which makes it hard to focus, but anyway… yeah, it was just a normal week, I guess.
It sounds like the busy work and the barking dog have been a bit challenging—when you’re dealing with those things, what kinds of thoughts usually come up for you?

Well, I try to just ignore the noise or distract myself, but honestly it gets a bit frustrating after a while. At work, I sometimes start thinking maybe I’m not keeping up as well as everyone else, especially when I make small mistakes—like I spelled someone’s name wrong in an email, and then I kept thinking about it all day. The barking dog, I guess it just makes me feel kind of on edge, like I can’t really relax at home. Oh, that reminds me—did you know some people actually train their dogs to do tricks just by using hand signals? I saw it on that documentary, and it was pretty impressive.
It sounds like those small mistakes at work stick with you for a while—what do you notice happens inside you when you keep thinking about the misspelled name?

Yeah, it’s like I keep replaying it in my head, worrying that the person probably thinks I’m careless or something. I start getting sort of tense, almost like my chest gets tight, and then I try to distract myself—but then I worry I’m just avoiding stuff. Sometimes I think, “Well, maybe if I just don’t say or do much, I won’t mess up again.” Speaking of mistakes, my friend once wore two different shoes to work by accident, and everyone just laughed it off, but I don’t think I’d be able to handle that—I’d probably want to just run away.
That sounds really uncomfortable, like the fear of being judged makes you want to avoid drawing attention—how do you feel that impacts what you do next at work or in social situations?

I guess it makes me kind of hang back and just keep to myself. Like, in meetings, I’ll have something I could say, but then I worry it’s not worth mentioning, so I just stay quiet. Even at lunch, I usually just check my phone or eat quickly so I don’t have to try to make conversation. It’s funny, sometimes I think people probably don’t even notice if I’m there or not—oh, but one time, I did try to join in on a group chat about weekend plans, and I accidentally interrupted someone, which was awkward… so, yeah, I just try not to get in the way.
It sounds like staying quiet feels safer but maybe leaves you feeling a bit invisible—what goes through your mind when you think about speaking up or joining in those conversations?

Usually I just think, "What if I say something dumb and everyone thinks I'm weird?" Or, I worry I’ll start talking and people will just look bored or ignore me, and then I’ll feel really awkward. Sometimes I try to come up with something to say, but then I second-guess myself so much that the moment passes. Oh, and sometimes I even practice bits of small talk in my head—like yesterday, I thought about mentioning the whale documentary, but then I thought, “No one cares about that.”
When you imagine others thinking you’re “weird” or bored by what you say, what evidence do you have from your past experiences that supports or challenges those thoughts?

Well, I guess I remember times when I’ve said something and people just kind of nodded and moved on, or didn’t really make eye contact. That happens a lot at family get-togethers, actually—like, I’ll try to join in, but it kind of feels like I’m not really part of the group. I can’t really remember a time when my opinion made much difference, unless it was something practical. Oh, but sometimes, like once in college, this classmate laughed at my joke, but I always wonder if they just felt sorry for me or something… I dunno.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has a long history of feeling unworthy in relationships, often recalling times when they were criticized or rejected by close family members. They tend to internalize these experiences, leading to a persistent sense of being fundamentally flawed or bad.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I am immoral, then others will see me as a bad person and reject me. I must hide my true self to avoid judgment. If I make a mistake, it means I’m a terrible person, not just someone who errs.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient often withdraws emotionally or physically from partners to avoid conflict or exposure. They may also engage in over-apologizing and excessive self-criticism to preempt perceived rejection or disapproval.
ccd.situation: When my partner brings up a concern or disagreement, I immediately feel like I’ve done something wrong and that they might think less of me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m such a bad person for causing this problem.” “They must think I’m immoral and don’t deserve their love.” “If they really knew me, they’d leave.”
ccd.emotions: Shame, anxiety, and sadness dominate in these moments, often accompanied by a deep sense of loneliness and hopelessness.
ccd.behaviors: I tend to clam up, avoid talking about my feelings, or sometimes push my partner away before they can reject me. Occasionally, I might over-apologize or try to fix everything on my own, even when it’s not my fault.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, my week’s been okay, I guess. I had work stuff—nothing too exciting, just the usual meetings. Oh, actually, my partner and I had this weird moment last night. But mostly I’ve just been tired, probably from not sleeping well.
When you think about that weird moment with your partner, what thoughts or feelings come up for you?

I, uh, I guess I just worry that I messed something up. They brought up something I said a few days ago, and I immediately started thinking, “Oh no, I did it again.” It reminded me of when my mom used to get upset with me for saying the wrong thing—it’s like my chest tightens up and I can’t really talk anymore. Honestly, I kind of just changed the topic and then apologized a lot, even though I’m not sure what I was even apologizing for.
That sounds really tough—when you notice your chest tightening and feeling stuck, what do you usually say to yourself in those moments?

Um… I usually just tell myself that I should’ve known better, you know? Like, “Why did you even say that?” or “You always make things worse.” Sometimes I start thinking maybe they’d be happier if I just kept quiet. Oh, that reminds me—there was this time in college when I said something in class and everyone stared at me like I’d grown another head, and I just wanted to disappear.
It sounds like those critical thoughts really weigh on you—how do you feel physically and emotionally when those thoughts start running through your mind?

Yeah, it’s like, my stomach just kind of knots up and I feel warm, almost embarrassed, even if it’s just me and my partner. I get really tense, and I start fidgeting with my hands or tapping my foot—I can’t sit still. Emotionally, I feel mostly embarrassed, and a bit… I don’t know, kind of like I’m on the outside looking in. Sorry, I’m rambling—this just reminds me of when I’d try to talk to my dad about something and he’d just give me that look, like I’d disappointed him again.

Thanks for sharing that; it sounds like those experiences have been really painful. When you notice those physical sensations and feelings of being on the outside looking in, what do you usually do next?

Um, usually I just sort of pull back, like I get really quiet or I find something else to do—cleaning, checking my phone, stuff like that. It’s almost like if I fade into the background, no one can get mad at me. Sometimes I’ll just leave the room altogether, or I’ll keep apologizing until they tell me to stop. Oh, and sometimes I’ll replay the whole thing in my head over and over, trying to figure out what I could’ve said differently, which is exhausting.
When you replay those moments and think about what you might have said differently, do you notice any patterns or themes in the thoughts that come up?

I guess… yeah, it’s usually me thinking I’ve done something wrong, that I’m the problem. I keep trying to remember every detail, like if I said something that sounded rude or selfish or… I don’t know, just bad. There’s this thing where I’ll convince myself that if I’d just acted differently, nobody would be upset, so it must be my fault. Oh, and then I end up feeling kind of hopeless because it seems like no matter what I do, I always mess up somehow.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt overlooked and powerless at work, especially when deadlines tighten and expectations rise. Past experiences where I tried to speak up but was ignored have left me feeling stuck and unable to change my circumstances.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to take control, I’ll just end up overwhelmed or pushed aside again. It’s safer to keep quiet and accept things as they are because fighting back won’t make a difference.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to withdraw and avoid confrontation, keeping my head down to avoid additional stress. Sometimes I procrastinate to escape the pressure, even though it makes things worse later.
ccd.situation: At work, when the pressure builds and my manager piles on more tasks, I feel like I have no power to influence the outcome or ask for help.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “There’s nothing I can do to change this.” “I’m just a victim of circumstances beyond my control.” “If I speak up, I’ll be ignored or blamed.”
ccd.emotions: Hopeless, frustrated, anxious, and resigned.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet during meetings, avoid asking for support, and sometimes delay starting my tasks because I feel overwhelmed.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been kind of busy, but nothing out of the ordinary. I just tried to keep my head down and get things done. Not much else to report, really.
When you say you kept your head down and got things done, what kind of thoughts were going through your mind while you were working?
I don’t know… I guess I was just focusing on not messing up. Sometimes I kept thinking, “Just get through it.” I tried not to think too much about anything else. It’s easier that way.
What do you notice happens for you when you try not to think too much about anything else?

Um… it feels a bit easier in the moment, like I’m less stressed if I just focus on what’s right in front of me. But sometimes I still feel kind of tense, like there’s this pressure building up. It doesn’t really go away. I guess I just try to ignore it.
When you notice that pressure building up, what do you usually do next?

Honestly, I just keep quiet and keep working. Sometimes I’ll take a quick break, like scrolling on my phone or making more coffee. I don’t really talk to anyone about it. I just try to push through until things calm down.
What thoughts or feelings come up for you when you think about talking to someone about how you’re feeling at work?
Uh… I don’t know, it just feels pointless. Like, I doubt it would change anything. I usually feel nervous, maybe a bit embarrassed. I prefer to just handle it myself.
When you feel nervous or embarrassed about sharing your feelings, what do you imagine might happen if you did open up to someone?

I guess I kind of expect that they wouldn’t really listen, or they’d just brush it off. Maybe they’d think I’m complaining or not handling things well. Or they might just ignore it and move on. It feels like it wouldn’t make much of a difference.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced several setbacks in pursuing personal goals, often feeling unsupported and overwhelmed. Past attempts at change have felt futile, leading to a growing sense of powerlessness in their own life.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to improve myself, it won’t make a difference. I don’t have what it takes to change things. It’s safer not to try because I’ll just fail again.
ccd.coping_strategies: Avoiding new challenges, procrastinating on personal goals, and withdrawing from opportunities for growth. Sometimes they rely on others to make decisions or take action for them.
ccd.situation: The patient is considering enrolling in a course to develop new skills but feels uncertain and doubtful about whether they can succeed.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “There’s no point in trying because I’ll just mess up.” “I’m not strong enough to handle this.” “I’ll probably fail like I always do.”
ccd.emotions: Hopelessness, frustration, sadness, and anxiety about the future.
ccd.behaviors: Putting off signing up for the course, making excuses to avoid commitment, and engaging in passive activities instead of active growth efforts.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing really special happened. I thought about signing up for this course I saw, but I haven’t done it yet. Just not sure if it’s worth it.
What thoughts come up for you when you think about signing up for the course?
I just keep thinking I’ll mess it up somehow. Like, even if I try, I probably won’t do well. It feels like there’s no point, because things never really work out for me anyway.
When you notice those thoughts that you’ll mess it up or that things never work out, how do they make you feel?
It just makes me feel tired, honestly. Kind of hopeless, like why bother trying at all? Sometimes I get anxious just thinking about trying something new. Mostly I just feel stuck.
When you feel stuck and anxious about trying something new, what do you usually do next?

Usually, I put it off. I find excuses not to do it—like telling myself I’ll look into it later, or that I’m too busy right now. Sometimes I just distract myself with TV or scrolling on my phone instead. It’s easier than dealing with all the worries in my head.
What do you notice happens to your feelings or thoughts after you put it off or distract yourself?

For a little while, I feel some relief, I guess—like I don’t have to think about it. But then the guilt creeps in, or I feel annoyed at myself for not doing anything. The worries just come back anyway. It’s like I can’t really escape it for long.
That sounds really frustrating. When the guilt or annoyance comes up, what do you say to yourself in those moments?

Mostly I just think, “Why can’t I just get it together?” or “Other people don’t have this problem.” Sometimes I tell myself I’m just lazy, or that I’ll probably always be like this. It’s not a great feeling.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up in a household where conflicts were frequent and unresolved, I often felt powerless to change the situation or protect myself. This led me to believe that no matter what I do, I won’t be able to influence my family dynamics positively.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to stand up for myself, I will just make things worse or be blamed. It’s safer to accept that I have no control and just endure the situation. People in my family don’t really care about my feelings or needs.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to withdraw or avoid family gatherings to escape the tension. Sometimes, I try to appease others by agreeing with them even when I disagree, to prevent conflict. I also vent my frustration to close friends instead of addressing issues directly with family.
ccd.situation: During a recent family dinner, a disagreement erupted over plans for the holidays, and I felt overwhelmed and silenced as others dominated the conversation.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one is listening to me; I’m just a burden.” “There’s nothing I can do to change how they treat me.” “I’m stuck and have to accept this.”
ccd.emotions: I felt helpless, sad, and frustrated. There was also a deep sense of loneliness and being misunderstood.
ccd.behaviors: I stayed quiet and avoided sharing my opinion. Afterwards, I isolated myself in my room and avoided further interaction for the evening. I also ruminated over the experience, feeling stuck in the same pattern.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Honestly? Not great. I had dinner with my family a couple days ago and, I don’t know, it just felt exhausting. Everyone was arguing and I just kind of shut down. I’m just tired of it, I guess.
When you say you shut down during the argument, what was going through your mind at that moment?
I don’t know, it’s like… what’s the point? Whenever things get heated, nobody listens to me anyway. I just thought, “Here we go again.” I just wanted it to be over so I could leave.
What do you notice happening inside you—thoughts, feelings, or physical sensations—when you think, “Here we go again”?

I get this heavy feeling in my chest, like a weight or something. My stomach gets kind of tight too. I just feel really small, like I want to disappear. And I guess I start thinking that it doesn’t really matter what I say, so I’d rather just stay quiet and get through it.
That sounds really tough. When you feel that way and decide to stay quiet, what do you usually do afterward—do you reflect on what happened, or notice any changes in how you feel about yourself?

Yeah, I definitely think about it a lot afterwards. I usually end up stuck replaying everything in my head, wishing I’d said something different but also convincing myself it wouldn’t have mattered. I just feel kind of numb and angry at myself for not speaking up, but also like, what’s the use? Mostly, I just want to be alone after.
When you replay those moments and wish you’d said something different, what do you imagine would have happened if you had spoken up?

Honestly, I just picture it going badly. Like, people would talk over me or maybe even get mad at me for causing more drama. I’d probably just end up feeling worse, like I made things more tense. That’s why it feels safer not to say anything at all.
It sounds like you’re expecting that speaking up might lead to more conflict or negative reactions. Have you ever had an experience where speaking up led to a different, maybe better, outcome?

Not really, at least not that I can remember. Every time I’ve tried to say something, it either gets ignored or someone shuts me down. I just end up feeling embarrassed or stupid for even trying. That’s why I don’t bother anymore.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like I don’t have what it takes to handle things on my own, especially at work. Growing up, I often depended on others to solve problems for me and was told I wasn’t strong enough to do things by myself. This has made me feel like I’m constantly vulnerable and in need of help.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t get support, I won’t be able to manage the pressure. Asking for help is the only way I can stay afloat. I’m not capable of handling challenges alone, so I need others to step in or I’ll fail.
ccd.coping_strategies: I often ask colleagues for reassurance or step-by-step guidance when tasks feel overwhelming. Sometimes, I avoid taking on new responsibilities because I’m worried I won’t do them well without help. I also spend a lot of time double-checking my work to avoid mistakes.
ccd.situation: At work, when deadlines pile up and I’m given new projects, I feel overwhelmed and unsure if I can handle everything without support. The pressure to perform makes me anxious and uncertain about my abilities.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I can’t do this without someone helping me.” “If I don’t ask for help, I’ll mess up.” “I’m not strong enough to handle this on my own.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, helplessness, and frustration. I feel small and worried that I’m going to disappoint others or myself.
ccd.behaviors: I reach out frequently to coworkers for advice or reassurance. I might delay starting tasks until I feel I have enough guidance, and sometimes I avoid taking on new challenges altogether.

messages.content: How has your week been for you so far?
It’s been kind of stressful, honestly. Work’s felt a bit overwhelming with all the projects coming in at once. I keep second-guessing myself. I guess I’ve just been a little anxious about keeping up.
When you notice yourself second-guessing at work, what kinds of thoughts are going through your mind?

Mostly I just think, “What if I mess this up?” Or like, “I’m not sure I can do this right on my own.” Sometimes I feel like I need to check with someone else before I move forward, just in case I’m missing something. It’s almost like I don’t trust myself to get it right the first time.
What feelings come up for you when you have those thoughts about messing up or not being sure?

I feel really anxious—like my chest gets tight and I can’t relax. There’s this worry that I’ll let people down if I get it wrong. Sometimes I just feel kind of helpless, like I don’t know what to do next. It’s pretty frustrating.
When you start to feel that tightness in your chest and the worry about letting people down, what do you usually do next?

Usually, I end up reaching out to someone at work—like asking a coworker to look over what I’ve done or walk me through the next steps. Sometimes I put off starting things until I’ve double-checked everything or gotten advice. I just feel better if I know someone’s there to help. If I can’t get that, I get even more anxious and sometimes just avoid the task for a while.
How do you feel about those moments when you avoid the task or wait for help before starting?

Honestly, I feel kind of disappointed in myself afterward. I know I should probably just try to handle it, but I get so worried about messing up. Sometimes I think maybe I’m just not as capable as other people. It makes me feel a bit small, I guess.
When you notice those feelings of disappointment and thinking you’re not as capable, what do you usually tell yourself about those thoughts?

I usually just kind of accept it, like, “Yeah, that figures—I always need help.” Sometimes I try to push the thought away, but it keeps coming back. I tell myself I should be better at this by now, but I can’t seem to shake the feeling. It’s hard not to believe it’s true.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like no matter what I do, I can’t change my circumstances. Growing up, I was told to just accept things as they are, so I learned to avoid rocking the boat and just try to keep others happy.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t keep everyone else happy, they might get upset or leave me. It’s safer to go along with what others want because I can’t handle conflict or change on my own.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to agree with others even when I disagree, avoid speaking up about my needs, and try to please people to prevent any problems or rejection.
ccd.situation: I was asked to take on extra work at my job even though I’m already overwhelmed, and I felt like I couldn’t say no.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I say no, they’ll think I’m useless or not a team player. I won’t be able to handle the added pressure, and everything will fall apart.”
ccd.emotions: Anxious, overwhelmed, and a bit defeated.
ccd.behaviors: I reluctantly agreed to do the extra work, stayed quiet about my struggles, and later withdrew from social interactions to cope with stress.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too out of the ordinary. Work’s been a bit busy, but that’s normal, right? I just try to keep up and not let anyone down.
When you say you try not to let anyone down at work, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind during those busy times?

Um, well, I just… I worry a lot about making mistakes, I guess. I keep thinking I should do more, or that maybe people are counting on me and I shouldn’t disappoint them. Sometimes I worry that if I mess up, people will be upset, or maybe think I’m not good enough to handle things. I know it probably sounds silly.
That sounds really tough. When you notice those worries about making mistakes or disappointing others, how do you usually respond or cope with those feelings in the moment?

Oh, um, I usually just try to push through and do whatever is asked of me. I guess I don’t really say anything—I just keep my head down and focus on getting things done. Sometimes I try to tell myself it’s fine, but I still feel pretty anxious inside. I don’t want anyone to think I can’t handle it, so I just keep quiet.
It sounds like you put a lot of pressure on yourself to keep going even when you’re feeling anxious; what usually happens to your anxiety or thoughts after you push through like that?

Yeah, I guess I do. After I push through, sometimes I feel a little relieved that I didn’t mess up or let anyone down. But honestly, the anxiety doesn’t really go away—it sort of stays there in the background, and I just get really tired. I end up feeling a bit drained, and I start worrying about the next thing that might come up.
When you notice that lingering anxiety and fatigue, how does that affect what you do next or how you approach your work or other responsibilities?

Um, well, I usually just try to keep going—like, I don’t really slow down or take a break, even if I feel exhausted. I guess I worry that if I stop or say anything, people might notice and be disappointed or annoyed. So I just kind of push those feelings aside and focus on what needs to get done. I try not to make a fuss about it.
When you push aside your feelings to avoid disappointing others, what impact does that have on how you feel about yourself or your work over time?

I guess… after a while, I just start to feel kind of empty, or like I’m running on autopilot. Sometimes I wonder if I’m really doing a good job, or just barely managing to hold it all together. It’s like, no matter how hard I try, it never feels quite enough. I don’t want anyone to know I feel that way, though.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like I can’t handle things on my own, especially in my close relationships. Growing up, I depended a lot on others for support and validation, which made me feel vulnerable and unsure of myself. This has carried over into my adult relationships, where I worry that I’m too much to handle.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t get constant reassurance or help, I will fall apart or be abandoned. People won’t want to be around me if I’m too needy or ask for too much. I have to keep others close because I can’t trust myself to manage alone.
ccd.coping_strategies: I often reach out to friends or partners repeatedly for support, even when it might feel overwhelming to them. Sometimes, I avoid talking about my deeper needs to not scare people away but then feel resentful or lonely. I also try to distract myself with activities when I feel too dependent.
ccd.situation: I noticed that my partner didn’t reply to my message for a few hours, and I started feeling anxious and upset about what that might mean for us.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “What if they’re mad at me because I’m too needy?” “Maybe I’m bothering them and they want to leave.” “I can’t do this on my own; I need them to be there for me.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, fear of abandonment, and feeling overwhelmed.
ccd.behaviors: I sent several follow-up messages asking if everything was okay and tried to get their attention. Later, I withdrew a bit, feeling hurt and unsure whether to keep reaching out or just give up.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been kind of up and down, I guess. Some days I feel alright, you know, just doing work and keeping busy, but then little things will throw me off. Like, yesterday I was trying to cook dinner, and it reminded me of this time in college when I tried to make pasta and totally messed it up because I was distracted. Anyway, yeah, it’s just been… a mix.
When you think back to that college pasta moment, what thoughts were going through your mind at that time and when it comes up now?

Oh, wow, that was such a mess. I remember feeling like everyone else just knew how to do things and I was always scrambling—like, my roommate just laughed and ended up helping me finish it. I guess I felt kind of embarrassed but also kind of relieved she was there. Thinking about it now, it’s funny, but sometimes it’s like… I still worry people will think I can’t handle simple stuff on my own, you know?
When you notice that worry about what others might think, what feelings or physical sensations tend to come up for you?

Uh, I get this tightness in my chest sometimes, like it’s hard to breathe for a second. My stomach will twist up, too, especially if I start overthinking about it. And I just feel… sort of panicky, like my mind keeps going back and forth between whether I should reach out to someone or just stay quiet. Oh, that reminds me of this one time at work when I needed help with a project, and I spent half an hour just debating whether to ask my coworker or not.
What goes through your mind when you’re debating whether to ask your coworker for help?

It’s like… I start thinking, “What if they’re annoyed by me?” or “Am I asking for too much?” I worry that maybe they’ll talk about me later, like I’m not capable or something. And then sometimes I think, well, maybe I should just figure it out myself, but then I get stuck and frustrated. It’s kind of exhausting, honestly—oh, and then I’ll usually remember times when I did ask and felt awkward afterward, like in high school when I always needed help with math.
When you recall those past times feeling awkward after asking for help, what do you tell yourself about your ability to handle things on your own?

I guess I sort of tell myself that I’m not really cut out for doing things alone. Sometimes I feel like maybe people see me as… I don’t know, a burden, I guess. It’s weird because logically I know everyone needs help sometimes, but it just feels different for me, like I need it more than most. Oh, and then I’ll end up replaying old conversations in my head, wondering if I came across as too much.
When you replay those conversations in your mind, what feelings or conclusions tend to stand out the most for you?

Mostly I feel embarrassed, I think. I keep worrying that I said the wrong thing or leaned on them too much, and then I start feeling kind of ashamed. It’s like this sinking feeling in my stomach, especially if I think they seemed distant afterward. Then I get stuck thinking maybe I should’ve just kept quiet, but it’s hard because I really needed someone in those moments—oh, and sometimes I wonder if they were just being polite and didn’t actually want to help.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and misunderstood by my family, which made me believe that I could never truly connect with others. Repeated experiences of being excluded or criticized have reinforced my sense of loneliness and isolation.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I think that if I open up or show my true self, people will reject me. Relationships are risky and usually end in disappointment. It’s safer to keep my distance because I’m not meant to be close to anyone.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid social gatherings and keep conversations brief to protect myself from feeling vulnerable. I also withdraw emotionally and don’t share much about myself, hoping this will prevent others from leaving or hurting me.
ccd.situation: During family dinners, I often feel like an outsider because no one asks me questions or seems interested in what I have to say. This lack of attention makes me feel invisible and confirms my fears about being unlovable.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one cares about me.” “I’m always going to be alone.” “If I say something, they’ll think I’m weird and push me away.”
ccd.emotions: I feel lonely, sad, and anxious. Sometimes, there’s also a sense of hopelessness and shame about not being able to connect with my family.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet during family gatherings and avoid initiating conversations. I often leave early or find excuses to be alone afterward, reinforcing my isolation.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it was okay, I guess. Nothing special, really. I just did my usual stuff, went to work and came home. Not much to talk about.
What thoughts or feelings came up for you during your usual routine this week?
I don’t know, I guess I just felt kind of… tired. Maybe a bit down, but that’s pretty normal for me. Sometimes I just feel like I’m going through the motions. Nothing really stood out.
When you notice those feelings of tiredness and going through the motions, what kinds of thoughts tend to run through your mind at those moments?
Um… sometimes I just think it’s not worth trying too hard, you know? Like, nothing really changes anyway. I guess I wonder what the point is. It’s hard to shake that feeling.
What do you think would happen if you did try harder, despite feeling that nothing changes?
I don’t know. I guess I just worry that it wouldn’t matter. Maybe people wouldn’t notice, or things would end up the same. It feels safer not to expect too much.
Can you tell me about a time when you did put in effort and noticed a positive change, even if it was small?

I’m not sure. It’s hard to remember something like that. I guess there might have been times at work when I spoke up and someone listened, but it doesn’t really stick with me. Most of the time, I just keep quiet.
When you think about those moments at work when you spoke up and were heard, how did that affect how you felt about yourself or the situation afterward?
I suppose I felt a little better, maybe more noticed. But it didn’t last long. Afterward, I just went back to feeling the same as usual. It’s like it didn’t really change things.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored or criticized when I tried to speak up, which made me believe that my opinions didn’t matter. Over time, I started to think there was something wrong with me that made others reject or overlook me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t try to please others, they won’t like me or will get upset with me. I have to be agreeable and avoid conflict because I’m not capable of handling rejection or disapproval.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to agree with whatever others say, even if I don’t really feel that way, to keep the peace and avoid negative reactions. I also avoid sharing my true thoughts or feelings to prevent others from seeing my flaws.
ccd.situation: During a group conversation at work, people are sharing their opinions about a project, and I feel like I don’t have anything valuable to add.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I say what I really think, they’ll think I’m stupid or annoying.” “I’m probably just wasting everyone’s time.” “I better just keep quiet and agree with them.”
ccd.emotions: Anxious, insecure, and sad because I feel invisible and unimportant.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet, nod along, and try to smile to seem friendly, even though I feel disconnected and ignored inside.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work has been a little busy, but nothing too bad. I just tried to keep up and not get in anyone’s way. Honestly, I think things are fine overall.
When you say you tried to keep up and not get in anyone’s way, what was going through your mind at that moment?

Oh, um… I guess I just didn’t want to mess anything up, you know? I felt like if I made a mistake or said the wrong thing, people might get frustrated or think less of me. So I just stayed quiet and tried to do what was expected. I didn’t really want to draw much attention to myself.
That sounds like a lot of pressure to avoid making mistakes or upsetting others. How did you feel inside while you were staying quiet and trying to meet expectations?

Yeah, I guess it did feel like a lot of pressure. Honestly, I felt kind of nervous and maybe a bit tense, just hoping I wouldn’t slip up or say something wrong. Sometimes I feel a little sad, too, like I’m just sort of… there, but not really part of things. But I try not to think about it too much and just focus on doing what I’m supposed to.
It sounds like those feelings of nervousness and sadness are pretty uncomfortable. When you notice those feelings, what do you usually do to cope or manage them?

Um, I usually just try to push those feelings down and not really show them. I try to act like everything’s fine—smile, nod, agree with people—so nobody notices I’m struggling. I guess I just hope the feelings will go away if I don’t pay too much attention to them. I don’t really talk about it with anyone.
That sounds exhausting, trying to keep those feelings hidden. What do you think might happen if you did share some of these feelings with someone you trust?

I don’t know… I guess I worry that they might think I’m being dramatic or just too sensitive. Or maybe they’d get annoyed or not really understand what I’m feeling. I’d hate to make anyone uncomfortable or have them see me differently. It’s just easier to keep it to myself, I think.
Those concerns make a lot of sense. When you imagine someone thinking you’re being dramatic or sensitive, what does that say to you about yourself?

Um… I guess it kind of makes me feel like there’s something wrong with me, or like I’m just not good enough at handling things. I worry that I’m not as strong as other people, or that I’m just a burden. It’s not a great feeling, honestly. But I try to just move past it and not let anyone see.

ccd.relevant_history: I've often felt like I can’t manage money well, especially after a few times when unexpected bills piled up and I had to ask for help. Growing up, my family struggled financially, and I learned to keep my worries to myself because I didn’t want to burden anyone.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I admit I’m struggling, people will see me as weak and might distance themselves. I have to handle things on my own because no one else will step in. Asking for help means I’m failing and confirms that I’m not capable.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid talking about money worries and try to minimize how bad things feel. Sometimes I withdraw and keep a low profile, hoping problems will resolve themselves. At times, I carefully budget but often feel overwhelmed and procrastinate dealing with bills.
ccd.situation: I just received a notice that my credit card payment is late, and the balance is higher than I expected. I’m worried about how to cover it and whether I’ll be able to avoid falling further behind.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: I’m such a mess with money. I shouldn’t have let it get this bad. Nobody really wants to hear about my problems—I’m just being a burden. I don’t know how I’m going to fix this.
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, ashamed, and hopeless. There’s a tight knot in my stomach and a growing sense of panic.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid opening my mail and delay looking at my bank statements. I keep my worries to myself and pull away from friends or family who might offer support.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it’s been okay, I guess. Just, you know, the usual stuff—work, trying to keep up with things. Nothing too exciting. I’ve just been kind of tired.
When you say you’ve been tired, can you tell me more about what that feels like for you and what’s been going on around those times?

It’s just… I feel sort of drained most days, like there’s a lot on my mind. Sometimes I can’t really pinpoint why. I guess I haven’t been sleeping that well either. I just… keep thinking about stuff I need to do.
What kinds of thoughts tend to come up when you’re trying to sleep or when you notice that feeling of being drained?

Um, mostly just worries, I guess. Like things I might’ve forgotten or stuff I have to sort out—just a lot of little things piling up. Sometimes money pops up in my head, but I try not to dwell on it. I don’t know, it’s just kind of a jumble.
When you notice those worries about things piling up or money, how do you usually respond or cope in the moment?

I usually just try to push the thoughts away and distract myself—maybe watch something or scroll on my phone. Sometimes I just go to bed early and hope things will seem better in the morning. I don’t really talk to anyone about it. I figure it’s just something I need to handle myself.
When you try to push the thoughts away or distract yourself, how effective does that feel in helping you manage the worries or feel better?
It works for a little while, I guess. The worries usually come back, though—sometimes even stronger. It can get kind of overwhelming. But I’m not really sure what else to do.
When those worries come back stronger, what kinds of feelings or physical sensations do you notice in your body?
Um, I get kind of tense, like my shoulders and neck get tight. My stomach feels weird—like a knot or something. Sometimes my chest feels heavy, and I just want to hide. It’s not a good feeling.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has a history of feeling undervalued at previous jobs and often received critical feedback, which reinforced their sense of inadequacy. They tend to internalize criticism and rarely seek support, leading to ongoing self-doubt.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I don't perform perfectly, I am a failure." "Others will think less of me if I make mistakes." "I have to prove my worth through constant hard work."
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to withdraw during stressful times and works longer hours to avoid criticism. They avoid asking for help and often ruminate on perceived shortcomings, which increases their stress.
ccd.situation: At work, the patient is given a tight deadline for a complex project with high visibility. They feel the pressure to deliver flawless results to avoid disappointing their team and manager.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m going to mess this up and everyone will see that I’m useless." "If I can’t do this perfectly, it means I’m a complete failure." "I’m just wasting everyone’s time."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, and hopelessness dominate the patient’s emotional experience in this situation. They also feel overwhelmed and tense.
ccd.behaviors: The patient isolates themselves, avoids asking for clarification or help, and pushes through the work with increasing anxiety. They may procrastinate briefly out of fear, then overwork to compensate.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Uh, it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been kind of busy. I haven’t really done much outside of that. Nothing too exciting.
When you say work has been busy, what kinds of thoughts have been going through your mind during those busy times?

I don’t know, just… hoping I don’t mess up, I guess. Sometimes I worry I’ll forget something important or do something wrong. I try not to think about it too much, but it’s kind of always there in the back of my mind.
What do you notice happens to your mood or energy when those worries come up?
Um, I get pretty tense. It’s hard to focus on anything else. I start feeling kind of anxious and tired, even if it’s still early in the day. Sometimes I just want to hide at my desk until it passes.
When you feel that tension and anxiety at work, what do you usually do to try to manage or cope with those feelings?
I usually just keep my head down and try to get through my tasks. I don’t really talk to anyone about it. Sometimes I’ll stay late to double-check everything. I just try to avoid making mistakes.
How do you think staying late and double-checking your work affects your anxiety or stress levels afterward?

I guess it helps a little in the moment, because I feel like I’m being careful. But honestly, I’m usually still pretty stressed when I leave. I end up thinking about what I missed anyway. It’s like it never really goes away.
When those worries about what you might have missed come up after work, what kinds of thoughts or images do you notice going through your mind?

Um… I just picture people finding out I messed up. Like my boss being disappointed or my team thinking I don’t know what I’m doing. Sometimes I worry they might regret having me there. It kind of just loops in my head.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has a long history of struggling in social settings, often feeling inadequate and fearing judgment from others. Past experiences of criticism and failure have reinforced their belief that they cannot handle social challenges successfully.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to engage with others, I will mess up or embarrass myself. People will think less of me if I show any weakness or make mistakes. I must avoid situations where I might be exposed as incompetent.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid social gatherings and limits conversations to brief, surface-level interactions. They may also rehearse what to say in advance or withdraw emotionally during social encounters to protect themselves from feeling exposed.
ccd.situation: Being invited to a group event where the patient is expected to contribute to conversations and interact with new people.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I'm going to say something stupid and everyone will notice how clueless I am." "They probably don’t want me here." "I can’t keep up with what they’re talking about."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, and a deep sense of inadequacy.
