ccd.behaviors: Avoiding eye contact, staying quiet, leaving early, or finding reasons to not attend future social events.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It's been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting. I mostly just kept to myself at work. I didn’t really do much outside of that.
When you say you kept to yourself at work, what were you thinking or feeling in those moments?

Um, I don’t know… I guess I just felt a bit on edge. I worry a lot about saying the wrong thing. It’s easier to just keep my head down and not draw attention to myself. I always end up feeling kind of tense.
What kinds of thoughts go through your mind when you worry about saying the wrong thing?

I guess I start thinking that I’ll embarrass myself somehow, or that people will notice if I mess up. Sometimes it feels like everyone’s watching, waiting for me to slip. I sort of freeze up and can’t figure out what to say. It just feels safer not to say much at all.
When you notice those thoughts about embarrassing yourself or being watched, how does your body feel in those moments?

I get really tense—like my shoulders get tight and my stomach kind of knots up. My heart beats faster, and sometimes my hands feel shaky. I just want to disappear or get out of there as soon as I can. It’s pretty uncomfortable.
What do you usually do right after feeling that tension and wanting to get away?

Usually, I just go quiet and try not to make eye contact with anyone. If I can, I’ll find an excuse to leave the situation early. Sometimes I’ll pretend to be busy with something else. Mostly, I just hope no one notices how awkward I feel.
Looking back, can you recall any times when you chose to speak up or stay present despite feeling that way? What was different about those moments?

Yeah, there have been a few times when I forced myself to say something, usually when I felt like I had to. I remember once in a meeting, my boss asked me a direct question, so I spoke up. I was really nervous, but I got through it. I think I only managed because I didn’t have much choice—it wasn’t like I felt any more confident or comfortable.

core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt rejected and blamed by family members, which made me believe I was inherently bad and unlovable. Past relationships have reinforced this feeling, with partners sometimes accusing me of being controlling or harmful. These experiences have deeply shaped how I see myself in relationships.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I get close to someone, I will hurt them or make things worse. People will leave me if they really knew me. I have to keep my distance or control situations to avoid causing harm. It’s safer not to show my true self because I’m too dangerous to be loved.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid deep emotional conversations or pull away when things get too intense to prevent potential harm. Sometimes I over-apologize or try to fix everything to make sure I’m not causing pain. Other times, I detach or shut down to keep from feeling overwhelmed or rejected.
ccd.situation: My partner asks me why I seem distant and says they want to understand what I’m feeling. I start to worry that if I open up, I’ll say something wrong or hurt them, confirming that I’m toxic and should stay away.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I tell them how I really feel, they’ll hate me or leave.” “I’m just going to mess this up like I always do.” “I’m dangerous to be around.” “It’s better if I stay quiet and keep my distance.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, sadness, and fear of rejection. There’s a heavy weight of loneliness mixed with the dread of causing pain to someone I care about.
ccd.behaviors: I withdraw emotionally and physically, giving short or vague answers. I avoid eye contact and may become irritable or shut down completely. This creates distance and confusion, making it harder for the relationship to feel safe or connected.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been… alright, I guess. Work’s been keeping me pretty busy, so I haven’t really had much time to think about other stuff. My partner and I had this conversation the other day that kind of stuck with me, but it’s probably not a big deal. Oh, and my neighbor’s dog keeps barking at all hours, which is just—I don’t know, kind of annoying, honestly.
That conversation with your partner—what about it has stayed with you?

Oh, it was just this thing where they asked me why I seemed distant lately. I don’t know, I felt kind of put on the spot, and I probably didn’t explain myself very well. I just said I was tired from work, which is true, but… yeah. Anyway, it made me think about how I always seem to end up in these weird situations where I don’t know what to say, kind of like in high school when my friend Sarah confronted me about not inviting her to my birthday party—totally different, but it just popped into my head.
When those memories from high school come up, what thoughts or feelings tend to go along with them?

Um, I guess I just feel kinda awkward and embarrassed, like I messed something up again. Back then, I remember feeling really guilty, even though I didn’t mean to hurt anyone. It’s weird how those things just pop up years later, you know? Sometimes it makes me wonder if I’m just not good at this whole… being close to people thing.
That sounds really tough. When you notice those feelings of embarrassment or guilt now, what do you usually do or say to yourself in response?

Uh, well, I mostly just try to brush it off or distract myself, like scrolling through my phone or watching a show. Sometimes I’ll tell myself it’s not a big deal and try to move on, but it sorta sticks around anyway. Or I just get really quiet—my partner notices that sometimes and asks what’s wrong, but I never know what to say. Honestly, I usually just hope it goes away if I don’t pay attention to it.
When you get quiet and your partner asks what’s wrong, what thoughts or feelings make it hard for you to share what’s on your mind?

Uh, I guess I worry that if I really say what’s going on, they’ll think I’m too much or that something’s wrong with me. There’s this… I don’t know, this heavy feeling, like I might mess things up if I’m honest. Sometimes I think, what if I say something and it just makes them upset or pushes them away? It’s almost easier just to keep quiet, even though that probably doesn’t help either.
That sounds like a really difficult balance to navigate. When you imagine sharing your feelings and your partner reacting negatively, what specific thoughts or images come to mind?

Um… I picture them looking disappointed or upset, maybe even walking away or just going really quiet themselves. I start thinking about old arguments where things got blown out of proportion, or even times when people in my family would say I was causing problems. Sometimes I imagine them saying something like, “Why are you like this?” which is… kind of a horrible thing to think. I guess I just expect it to go wrong, even before I open my mouth.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

ccd.relevant_history: I've always felt like I have to work extra hard to be accepted by others, especially in friendships and relationships. Growing up, I often felt overlooked or dismissed, which made me believe I’m not truly lovable. These feelings come up most when I’m around new people or in social situations where I want to fit in.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t please others, they will reject me. I have to keep everyone happy to avoid being abandoned. Being myself isn’t enough to be liked or loved by others.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to say yes to every request and avoid disagreeing with people. I often hide my true feelings or opinions to keep the peace and make sure others don’t get upset with me. Sometimes I over-apologize even when I haven’t done anything wrong.
ccd.situation: I was at a small gathering with coworkers, and someone asked if I wanted to join a weekend outing. I wasn’t really interested, but I said yes because I didn’t want them to think I was rude or didn’t care.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I say no, they’ll think I’m unfriendly or don’t want to be part of the group.” “They’ll probably stop inviting me if I don’t go.” “I’m just going to be ignored or left out again.”
ccd.emotions: Anxious, worried, and a bit sad. I felt tense and like I was walking on eggshells, scared of disappointing others.
ccd.behaviors: I agreed to go even though I didn’t want to. During the outing, I kept quiet and tried to agree with everyone to avoid conflict. Afterwards, I felt exhausted and like I wasn’t really being myself.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too out of the ordinary. Work’s been a bit busy, but I’ve managed. I’m just trying to keep up and not let anyone down, you know?
What kinds of thoughts go through your mind when you’re trying to keep up and not let anyone down at work?

I guess I just worry a lot about what people might think if I mess up or if I can’t do something they ask. I find myself thinking, like, “What if they’re disappointed in me?” or “What if they think I’m not pulling my weight?” Most of the time I just try to do whatever is needed so no one gets upset. I mean, I know it probably sounds silly, but I really don’t want to be the reason things go wrong.
That sounds really tough to carry around. When you notice those worries about disappointing others, how do you usually respond or cope with those feelings in the moment?

Um, I usually just try harder to do whatever is expected. If someone asks for something, I’ll say yes even if I’m already swamped. Sometimes I’ll go over things a bunch of times to make sure there aren’t any mistakes. I don’t really bring up if I’m struggling—I just kind of keep it to myself.
What do you notice happens to your stress or energy levels when you keep saying yes and double-checking things without sharing how you’re feeling?

Honestly, I get pretty worn out. By the end of the day, I feel really drained, like I just want to hide away for a bit. Sometimes I get headaches or just feel kind of low. But I don’t want to make a fuss, so I just push through it.
When you feel drained and low like that, what do you tell yourself about taking breaks or asking for help?

Oh, um, I usually just think I should be able to handle it on my own. I worry that if I ask for help, people might think I’m not capable or that I’m just being difficult. Taking breaks feels kind of selfish sometimes, like I’m letting others down. So I just try to keep going, even if I’m tired.
Where do you think those beliefs about needing to handle everything on your own and not wanting to let others down might have come from?

I’m not totally sure, but I guess it’s just sort of always felt that way. Growing up, I think I kind of learned not to ask for too much or make waves. I noticed people seemed to like me better when I didn’t complain or need anything. It just became a habit, I guess.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and rejected by my family and peers, which left me with a deep sense that I am not valued or wanted. These early experiences have shaped how I see myself in relationships, making me constantly worry about being accepted or loved.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I believe that if people really got to know me, they wouldn’t want to be close to me. I often think that I need to be perfect or change myself to be worthy of love and attention. This makes me feel like I have to hide parts of myself to avoid rejection.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to keep my distance emotionally, avoiding opening up to others to protect myself from potential hurt. Sometimes, I try to please people excessively or change my behavior just to be liked, but this often leaves me feeling exhausted and still lonely.
ccd.situation: When I notice my partner or someone I care about not responding to my messages or seeming distant, I immediately worry that they don’t want me around or that I’ve done something wrong.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: They must be losing interest in me because I’m not good enough. I’m probably annoying or boring them, and soon they’ll want to leave. Maybe I’m just not lovable or desirable to anyone.
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious and sad, with a heavy weight of loneliness and fear of abandonment. Sometimes, I also feel ashamed and worthless, like there’s something fundamentally wrong with me.
ccd.behaviors: I withdraw and stop reaching out, hoping to avoid further rejection, or I become clingy and overly accommodating to try to fix the distance. This often leads to misunderstandings and makes the relationship feel more strained.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Uh, it’s been...okay, I guess. Nothing major happened, but I’ve just been feeling a bit off, I think. It’s hard to explain—like, small things keep bothering me but I don’t really know why. I guess I’m just tired, or maybe I’m overthinking things again.
When you notice those small things bothering you, what kinds of thoughts usually go through your mind?

Well, um… I guess it depends, but a lot of the time it feels like I’m kind of on edge, worrying about whether I’ve done something wrong. Like, if I text someone and they don’t reply for a while, I start thinking maybe I said something weird or maybe I’m just… bothering them. I try not to let it get to me, but my mind kind of spirals, I suppose. It’s like this nagging feeling that I’m just… not enough somehow.
That sounds really tough to carry around. When your mind starts to spiral like that, what do you usually do next—do you try to distract yourself, or do you stay with those feelings and thoughts?

Yeah, it is tough, honestly. Most of the time, I’ll try to distract myself—like, I’ll scroll on my phone or throw myself into work, just to not have to think about it so much. But other times, I can’t help but sit with it, and then I just end up feeling kind of stuck and alone, you know? It’s like I want to reach out but I’m also scared it’ll make things worse.
When you think about reaching out to someone, what’s the part that feels most scary or risky to you?

I guess… I’m afraid that if I reach out and they don’t respond, or they seem annoyed, it’ll just confirm the stuff I already worry about. Like, maybe they really don’t want to hear from me, or I’m bothering them. I worry a lot about pushing people away, so sometimes it feels safer just not to say anything at all. But then I end up feeling even more isolated, so it’s kind of a lose-lose.
That fear of confirmation sounds really painful. When you notice that worry about pushing people away, what do you tell yourself about your own worth or about relationships in those moments?

Um, I guess I end up telling myself things like, maybe I’m just not very likable, or that there’s something wrong with me that makes people keep their distance. Sometimes I wonder if I’m just too much, or not enough, and that’s why it feels so hard to connect. It’s embarrassing to even admit, but I sometimes feel like I’ll never really be close to anyone for long. I know it sounds dramatic, but in those moments, it just really feels that way.

Thank you for sharing that—it sounds really painful to feel that way about yourself. When you reflect on those thoughts about not being likable or being too much or not enough, is there any evidence you can think of that challenges or doesn’t fully support those beliefs?

Um… I mean, I guess I do have people in my life who care about me, like my partner and a couple friends. Sometimes they reach out or want to spend time with me, so I know, logically, I can’t be as awful as I tell myself. But even then, I end up doubting it—like maybe they’re just being polite or they don’t see the real me yet. It’s hard to really believe they’d want to stick around if they knew all of me.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like I didn’t quite fit in or measure up, especially at work where people seem more confident and liked. Past experiences of being overlooked or criticized have made me doubt myself a lot.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m not attractive or likable, then I’m not worthy of attention or respect from my coworkers. I have to hide parts of myself to avoid being judged or rejected.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to keep my head down and avoid speaking up in meetings. Sometimes I try to overwork myself to prove my value, but I often withdraw when I feel overwhelmed or invisible.
ccd.situation: At work, there’s a lot of pressure to perform and be part of the team. I noticed that others get praised and included more easily, which makes me feel left out and self-critical.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one wants to work with me because I’m not attractive or interesting.” “I’m going to mess up and everyone will see I don’t belong.” “I’m invisible here.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, lonely, and ashamed. There’s also a strong sense of sadness and sometimes frustration with myself.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid social interactions and keep quiet during group discussions. I work extra hard alone but don’t reach out for help or support.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work has felt a bit stressful, but that’s kind of normal for me. I’ve mostly just kept to myself and tried to get things done. Nothing really special happened.
When you say work has felt stressful, can you tell me more about what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you during those times?

I guess I just feel kind of on edge a lot. Like, when I’m in meetings or around people, I start worrying that I’m not doing enough or that people are judging me. Sometimes I feel sort of… out of place, like I don’t really fit in. It makes me want to just keep quiet and not draw attention to myself.
When you notice those worries about not doing enough or being judged, what do you usually tell yourself in those moments?

Honestly, I usually just think that I’m probably not as good as everyone else there. Sometimes I tell myself I should just keep quiet so I don’t make a fool of myself. It sort of feels safer that way. I don’t really argue with those thoughts—I just kind of accept them.
What do you think might happen if you challenged those thoughts or tried speaking up despite feeling that way?

I don’t know, I guess I worry that if I did speak up, I’d say something stupid or people would notice how awkward I am. Maybe they’d just ignore me, or worse, think less of me. It seems easier not to risk it. I’m not sure I’d handle it well if things went badly.
When you imagine those possible negative outcomes, how do they affect what you do in meetings or around others afterward?

They make me want to shrink back even more. I usually just stay quiet and hope no one calls on me. Sometimes I avoid making eye contact or try to look busy so I don’t get noticed. It just feels safer not to put myself out there.
What do you notice happens to your feelings or mood after you stay quiet and avoid attention like that?

Honestly, I usually end up feeling kind of down. There’s this sort of heaviness—like I’m missing out or not really part of things. Sometimes I even get frustrated with myself for not trying harder. But mostly, I just feel alone.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has a history of feeling judged and criticized by family members, which has reinforced a deep sense of being fundamentally flawed. Past experiences of guilt over mistakes have contributed to a belief that they are a bad person.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I am immoral, then others will reject me. I must hide my true self to avoid being seen as a bad person. Making mistakes means I’m a failure and unworthy of kindness.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid social situations where they might be scrutinized and often ruminates on perceived moral failings. They also try to overcompensate by being overly helpful to others to gain approval.
ccd.situation: The patient forgot to return a borrowed item to a friend on time and is now feeling stressed about their relationship.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m such a terrible person for being careless. If I don’t fix this, they’ll think I don’t care about them and will stop liking me."
ccd.emotions: Guilt, shame, anxiety, and sadness.
ccd.behaviors: The patient avoids calling the friend to apologize, spends a lot of time replaying the situation in their mind, and isolates themselves to avoid potential confrontation.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

It’s been alright, I guess. I’ve been kind of stressed about something that happened with a friend. I borrowed something from them and forgot to give it back when I said I would. It’s just been on my mind a lot.
What thoughts go through your mind when you think about the situation with your friend and the borrowed item?

I keep thinking I messed up and that I’m just careless. I worry that my friend probably thinks I don’t respect them. Honestly, it makes me feel like I’m not a good friend. I keep going over it in my head and just feel bad about it.
When you notice these thoughts about being careless and not a good friend, how do they affect what you do next or how you behave toward your friend?

I kind of avoid reaching out to them. I want to apologize, but I keep putting it off because I’m worried they’ll be upset with me. I just feel really guilty, so I end up not saying anything at all. I also catch myself pulling back from other people too.
When you imagine reaching out to apologize, what do you expect might happen, and how does that expectation influence your feelings and actions?

I guess I expect that they’ll be disappointed or annoyed with me. I worry they’ll think I don’t care about them or I’m just unreliable. That makes me anxious, so I keep delaying reaching out. I’m scared they might not want to be friends with me anymore.
Have there been times in the past when you worried about disappointing someone but reached out anyway—what happened then?

Yeah, there have been a few times. Usually, the person just says it’s okay or doesn’t make a big deal out of it. But even then, I can’t shake the feeling that they’re secretly upset with me or judging me. I keep replaying the whole thing afterwards, wondering if I said or did the wrong thing.
What do you notice about those repeated thoughts after reaching out—how do they impact your mood or your view of yourself?

They make me feel pretty down, honestly. Even if someone says everything’s fine, I still feel like I messed up or that I’m just not good enough. It’s like I can’t let go of the idea that I did something wrong. It makes it really hard to feel okay about myself.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt powerless in my family, where decisions were made without my input and my feelings were overlooked. Past relationships reinforced this sense of being controlled or taken advantage of, making me doubt my ability to influence outcomes. These experiences have left me feeling stuck and unable to stand up for myself.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to assert myself, I’ll just end up getting hurt or blamed. People will always take advantage of me because I’m weak and can’t protect myself. It’s safer to accept whatever happens rather than risk confrontation or rejection.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid difficult conversations or withdraw when conflicts arise, hoping the problem will resolve on its own. Sometimes I deflect or change the subject when someone brings up issues, steering away from uncomfortable topics. I also try to please others to prevent them from getting upset with me.
ccd.situation: My partner criticized me for not sharing my feelings about a problem in our relationship, and I found myself shutting down instead of responding. I felt overwhelmed and convinced that I had no control over fixing things between us.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “There’s no point in speaking up because nothing will change.” “If I say how I feel, they’ll just get angry or leave me.” “I’m powerless in this relationship, just like always.”
ccd.emotions: Helplessness, sadness, anxiety, and a quiet fear of abandonment or rejection.
ccd.behaviors: I withdrew emotionally and physically, avoiding further discussion. I stopped expressing my needs and tried to keep the peace by agreeing with my partner, even though I felt hurt inside.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um... it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been kind of busy, and my partner and I had this little disagreement, but it’s nothing major. Honestly, I’ve mostly just kept to myself after that—just trying to stay out of trouble, you know? Actually, speaking of work, my coworker brought in these weird muffins the other day, and I couldn’t tell if they were blueberry or something else—the color was kind of off.
That sounds like a mix of things going on. When you think about the disagreement with your partner, what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you?

Well... I don’t know, I try not to think about it too much because it just makes me feel sort of tense, I guess. I just keep wondering if I did something wrong or if I should have said more, but I didn’t really know how to start. Maybe I just wasn’t in the right mood for a deep talk—I get that way sometimes. Oh, and then my neighbor’s dog started barking like crazy that night, which didn’t help me sleep at all.

It sounds like you’ve been feeling tense and uncertain about how you handled the situation. When you notice those thoughts about whether you did something wrong, how do they affect what you do or how you feel afterward?

Yeah, I guess I just sort of shut down a bit—I go quiet and keep to myself. I start worrying that if I try to talk about it, I’ll just make things worse or get blamed. After that, I just feel kind of heavy, like there’s this cloud hanging over me. Actually, it reminds me of when I was a kid and I’d just go to my room and listen to music whenever things got tense at home.

That feeling of shutting down and keeping to yourself seems familiar and connected to past experiences. When you think back to those times as a kid listening to music, what was that like for you—did it help you feel better or just distract you?

Hmm… I think it was more of a distraction, honestly. I’d just put my headphones on and zone out—sometimes for hours—so I didn’t have to hear what was happening or think about it. I guess I’d feel a little better in the moment, but things didn’t really change after. It’s funny—I still do that sometimes, just sort of check out and hope things blow over.

Noticing that pattern of zoning out to avoid difficult feelings, what do you think usually happens when you check out—does it help you feel more in control, or does it sometimes leave you feeling stuck?

I mean, in the moment it feels like I’m keeping the peace, so I guess there’s a bit of relief. But honestly, afterwards I just feel kind of stuck, like nothing really gets solved and I’m just…waiting for the next thing to happen. It’s almost like I’m watching everything from the outside, not really part of it. Oh, and then I end up overthinking everything later, so it just keeps going around in my head.
That sense of watching things from the outside sounds difficult. When you find yourself overthinking later, what kinds of thoughts tend to come up most often?

Um… mostly stuff like, “Why didn’t I just say something?” or “Maybe it’s my fault things are awkward now.” I worry that I missed my chance to fix things, or that if I do try, it’ll just turn into an argument. Sometimes I wonder if people just get annoyed with me because I’m quiet or don’t stand up for myself. Oh, and I start replaying old conversations, trying to figure out what I should have said instead.

core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

ccd.relevant_history: I often felt rejected and blamed as a child, which made me believe I’m a harmful person. Growing up, I learned that if I wasn’t careful to please others, I might cause problems or be abandoned. These experiences left me feeling isolated and fearful of being truly seen.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t keep everyone happy, they’ll get upset with me or leave. I must hide my true feelings because showing them would prove I’m dangerous or bad. People will only accept me if I’m perfect and agreeable.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to say yes to everything and avoid conflict at all costs. I suppress my own needs and opinions to prevent others from getting mad. I constantly monitor how others react to make sure I’m not causing trouble.
ccd.situation: At a small gathering, someone gently disagrees with my opinion during a group conversation.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “Oh no, I’ve upset them. They probably think I’m a bad person for saying that.” “I should’ve just stayed quiet; I’m always causing problems.” “They might not want to be around me anymore.”
ccd.emotions: Anxious, ashamed, fearful of rejection, and tense.
ccd.behaviors: I quickly apologize and change the subject, smiling nervously and trying to lighten the mood. I avoid sharing my thoughts for the rest of the event and keep my body closed off to avoid drawing attention.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

messages.content: How has your week been so far? Are there any particular moments or experiences that stand out to you?

Um, I guess it’s been okay... nothing too major happened. I went to a friend’s get-together the other night, which was nice. There was a moment when I said something that maybe didn’t land so well, but it wasn’t a big deal. Otherwise, things have been pretty normal, I think.
When you noticed that your comment didn’t land well, what thoughts or feelings came up for you in that moment?

Oh, um… I just felt kind of awkward, I guess. I worried that maybe I said the wrong thing or made things uncomfortable for everyone. Honestly, I started thinking maybe I shouldn’t have spoken up at all. I tried to just move on and not make a fuss about it.
That sounds uncomfortable. When you thought you shouldn’t have spoken up, what did you imagine might happen if you had stayed quiet?

I guess… if I had stayed quiet, maybe nobody would’ve noticed me as much, and I wouldn’t have risked saying something wrong. I just feel like, sometimes, when I speak up, I mess things up or annoy people. If I’m quiet, at least I know I’m not causing any problems. I just really don’t want anyone to be upset with me.

It sounds like you’re trying to avoid upsetting others by staying quiet, but also worry about being noticed or making mistakes when you speak. How do these feelings affect the way you interact with people in other situations?

Yeah, I think that’s true. I usually just go along with what others want, even if I feel differently. I’m always sort of paying attention to everyone’s reactions, just to make sure I’m not saying or doing something wrong. I don’t really talk about my own opinions much—I guess I just don’t want to bother anyone.
When you hold back your opinions to avoid bothering others, how does that impact how you feel about yourself afterward?

Um… honestly, sometimes it feels safer, but other times I just feel kind of invisible, or like I don’t really matter. I end up replaying things in my head and wondering if I even have anything worth saying. It gets pretty lonely sometimes, but I’d rather that than risk upsetting someone. I guess I just don’t want people to think badly of me.
That sounds really tough, feeling both invisible and lonely while trying to keep the peace. When you replay those moments, what kinds of thoughts come up about yourself or your worth?

Yeah, it’s kind of exhausting. I find myself thinking that maybe there’s something wrong with me for always messing things up, even when I try hard not to. Sometimes I worry that people might see me as a problem, or just… not want me around. It makes me question if I’m really someone anyone would actually want to get to know.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve struggled with feeling like I’m not good enough for others since I was a teenager. Past experiences of rejection and criticism about my appearance have made me believe that people won’t like me because of how I look.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m not attractive, people won’t want to be around me or care about me. I have to look a certain way to be accepted or loved. If I’m rejected, it means there’s something wrong with me.
ccd.coping_strategies: I avoid social situations where I might be judged on my appearance. I spend a lot of time trying to improve how I look, like checking myself in the mirror or comparing myself to others online. Sometimes I withdraw or stay silent because I worry people will notice my flaws.
ccd.situation: I went to a small gathering with some new people and noticed they didn’t really pay attention to me or include me in the conversation.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one wants to talk to me because I’m ugly.” “They’re ignoring me because I don’t look good enough.” “I’m just not interesting or worthy of their time.”
ccd.emotions: I felt lonely, sad, and ashamed. There was also a sense of anxiety and self-doubt that made me want to disappear.
ccd.behaviors: I stayed quiet and avoided eye contact. I left the gathering early to avoid feeling more rejected. Afterward, I isolated myself and replayed the event in my head, feeling worthless.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It's been okay, I guess. I went to this small get-together with some people I don't know very well. It was kind of awkward. I just felt a bit out of place.
What was going through your mind when you felt out of place at the get-together?

I just kept thinking that no one really noticed me or wanted to talk to me. I felt like I probably didn't fit in with them. It made me kind of anxious. I tried to just blend in and not draw attention to myself.
When you noticed those thoughts about not fitting in and feeling unnoticed, how did your body feel in that moment?
I felt tense, like my shoulders were really tight. My stomach kind of hurt, and my heart was beating faster. I just wanted to leave. It's like I wanted to shrink away so no one would look at me.
What did you do next after feeling that tension and wanting to leave?

I mostly stayed quiet and didn’t really join in the conversation. After a while, I just said I had to go and left early. When I got home, I kept thinking about how awkward I felt there. I ended up just staying alone in my room for the rest of the night.
When you reflect back on the get-together now, is there any part of the situation or your experience that you think might have been different from what your initial thoughts suggested?

I don’t know, maybe. I mean, I guess it’s possible they were just caught up in their own conversations or didn’t know me yet. But in the moment, it really felt like I didn’t belong. Part of me wonders if I just imagined it being worse than it was, but it’s hard not to feel that way.
What do you think might happen if you tried to join in or start a conversation next time, instead of blending in or leaving early?

Honestly, I worry I’d just end up embarrassing myself or saying something stupid. I feel like people would just look at me weird, or maybe ignore me anyway. But I guess there’s a chance they might actually talk to me. I’m just really scared of being rejected or brushed off.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up in a family where conflicts were frequent and unresolved, I often felt powerless to change the situation. My parents rarely supported me emotionally, which left me feeling alone and vulnerable during difficult times.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to stand up for myself, I’ll only make things worse or be blamed. It’s safer to accept whatever happens because I don’t have the strength to change anything. Others will always have more control than I do.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid confronting family issues and keep my feelings to myself to prevent more conflict. Sometimes I withdraw completely or agree with others just to keep the peace, even if it makes me feel worse inside.
ccd.situation: During a recent family gathering, an argument broke out between relatives, and I felt unable to speak up or defend myself when I was unfairly criticized.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “There’s nothing I can do to stop this.” “I’m just a victim here, and no one will listen to me.” “If I say something, it will only get worse.”
ccd.emotions: Helplessness, sadness, and anxiety.
ccd.behaviors: I stayed quiet, avoided eye contact, and later isolated myself to avoid dealing with the tension. I also ruminated over the event instead of addressing my feelings directly.

messages.content: How has your week been so far? Are there any moments or experiences that stood out to you?

Um, it’s been kind of a rough week, I guess. There was this family thing over the weekend and I just… I don’t know, I didn’t feel great afterward. Nothing out of the ordinary really, just the usual stuff. I’ve just been thinking about it a lot since then.
When you think back to that family event, what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you?

I mostly just felt really tense and uncomfortable while I was there. When people started arguing, I just sort of wanted to disappear. I kept thinking, "Why does this always happen?" It left me feeling pretty drained and kind of sad afterward.
What do you think makes those arguments happen repeatedly, and how do you usually cope when you notice that tension building?

Honestly, I’m not really sure why it keeps happening. It just seems like nobody ever listens to each other, and things get out of hand fast. When I notice things getting tense, I usually just keep quiet and try to stay out of it. I guess I hope if I don’t get involved, it’ll blow over.
When you stay quiet and try to stay out of the conflict, how does that affect your feelings both in the moment and afterward?

In the moment, I feel kind of relieved because I'm not drawing attention to myself, but also really anxious and stuck. Afterward, though, I just feel bad—like I didn't say what I wanted to, and I keep replaying it in my head. It’s like I let myself down, but I also don’t know what else I could’ve done. I just end up feeling alone with it.
What do you imagine might happen if you were to express some of what you’re feeling or thinking during those moments, instead of staying quiet?

Honestly, I get worried it would just make things worse. I feel like people might dismiss what I’m saying or maybe even turn on me for speaking up. It’s hard to picture anyone actually listening or understanding. I guess I’m afraid I’d just end up feeling even more overwhelmed.
Have you had any past experiences where you spoke up and it went better or worse than you expected? What did you learn from those moments?

Hmm… there were a couple times I tried to say something, but I ended up feeling shut down or blamed for causing trouble. I can’t really think of a time when it went well, honestly. After that, I sort of stopped trying because it just felt pointless. Mostly, I learned it’s safer to keep things to myself.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient often feels overwhelmed by daily challenges and has a long-standing sense of powerlessness stemming from past experiences where they felt taken advantage of or ignored. They describe a pattern of feeling like things happen to them rather than being in control.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I try to assert myself, I’ll just get hurt or rejected." "No matter what I do, I won’t be able to change my situation." "People will always take advantage of me."
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid confrontation and difficult decisions, often withdrawing or distracting themselves to escape uncomfortable feelings. They may also downplay their needs and avoid asking for help, fearing further victimization.
ccd.situation: Facing a disagreement with a coworker about responsibilities, the patient feels overwhelmed and unsure how to speak up without causing conflict or being dismissed.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I'm just going to get blamed again." "It’s useless to say anything; they won’t listen." "I’m stuck in this no matter what I do."
ccd.emotions: Helplessness, frustration, anxiety, and sadness.
ccd.behaviors: Avoiding the conversation, staying silent despite feeling wronged, and later ruminating on the unfairness of the situation while feeling drained and defeated.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um, it’s been… I guess it’s been alright. Work’s been kind of busy, and I haven’t really had time to, you know, catch my breath. Actually, the other day I spilled coffee on my shirt right before a meeting, which was just… typical, I guess. Anyway, yeah—nothing too exciting.
When that coffee spill happened, what were some of the thoughts that went through your mind in that moment?

Well, honestly, my first thought was, “Of course this would happen to me,” which sounds silly, but it’s just… things like that always seem to happen at the worst time. I felt really embarrassed, like people would notice and think less of me. And then I kind of started worrying that I’d mess up during the meeting too. Actually, the rest of the day just felt off after that—like everything I did was just a little bit wrong.
It sounds like that moment brought up a lot of self-judgment and worry—what do you think made you feel that things always go wrong at the worst times?

I don’t know, it just… feels like that’s how things have always been for me. Like, whenever I really need something to go smoothly, it just falls apart somehow. It’s sort of been that way since I was a kid—even in school, if there was a group project, I’d end up with all the work, or if something went wrong, I’d get blamed. I guess I just kind of expect that stuff now, even if it’s just a spilled coffee.
When you notice that expectation coming up—that things will fall apart—how does it affect what you do or how you feel in those moments?

Uh, well, I usually just try to get through it without making it any worse. Sometimes I’ll just keep my head down, try not to attract attention, that kind of thing. It’s almost like, if I don’t say anything or just let things slide, maybe it won’t get worse. But then, I end up thinking about it all day anyway, and it just sits with me.
When you keep your head down and try to let things slide, what emotions or thoughts tend to stay with you afterward?

Mostly I just feel pretty drained, honestly. I get frustrated with myself for not saying or doing anything—and then kind of sad, too, like I’m always stuck in the same spot. Sometimes I replay things in my head and wonder if I could’ve handled it differently. But mostly it just leaves me feeling powerless, I guess.
That feeling of powerlessness sounds really tough—can you remember a time when you did speak up or handle a similar situation differently? What was that like?

Hmm. I mean, there was this time a while back when I tried to talk to a coworker about splitting up our work more fairly. I remember I was really nervous, and my hands were shaking a bit. I thought maybe if I just explained how I was feeling, things would change, but… honestly, I felt like I just got brushed off. Afterward, I kind of regretted even bringing it up.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced ongoing financial instability for several years, including job loss and mounting debt. They often felt unsupported by family during these times, reinforcing feelings of vulnerability and lack of control.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I can't manage my money well, it means I'm weak and incapable. Asking for help won't change anything because no one can really fix my problems. I have to handle everything on my own or things will get worse.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid looking at bills or financial statements, hoping problems will resolve themselves. They withdraw from friends and family to hide their struggles and sometimes overspend impulsively to momentarily escape stress.
ccd.situation: Facing mounting bills and an unexpected car repair, the patient worries about how they will cover the costs and fears falling behind even more.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I'm never going to get out of this mess." "I can't do anything right with money." "No one can help me, so why even try?"
ccd.emotions: Hopelessness, anxiety, shame, and frustration.
ccd.behaviors: Avoiding phone calls from creditors, skipping budgeting tasks, isolating themselves socially, and procrastinating on finding solutions.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

It’s been a tough week, honestly. I’ve been feeling kind of overwhelmed, especially with some bills piling up. I just keep worrying about how I’m supposed to handle everything. I guess I’m just tired.
When you notice yourself worrying about the bills, what kinds of thoughts usually go through your mind?

I just keep thinking I’m not going to be able to pay them all. It feels like no matter what I do, I can’t seem to get ahead. Sometimes I wonder if I’m just bad at managing money or if I’m always going to be stuck like this. It’s pretty discouraging.
What emotions come up for you when you think you might be bad at managing money or stuck in this situation?

Mostly I feel anxious, like there’s this knot in my stomach that won’t go away. There’s also a lot of shame—I don’t really like talking about it with anyone. Sometimes it just feels hopeless, like nothing I do will actually help. It’s pretty hard to shake that feeling.
When you notice that knot of anxiety or the feeling of hopelessness, what do you usually do next? How do you respond to those feelings?

Honestly, I usually just try not to think about it. I avoid looking at my bank account or the bills—I just push it aside and hope it’ll somehow get better. Sometimes I just distract myself, maybe watch TV or go online for a while. It helps for a bit, but the worry always comes back.
When the worry comes back after distracting yourself, what do you notice about your thoughts or feelings in that moment?

It usually hits even harder after I’ve been avoiding it. I start thinking I’m just making things worse by not dealing with it. There’s this sense of panic, like I’m running out of time or options. I end up feeling even more stuck and frustrated with myself.
That sounds really challenging. When you feel that panic and frustration, is there anything you say to yourself or any specific thoughts that stand out?

Yeah, I just keep thinking things like, “I’m never going to get out of this.” Sometimes I even tell myself I’m just screwing everything up. It’s almost like I convince myself that no one could actually help, even if I asked. I end up feeling like it’s all on me, and I’m not handling it well at all.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up in a family where decisions were often made without her input, she learned to feel powerless and doubted her ability to influence outcomes. Past experiences of being ignored or overridden during conflicts have reinforced her sense of helplessness.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to assert myself, I will lose control or make things worse. It’s safer to just go along with what others want because I can’t change the situation anyway.
ccd.coping_strategies: She tends to avoid confrontations and keeps her opinions to herself, hoping to prevent conflict. Sometimes she withdraws emotionally or becomes overly compliant to maintain peace, even if it means suppressing her own needs.
ccd.situation: During a family discussion about plans for the holidays, she feels overwhelmed as others dominate the conversation and decisions are made without her input.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I have no say in this. Nothing I do will change what happens. I’m completely out of control.”
ccd.emotions: Frustration, sadness, and anxiety as she feels powerless and disconnected from the family decision-making.
ccd.behaviors: She stays quiet, nods along without sharing her preferences, and later avoids further family interactions to escape feeling helpless.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Nothing too out of the ordinary. I’ve just been busy with work and some family stuff. I don’t know, I guess I’ve felt a bit tired.
When you say you’ve felt tired, what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you when you notice that tiredness?

Um… I guess sometimes it’s like a heaviness, mostly in my chest. I just feel worn out, especially after spending time around my family. It feels like I’m just going through the motions sometimes, and it’s hard to want to speak up or get involved. I end up feeling kind of frustrated with myself, honestly.
What do you tell yourself or think about when you notice that frustration with yourself?

I usually think I should be able to handle things better, or that maybe I’m just making a big deal out of nothing. Sometimes I wonder if something’s wrong with me for feeling this way. I just tell myself to push it aside and move on, because it’s easier than dealing with it.
When you try to push those feelings aside, what usually happens to your mood or energy afterward?

Honestly, I just end up feeling more drained. It's like the feelings don't really go away, they just sort of build up in the background. Sometimes I get kind of numb, or I end up avoiding people for a while. I wish it didn’t bother me so much.
What do you notice changes for you when you do avoid people—how does that affect how you feel or think about yourself?

When I keep to myself, it’s a bit of relief at first because I don’t have to pretend everything’s fine. But then I start to feel lonely and wonder if I’m just making things harder on myself. I end up thinking there’s something wrong with me for not being able to just join in like everyone else. It makes me question if I’m ever going to handle things differently.
What do you imagine might happen if you tried sharing some of these feelings with someone you trust?

I’m not really sure. Part of me thinks they probably wouldn’t get it, or maybe they’d just brush it off. I worry I’d say something wrong and make things awkward. I guess I’m afraid it might make things worse, not better.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve experienced a lot of rejection and neglect in my past relationships, especially during my childhood when I felt ignored by my parents. These experiences have made me expect that people I care about will eventually leave me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I get too close to someone, they will leave me. I’m not worthy of lasting love or attention. It’s safer to keep my distance so I don’t get hurt again.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid getting too involved with others or push people away before they can leave me. Sometimes I become overly clingy, hoping that if I’m needy enough, they won’t abandon me.
ccd.situation: I was texting a close friend and didn’t get a reply for a few hours, which made me start worrying about our friendship.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They must be tired of me and want to end our friendship. I’m going to be left alone again.”
ccd.emotions: I felt anxious, sad, and lonely.
ccd.behaviors: I avoided reaching out to anyone else and spent the evening feeling isolated, replaying the situation in my head and convincing myself I’m unlovable.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Nothing major happened. I’ve just been kind of tired and keeping to myself more than usual. I don’t know, it’s been a quiet week.
When you say you’ve been tired and keeping to yourself, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind in those moments?

I just feel kind of drained, like I don’t have the energy to talk to people. Sometimes I think maybe it’s easier if I just stay on my own. I worry that if I reach out, people might not really want to hear from me. So I end up just being quiet and not bothering anyone.
What do you notice happens inside you—thoughts, feelings, or physical sensations—when you consider reaching out to someone?

Honestly, I get this tight feeling in my chest and my stomach feels kind of unsettled. My mind starts running through all the reasons they might not want to talk to me. I feel kind of anxious, like I’m bothering them or they’ll just ignore me. It makes me hesitate and overthink whether I should even send a message.
When you catch those anxious thoughts about bothering others or being ignored, what do you usually tell yourself in response?

I usually just tell myself to leave it alone, that it’s better not to risk it. I convince myself they’re probably busy or don’t want to talk anyway. Sometimes I wonder if I’m just being needy or annoying. So I end up pulling back and not saying anything.
Can you think of a recent time when you reached out to someone despite those worries? How did it turn out?

Yeah, a few days ago I texted a close friend, even though I was nervous about it. They didn’t reply for a few hours, and I started feeling really anxious. I kept checking my phone and thinking I must’ve done something wrong. When they finally replied, they were just busy and acted totally normal, but I still felt kind of on edge after.
What do you think made it hard to believe their response was just normal and not a sign of rejection?

I guess I just always expect something bad to happen, like people pulling away. Even if they say everything’s fine, it’s hard for me to trust that it really is. Part of me keeps waiting for the other shoe to drop. I just can’t shake the feeling that I’m about to be pushed aside.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated feelings of rejection and neglect in past relationships, leading to deep-seated beliefs of being unworthy. They often recall moments where their needs were dismissed, reinforcing a sense of invisibility and low self-esteem.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "I am a burden to others," "People will leave me if they really know me," and "I am fundamentally flawed and unlovable." These thoughts act as rules or assumptions about themselves and relationships, shaping how they interpret social interactions.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to withdraw from close connections to avoid potential rejection, uses self-criticism to preempt others’ judgments, and sometimes distracts themselves with work or solitary activities to escape feelings of worthlessness.
ccd.situation: During a disagreement with their partner, the patient feels ignored and believes their feelings don’t matter, leading them to question their value in the relationship.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "They don’t care about me," "I’m just a waste of space," and "I don’t deserve to be happy or loved."
ccd.emotions: Feelings of sadness, hopelessness, and intense shame arise, accompanied by a sense of emptiness and despair.
ccd.behaviors: The patient withdraws emotionally and physically, avoids communication, and may engage in self-isolation or self-criticism, further straining the relationship.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, I guess it’s been okay. Work’s kept me busy, which is good, I suppose. There was this thing with my partner that kind of threw me off, but…I don’t know, maybe I’m overthinking it. Oh, and my neighbor’s dog keeps barking at like, 6 a.m., so that’s not helping my sleep either.
That sounds like a mix of things on your mind—what was it about the situation with your partner that felt off to you?

Yeah, it was just…we got into this disagreement, nothing major, but I tried to say how I was feeling and it just felt like I wasn’t really being heard. I sort of shut down after that. Sometimes I wonder if I’m just making things harder by bringing up stuff like that. Oh, and then I started thinking about this dumb argument I had with my mom years ago, which is weird, but it popped into my head.
When you noticed yourself shutting down after trying to express your feelings, what thoughts were going through your mind at that moment?

Honestly, I felt kind of stupid for even bringing it up, like maybe I was just being too sensitive or needy. I started thinking, “Why do I always mess things up?” and then I sort of drifted off in my head, just thinking about all the times I’ve said the wrong thing. I guess I also worried they were annoyed with me. I mean, who wants to deal with someone who can’t even have a simple conversation without getting all emotional, right?
It sounds like you were really critical of yourself in that moment—how did those thoughts and feelings affect what you did or how you behaved afterward?

Yeah, I guess I just felt embarrassed, so I kind of avoided them for the rest of the night. I went into the other room and just stared at my phone for a while, scrolling even though nothing really caught my interest. Actually, I ended up thinking about work stuff I needed to get done instead, just to distract myself. Um, I don’t know, it’s just easier not to talk when I feel like that.
When you chose to avoid your partner and distract yourself with work, how did that impact your feelings toward the disagreement or your connection with them afterward?

Well, I thought maybe if I just kept quiet, things would blow over, but honestly, I just felt more distant. I started worrying that I was being cold or that maybe they were mad at me now too. The next day I kept replaying what happened and felt kind of empty, like I’d messed things up again. It’s weird, because when I try to avoid conflict, I still end up feeling worse.
That sounds really tough—when you notice yourself replaying the situation and feeling empty afterward, what do you tell yourself about what that means for you or your relationship?

Um, I guess I start thinking that maybe I’m just not cut out for relationships, like there’s something wrong with me. Sometimes I tell myself that I’m just too much for people, or that I’ll end up pushing them away. I can’t help but wonder if they’d be happier with someone else who doesn’t make things complicated. Sorry, I know that sounds dramatic—it just kind of spirals in my head sometimes.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt rejected by friends and partners, which has made me believe I’m not someone others want to be around. Growing up, I rarely felt truly supported or loved, which deepened my sense of isolation.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I let people get too close, they’ll eventually leave or hurt me. Trying to connect deeply is risky because it usually ends in disappointment or loneliness. It’s safer to keep my distance than to face rejection again.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid opening up or sharing my feelings, often changing the subject when conversations get too personal. Sometimes I distract myself with work or hobbies to keep from feeling lonely. I also find myself withdrawing when I sense others pulling away.
ccd.situation: I was talking with a close friend who seemed a bit distant lately, and when I asked if everything was okay, they said they’d been busy and didn’t have much time to hang out.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: They don’t really want to spend time with me. I’m probably annoying or boring. This just proves I’m going to end up alone anyway.
ccd.emotions: Sadness, loneliness, and a creeping sense of hopelessness. I also feel anxious about reaching out again and fear being rejected.
ccd.behaviors: I stopped texting back as quickly and started avoiding making plans. I keep conversations light and steer away from anything meaningful to avoid feeling vulnerable. Sometimes I isolate myself for days.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been kind of busy, so I’ve just been trying to keep up with everything. I did see a movie last weekend, but honestly I don’t remember much of it—I sort of zoned out halfway through. I guess things feel kind of… the same as usual.
When you say you “zoned out” during the movie, what was going through your mind at that moment?

Oh, uh, I don’t know. My mind kind of drifted to this conversation I’d had with a friend earlier that day, so I wasn’t really paying attention to the movie anymore. Sometimes I do that—just start replaying stuff in my head instead of staying in the moment. Honestly, sometimes a movie or TV show just feels like background noise lately.
When you notice your mind drifting to replaying conversations, what kinds of thoughts or feelings tend to come up for you?

It’s usually stuff like, Did I say something weird? Or, maybe I came off as too much—or not enough. I start overanalyzing every little thing I said, like I’m looking for some clue that explains why things feel off. It’s kind of exhausting, honestly… sometimes I wish I could just let things go, but my brain keeps spinning.
What do you think might happen if you tried to let those thoughts go instead of replaying them?

I mean, I guess I worry that if I just let it go, I might miss something important—like maybe there’s a reason why people drift away and I’m just ignoring it. But at the same time, holding onto it doesn’t really help either; it just makes me feel tense and kind of alone. Uh, speaking of alone, my neighbor got this new puppy, and it barks nonstop at night—makes it impossible to sleep sometimes. Anyway, sorry, what were we talking about again?
It sounds like your mind is really busy and it’s hard to stay focused—how do you usually try to calm yourself when these worries or distractions come up?

Yeah, it’s like my brain just refuses to quiet down, especially at night. Sometimes I’ll watch YouTube videos or read random articles online to distract myself, or I’ll just organize stuff in my room—like, I spent an hour last night putting my books in color order, which is kind of silly. I guess I just try to keep busy so I don’t have to sit with all that noise. Oh, and sometimes I’ll message a friend, but then I immediately second-guess if I’m bothering them.
When you find yourself second-guessing reaching out to friends, what thoughts come up about how they might respond?

I just get this feeling like maybe they’re annoyed or they’ll think I’m being too needy. Sometimes I imagine them rolling their eyes at their phone when they see my name pop up, even though they’ve never actually said anything like that. It makes me want to just leave my phone alone and not say anything at all. Actually, I ended up baking banana bread yesterday instead—it turned out kind of weird because I didn’t have enough sugar, but it was something to do.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored and unappreciated by family and peers, which made me believe I didn’t matter. Over time, these feelings became a part of how I see myself, especially in social settings where I expect to be dismissed or overlooked.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I think if I try to engage with others, I’ll just end up annoying them or being rejected because I have nothing valuable to offer. People probably don’t want to spend time with someone like me since I’m just a burden.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid social gatherings or keep conversations very brief to minimize the chance of feeling rejected. Sometimes, I distract myself with activities alone or retreat into daydreams where I’m not judged.
ccd.situation: At a party, I try to join a group conversation but quickly feel like I’m interrupting or being ignored, which makes me want to leave or stay quiet.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They’re just pretending to listen because they feel sorry for me. I’m boring and nobody really cares what I say. I’m useless and wasting everyone’s time.”
ccd.emotions: I feel ashamed, anxious, and deeply sad. There’s also a heavy sense of loneliness and hopelessness that I’m never going to fit in.
ccd.behaviors: I withdraw from the group, avoid eye contact, and sometimes leave the party early to escape feeling so out of place. I don’t speak up again and often replay the negative experience in my head afterward.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um… I guess it’s been okay, just sort of the usual stuff. Work was kind of slow, and I didn’t really do much after, just watched some shows—oh, actually, I tried to make this new recipe I saw online, but it came out kind of weird. I think I added too much salt, or maybe it was the wrong kind of cheese? Anyway, I haven’t really talked to many people this week.
When you think about that cooking experience and how it turned out, what went through your mind?

Honestly, I just felt kind of stupid about messing it up, like, how hard is it to follow a recipe, right? It’s not a big deal, but I guess I started thinking, “Of course I’d screw it up, why would it go right?” Oh, and actually, it reminded me of this time in school when I tried to bake cookies for a class party and they turned out all burnt, and nobody really wanted them. It’s funny how those things just pop up again, even though it seems so random.
When those thoughts like “Of course I’d screw it up” come up, how do they affect how you feel in the moment?

Uh, I guess I just feel kind of small, like, it’s embarrassing even though there’s nobody there to see it. Sometimes I get this heavy feeling in my chest, like I just want to give up and do something else—watch a video or scroll on my phone so I don’t have to think about it. It’s weird because it’s just a recipe, but it feels bigger than that. Oh, and then I usually end up losing my appetite, so I might not even eat what I made.
What do you notice about what you tell yourself or how you explain the situation to yourself when you start feeling that heavy feeling in your chest?

Hmm… I guess I start telling myself stuff like, “Why did I even bother? No one would care anyway.” I sort of convince myself that it’s pointless to try, like there’s always going to be something wrong with what I do. Sometimes I’ll start thinking about other times things have gone badly, like these little mistakes just add up or something. Oh, and then I end up feeling kind of stupid for even getting upset over something so minor.
When you think about those messages you tell yourself, like “Why did I even bother?” or “It’s pointless to try,” what impact do they have on what you choose to do next?

Yeah, usually I just stop whatever I was doing. Like with the cooking, I put everything away and didn’t really clean up until later—just didn’t see the point. Sometimes I’ll just go hide in my room or start doing something mindless, like scrolling through my phone for way too long. Actually, that reminds me—last weekend, I was invited to a friend’s get-together, but after thinking about all the ways I could mess up or say something weird, I just made up an excuse not to go.
What do you imagine might happen if you went to that friend’s get-together despite those worries about messing up or saying something weird?

Honestly, I just picture myself standing around awkwardly, not really knowing what to say, and then probably saying something dumb or boring if I do try to join in. I kind of imagine people just smiling politely but not really wanting me there—like, maybe they’d look around for someone else to talk to or just nod until I stop talking. It’s sort of like I’d just be this extra person nobody knows what to do with. Oh, and then later I’d probably replay everything in my head and cringe about it for days.

ccd.relevant_history: I often feel like I don’t fit in or matter to others, especially during group events or casual conversations. Growing up, I was frequently overlooked or criticized, which made me believe I was a burden and not good enough.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to join in, people will just ignore me or think I’m annoying. It’s safer to stay quiet because I’m not interesting or worthy of attention. No one really wants me around, so I shouldn’t expect to be included.
ccd.coping_strategies: I usually avoid social gatherings or keep to myself when I’m around others. Sometimes I make excuses to leave early or don’t speak up at all to avoid feeling embarrassed or rejected.
ccd.situation: I’m at a small party with some coworkers, and they’re chatting and laughing together. I try to join the conversation, but I notice they don’t really respond to what I say.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m just wasting their time.” “They probably wish I’d stop talking.” “I don’t belong here.” “I’m invisible and useless.”
ccd.emotions: I feel sad, anxious, and ashamed. There’s a heavy sense of loneliness and hopelessness that I won’t ever be accepted.
ccd.behaviors: I withdraw from the group, stop contributing to the conversation, and eventually find a reason to leave the party early. I avoid similar social situations in the future to protect myself from feeling this way again.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Uh, it’s been alright, I guess. Work was busy, and I went to a thing with some coworkers. It was just a small party, nothing major. I don’t know, I felt a bit out of place.
What was going through your mind when you started to feel out of place at the party?

I just noticed everyone was talking and laughing, and I didn’t really know how to join in. When I tried to say something, I felt like no one really heard me or cared. It made me want to just stop talking. I kind of thought, maybe I’m not supposed to be there.
When you had the thought that maybe you weren’t supposed to be there, how did that affect how you felt in the moment?
I felt really anxious, like my chest was tight. It also made me feel pretty sad, honestly. I just wanted to disappear or go home. It was like, what’s the point of even trying?
When you noticed those feelings of anxiety and sadness, what did you do next? Did you stay and try to engage, or did you leave or withdraw?

I just kind of went quiet and stopped trying to join in. After a while, I made up an excuse to leave early. I felt embarrassed for even trying to talk in the first place. I guess I thought it was better to just get out of there.
What do you think would have happened if you had stayed a bit longer and kept trying to join the conversation?

Honestly, I feel like it would’ve just gotten more awkward. I’d probably end up saying something stupid or get ignored again. That always makes me feel worse. It just seems safer to leave before it gets to that point.
When you imagine saying something “stupid” or getting ignored, what does that mean to you about yourself?

I don’t know. It kind of feels like proof that I don’t really belong or that people don’t want me around. I start thinking maybe I’m just not interesting or good company. It makes me feel like I’m just taking up space.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated situations where they felt powerless in social settings, such as being excluded by peers or ignored during conversations. These experiences have reinforced a deep sense of being stuck and unable to change their social standing.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "I can’t influence what others think of me," "If I try to speak up, I’ll just make things worse," and "There’s no way out of this social trap."
ccd.coping_strategies: Avoiding social events, staying quiet during group interactions, and withdrawing from conversations to minimize feelings of embarrassment or rejection.
ccd.situation: Attending a small gathering where the patient feels overlooked and unable to join the ongoing conversations.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "No one wants me here," "I’m stuck being invisible," and "If I say something, I’ll just mess up."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and a sense of helplessness.
ccd.behaviors: Remaining silent, avoiding eye contact, and physically distancing themselves from the group.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Nothing much happened. I went to this get-together, but I didn’t really talk to anyone. I just kind of hung back and watched.
What were you thinking or feeling while you were hanging back and watching at the get-together?

I just felt kind of out of place. Like, everyone else was talking and laughing, and I didn’t really know how to join in. I kept thinking maybe they didn’t notice I was even there. It was a bit uncomfortable, to be honest.
When you noticed those thoughts about being out of place and unnoticed, what emotions came up for you in that moment?
I mostly felt anxious, like my chest was tight. There was this heaviness too, sort of like sadness, I guess. It made me want to just leave. I didn’t really want to stick around after that.
What did you usually do or think when you felt that anxiety and sadness during social situations like this?

Honestly, I usually just stay quiet and hope no one talks to me. Sometimes I find an excuse to go to the bathroom or step outside for a bit. I just try to avoid drawing attention to myself. It feels safer that way.
Have you noticed any particular thoughts that run through your mind when you try to avoid attention or leave the situation?

Yeah, I guess I usually think something like, “No one’s going to miss me if I leave.” Or, “If I try to say something, I’ll probably just make it awkward.” Sometimes I just tell myself it’s better to fade into the background. It feels like I can’t really change how things go anyway.
What do you think might happen if you did try to join in or say something, despite those thoughts?
Honestly, I feel like I’d just mess things up. Maybe I’d say something stupid or people would just ignore me anyway. I’m worried it would get even more awkward. I don’t see it going well.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

ccd.relevant_history: I've always struggled with feeling like I don't deserve good things, especially when it comes to money. Growing up, my family often argued about finances, and I was made to feel like a burden whenever I asked for help. This has stayed with me, making me anxious about money and relationships.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t have enough money, people will see me as worthless and reject me. Asking for help means I’m weak and unlovable. I have to handle my problems alone or risk being abandoned.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid talking about money or asking for support because I’m afraid of being judged or rejected. Sometimes I withdraw from friends and family or push people away to protect myself from getting hurt.
ccd.situation: I just found out I won’t get a bonus this year, and I’m worried I won’t be able to cover my bills or pay back a loan I took from a friend.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to mess everything up again.” “They’ll think I’m a failure and stop caring about me.” “I’m bound to be rejected if I can’t handle this.”
ccd.emotions: I feel scared, ashamed, and really upset. There’s a heavy weight of loneliness and hopelessness that makes it hard to see a way out.
ccd.behaviors: I shut down and avoid answering calls from my friend or family. I spend time alone worrying instead of asking for help or making a plan. I might even spend impulsively to numb the stress, which only makes things worse.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Honestly, not great. I found out at work that I’m not getting a bonus this year, and it just feels like everything is falling apart. I’m really stressed about money, but I don’t exactly want to talk about it. I don’t know, it just feels pointless.
When you think about not getting the bonus and feeling like everything is falling apart, what specific thoughts or images come to mind?

I just keep thinking I’m going to screw everything up. Like, I can picture myself not being able to pay my bills, and people just… giving up on me. My friend lent me money a while back and now I’m panicking about how I’ll pay them back. It feels like everyone’s going to be disappointed in me.
What do you notice in your body or emotions when those thoughts about disappointing others and struggling to pay bills come up?

I get this tight, heavy feeling in my chest—like I can’t breathe right. My stomach twists up, and I just feel… sick. It’s like this wave of shame and fear hits me all at once. Sometimes I just want to crawl into bed and not come out.
When you feel that heavy chest and the wave of shame and fear, what do you usually do next? How do you cope in those moments?

I mostly just shut down. I ignore my phone, especially if it’s my friend or family calling, because I don’t want to deal with it. Sometimes I end up just sitting alone, worrying and feeling stuck, or I’ll distract myself with pointless stuff online. Honestly, I know I should probably talk to someone, but I just can’t bring myself to do it.
What do you imagine might happen if you did reach out and talk to someone about how you’re feeling?

I don’t know… I just feel like they’d think less of me, or maybe get annoyed that I can’t sort things out on my own. I don’t want to be someone’s burden, you know? I worry they’ll just end up judging me or pulling away. It just feels safer to keep it to myself.
Have there been times in the past when you shared something difficult with someone and their reaction was different from what you feared?

I mean, maybe once or twice, but it’s hard to remember. Most of the time it just feels like people get awkward or don’t really know what to say, so I end up regretting bringing it up. Even if they try to help, I still feel like I’ve done something wrong by asking. I guess part of me expects things to go badly, even if they don’t every single time.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored or dismissed by my family, which made me feel like I didn’t matter or wasn’t good enough. Over time, these experiences have left me believing deep down that I am flawed and powerless to change how others see me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to express myself, I will only cause more problems or be rejected. It’s safer to stay quiet and avoid drawing attention to my shortcomings. No one really understands or values me, so I have to manage on my own.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to withdraw from family conversations or avoid sharing my feelings to prevent conflict. Sometimes I over-apologize or try to please others, even when it’s uncomfortable, just to keep the peace.
ccd.situation: During a family gathering, when a disagreement arose, I felt overwhelmed and stayed silent as others argued. I noticed people looking at me, but I didn’t speak up because I feared saying something wrong or being judged.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m useless and only make things worse if I speak up.” “They don’t want to hear what I have to say.” “I’m just a burden to everyone here.”
ccd.emotions: I felt anxious, sad, and ashamed. There was also a deep sense of loneliness and frustration that I couldn’t be heard or accepted by my family.
ccd.behaviors: I withdrew physically and emotionally, avoiding eye contact and staying quiet. Later, I isolated myself in my room and avoided future family events to protect myself from feeling this way again.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

I don’t know, it’s just been kind of exhausting. Honestly, I don’t even want to talk about it, because it feels like nothing ever really changes. I’m just tired of feeling like I’m always messing up somehow. What’s the point of going over it again?
It sounds really tough to feel stuck like that—can you tell me about a recent moment when you felt like you messed up?
