I mean, I guess there was this thing at my parents’ house. People started arguing, and I just… sat there. I didn’t say anything, even though maybe I should have, but I just froze. Afterwards, I couldn’t stop thinking that everyone probably thought I was useless or just in the way.
When you noticed yourself freezing in that moment, what were some of the thoughts running through your mind?

I just kept thinking, “Don’t say anything, you’ll only make it worse.” And then stuff like, “No one cares what you think anyway,” kept popping up. It’s like, no matter what I do, I’ll end up looking stupid or annoying. I really hate feeling like that, but I couldn’t stop it.
That sounds really painful. When those thoughts like "No one cares what you think" come up, how do they affect the way you feel physically or emotionally in that moment?

Honestly, it just makes me want to disappear. My chest gets tight, and I feel kind of sick, like I can’t breathe right. I get this wave of shame, and I just want to hide or run away. It’s like I don’t even belong there with them.
I’m really sorry you feel that way. When you imagine a situation where you could express yourself differently in those moments, what kind of response or outcome do you hope for?

I don’t know, I guess I just wish someone would actually listen for once—like, not roll their eyes or dismiss what I’m saying. Maybe just… I don’t know, actually care about what I think. Sometimes I imagine saying something and people actually respecting it, but that just seems impossible. I honestly doubt it would ever happen, so I don’t even bother anymore.
It sounds like feeling heard and respected means a lot to you. What do you notice happening inside you when you start thinking that it’s impossible for others to care or listen?
I just get kind of numb, honestly. Like, what’s the point in trying? It just feels hopeless and lonely, like I’m completely on my own. I end up shutting down and not wanting to deal with anyone.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like no matter what I do, I can’t really fix things in my relationships. Growing up, I was criticized a lot and left feeling like I wasn’t good enough, which stuck with me into adulthood. This makes me doubt myself whenever conflicts arise with people I care about.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I show my true self, people will see I’m flawed and reject me. I have to hide my mistakes or I’ll be abandoned. I’m powerless to change how others feel or behave toward me.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid deep conversations or conflicts because I’m afraid I’ll mess up and push people away. Sometimes I distract myself or jump onto unrelated topics to avoid dealing with uncomfortable feelings. I also try to please others excessively to keep them close.
ccd.situation: My partner seemed distant after I brought up a problem, and I wasn’t sure if they were upset with me or just busy.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They’re pulling away because I’m not good enough.” “I must have done something wrong, I’m defective.” “If I say too much, I’ll just make things worse.”
ccd.emotions: Anxious, sad, and helpless, feeling like I have no control over the situation or how my partner feels about me.
ccd.behaviors: I withdrew and stopped trying to communicate openly, instead changing the subject or avoiding the topic altogether. I became quieter and less engaged, hoping to not rock the boat.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been kind of up and down, I guess. Work has been busy, but that’s nothing new. I did have this weird moment with my partner the other day, but maybe it’s nothing—I mean, people get busy, right? Oh, and my cat knocked over a plant again, so I spent like twenty minutes cleaning up dirt instead of doing what I was supposed to.
When you think about that moment with your partner, what thoughts went through your mind at the time?

Uh… well, at first I just figured maybe they were tired or something, but then I started wondering if maybe I’d said something wrong earlier. It’s kind of silly, but my mind just keeps going back to little things I might’ve done or said. Oh, actually, speaking of little things, I remembered this story my friend told me about forgetting their anniversary, which made me start thinking maybe I forgot something important too. I don’t know, my brain kind of spirals sometimes.
When your mind starts to spiral like that, how do you usually feel emotionally in those moments?

Honestly, I get pretty anxious, and sometimes there’s this heavy feeling in my stomach—like dread, almost. It’s kind of like waiting for something bad to happen, you know? But, uh, I usually just try to distract myself before it gets too overwhelming—I’ll scroll through my phone or go organize something random, like, I literally cleaned out a drawer full of old receipts last time that happened. Speaking of which, I found a movie ticket from years ago, and it made me think about how much simpler things felt back then.
That heavy feeling sounds really uncomfortable—when you distract yourself, like organizing or scrolling, does it help ease that anxiety, or does the feeling tend to come back afterward?

It helps for a little while, like it gives me something else to focus on, but it doesn’t really last. The uncomfortable feeling usually creeps back once I stop what I’m doing—kind of like it’s waiting for me to finish. I guess sometimes I end up bouncing from one thing to another just to avoid thinking about it. Oh, which reminds me, I tried out this new recipe the other night—ended up burning the rice, but at least it kept me busy for a bit!
It sounds like staying busy helps, but the anxious feelings return when things quiet down; when you do start to notice those feelings coming back, what do you usually tell yourself about them?

Um… I guess I tell myself that I’m probably just overreacting, or that I shouldn’t be feeling this way. Sometimes I think maybe I did something to deserve it, like maybe if I’d handled things better, I wouldn’t feel so uneasy. I sort of try to convince myself it’ll just go away if I ignore it long enough. Oh, actually, that reminds me—my mom used to tell me not to dwell on things, just keep moving, so maybe that stuck with me more than I thought.
When you tell yourself you’re overreacting or that you shouldn’t feel this way, how does that affect your anxiety or the heavy feeling in your stomach?

I think, um, it makes me feel kind of stuck, honestly. Like, I still feel anxious but now I’m also annoyed at myself for not being able to just, you know, shake it off. Sometimes it even makes me feel worse, because I start thinking I’m being too sensitive or dramatic. Oh, and then I end up getting really quiet around people, just so I don’t accidentally say something weird.

ccd.relevant_history: I have often felt stuck in my life, like no matter how hard I try, I can't seem to move forward or improve my situation. Past experiences where I faced repeated setbacks, such as struggling at work and in relationships, have reinforced this feeling of being trapped and unable to change things for the better.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t have control or ways to improve my circumstances, then I’m doomed to stay stuck forever. I must accept that I’m powerless in many areas of my life, and trying to change things is pointless because nothing will ever really get better.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid making decisions or taking risks because I fear failure and the disappointment that comes with it. Sometimes I distract myself with TV or social media to escape the feeling of being trapped, but this only provides temporary relief and leaves me feeling more stuck afterward.
ccd.situation: I’ve been offered a chance to apply for a new job that could lead to growth, but I feel overwhelmed and doubtful about whether I can succeed or if it’s even worth the effort.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “What’s the point in trying? I’m just going to mess it up like I always do.” “I’m trapped in this dead-end routine and there’s no way out for me.” “If I fail, it will just prove I’m helpless and can’t change anything.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, hopeless, and frustrated. There’s a deep sadness that comes from believing I have no control over my future, mixed with a sense of fear about taking any steps forward.
ccd.behaviors: I procrastinate and avoid applying for the job, spending more time on passive activities instead. I withdraw from friends and family because I don’t want to talk about my feelings or face their questions about my future.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um… I guess it’s been kind of the same as usual. Nothing really exciting happened, to be honest. I mostly just went to work, came home, and sort of zoned out in front of the TV a lot. I don’t know, I just haven’t really felt motivated to do much lately.
When you say you haven’t felt motivated, can you tell me more about what goes through your mind when you think about doing things or making plans?

Yeah, it’s like… I’ll think about doing something—like, even simple stuff, like replying to a text or making dinner—and I just kind of stop myself before I even start. I get these thoughts like, “What’s the point?” or “It’s just going to be the same as always.” I start imagining everything going wrong or just not working out, so then I just don’t bother. It’s sort of like I already feel tired before I even try.
When you notice those thoughts like “What’s the point?” or imagining things going wrong, how do those thoughts affect how you feel in that moment?

Honestly, it kind of drags me down right away. I start feeling this heaviness in my chest, like a mix of dread and sadness. It’s hard to describe, but sometimes I even feel a little panicky, like my heart beats faster and I just want to hide. I usually end up feeling pretty hopeless and frustrated with myself.
When you feel that heaviness and panic, what do you usually do next? How do you respond to those feelings in that moment?

Most of the time, I just try to distract myself—I'll turn on the TV or scroll through my phone for way longer than I should. Sometimes I just sit there, staring at nothing, kind of zoned out. I avoid talking to people or doing anything that might remind me of what I’m trying to escape. It feels easier to numb out than to actually deal with those feelings, even though I know it doesn’t really help in the long run.
What do you think might happen if you allowed yourself to sit with those feelings instead of distracting yourself—what comes up for you when you imagine that?

Honestly, it makes me really uncomfortable just thinking about it. I feel like if I let myself actually sit with all those emotions, it would be too much, like I might just break down or something. I worry that I wouldn’t be able to handle it, and I’d just end up feeling even worse. There’s this fear that if I actually face it, I’ll realize I really am stuck and nothing’s going to change.
That fear sounds really intense—what do you notice about what you tell yourself when you start thinking you might break down or be stuck?

Yeah, I guess I start saying things to myself like, “You’re just making things harder for yourself,” or, “Other people handle this kind of stuff, why can’t you?” There’s this running commentary in my head that tells me I should just accept things the way they are, because getting my hopes up will only end in disappointment. Sometimes I catch myself thinking that maybe I’m just not strong enough, or I’m missing something everyone else seems to have. It’s a pretty constant loop, honestly.

core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s… whatever, I guess. Nothing really new. Just the same old stuff with my family, you know? Honestly, I don’t even know why I bother trying anymore.
Can you tell me more about what’s been happening with your family that makes you feel like you don’t want to keep trying?

I mean, every time I say something, it feels like no one actually cares or listens. If I try to talk about myself, they just ignore it or act like I’m causing problems. It’s like… why even speak up if it just makes things awkward? I’m probably better off just keeping quiet.
When you decide to keep quiet, what thoughts go through your mind about yourself and your family?

Honestly, I just feel like I must be the problem. It’s like, maybe if I just disappear or stay out of the way, things would be better for everyone. Sometimes I tell myself they’d all be happier if I just wasn’t around as much. I hate that I even think that, but I can’t help it.
That sounds really painful to feel that way. When those thoughts come up about disappearing or being better off not around, what emotions do you notice in your body or mind?

I mean, it just feels heavy, like this sinking feeling in my chest. I get kind of anxious, and sometimes I’m just angry at myself for even being there. There’s this tightness, like I’m bracing for something bad to happen every time I’m with them. I mostly just want to hide, honestly.
What do you usually do or how do you cope when you notice that sinking feeling or tightness starting to build?

I usually just shut down and stop talking. Sometimes I’ll leave the room or go outside, just to get away from everyone. It’s easier to just make myself invisible than to deal with whatever I’m feeling. If I stay, I might end up snapping or saying something I regret, so I just try to disappear.
When you look back at those moments you’ve made yourself invisible or stepped away, do you notice any short-term or longer-term effects on how you feel or how your family responds?

I mean, in the moment, it sort of feels safer, like I’m not making things worse. But later, I just end up feeling even more alone. No one really checks on me or seems to care that I left—if they notice at all. It just kind of confirms that I don’t really matter to them.

core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

ccd.relevant_history: I often felt criticized and rejected growing up, which made me believe that I’m fundamentally bad and that people don’t really want me around. Over time, I started expecting others to see me as dangerous or toxic, so I avoid getting too close to anyone.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If people get to know the “real me,” they’ll see how harmful or evil I am and push me away. I have to keep my distance or hide parts of myself to avoid hurting others or being rejected.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to change the subject quickly or go off on tangents when conversations get personal because I’m afraid of revealing too much. I also withdraw from social situations or stay silent to avoid potential conflict or rejection.

ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They’re going to see how messed up I really am if I answer honestly. I’m probably going to say something wrong or offensive and make everyone uncomfortable.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety spikes with a mix of shame and fear of being judged or rejected. I feel isolated and empty, like I don’t belong here.
ccd.behaviors: I quickly change the subject or make a joke to distract everyone. Sometimes I just clam up and avoid eye contact, hoping the spotlight moves away from me.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, uh, my week’s been alright, I guess. Work’s just been the usual—nothing exciting. Actually, did you hear about the weather this weekend? They’re saying there might be thunderstorms, which reminds me of when I was a kid and the power went out for two days. Anyway, I’ve mostly just been keeping busy.
When you think about the upcoming storm, what thoughts or feelings come up for you?

Hmm, well, storms always make me a little uneasy, I guess. Not really because of the weather itself, but more like… I don’t know, it just reminds me of feeling kind of powerless or small. When I was a kid, everyone else seemed way calmer about it, but I’d always sort of freeze up inside. Oh, and I remember my neighbor’s dog would bark nonstop every time it thundered, which was actually kind of funny.
What do you notice happening inside you physically or emotionally when you start to feel that sense of being powerless during storms?

Uh, well, I guess I get kind of tense? Like, my shoulders go up and I get this weird knot in my stomach. It’s not just storms, though—it happens other times too, but I can’t really explain why. Oh! Sometimes my hands get cold, which is odd because I don’t even notice until later.
When you notice those physical signs like tension or a knot in your stomach, what do you usually tell yourself about what’s happening or what might happen next?

Um, honestly, I usually just hope nobody notices. I try to act normal, or change the topic if someone asks if I’m okay. I kind of tell myself that if I say the wrong thing or react the wrong way, people will think I’m weird or… I don’t know, maybe even dangerous. Speaking of acting, did you ever do drama club in school? I always thought about it but chickened out.
It sounds like there’s a worry about how others might judge your reactions—what do you imagine might happen if someone did notice or asked about how you’re feeling?

Yeah, I guess I worry they’d look at me differently, like maybe they’d think I’m not trustworthy or I’m just…too much to handle. Or they might just get uncomfortable and avoid me, which has kind of happened before. Not that it’s always obvious, but sometimes people just get quiet or change the subject themselves. Oh, that reminds me—one time in high school, I freaked out during a group project and everyone just kind of ignored me after that.
When you recall that group project and how people responded, what thoughts went through your mind about yourself or the situation at that moment?

I remember thinking, like, “Here we go again, I messed up and now they all see what I’m really like.” I felt like I’d just confirmed all the worst things people could think about me. Mostly I just wanted to disappear, or at least make myself invisible somehow. Oh, and I started doodling in my notebook because it was easier than sitting there thinking about it.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

They often felt criticized or ignored by authority figures growing up, reinforcing their belief that others will reject them.

ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

messages.content: How has your week been so far? What kinds of experiences or feelings have stood out to you?

Uh, it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been kind of stressful—there’s a lot going on and a lot of pressure to get things right. I’ve mostly just kept my head down and tried not to mess up. I don’t know, sometimes I just feel tired.
When you notice that feeling of tiredness at work, what thoughts tend to go through your mind about yourself or the situation?

I just sort of tell myself I can’t afford to screw up. Like, if I relax or let something slip, people will definitely notice and probably get annoyed with me. Sometimes I think everyone’s waiting for me to fail. It makes me just want to keep quiet and not draw attention.
That sounds really stressful. When you think that others are waiting for you to fail, how does that affect the way you act or feel during the workday?

Yeah, it makes me pretty anxious, honestly. I end up double-checking everything over and over, and I barely talk to anyone unless I have to. I try to stay out of the way so I don’t give anyone a reason to complain about me. By the end of the day, I just feel drained.
It sounds exhausting to be so on edge all day. Have there been times when your worries about others’ reactions didn’t come true, or when you felt a little more at ease at work?

Um, I guess there have been a few times when things were okay. Like, if I finished something and didn’t get any negative feedback, I might feel a bit relieved. But even then, I’m kind of waiting for something to go wrong, or for someone to say I missed something important. It’s hard for me to actually feel relaxed, even when things seem fine.
When you find yourself waiting for something to go wrong, what do you notice happening inside—thoughts, feelings, or physical sensations—that signal you’re starting to feel that way?

Usually my chest feels tight, and I just get this knot in my stomach. My mind starts racing with all the things that could go badly. I catch myself thinking stuff like, “What if I missed something and they call me out on it?” I just feel tense, like I can’t let my guard down.

That tension sounds really uncomfortable. When those thoughts like “What if I missed something?” come up, how do you usually respond to them—do you try to push them away, or do you let yourself think them through?

I mostly just get stuck on them, honestly. I go over my work again and again, trying to make sure there aren’t any mistakes. I don’t really talk to anyone about it—I just keep it to myself. I guess I hope if I’m careful enough, maybe nothing bad will happen.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored and criticized by my family. I rarely received positive attention or affection, which made me believe that no one truly cared about me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I show my true self, people will reject me. I have to be perfect to be accepted. Nobody will stick around if they see my flaws.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to avoid sharing my feelings or asking for help. I keep people at a distance to protect myself from being hurt. Sometimes I overwork myself to gain approval.
ccd.situation: During a family gathering, I noticed that my relatives were chatting without including me. I felt invisible and disconnected from everyone.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: They don’t want me here. I’m not interesting or important enough to be part of the group. I’m always going to be left out.
ccd.emotions: Sadness, loneliness, and anxiety about being unwanted.
ccd.behaviors: I withdrew from the conversation and stayed quiet. I avoided making eye contact and eventually left the room early.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Nothing special. I went to a family thing this weekend, but I didn’t stay very long. Just felt kind of out of place, to be honest.
What kinds of thoughts were going through your mind while you were at the family event that made you feel out of place?

I kept noticing everyone talking and laughing together, but I didn’t really feel like part of it. I just sort of watched from the side. I started thinking maybe they didn’t really want me there, like I was just extra. It made me feel pretty uncomfortable.
When you noticed those feelings of being “extra” or not wanted, how did you respond to yourself in that moment?

I just kind of shut down. I didn’t really try to join in or say anything. I kept quiet and avoided eye contact. After a while, I left the room because it felt easier than trying to pretend I was fine.
What do you think might have happened if you had tried to join the conversation or interact with others, even just a little?

Honestly, I feel like it would’ve just been awkward. I worry they’d notice I was trying too hard or maybe just ignore me. I guess I’m afraid I’d say something stupid or people would look at me like I didn’t belong. That’s why I usually just stay quiet.
When those worries about saying something “stupid” or not belonging come up, what evidence do you have that supports or challenges those thoughts?

Well, I guess the main thing that supports it is just how people act. Like, they don’t really look my way or ask me questions, so I assume they’re not interested. But at the same time, I don’t really give them much of a chance since I barely talk. I’m not sure if that means I’m right or if I’m just assuming the worst.
That’s a thoughtful observation—how do you think your quietness might influence how others respond to you in those situations?

I think maybe if I’m quiet, people might think I don’t want to talk. Or they might just forget I’m there if I don’t say much. Sometimes I wonder if I’m making it harder for people to include me without realizing it. But it’s tough to break out of it because I’m worried about being rejected.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt like I couldn’t keep up with my siblings or friends, and I was frequently told I wasn’t good enough. In relationships, I’ve noticed a pattern where I assume I’ll mess things up or be a burden to others, which has made me hesitant to fully open up or trust. These experiences have reinforced my feeling that I’m not capable or competent.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I believe that if I don’t do everything perfectly, people will be disappointed or leave me. I also think that asking for help means I’m weak or inadequate, so I try to handle things on my own even when I’m struggling. This makes me feel like I have to prove my worth constantly, or else I’m failing.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid expressing my true feelings or needs because I worry it will confirm others’ negative views of me. Sometimes I overwork myself to appear competent, but other times I just shut down or withdraw when I feel overwhelmed. I also catch myself overthinking and doubting my abilities, which keeps me stuck.
ccd.situation: My partner asked me to help plan a weekend trip together, and I immediately felt overwhelmed and unsure if I could do it well. I started worrying that I’d pick the wrong places or mess up the details, thinking this would make them disappointed in me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to mess this up like I always do.” “They’ll think I’m useless if I can’t handle this.” “Maybe I should just let them do it so I don’t cause problems.”
ccd.emotions: I felt anxious and ashamed, with a heavy sense of dread that I was failing. There was also a deep sadness and frustration that I couldn’t seem to be good enough in their eyes.
ccd.behaviors: I hesitated to offer ideas and kept quiet during planning, avoiding taking responsibility. Later, I withdrew and didn’t share my feelings, which created distance between us and made me feel even more isolated.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um, it’s been kind of all over the place, honestly. Work was really busy, and then my partner mentioned planning a weekend trip, which… I don’t know, I guess that threw me off more than I expected. Actually, that reminds me—have you ever tried to juggle like, five calendars at once? It feels impossible, and somehow I always pick the wrong date for everything. Anyway, I guess I’m just tired.
When you think about juggling all those calendars and planning the trip, what thoughts come up for you?

Well, I guess… I just start worrying that I’ll mess something up or forget something important, and then everyone will be annoyed with me. Honestly, I almost didn’t even want to say yes to helping, because what if I pick a place no one likes? It’s like, I want to be helpful, but sometimes I wonder if I just make things harder. Oh, actually, that reminds me—last year I tried planning a dinner for some friends, and I picked a place that ended up being closed for renovations, and I still think about how I should’ve checked more carefully.
That sounds like a lot of pressure to put on yourself—how do you usually respond or cope when you start having those worries about messing things up?

Yeah, it’s… it gets pretty overwhelming sometimes. Usually, I just kind of freeze up and try to keep my ideas to myself, so I don’t accidentally make things worse. Or I’ll end up overthinking every tiny detail, which is honestly exhausting—and sometimes I just give up and hope someone else will take over. Oh, and then I get stuck replaying everything in my head, like even silly things from years ago, which doesn’t help at all.
When you notice yourself freezing up or overthinking, what feelings tend to come up in your body or mind in those moments?

I mostly just feel really tense, like my shoulders get all tight and I can’t seem to relax. My stomach gets all knotted up, too, and it’s hard to focus on anything else. In my head, it’s like this heavy, sinking feeling—kind of embarrassed, I guess, and frustrated with myself. Oh, that actually reminds me of when I was a kid and I’d get called on in class—I’d just freeze and hope the teacher would move on.
That’s a vivid connection—how do you think those early experiences of freezing and feeling embarrassed might relate to how you handle these situations now?

Hmm, I hadn’t really thought about it like that, but maybe it’s kind of the same feeling. Back then, I was always scared I’d say something wrong and everyone would notice, so I just kept quiet. I guess that carried over, because now I still worry about messing up in front of people, especially people I care about. Oh, and sometimes I wonder if that’s why I have a hard time asking for help, too—like I’m supposed to already know what I’m doing.
That makes a lot of sense. When you notice that worry about messing up or feeling like you should already know what to do, what do you tell yourself in those moments?

Um… usually it’s something like, “Why can’t you just figure this out? Other people don’t struggle like this.” Or I’ll think, “If you mess up, they’ll probably be disappointed or wish they hadn’t asked you.” I guess I put a lot of pressure on myself to not let anyone down. Oh, and sometimes I just tell myself it’s better to stay quiet, because then at least I won’t make things worse.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt neglected by my parents, who were emotionally distant and inconsistent with their support. There were frequent arguments and periods when I felt completely ignored, which made me feel isolated and unsure if I was truly loved or valued.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I get close to people, they will eventually leave me. I have to be careful about trusting others because they won’t stick around. Showing my true feelings might push people away, so it’s safer to keep my guard up.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to withdraw from family gatherings or keep conversations very surface-level to avoid potential rejection. Sometimes, I overcompensate by trying too hard to please others, hoping they won’t abandon me, even when it makes me feel exhausted or resentful.
ccd.situation: During a recent family dinner, I noticed my sibling didn’t include me in a conversation, and my parents seemed distracted and uninterested in what I was saying.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They don’t care about me. I’m always going to be left out and alone. Maybe they’re tired of me and want me gone.”
ccd.emotions: I felt hurt, rejected, and anxious, like I don’t belong and will be abandoned again.
ccd.behaviors: I withdrew from the conversation, stopped sharing my thoughts, and left the room early to avoid feeling more hurt.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

I don’t know, it’s been... whatever, I guess. Not great. I had to go to this family dinner thing and honestly, I wish I hadn’t bothered. Everyone just did their own thing and I felt like I could’ve disappeared and nobody would notice.
When you felt like you could’ve disappeared and nobody would notice, what thoughts were going through your mind in that moment?
I don't know, honestly. Maybe just that... what’s the point? Like, if no one really cares if I’m there or not, why even try? It just made me feel stupid for hoping it would be different this time.
That sounds really tough. When you notice those thoughts about things not mattering and feeling stupid for hoping, how does your body feel or what emotions come up for you?

I just feel this tightness in my chest, like it’s hard to breathe for a minute. I get kind of angry, too, but mostly I just feel... empty, I guess. Like, sad and sort of embarrassed for even caring. Sometimes I just want to get out of there as fast as I can.
When you experience that tightness, anger, and sadness during these moments, what do you usually do or how do you respond to those feelings?

I usually just shut down. I stop talking and kind of zone out, or I make an excuse to leave the room. It’s just easier to not deal with anyone at that point. I don’t really see the point in sticking around if I’m just going to feel worse.
What do you think might happen if you stayed present in those situations instead of shutting down or leaving?

Honestly? I think I’d just feel even more ignored or unwanted. It’s like, if I stay, I’m just sitting there feeling awkward and invisible, and that just sucks. I don’t really see how staying would change anything. If anything, it just drags it out longer.
That makes a lot of sense. Can you recall a time when you did stay in a similar situation—what was different about that experience, if anything?

I mean, I’ve tried before to stay and just push through it. But usually, it ends up the same—me sitting there, barely saying anything, hoping someone will notice or include me, but they don’t. Maybe someone asks me something random, but it always feels forced or just out of politeness. Honestly, it just makes me feel more awkward and like I don’t fit in.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

ccd.relevant_history: I often felt ignored or dismissed by people I cared about growing up, which made me believe that I wasn’t worth their attention or love. Past relationships have ended with my partners distancing themselves, reinforcing my fear of being rejected.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to be myself, people will see my flaws and leave me. I have to keep others happy or they won’t want to stay close to me. It’s safer to avoid conflict and just please others rather than risk being abandoned.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try really hard to make others like me by agreeing with everything they say and putting their needs before my own. I avoid expressing my true feelings or opinions because I worry it might push people away.
ccd.situation: I’m talking to someone I care about, and I notice they seem distracted and less engaged than usual during our conversation.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: They must be losing interest in me. I’m probably annoying them or not good enough. I bet they’re thinking about ending things or pulling away.
ccd.emotions: Anxious, sad, and insecure. I feel worried that I’m not lovable and scared of being rejected again.
ccd.behaviors: I become overly accommodating, agreeing with everything they say and trying to cheer them up. I avoid bringing up my own feelings or concerns to keep the peace and stay close to them.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting, just the usual stuff at work and at home. I’ve been trying to keep busy and stay positive. Thanks for asking!
When you say you’re trying to stay positive, what kinds of thoughts or things do you tell yourself to help with that?

Oh, um… I just try to remind myself that things aren’t that bad and that I shouldn’t complain. I tell myself to focus on what’s going well, like having a job and friends, even if sometimes I feel a little off. I try not to dwell on anything negative because I don’t want to make things awkward for anyone. I guess I just try to go with the flow and not be a burden.
That sounds like a thoughtful way to cope. When you say you sometimes feel a little off, can you describe what that feels like for you?

Yeah, um… I don’t know, it’s kind of like this feeling that I’m not really connecting with people the way I want to. Sometimes I worry that I’m saying the wrong thing or that I’m just not interesting enough. I notice I get a bit anxious in those moments, but I try to hide it. I don’t want to seem needy or anything.
When you notice that anxiety and those worries about connecting, what do you usually do or think in response?

I usually just try to be extra nice, you know? Like, I’ll listen more, agree with what the other person is saying, and maybe even try to make them laugh or feel better. I try not to talk about what’s bothering me, because I’m afraid it might push them away. Most of the time, I just hope the feeling will pass if I can keep things light and positive.
It sounds like you put a lot of effort into managing these feelings by focusing on others. Have you noticed if this approach affects how you feel about yourself afterward?

Yeah, sometimes I end up feeling kind of drained or, um, a little empty. I guess I worry that I’m not really being myself, but I just want people to like me. Afterward, I sometimes wonder if I’m actually annoying or if I’m just not enough. But I try not to think about it too much—I don’t want to sound ungrateful or anything.

It sounds like there’s a part of you that wishes you could be more authentic but worries about how that might affect your relationships. What do you think might happen if you shared some of those feelings with someone close to you?

Um… I think I’d be really scared that they might think I’m too much, or maybe that I’m too sensitive. I worry they’d pull away or just not want to deal with it. I guess I feel like my feelings could be a burden, so it’s safer to keep them to myself. I don’t want to risk losing someone just because I opened up.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like I don’t quite fit in or belong, especially when I’m around new people. Growing up, I was frequently criticized and told I wasn’t good enough, which made me doubt myself a lot. These experiences have made me expect rejection or failure in social settings.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to connect with others, I’ll probably mess up or say something wrong because I’m not good enough. People won’t accept me if they really get to know the real me. It’s safer to keep my distance so I don’t get hurt or embarrassed.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid social gatherings or keep conversations very surface-level to prevent others from seeing my flaws. When I do engage, I might change the subject abruptly or jump between topics to avoid deeper connection or scrutiny. Sometimes I withdraw completely if I feel overwhelmed.
ccd.situation: I’m at a small party where I don’t know many people and feel pressure to join in conversations. The group starts talking about shared experiences, and I’m worried about saying something that reveals how different or awkward I feel.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “Everyone can tell I don’t belong here.” “If I say something, they’ll think I’m weird or stupid.” “I’m just going to embarrass myself again.” “Maybe I should just stay quiet or change the subject before they notice me.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, loneliness, and a deep sense of being isolated even in a crowd. I feel tense and self-conscious, like I’m walking on eggshells.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid eye contact and speak very little, often interrupting myself to talk about unrelated topics. I might leave the room early or find ways to be physically distant from others to minimize interaction.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been a little stressful, but that’s kind of normal. I went to this thing my coworker invited me to—it was just a small get-together, nothing fancy. Oh, speaking of work, did I tell you the printer jammed again? It always does that when I’m in a hurry.
When the printer jammed, what was going through your mind in that moment?

Oh, I just got really flustered, you know? I kept thinking people were watching me struggle, like maybe they’d think I couldn’t even handle something simple. It’s funny, I remembered this time in school when I messed up a group project and everyone stared—it kind of felt like that all over again. Anyway, I ended up just asking someone to help, but I felt kind of silly doing it.
What do you think that memory from school means to you now when you face situations like the printer jam?

Hmm, I guess it just pops up whenever I feel like I’ve messed up, even if it’s something small. It’s like this reminder in the back of my head that maybe I’m just not good with these things, or that people will notice every mistake I make. Oh, and sometimes I try to act like I don’t care, but honestly, I just want to disappear in those moments. It’s weird, because it can really mess up my day, even if everything else is fine.
When you notice that feeling of wanting to disappear, what do you usually do next?

Um, usually I just sort of drift away from whoever’s around—like, I’ll pretend I have something urgent to do or suddenly remember an email I “forgot” to send. Or I’ll just stick to myself for the rest of the day and hope nobody brings it up again. Actually, sometimes I start talking about random things to change the subject, like movies or something totally unrelated. It’s just easier to avoid whatever happened, even if it’s obvious I’m dodging it.
How do you feel after you’ve drifted away or changed the subject like that?

Usually kind of relieved at first, honestly. But then I start overthinking—like, maybe people noticed I was being weird or that I bailed on the conversation. Sometimes I replay everything in my head, trying to figure out if I said something stupid or looked awkward. Oh, and then I’ll just get really tired, like all my energy is gone from worrying about it.
When you catch yourself replaying these moments, what do you tell yourself about why you acted the way you did?

I guess I tell myself it’s because I just can’t help being awkward or messing things up. Sometimes I think, “Well, that’s just me, I always find a way to make it weird.” I convince myself it’s safer not to say much next time, just in case. Oh, and then I start thinking about all the other times I felt out of place, even if they happened years ago.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored and overlooked by my family, especially during important events. My parents rarely expressed affection, and I was made to feel like I didn’t really belong or matter. These experiences have left me feeling isolated and doubtful about my worth.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m not wanted or desired by my family, it means I’m fundamentally unlovable. People will eventually reject me if they get to know the real me. I have to be careful about showing my true feelings because others won’t accept me.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to withdraw and avoid family gatherings to protect myself from feeling hurt. Sometimes I try to please others excessively, hoping to be noticed or accepted. I also get defensive quickly when I feel criticized or ignored.
ccd.situation: During a recent family dinner, no one acknowledged my achievements or asked about my life, making me feel invisible and unimportant. I noticed others connecting easily while I sat quietly, feeling left out and unwanted.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one cares about me here.” “I’m unwanted and don’t belong.” “If I speak up, they’ll just dismiss me again.”
ccd.emotions: Sadness, loneliness, and frustration. I also felt a deep sense of rejection and hurt.
ccd.behaviors: I stayed silent for most of the dinner and avoided eye contact. Later, I isolated myself in my room and didn’t return any messages from family members.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
I mean, I guess it’s been fine. Just kind of the same as usual. Nothing really interesting happened. I don’t know, I just feel like no one really notices me anyway.
When you say you feel like no one notices you, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind in those moments?

I don’t know, it’s just like… I’m there, but I might as well not be. People just talk over me or ignore what I say. Sometimes I think, “What’s even the point of speaking up?” It honestly feels like I’m invisible half the time.
How do you usually respond or behave when you start feeling invisible or like speaking up is pointless?
I just… stop trying, I guess. I keep quiet and sort of shut down. It’s easier than putting myself out there and getting ignored. Honestly, sometimes I just want to disappear completely.
When you notice yourself shutting down or wanting to disappear, what feelings are coming up for you in that moment?

I feel really sad, mostly. Sometimes there’s this heaviness, like I’m just tired of trying. It hurts, honestly, and I get frustrated too—like I’m angry at them for not noticing, but also at myself for caring so much. I just… feel alone.
That sounds really tough to carry. When you feel that sadness and frustration, what do you usually do to cope or manage those feelings?

I just want to be left alone, so I’ll go to my room and shut the door. Sometimes I ignore my phone or messages from people—like, what’s the point? I might distract myself with TV or music, but honestly, it doesn’t really help. Most of the time, I just wait for the feeling to pass.
When you spend time alone and wait for the feelings to pass, do you notice anything that helps those feelings lessen or anything that might make them linger longer?

Honestly, the feelings just kind of linger. Sometimes they get worse if I start thinking about stuff that’s happened before, like old arguments or times I got ignored. If anything helps, I guess it’s when I finally get tired enough to fall asleep. But then it just sort of starts all over again the next day.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has a history of persistent financial difficulties, including job instability and mounting debt, which have reinforced feelings of inadequacy and failure over time. They have rarely shared these struggles with others, maintaining a reserved demeanor that limits social support.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "I must never be a burden to others," "If I can't provide financially, I am useless," and "People will reject me if they knew how much I’m struggling." These beliefs shape how the patient interprets their financial situation and themselves.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to withdraw from friends and family to avoid judgment, avoids discussing money issues, and often procrastinates on addressing financial problems out of fear and shame, which temporarily reduces anxiety but worsens the situation.
ccd.situation: Facing mounting bills and an uncertain job future, the patient is overwhelmed by their financial concerns and fears that they will never improve their situation.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m a failure who doesn’t deserve to live," "I’m worthless because I can’t manage money," and "Everyone would be better off without me."
ccd.emotions: Deep sadness, hopelessness, shame, and anxiety dominate the patient’s emotional experience during financial stress.
ccd.behaviors: The patient isolates themselves, avoids seeking help or discussing finances, neglects budgeting or bill payments, and sometimes engages in self-critical rumination late into the night.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing much to say, just… same as usual. Work’s been a bit stressful, but that’s not new. I’ve just been trying to keep busy.
When you say work has been stressful, can you tell me about a specific moment or situation at work that felt particularly challenging?

Uh… well, there was this meeting where they talked about changes coming up, maybe some cuts. I guess I just felt kind of… out of place, like I didn’t belong. It was hard to focus after that. I tried not to think about it too much.
What thoughts went through your mind when you started feeling out of place during that meeting?

I don’t know, it’s hard to explain. Just… I kept thinking I’m probably not doing enough, or that I’ll be the first to go if they start letting people go. Felt like everyone else was more confident, like they weren’t worried at all. I just sort of wanted to disappear.
When you notice those thoughts about not doing enough or being the first to go, how do those thoughts affect your feelings and what you do afterward?

I usually just get really anxious, and then really tired, kind of numb. I stop talking much to people at work, just keep my head down. Sometimes I just… avoid checking emails or doing anything extra, in case there’s more bad news. I mostly just try to get through the day without drawing attention to myself.
What do you think might happen if you were to engage with those emails or tasks, instead of avoiding them?

I guess I’m scared I’ll see something I can’t handle, like more problems or signs I’m messing up. Or maybe I’ll get feedback that just confirms what I already think about myself. It just feels safer not to know. I know that probably makes things worse, but it’s hard to face it.
That makes a lot of sense. When you imagine facing that feedback or those problems, what do you think you might be able to do or how might you cope with it?

Honestly, I’m not sure. Most of the time, I just feel like I’d freeze up or not know what to say. I guess… maybe I’d try to fix it, but I always worry it won’t be enough. It just feels kind of hopeless, like whatever I do isn’t going to change anything.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up in a family where emotional needs were often overlooked, I learned to keep my feelings to myself and avoid asking for help. This led to feeling isolated and unsure if my needs mattered. Over time, I started believing that needing others was a weakness.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I show I need help, people will see me as weak or burdensome. I have to manage on my own because relying on others only leads to disappointment. Being self-sufficient is the only way to protect myself.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to withdraw and avoid sharing what I’m struggling with to prevent feeling vulnerable. I keep my problems to myself, hoping they will resolve on their own. Sometimes I push people away to avoid being seen as needy.
ccd.situation: In family conversations, when others discuss plans or decisions, I often feel left out or ignored. I notice myself hesitating to voice my opinions or ask for support because I fear being a burden.
ccd.automatic_thoughts:
“No one really cares about what I need.”  
“If I ask for help, they’ll think I’m weak.”  
“I should handle this alone; otherwise, I’ll just be a problem.”
ccd.emotions: I feel lonely, anxious, and a bit ashamed for needing support. There’s also a sense of frustration and helplessness because I want connection but don’t know how to get it.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet during family discussions and avoid reaching out for help. I often isolate myself and try to fix things independently, even when I’m overwhelmed. This sometimes leads me to feel more distant and disconnected.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too out of the ordinary. Just, you know, work and regular stuff. Not much else to say, really.
When you say “just work and regular stuff,” what kind of thoughts usually come up for you during your day?

I don’t know… I guess I try not to think about things too much. Mostly I just focus on getting through whatever needs to be done. Sometimes I get a little stressed, but I just keep it to myself. It’s easier that way.
When you notice that stress coming up but choose to keep it to yourself, what do you tell yourself about sharing or expressing those feelings?

I just… I don’t really see the point in saying anything most of the time. I tell myself it’s better not to bother anyone with it. People have their own stuff to deal with, so I should just handle things on my own. I guess I worry it might come off as complaining.
That makes sense. When you think about handling things on your own without sharing, how does that affect how you feel emotionally or physically?
Um, I guess I feel kind of tired, sometimes. It gets a bit lonely, honestly, but I don’t really know what else to do. I just try to push through it. I’m used to it by now.
It sounds like pushing through alone feels familiar but also lonely. When you notice that loneliness, what do you usually do next?

I usually just distract myself—watch something, read, or just keep busy. Sometimes I just go to my room and stay there for a while. I try not to think about it too much. It’s not really something I talk about.
When you use distractions or withdraw to your room, how does that impact your mood or energy later on?
It helps for a bit, I guess. But after a while, I just end up feeling kind of empty or tired. Sometimes it feels like nothing really changes. I just go back to doing the same thing again.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

ccd.relevant_history: I have often felt isolated and misunderstood growing up, with several experiences where I was excluded or criticized by peers and family members. These early moments made me believe that I’m not worthy of love or acceptance.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t make others happy or agree with them, they will reject me. I have to put others’ needs before my own to avoid being alone. Showing my true feelings might push people away.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to agree quickly with others, even when I disagree, and often avoid sharing my honest opinions. I try to be overly helpful and accommodating to gain approval and prevent rejection.
ccd.situation: I’m at a small social gathering where people are sharing their opinions, and I feel like I don’t fit in or that I might say something wrong.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I say what I really think, they’ll reject me. I’m probably annoying them by talking. They don’t really want me here.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and a deep sense of loneliness.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet most of the time, smile too much, and try to find ways to please others rather than engaging genuinely. I avoid initiating conversations or expressing my true feelings.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting, just the usual stuff. I’ve been trying to keep busy and stay positive. How are you?
It sounds like you’ve been focusing on staying positive—what kinds of things have you been doing to keep busy?

Oh, mostly just work and helping out where I can—like running errands for my family or coworkers, things like that. I try to be useful, you know? Sometimes I’ll hang out with people if they invite me, but I don’t really make plans myself. I just try to be there for others, I guess.
When you say you don’t usually make plans yourself, what goes through your mind in those moments?

Um, I don’t know, I just feel like… maybe people wouldn’t really want to hang out with me unless they ask first. I worry I might be bothering them if I reach out. Honestly, I just don’t want to be a burden, so I usually wait for someone else to make the first move. It just feels safer that way.
That sounds like a tough feeling to carry. When you notice those worries about bothering others, what do you typically tell yourself about your relationships or about yourself?

I guess I just think, like, maybe I’m not really that important to them, you know? Or maybe I’m just kind of… there, but not someone they’d really miss if I wasn’t around. Sometimes I tell myself it’s better not to expect much so I won’t be disappointed. I know it sounds negative, but I don’t want to set myself up for feeling bad.
It sounds like you’re trying to protect yourself from disappointment by keeping your expectations low—how do you feel emotionally when you have these thoughts about not being important to others?

Yeah, I guess that’s true. Um… it makes me feel pretty sad, honestly. I try to brush it off and just act normal, but deep down I feel kind of alone. Sometimes I wonder if I’m just not good enough for people to really care about me.
That sadness and loneliness sound really painful. When you notice these feelings and thoughts, how do you usually respond or cope with them?

I usually just try to hide it and act like everything’s fine. I’ll focus on what other people need or what I can do for them, so I don’t have to think about how I’m feeling. Sometimes I just keep busy and hope the feelings will go away. I don’t really talk about it with anyone—it feels easier to just keep it to myself.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and criticized by my family, which made me believe I could never meet their expectations. These experiences shaped my view that I am not good enough and destined to fail in important relationships.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t succeed or please others, I am worthless and will be rejected. Being vulnerable or asking for help shows weakness and will only confirm that I’m a failure.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to keep my feelings to myself and avoid sharing my struggles, hoping that if I don’t speak up, I won’t disappoint anyone. I also push myself to overwork, trying to prove my worth silently.
ccd.situation: During a family gathering, when my relatives asked about my career progress, I felt pressure and noticed myself withdrawing from the conversation.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They’re going to think I’m a loser because I haven’t achieved much. I’m just a failure and always will be.”
ccd.emotions: I felt ashamed, anxious, and hopeless.
ccd.behaviors: I stayed quiet, avoided eye contact, and left the room early to escape the discomfort.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Just the usual stuff, work and things at home. Nothing too out of the ordinary.
When you say "nothing too out of the ordinary," can you tell me about a specific moment this week that stood out to you, whether positive or challenging?
Uh, well… I went to a family dinner over the weekend. It was fine, just the usual talk. I can’t really think of anything special that happened. Honestly, I sort of kept to myself most of the time.
What was going through your mind when you decided to keep to yourself during the dinner?
I don’t know, I guess I just didn’t feel like talking much. Sometimes it seems easier to just listen and stay quiet. I didn’t really want to get into anything about me. It just felt safer that way.
When you think about staying quiet feeling safer, what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you about sharing more about yourself?

Um… I guess I worry about what people might think. Like, if I say the wrong thing or talk about myself, it might just make things awkward. I don’t really want to be judged or have everyone look at me differently. It’s just uncomfortable.
That makes sense. When those worries about being judged come up, how do you usually respond or cope in that moment?

I mostly just try to keep a low profile. I’ll nod along or smile, but not really say much. If it gets too much, sometimes I’ll just find a reason to leave the room for a bit. It helps me avoid the spotlight, I guess.
How do you feel afterward when you’ve stayed quiet or stepped away like that—does it bring relief, or do other feelings come up?

At first, I feel kind of relieved, like I dodged something. But later on, I usually start feeling bad about it. I end up thinking maybe I should’ve just tried harder to join in. Sometimes it makes me feel sort of alone.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like I can’t manage on my own, especially around people I don’t know well. Growing up, I was told I needed constant help, which made me doubt my own abilities and feel like a burden to others.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t ask for help, I won’t get what I need or I’ll be left out. People won’t care about me unless I show how much I need them. It’s safer to keep talking and avoid the main point so they don’t get annoyed or leave.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to ramble or go off on tangents in conversations to keep people engaged and avoid silence, hoping they’ll stick around and not reject me. Sometimes I over-explain myself to prove that I’m worth listening to.
ccd.situation: I’m at a small gathering with coworkers, and someone asks me a simple question about a project I’m working on.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They probably think I’m too needy or annoying. I need to say more so they don’t lose interest. Maybe if I keep talking, they’ll like me and not think I’m helpless.”
ccd.emotions: Anxious, insecure, and a bit desperate for approval.
ccd.behaviors: I talk a lot, often going off-topic, and avoid directly answering the question. I check others’ reactions closely and keep pushing the conversation to stay connected.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um, it’s been alright, I guess. Work’s been kind of busy, you know, with everyone getting ready for that big meeting next week—oh, and actually, speaking of work, I ran into this issue with the printer on Tuesday and it made me late for a team call, which was kind of embarrassing. I always seem to get caught up in these little things. But, um, yeah, nothing too out of the ordinary, I suppose.
When you think about the printer issue and being late to the call, what thoughts went through your mind at that moment?

Well, honestly, my first thought was, “Great, now everyone’s going to notice I’m late,” and I started worrying if they were annoyed at me. Then, while I was trying to fix it, I kept thinking about the last time I was late—there was this one time last year when I accidentally missed a deadline because I was waiting for someone’s feedback…and I still feel bad about that. I guess I just sort of panicked and started over-explaining myself when I finally joined the call, trying to make sure everyone knew it wasn’t on purpose. I probably talked way too much about the whole printer thing.
What feelings were you experiencing as you worried about what others might think and then joined the call?

Um, I felt really anxious, mostly. My heart was beating fast, and I kept thinking I needed to say the right thing so people wouldn’t be upset with me. Honestly, I get this knot in my stomach whenever I feel like I’ve let someone down, even over something small. I guess sometimes I feel like I just need to keep talking so no one thinks I’m just…not trying hard enough or something.
When you notice that knot in your stomach and that urge to keep explaining, what do you usually do next?

Oh, well, I usually just start talking more—like, I’ll try to explain every little detail about what happened, even if no one really asked. Sometimes I’ll tell a story about something similar that happened before, or bring up something unrelated I think might help people relate. I kind of lose track of the main point, honestly. It just feels safer, somehow, to keep the conversation going.
How do you feel after the conversation ends when you’ve explained everything in that way?

Um… honestly, I usually feel pretty exhausted, maybe a little embarrassed too. I end up replaying the whole conversation in my head, wondering if I talked too much or annoyed people. Sometimes I wish I could just answer simply and not go off on these tangents, but I worry people would think I’m boring or not worth listening to. I just can’t seem to stop myself in the moment.
When you imagine responding more simply without the extra details, what do you think might happen?

I guess I worry people would just sort of…lose interest, or maybe think I don’t really know what I’m talking about. Or they’d just move on to someone else and I’d get left out of the conversation. It’s like, if I don’t keep talking, they might forget I’m there or not care what I have to say. I mean, sometimes I even feel a bit invisible unless I make sure to contribute a lot.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like no matter what I do, things just don’t get better. Growing up, I didn’t have much control over what happened around me, and that feeling has stuck with me into adulthood.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to stand up for myself or take charge, I’ll just fail or make things worse. It’s safer to stay quiet and avoid drawing attention to my weaknesses because that protects me from more pain.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid challenges and back away from opportunities that feel overwhelming. Sometimes I distract myself with TV or social media to escape feeling powerless, even though it doesn’t solve anything.
ccd.situation: When my boss gave me extra work unexpectedly today, I felt overwhelmed and unsure how to handle it.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I can’t handle this. I’m just going to mess up and let everyone down. There’s nothing I can do to change this.”
ccd.emotions: Anxious, hopeless, and small—like I’m trapped and don’t have any strength to fight back.
ccd.behaviors: I procrastinated on starting the work and avoided asking for help because I felt ashamed and didn’t want to seem weak.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Uh, it’s been… I guess it’s been kind of hectic, actually. Work’s been piling up, which always happens when you think things are finally slowing down, you know? Yesterday my boss gave me this big new project out of nowhere, and I was just sitting there thinking, like, “Really? Now?” I mean, I did watch this documentary last night about whales, which was really interesting—the way they communicate is wild—but anyway, yeah, mostly just feeling a bit all over the place.
When you think about that big new project from your boss, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind?

Well, um... I guess mostly just, “How am I supposed to do this?” It’s kind of like when you’re handed a jigsaw puzzle with a thousand pieces and no picture to look at. I keep thinking I’ll probably mess it up or fall behind, and then everyone will notice. I actually remembered this time in school when I got called on in front of the class and my mind just went blank—it kind of feels like that a bit, if that makes sense.
That makes a lot of sense—feeling overwhelmed and worried about making mistakes. When those thoughts about messing up or falling behind come up, how do you usually respond to them?

Um, I don’t really do much, honestly. I just kind of... freeze, I guess? Like, I’ll sit at my desk and just stare at the screen, thinking about all the ways it could go wrong. Sometimes I end up rewatching old episodes of sitcoms on my phone instead, which is dumb, I know, but it sort of helps me not think about it for a while.
Noticing that freezing and distracting yourself with sitcoms helps in the moment, what do you notice afterward—how do you feel once you’ve been avoiding the task like that?

Afterward? Uh, usually worse, actually. I end up feeling guilty for wasting time and still have the work hanging over me, which just makes me more stressed. Sometimes I’ll start worrying about what people might think if I don’t get things done. Oh, and the weird thing is, like, I’ll start worrying about other stuff too, like forgetting to call my aunt back or not having done my laundry—it sort of piles up.
It sounds like the stress and worry tend to build up and spread to other parts of your life. When you notice these worries piling up, what do you usually do to try to manage or cope with them?

Hmm, I don’t know, I guess I just try to distract myself more? Like, I’ll scroll through my phone for a long time, or sometimes I’ll just take a really long shower and hope the feeling goes away. I’ll make a cup of tea or snack on something, even though I’m not really hungry. Sometimes I just end up going to bed early, but then I can’t sleep because my brain won’t shut off—oh, and I’ll start thinking about random stuff, like, do whales ever get insomnia?

It sounds like you’re using comfort activities to soothe yourself, even if they don’t fully ease the worries. When your mind won’t shut off and you start thinking about things like whales and insomnia, what do those thoughts feel like to you? Are they distracting, calming, or something else?

Yeah, it's kind of weird… sometimes those random thoughts are a little distracting, almost like my brain is trying to run away from the stuff I’m actually worried about. I guess it can be sort of calming, at least for a few minutes, just thinking about something totally different and not about work or mistakes. But then it feels pointless too, because nothing’s really changed and I still feel stuck. It’s like I’m floating around, not really fixing anything, just… drifting, I guess.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like I don’t have a good handle on money, even when things were okay. Past experiences with debt and unexpected expenses have made me feel overwhelmed and like I’m always behind. I grew up in a family where money was a constant source of stress and arguments.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t control my finances perfectly, everything will fall apart. I can’t trust myself to make good decisions with money. Asking for help means I’m weak and incapable.
ccd.coping_strategies: Avoiding looking at bills or financial statements because it feels too stressful. Spending impulsively to momentarily distract myself from worries. Withdrawing from conversations about money to avoid feeling judged or criticized.
ccd.situation: I just found out an important bill is overdue, and I don’t have enough money in my account to cover it.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m completely out of control.” “I’m going to ruin everything.” “There’s no way I can fix this.” “I’m such a failure.”
ccd.emotions: Overwhelmed, anxious, hopeless, and frustrated with myself.
ccd.behaviors: Avoiding opening my bank app, procrastinating on calling the billing office, snapping at family members, and feeling paralyzed about making a plan.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Honestly, not great. I just found out I missed a bill and I don’t even want to deal with it right now. Stuff like this just keeps happening and I’m really sick of it. It feels like no matter what I do, it’s never enough.
When you think about missing that bill and feeling it’s never enough, what thoughts go through your mind in that moment?

I just feel like I messed everything up again. It’s like, why can’t I get it together? I keep thinking I’m just terrible with money and I’m probably going to screw something else up soon. I honestly don’t see the point in even trying sometimes.
What do you notice happening inside you—like in your body or feelings—when those thoughts about messing up and not seeing the point come up?

I get this tight feeling in my chest, like I can’t breathe right. My stomach feels sick and I just want to crawl into bed and ignore everything. Sometimes I feel really angry too, but mostly it’s just this heavy, hopeless feeling. I can't even focus on anything else when it hits.
When you notice that heavy, hopeless feeling and the urge to avoid everything, what do you usually do next?

Honestly? I just avoid it. I don’t open my bank app, I don’t look at my emails, nothing. I’ll just do something else to distract myself, like scroll on my phone or watch TV. It doesn’t help, but at least I don’t have to think about it for a while.
What do you think might happen if you were to check your bank app or emails, even just for a few minutes?

I feel like I’d just freak out and spiral even more. I’d probably see more bad news and get overwhelmed. Then I’d start panicking about what to do next and end up feeling even worse about myself. It just seems easier not to look.
Can you recall a time when you did face a difficult situation like this—checking something that worried you—and it turned out better than you expected or you managed it in some way?

I mean, I guess there have been times where I finally opened something and it wasn’t as bad as I’d imagined. Like, sometimes the number’s not as huge as I thought, or there’s actually an option to pay later or set up a plan or whatever. But honestly, most of the time it still feels awful, so I don’t really trust that things will ever be okay. Even when it goes sort of okay, I just end up worrying about the next thing I’ll mess up.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt isolated throughout my life, especially during important moments when I needed support. Past experiences of rejection and feeling misunderstood by close friends and family have reinforced my belief that I’m destined to be alone. These feelings have been with me since childhood, making it hard to trust that anyone truly cares about me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I get close to someone, they will eventually leave or hurt me, so it’s safer to keep my distance. Showing my true self will only push people away because I’m not worthy of love. Being alone means I won’t have to face the pain of rejection or disappointment again.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid deep conversations or situations where I might become vulnerable because I’m afraid of being rejected. Sometimes I withdraw from social activities or make excuses to keep people from getting too close. I also try to convince myself that I don’t need others, even though it feels lonely.
ccd.situation: I was invited to a small gathering with some coworkers, but as the event approached, I found myself feeling anxious and convinced that no one there really wanted me around. I struggled with whether to go, worrying that if I showed up, I would just be ignored or left out.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one here actually likes me.” “If I say something, they’ll think I’m weird and won’t want to talk to me again.” “I’m just going to end up alone anyway, so why bother trying?”
ccd.emotions: I felt anxious, rejected, and deeply sad, with a heavy sense of loneliness that made me want to retreat from the situation entirely. There was also a quiet hopelessness, like this pattern would never change.
ccd.behaviors: I decided not to go to the gathering, staying home instead and avoiding contact with my coworkers. Later, I isolated myself further by scrolling through social media and avoiding phone calls or messages, reinforcing my belief that I am alone and unlovable.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um… it’s been okay, I guess. Just kind of the usual—work, coming home, not much else. I actually had this invitation to hang out with some coworkers, but I didn’t end up going. Mostly just kept to myself, like always.
What were you thinking or feeling when you decided not to go to the hangout with your coworkers?

I just… I don’t know, I started getting this really uneasy feeling about it the closer it got. I kept imagining showing up and no one actually wanting me there, maybe people just tolerating me. I guess I felt pretty anxious and sort of convinced it would be better for everyone if I just stayed home. I told myself I probably wouldn’t really fit in anyway.
That sounds really tough—when you imagine those situations, what do you notice happening in your body or your mind?

Yeah, it is tough. I notice my heart starts racing a bit and I feel almost frozen, like it’s hard to breathe or move. My mind kind of goes in circles, running through all the things that could go wrong or all the ways I might embarrass myself. Sometimes my chest feels heavy, and I just get this urge to disappear or hide.
When you notice those physical sensations and racing thoughts, what do you usually do next? How do you respond to those feelings in the moment?

Honestly, I usually just try to distract myself or avoid the situation completely. I’ll find an excuse not to go, or I’ll say I’m tired or have other plans, even if that’s not true. Sometimes I just retreat to my room and scroll through my phone for hours, trying not to think about it. I guess it’s easier to shut everyone out than deal with feeling unwanted.
When you look back on times you’ve avoided social situations like this, do you notice any patterns in what happens afterward—how you feel or what you tell yourself about those experiences?

Yeah, I can definitely see a pattern. After I skip something, I usually feel pretty bad about myself, like I’ve let another chance slip by. I start telling myself things like, “See, this is why I don’t have close friends,” or “I’m just not meant to be part of a group.” It’s almost like I’m proving to myself that I’m always going to be on the outside, no matter what I do.
When those thoughts come up about being on the outside or not fitting in, how certain do you feel that they are completely true? Are there times when those beliefs might not hold as strongly?

I mean… most of the time, it just feels true. It’s like, I can list out all these moments when I didn’t feel included or felt left out, and it just adds up in my head. But I guess, if I really think about it, there have been a few times—like maybe when someone reached out or seemed happy to see me—where it didn’t feel quite so hopeless. It’s just… those times feel really far away, or like maybe they don’t count as much as all the other times.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored and unimportant in my family, which made me believe that I didn’t really matter to anyone. Past relationships have reinforced this feeling when partners dismissed my needs or left without explanation.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to get close to someone, they will eventually see I’m not worth their time or love. It’s better not to expect much from others because I’m just going to be rejected or overlooked.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid deep conversations or sharing my feelings to protect myself from being hurt. Sometimes I distract myself with work or hobbies to keep from thinking about how alone I feel.
ccd.situation: I had an argument with my partner, and they said they needed some space. I immediately started thinking that I must have done something wrong and that they don’t really care about me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They’re probably tired of me and want to leave because I’m useless.” “I’m a waste of time, and no one really wants to be with me.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, sad, and hopeless. There’s also a deep sense of loneliness and rejection that weighs heavily on me.
ccd.behaviors: I withdraw from my partner and stop reaching out, hoping to avoid more pain. Sometimes I ruminate over what I did wrong, which makes me feel even worse and less likely to communicate openly.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been… I guess it’s been alright. Work’s been busy, so I’ve just been trying to keep up with everything. Oh, I did end up reorganizing my bookshelves over the weekend, which took forever because I couldn’t decide if I wanted them by author or by color—random, I know! Yeah, other than that, nothing major happened.
When you were trying to decide how to organize your bookshelves, what thoughts were going through your mind?

Oh, well, I kept second-guessing myself about every little thing—like, should I put the ones I haven’t read at the top so I remember them, or would that just look messy? Honestly, I probably spent more time worrying about messing it up than actually doing anything. Sometimes I feel like no matter what I pick, it won’t look good enough, and then I just… I don’t know, I end up feeling kind of silly for even caring so much about it. Sorry, I know that’s not super interesting.
It sounds like you had a lot of doubts about your choices and felt some frustration—what emotions did you notice in yourself as you were organizing the shelves and experiencing those thoughts?

Yeah, I guess I did feel kind of frustrated, and maybe a bit anxious too, like I was going to mess it up even though it’s just a shelf. I started to get this heaviness in my chest, like this uncomfortable pressure. I don’t know, it’s weird—sometimes the smallest things just make me feel really… off. But then I just distract myself or keep busy, so I don’t have to think about it too much.
