When you notice that heaviness or pressure in your chest, what do you usually do next—how do you respond to those feelings?

Honestly, I usually just try to ignore it. I’ll turn on some music or start scrolling on my phone, or sometimes I find something else to clean or organize. Sometimes I try to talk myself out of feeling that way, like telling myself it’s not a big deal, but it doesn’t really help much. Or I’ll just end up zoning out for a while and not really do anything at all.
When you tell yourself it’s not a big deal, what kinds of things do you say exactly?

Uh, I might just say stuff like, “Come on, it’s just a stupid shelf, why are you making this such a thing?” Or, “No one else would care about this, you’re overreacting.” Sometimes I even catch myself thinking, “You should just get over it—other people have real problems.” It’s not exactly helpful, but it’s kind of automatic at this point.
When you have those thoughts telling you to “get over it” or that you’re “overreacting,” how do those messages affect how you feel afterward?

Honestly, it usually just makes me feel worse. I end up feeling embarrassed on top of everything else, like I shouldn’t even be upset in the first place. It’s sort of like I’m disappointed in myself. Sometimes I just want to disappear for a bit, you know?

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced several financial setbacks over the past year, including job instability and mounting debt. They have felt unable to regain control over their finances, which has deepened their sense of vulnerability and despair.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I can't manage my money, I must be weak and incapable." "As long as I struggle financially, I will never be safe or secure." "Asking for help means I’m failing."
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid looking at bills or creating budgets, hoping problems will resolve on their own. They sometimes withdraw from family and friends out of shame and may impulsively spend to momentarily escape stress.
ccd.situation: Facing a growing pile of unpaid bills and feeling overwhelmed by financial responsibilities, the patient worries about their ability to provide for themselves and their family.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I'm going to lose everything." "I’m completely powerless to fix this." "I’m just weak and will never get out of this mess."
ccd.emotions: Overwhelming anxiety, hopelessness, shame, and frustration dominate their emotional state.
ccd.behaviors: The patient procrastinates dealing with financial tasks, isolates themselves socially, and occasionally engages in impulsive spending despite knowing it worsens their situation.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Honestly? Not great. I just feel like everything’s piling up and I can’t get a break. I don’t even know why I bother coming here sometimes—nothing ever really changes.
When you say everything feels like it’s piling up, can you tell me more about what specific things are on your mind right now?

It’s just… bills, mostly. Every time I try to get ahead, there’s another envelope, another thing I’m supposed to pay. I can’t keep up, and it feels like I’m just drowning in it all. And I don’t really want to talk about it, honestly.
It sounds really overwhelming to feel like you’re drowning in bills. When you notice those feelings, what kind of thoughts usually go through your mind about yourself or the situation?
I don’t know—I just think, what’s the point? I’m never going to dig myself out, so why bother even trying? Honestly, I just feel like a failure most days.
That’s a tough place to be. When you have thoughts like “what’s the point,” how does that affect what you choose to do next?

I guess I just shut down. I just end up ignoring everything—like the bills, emails, whatever. Sometimes I just zone out or do something stupid to distract myself. I know it just makes things worse, but I can’t help it.

It makes sense you’d want to escape when things feel so overwhelming. When you notice yourself shutting down or zoning out, what do you think might happen if you tried a different way of coping, even just a small change?

Honestly? I don’t see how it’d make a difference. I’ve tried before—making lists, trying to budget—and it just gets too much, so I quit. It always feels like it’s too late for small changes anyway. So I don’t really see the point in trying something else.

It sounds like past efforts felt exhausting and didn’t bring the relief you hoped for. What would it look like if you could take just one small step, something manageable, that wouldn’t feel overwhelming right now?

I don’t know. Even thinking about small steps just pisses me off, because it’s always “just do one thing” and I still end up stuck. Maybe I could open one bill, but honestly, even that feels pointless. I just don’t want to keep disappointing myself.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced several financial setbacks over the past year, including job loss and mounting debt. They have a history of feeling undervalued and dismissed by family members, reinforcing a negative self-view. These experiences have contributed to a deep sense of inadequacy and hopelessness about their financial future.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: “If I can’t manage my money well, it means I’m a failure.” “People will think less of me if I’m struggling financially.” “I don’t deserve to improve my situation because I’m not good enough.”
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid opening bills or checking bank statements, hoping the problems will go away. They isolate themselves from friends and family to avoid embarrassment. At times, they distract themselves with excessive screen time to escape uncomfortable feelings.
ccd.situation: The patient receives a notice about an overdue payment and worries about the mounting bills and inability to catch up financially.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m such a waste. I can’t even handle this basic responsibility.” “There’s no way I’ll ever get out of this mess.” “Everyone must think I’m a loser.”
ccd.emotions: Feelings of shame, hopelessness, anxiety, and sadness dominate in response to the financial stress.
ccd.behaviors: The patient withdraws from social situations, procrastinates dealing with financial tasks, and experiences difficulty concentrating on other responsibilities.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Uh, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too special. Just work stuff and, uh, trying to keep busy. Not much else.
When you say “trying to keep busy,” what kinds of things have you been doing, and how do you usually feel during or after those activities?

Um, mostly just watching shows or scrolling on my phone, stuff like that. It helps take my mind off things for a while. After, I don’t know, I usually just feel kind of tired. Sometimes it still feels like there’s a lot weighing on me.
When you notice that feeling of having a lot weighing on you, what kind of thoughts go through your mind at that moment?

Uh, I don’t know… I guess I just think about all the stuff I haven’t dealt with yet. Like bills piling up or, uh, things I keep putting off. Sometimes I just feel like I’m never going to figure things out. It’s hard to stop thinking about it once it starts.
What do you notice happens in your body or mood when those thoughts about not figuring things out come up?
I usually feel kind of tense, like my shoulders get tight. It’s hard to breathe sometimes. My stomach feels weird, too. And, uh, my mood just sort of drops—I get quiet and want to be alone.
When you get quiet and want to be alone, what do you usually do next, and how does that affect those feelings?

I just sort of sit around by myself, maybe turn on the TV. Sometimes I’ll ignore my phone or messages. It doesn’t really make things better, but I don’t feel like talking to anyone. The feelings just kind of stay there.
What do you think might happen if, instead of sitting alone, you tried reaching out or doing something different when those feelings come up?

I don’t know. I guess I just worry people wouldn’t really get it, or maybe they’d think I’m just complaining. It’s hard to imagine it making much of a difference. Mostly, I just don’t want to bother anyone.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked by family and friends, which made me believe that my presence wasn’t valued. Past relationships have ended with me feeling rejected and isolated, reinforcing my sense that I am destined to be alone.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I get close to someone, they will eventually leave me or reject me. I must keep my distance to protect myself from pain. People won’t truly care about me if they really know who I am.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid deep conversations and keep people at arm’s length. I rarely express my true feelings and often withdraw when I feel vulnerable. Sometimes, I distract myself with work or hobbies to avoid thinking about loneliness.
ccd.situation: I notice that I’m not invited to social gatherings or that people don’t reach out to me as often as others. This makes me question whether anyone really wants to be around me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one wants to spend time with me.” “I’m just going to end up alone like always.” “If I show who I really am, they’ll leave.”
ccd.emotions: I feel lonely, sad, and anxious. There’s also a sense of hopelessness about my chances of forming meaningful connections.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid social situations and cancel plans at the last minute. I keep my interactions brief and superficial to prevent others from getting too close. I isolate myself, which only deepens my feelings of loneliness.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um... it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting happened. Just work and the usual stuff. I’ve mostly kept to myself.
When you say you’ve mostly kept to yourself, what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you when you’re alone?
I don’t really think about it much—I just try to stay busy. Sometimes I feel a bit down, but I guess that’s normal. I just try not to dwell on things. It’s easier that way.
What kinds of things or worries come up when you notice yourself feeling a bit down?
I don’t know, it’s hard to explain. I guess sometimes I just wonder if people even notice when I’m not around. It makes me feel... kind of empty, I guess. I try not to let it get to me.
When you have the thought that people might not notice when you’re not around, what emotions or physical sensations do you notice in your body?

Uh, I guess I feel sort of heavy, like a weight in my chest. Sometimes my stomach feels weird, like a knot. I usually just get really tired when that happens. I’d rather just be by myself than deal with it.
When you choose to be by yourself to avoid those feelings, what do you notice happens to your mood or thoughts afterward?
Honestly, I just kind of go numb after a while. It’s not like I feel better, but at least things don’t hurt as much. Sometimes I end up feeling even more alone, though. But it feels safer, I guess.
What do you think makes being alone feel safer than reaching out to others, even if it sometimes leads to feeling more alone?

I guess…it just feels like there’s less chance of getting hurt. If I don’t put myself out there, people can’t really reject me or let me down. I’ve had that happen before and it’s not something I want to go through again. So, it’s just easier to keep my distance.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt distant from others and struggled to maintain close relationships. In past experiences, I was frequently left out or ignored by people I cared about, which made me feel isolated and unsure if I was truly valued.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I get close to people, they will leave me eventually. It’s safer to keep my distance so I don’t get hurt. People won’t stay around if they really see who I am.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid opening up or sharing my true feelings with others. Sometimes, I withdraw from social situations or keep conversations superficial to protect myself from potential rejection.
ccd.situation: I start a new friendship and notice the other person hasn’t responded to my last message. I wonder if they’re losing interest or planning to stop talking to me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They’re probably going to abandon me like everyone else.” “I must have done something wrong.” “I’m not worth sticking around for.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, sad, and lonely. There’s a persistent sense of fear that I will be left behind again.
ccd.behaviors: I pull back from the friendship by not reaching out as much and avoid deeper conversations. Sometimes, I shut down emotionally to avoid feeling vulnerable.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting. Just work and the usual stuff. Not much to say, really.
When you say "nothing too exciting," what kind of thoughts or feelings come up for you about your work and daily routine?

I don’t know… I guess I just go through the motions most days. Sometimes I feel a bit tired or like things are just kind of… flat. Work keeps me busy, but I don’t really feel much about it. It’s fine, I suppose.
When you notice those feelings of tiredness or things feeling "flat," what do you usually think about yourself or your situation in that moment?

Um, I don’t really think much, I just… I don’t know, maybe I feel a bit disconnected. Like I’m just going along with things and not really part of anything. It’s hard to explain. I guess I just try not to dwell on it.
That sense of disconnection sounds important—can you describe a recent moment when you felt that way most strongly?

Yeah, I guess… There was a time this week when I messaged a friend, and they didn’t reply. I just kind of sat there waiting, and the longer it went without a response, the more I felt like I was on the outside. It just made me want to back off a bit.
What thoughts were running through your mind while you were waiting for their reply and feeling on the outside?

I just kept thinking maybe I’d said something wrong, or maybe they didn’t actually want to talk to me. It sort of made me wonder if I was bothering them. After a while, I started thinking it might be better if I just stopped reaching out. I guess I felt a bit foolish for expecting anything different.
When you notice those thoughts about bothering your friend or feeling foolish, how do they affect what you do next or how you feel about yourself?

I usually go quiet—not just with them, but with other people too. I start to feel pretty down on myself, honestly. It’s like I just want to disappear for a bit. I end up avoiding conversations for a while.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and dismissed by my family, which left me feeling like my opinions and feelings didn’t matter. In past relationships, I was frequently in situations where I felt controlled or unable to assert myself, reinforcing a sense of helplessness. These experiences have shaped how I see myself and interact with others, especially in close relationships.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I believe that if I try to stand up for myself, I will be rejected or hurt because I am too weak to handle conflict. I also think that others are more powerful and that my needs are less important, so it’s safer to stay quiet and avoid upsetting the balance. Deep down, I feel that I am not capable of changing my circumstances or influencing others in a meaningful way.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid confrontation and suppress my true feelings to keep the peace, even when it leaves me feeling resentful or sad. Sometimes, I withdraw emotionally or physically from situations where I feel powerless, hoping the problem will resolve itself. At times, I might try to please others excessively to gain approval and avoid conflict, even if it means sacrificing my own well-being.
ccd.situation: In my current relationship, when my partner expresses frustration or disagreement, I immediately feel like I have done something wrong and that I am at fault. I find myself unable to speak up about how I feel because I worry it will only make things worse or push them away.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They’re upset because I’m not good enough.” “If I say something, they’ll leave me or get angry.” “I can’t handle this; I’m too weak to fix it.” “My feelings don’t matter as much as theirs.” “I’m powerless to change how this goes.”
ccd.emotions: I feel scared, sad, and overwhelmed. There’s a heavy sense of anxiety about being rejected or abandoned, mixed with deep frustration and helplessness. Sometimes, I also feel lonely because I believe I’m not truly seen or valued.
ccd.behaviors: I withdraw and become quiet, avoiding sharing my thoughts or feelings. I might agree with whatever my partner says, even if I disagree, just to keep things calm. Occasionally, I shut down emotionally or distance myself to protect against feeling vulnerable, which leads to further isolation and misunderstandings.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been kind of up and down, I guess. Work was really busy, so I was pretty tired most days. At home, things with my partner felt a little tense…like, there were a few moments where I felt like I was walking on eggshells, if that makes sense. I don’t know, I tried not to make a big deal out of it, but it was sort of weighing on me.
When you noticed that feeling of walking on eggshells with your partner, what thoughts were going through your mind in those moments?

I guess the first thing that goes through my mind is wondering if I did something wrong, like maybe I messed up without realizing it. I start overthinking every little thing I said or did, trying to figure out if that’s what caused the tension. There’s also this worry that if I say the wrong thing or try to talk about it, it’ll just make them more upset, so I end up keeping quiet. It’s like I just hope it’ll pass if I stay out of the way.
What do you notice happens to your feelings or your body when you start thinking that you might have done something wrong?

Um… I get this heavy, kind of sinking feeling in my chest, and my stomach gets all tight. I feel really anxious, like my heart beats faster and I can’t quite catch my breath. Sometimes I get shaky or fidgety, and it’s hard to focus on anything else because my mind keeps going over what happened. Emotionally, I just feel really small and scared, I guess, like I want to disappear or hide.
When you feel that heavy, anxious sensation and the urge to hide, what do you usually do next—how do you respond or cope in those moments?

Honestly, I usually just get really quiet and try to make myself as invisible as possible. I might go to another room or pretend I’m busy with something, so I don’t have to interact. Sometimes I just nod along and agree with whatever my partner says, even if I don’t really feel that way. I keep hoping things will blow over if I don’t draw any attention to myself.
When you respond by withdrawing or going quiet, what do you imagine might happen if you instead shared what you were feeling or thinking with your partner?

I imagine it would just make things worse, honestly. I worry that if I say how I’m actually feeling, my partner will think I’m being too sensitive or dramatic, or maybe even get angry with me. There’s this fear that I’d just be starting a fight or pushing them further away. I guess I just assume they wouldn’t really understand or care, so it feels safer to keep it to myself.
Where do you think those fears about your partner’s reaction come from—have you experienced similar situations before that shape this expectation?

Yeah, I think a lot of it probably goes back to growing up. In my family, if I ever tried to speak up or say how I felt, it was usually ignored or sometimes even made fun of, so I learned pretty quickly to just keep things to myself. And in past relationships, anytime there was conflict, I ended up feeling like I was always the one at fault or that my feelings just didn’t matter. So now, I guess I automatically expect that if I open up, it’ll just lead to rejection or more hurt.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like I don’t measure up at work, especially when tasks get tough. Past bosses have criticized me a lot, which made me believe I’m not good enough. This feeling has stuck with me and often comes up when I’m under pressure.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t do everything perfectly, I’m a failure. People will think less of me if I ask for help. I have to handle everything on my own or I’m useless.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid asking for help and push myself to work longer hours. Sometimes I shut down or procrastinate because I feel overwhelmed. I also try to hide my mistakes so others won’t see how “incompetent” I am.
ccd.situation: At work, I have a big project deadline coming up, and my boss just added more tasks to my plate. I’m feeling the pressure to get everything done perfectly and on time.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: I’m going to mess this up again. I’m such a waste, why do they even trust me with this? If I can’t handle this perfectly, it means I’m worthless.
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, hopeless, and ashamed. There’s also a heavy sense of fear that I’ll disappoint everyone.
ccd.behaviors: I work late into the night trying to fix every little detail. I don’t ask for help, even when I need it. Sometimes I freeze and avoid starting certain parts of the project because I’m scared of failing.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been kind of stressful, to be honest. Work’s been really busy and my boss keeps piling on more things for me to do. I feel like I’m barely keeping up. I’m just tired, I guess.
When you say you feel tired and like you’re barely keeping up, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind in those moments?

Mostly I just keep thinking I’m going to mess up. Like, no matter how hard I try, it won’t be good enough. Sometimes I wonder if people can tell I’m struggling, and that kind of freaks me out. It’s just this constant worry in the back of my head.
That sounds really tough. When you notice that worry in the back of your head, how does it affect what you do or how you handle your work?

It makes it hard to even start sometimes. I get stuck double-checking everything, or I just sit there staring at my screen because I’m scared I’ll do it wrong. I end up working way later than everyone else, just to make sure I don’t miss anything. But it never really feels like enough.

It sounds like this worry leads to a lot of extra effort but also leaves you feeling stuck and exhausted. When you’re in those moments of hesitation or double-checking, what are you hoping will happen or what are you trying to avoid?

I guess I’m hoping I won’t screw up—that if I check everything a million times, I won’t make any mistakes. I’m really trying to avoid anyone noticing I messed up or thinking I can’t handle it. I just don’t want to let anyone down. I really don’t want my boss or my team to think I’m useless.
It makes sense that you want to avoid mistakes and keep up a good impression. When you imagine your boss or team noticing a mistake, what do you worry might happen next?

I worry they’ll lose trust in me. Maybe they’ll think I’m not cut out for the job or that I can’t handle important stuff. I’m scared they’d start treating me differently or just stop relying on me. Sometimes I even imagine getting fired.
Those fears sound really intense. When you think about those possible outcomes, how do you usually cope or respond emotionally in the moment?

I mostly just panic inside. My chest gets tight and I feel kind of sick, but I try not to let anyone see it. Sometimes I’ll just keep my head down and work even harder, or I’ll freeze and avoid even starting things. It’s like I’m stuck between trying too hard and not being able to do anything.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and misunderstood by my family. There were many times when I tried to connect, but it seemed like my feelings didn’t matter or were dismissed. This left me feeling isolated and convinced that I wouldn’t be accepted or loved by those closest to me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I believe that if I show my true self or express my needs, people will reject me. I also think that relationships are inherently unstable and that I have to protect myself by not getting too close. Deep down, I feel that I am different in a way that makes me unworthy of lasting love or connection.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to keep people at a distance, avoiding deep conversations or emotional vulnerability. Sometimes, I withdraw completely to avoid the pain of rejection. I also try to please others or act in ways I think will make me less likely to be abandoned, even if it doesn’t feel authentic.
ccd.situation: During a recent family gathering, I noticed that no one asked me how I was doing or seemed interested in my life. I felt invisible and alone in the room, like I was on the outside looking in.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one cares about me.” “I don’t belong here.” “I’m always going to be alone.” “If I try to connect, I’ll just get hurt again.”
ccd.emotions: I felt sad, lonely, and a little hopeless. There was also a sense of anxiety about being rejected and a deep ache of emptiness.
ccd.behaviors: I withdrew from the group, kept my answers short, and avoided making eye contact. Later, I isolated myself in my room and avoided calling or reaching out to family members.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Nothing really special happened. I went to a family thing, but it was kind of just… there. I mostly kept to myself.
When you say you mostly kept to yourself at the family event, what were you thinking or feeling in those moments?
I just felt kind of out of place. Like, I was there, but it didn’t really seem like anyone noticed. I kept thinking it wouldn’t matter if I spoke up or not. Mostly I just felt kind of invisible.
What do you think it means to you if you were to speak up and be noticed by others in that setting?

I guess I worry that if I actually try to say something, it’ll just get ignored or brushed off. Or worse, that people will think I’m being needy or annoying. It feels safer to just stay quiet. At least that way I won't end up feeling stupid or rejected.
That sounds really tough. When you imagine people thinking you’re needy or annoying, what emotions come up for you?
I feel embarrassed, I guess. Maybe even ashamed, like I shouldn’t have tried in the first place. There’s this heavy sadness, too. It just makes me want to pull away even more.
When you notice that sadness and urge to pull away, what do you usually do next?

Usually, I just find a way to leave or step out of the room for a bit. Sometimes I’ll just keep really quiet and hope no one talks to me. After things are over, I end up going to my room or just not reaching out to anyone. It feels easier to just be alone.
How do you feel about the idea of trying something different next time, like sharing a small thought or comment, even if it feels uncomfortable?

Honestly, it makes me nervous just thinking about it. Part of me wants to try, but I keep imagining it going badly or people just not caring. I’m not sure if it would make any difference. But I guess I’ve never really given it a real shot.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like things are out of my control, especially growing up in a family where I had to handle a lot on my own. Whenever I faced challenges, I believed I couldn’t do much to change the outcome. This has made me doubt my ability to manage daily problems.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to take charge, I’ll probably fail or get overwhelmed. It’s safer to just accept things as they are because I don’t have the strength to fix them. Asking for help feels like admitting that I’m weak.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid taking on new tasks or making decisions because I’m afraid I’ll mess up. Sometimes I withdraw or stay quiet instead of speaking up. I also try to distract myself with TV or social media so I don’t have to think about the problems.
ccd.situation: At work, when my manager asks me to take on a new project, I immediately feel like I won’t be able to handle it and that I’m going to disappoint everyone.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I can’t do this. I’m going to mess it up and make everyone think I’m useless.” “I’m just not strong enough to handle more responsibility.” “If I ask for help, they’ll see I’m weak.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, fear, and discouragement wash over me. I feel overwhelmed and helpless, like there’s no way out. Sometimes I get sad and frustrated with myself for feeling this way.
ccd.behaviors: I procrastinate or avoid starting the project altogether. I might call in sick or try to shift the responsibility to someone else. When forced to engage, I do the bare minimum and avoid asking questions or seeking support.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been a bit stressful, but nothing too out of the ordinary. I’ve just been trying to get through each day. Not much else to say, really.
When you say work has been a bit stressful, can you tell me about a specific moment or situation that felt particularly challenging?

Yeah, I guess there was this one thing. My manager asked me if I could take on a new project. I kind of froze for a second—I just felt this wave of worry hit me. I wasn’t sure how to respond, so I just said I’d think about it.
What thoughts were going through your mind when that wave of worry hit you after your manager asked about the new project?

Mostly I just thought, “I can’t do this. I’m going to mess it up.” It felt like too much, and I started thinking everyone would notice if I struggled. I didn’t want to let anyone down or look like I didn’t know what I was doing.
When you imagine others noticing if you struggled, what do you think would happen or what would it mean about you?

I guess I just feel like they’d see I’m not really up to it. Like, maybe they’d start thinking I’m not reliable or that I can’t handle things. It kind of makes me worry they’d stop trusting me with anything important. That idea really gets to me.
How do you usually cope when you notice these worries about being seen as unreliable or not capable?

I usually just try to keep my head down and not draw attention to myself. Sometimes I’ll put off starting the work, or find reasons to avoid it for as long as I can. If it gets too much, I’ll distract myself with my phone or TV. I don’t really talk to anyone about it.
What do you notice happens to your feelings or stress levels when you avoid starting the work or distract yourself?

Honestly, it’s kind of a short relief at first. I feel less anxious for a little while, but then the stress creeps back in—sometimes even worse than before. I end up feeling guilty or annoyed with myself for not just dealing with it. It turns into this cycle that’s hard to break.

ccd.relevant_history: I have struggled with feeling incapable ever since I was young, especially when trying to learn new things or take on responsibilities. Past experiences of failure or criticism have reinforced the idea that I just don't measure up. This has made me hesitant to pursue opportunities for growth.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try something new and fail, it means I’m just not good enough. People will see me as useless if I don’t get things right the first time. It’s safer to avoid challenges than to risk proving how incompetent I really am.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid tasks that feel difficult or unfamiliar and stick to what I know I can handle. When I do take on something challenging, I often procrastinate or give up quickly. Sometimes I convince myself I’m just not cut out for improvement.
ccd.situation: I’m considering enrolling in a class to improve my skills, but I feel uncertain and anxious about whether I can succeed.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “What if I mess up and everyone thinks I’m a failure?” “I’m not smart enough to keep up with this.” “I’ll probably embarrass myself if I try.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, self-doubt, and a sense of discouragement.
ccd.behaviors: I hesitate to sign up for the class, avoid practicing new skills, and sometimes withdraw from conversations about personal development.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um... it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too special happened. Just, you know, regular stuff.
Can you tell me about a moment recently when you felt a strong emotion, whether positive or negative?

Uh, I don’t know... I guess I felt kind of nervous a couple days ago. There was this thing I was thinking about doing, but I didn’t end up going through with it. It just made me feel unsettled for a while.
What was the thing you were thinking about doing, and what went through your mind when you were feeling nervous about it?

It was this class I was looking at online. I thought maybe I could sign up, but then I started doubting if I could actually keep up. My mind just kinda went to all the things that could go wrong. So, in the end, I just closed the page.
When you imagined those things going wrong, what specific thoughts or worries came up for you?

I guess I just kept thinking I’d probably mess up or not understand what’s going on. Maybe people would notice and think I don’t belong there. It just felt like I’d embarrass myself. I don’t know… it seemed easier not to try.
How did thinking about those possibilities affect how you felt in your body or your mood at that moment?

I felt kind of tense, like my chest was tight and my stomach was turning. My mood just dropped, honestly. I started feeling really unsure about myself. It’s hard to shake off that feeling once it starts.
When you notice that tightness and those feelings of doubt, what do you usually do or tell yourself next?

Usually I just try to distract myself or find something else to do, so I don’t have to think about it. Sometimes I tell myself it’s probably not worth it anyway. I’ll say I’m just not cut out for that kind of thing. It sort of helps in the moment, but the feeling sticks around.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and was rarely praised or shown affection by my family. Past relationships ended with partners saying they needed space or were unhappy, reinforcing my sense of being unwanted.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to get close to others, they will eventually reject me. I have to keep my feelings to myself to avoid being hurt. People won’t like me unless I hide my true self.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to withdraw from social situations and avoid sharing personal details. I keep conversations short and neutral to prevent others from getting too close. Sometimes I distract myself with work or hobbies to avoid thinking about feeling lonely.
ccd.situation: I’m at a gathering where people are talking about their close friendships and relationships, and I notice I’m not part of any of those conversations.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one here really wants me around.” “I’m just a burden if I try to join in.” “They’d be better off without me.”
ccd.emotions: Loneliness, sadness, and a quiet sense of shame.
ccd.behaviors: I stay silent and move to the edge of the group, avoiding eye contact and not engaging in the conversation. Later, I leave early to be alone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Nothing much happened. Just kept busy with work mostly.
When you say "nothing much happened," how did you feel during your workdays this week?
Um, I guess I just felt…fine. Work keeps me occupied, so I don’t really think about much else. It’s easier that way.
What kinds of thoughts usually come up for you when you’re at work and trying not to think about other things?
I mostly just focus on what I have to get done. If my mind wanders, I try to pull it back to whatever task I’m working on. Sometimes I catch myself zoning out, but I try not to let it go too far.
When you catch your mind wandering or zoning out, what kinds of thoughts or feelings tend to come up?

Um, sometimes I just start thinking about conversations I’ve had, or… things I didn’t say. Or I notice I’m kind of tense, like I’m waiting for something bad to happen. I usually just try to shake it off and keep working.
When you notice that tension or those thoughts about things you didn’t say, what do you usually tell yourself about those moments or feelings?
I guess I just tell myself it’s not important. Like, “just move on, it doesn’t matter.” I try not to dwell on it because…I don’t know, it feels pointless.
How does telling yourself “just move on, it doesn’t matter” affect how you feel in those moments?

It sort of helps me get through the day, I guess. But sometimes I still feel a bit…empty, or like I’m just going through the motions. It’s not a big deal, though. I try not to think about it too much.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored or rejected by important people in my life, which made me believe I wasn’t worthy of love or attention. Past relationships have reinforced these feelings, as I tended to put others’ needs before my own and still felt unseen or unappreciated.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t make others happy, they will leave me or stop caring about me. I have to be perfect and accommodating to be accepted. Showing my true feelings might push people away because I’m not lovable as I am.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to please others constantly, avoiding conflict or expressing my own needs so that I won’t be rejected. I often hide my true feelings and opinions, hoping that by being agreeable, I’ll be accepted and loved.
ccd.situation: I am spending time with my partner, and I notice they seem distant and less affectionate than usual.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: They must be upset with me. Maybe I did something wrong. If I’m not always nice and accommodating, they won’t love me anymore.
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and fear of abandonment. I feel lonely and unworthy of love.
ccd.behaviors: I try harder to please my partner, becoming extra attentive and agreeable. I avoid bringing up my own feelings or concerns to keep the peace.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too out of the ordinary, really. I’ve just been keeping busy with work and, you know, making sure everyone around me is doing alright. I hope that’s helpful to share!
It’s definitely helpful—thank you for sharing that. When you say you’re making sure everyone around you is doing alright, how does that usually affect how you feel during or after those interactions?

Oh, I don’t know… I guess I just want to make sure everyone’s happy, so I try to check in a lot and do little things for people. Honestly, I don’t really think about how it makes me feel—I just sort of focus on what others might need. I mean, it feels nice when people are pleased, though sometimes I get a bit tired. But it’s no big deal, really!
It sounds like you put a lot of energy into caring for others—when you notice feeling tired afterward, what thoughts usually go through your mind about yourself or the situation?

Hmm… I guess I usually think I could’ve done more, or maybe I missed something someone needed. Sometimes I wonder if I’m being helpful enough, or if I said something wrong. I just don’t want to let anyone down, you know? But I try not to dwell on that—I just keep going.

That sounds like a lot of pressure to carry. When you catch yourself thinking you might not be helpful enough or worry about letting others down, how does that affect your mood or motivation in those moments?

Yeah, I guess it does feel like a lot sometimes. I usually feel a bit anxious, like there’s this knot in my stomach. It makes me want to try even harder to make things better, or to fix whatever I might’ve messed up. I don’t really bring it up with anyone though—I just keep it to myself and hope it passes.
When you notice that knot of anxiety and the urge to fix things, what do you usually do next? Are there any actions or thoughts that help you feel a little more at ease?

Um, I usually just try to be extra attentive—like, I’ll ask if someone needs anything, or I’ll do something nice for them. Sometimes I tell myself it’ll be okay, but mostly I wait to see if the other person acts happier or more relaxed. If they seem alright, I feel a bit better. I guess I don’t really do much for myself in those moments—I focus on them instead.
It sounds like you’re really focused on others’ reactions to gauge how you’re doing. When you focus so much on others, what do you notice happens to your own needs or feelings during those times?

Oh, um, I guess I don’t really notice my own needs much. I sort of put them aside because it feels more important to make sure everyone else is okay. Honestly, sometimes I’m not even sure what I need—I just hope things stay calm. I’m fine, though, really—I don’t want to be a bother.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Honestly? Not great. I went to this family dinner and, I don’t know, it just left me feeling… off. I tried to say something personal and it just kind of… fell flat. They barely even acknowledged me—I don’t really see the point of trying with them.
When you think back to that moment at dinner, what was going through your mind right before you tried to say something personal?

I guess I was kind of nervous, honestly. I kept wondering if I should even bother saying anything at all, because it always feels awkward. But I thought maybe this time would be different, that maybe they’d actually listen. I don’t know why I expected anything else.
What do you think it means about you or your relationships when you notice that expectation not being met?

I don’t know… it just feels like I’m never going to really fit in with them, no matter what I do. It’s like I’m on the outside looking in, and nothing I say makes a difference. Sometimes I wonder if there’s just something wrong with me. I mean, why else would they keep acting like that?
When you have the thought that something might be wrong with you, how does that affect the way you feel and behave around your family?

It just makes me want to shut down, honestly. I feel angry and kind of embarrassed, so I stop talking or just go through the motions. I try not to show how much it gets to me, but inside it’s like I’m bracing myself to be ignored. Sometimes I just avoid being around them altogether—it’s easier that way.
When you choose to avoid being around your family, what do you notice happens to your feelings or thoughts afterward?

I mean, at first it feels like a relief, like I don’t have to deal with all that awkwardness. But after a while, I just end up feeling alone. Then I start second-guessing everything—like maybe I really am the problem, or maybe I just made things worse by pulling away. It’s pretty exhausting, honestly.
What do you imagine might happen if you tried to stay engaged with your family, even when it feels awkward or difficult?

Honestly, I just think I’d end up feeling even worse. Like, I’d put myself out there and just get ignored again, or maybe even get into an argument. It’s not like they’re suddenly going to start caring, so why bother? I’d rather keep my guard up than get burned again.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Work kept me pretty busy, so I didn’t really have much free time. I just tried to keep my head down and get things done. Nothing major happened.
When you say you kept your head down and just got things done, what thoughts were going through your mind during those busy moments at work?

I guess I was mostly just focused on what needed to be finished. Sometimes, though, I’d notice people chatting or making plans together, and I’d feel sort of left out. I tried not to think about it too much, just kept telling myself to stay busy. I didn’t really want to deal with those feelings.
What do you think it means to you when you notice others chatting or making plans and feel left out?

It just kind of hits me that I’m not really part of things, you know? Like, I’m always on the outside looking in. Sometimes I wonder if there’s something about me that makes people not want to include me. It feels pretty crappy, honestly.
When you have those thoughts that there might be something about you that leads to being left out, how does that affect the way you feel and behave afterward?

I usually just feel kind of empty and a bit angry at myself. After that, I tend to pull back even more—like I’ll avoid talking to people or just keep my interactions really short. It’s hard to want to reach out to anyone when I feel like I’m not wanted. So I just bury myself in work or other things to distract myself.
What do you think might happen if you tried to reach out to someone despite feeling that way—what thoughts come up when you imagine that?

Honestly, I start to feel kind of anxious just thinking about it. I worry that I’d come off as needy or that they’d just brush me off. I guess I expect them to lose interest or not really care. It feels safer to just keep my distance instead.
When you notice those worries about being seen as needy or brushed off, how do you usually respond to those thoughts—do you challenge them, accept them, or something else?

Most of the time, I just accept them. It’s like—I just assume that’s how things are, so I don’t really question it. I don’t usually try to argue with those thoughts. I just sort of shut down and move on.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been alright, I guess. Nothing too exciting happened. Just the usual stuff with work and family. Not much else to say, really.
When you say “alright” and “usual stuff,” what thoughts or feelings come up for you about your work and family life these days?

I don’t know... I just feel kind of tired, I suppose. With family, it feels like I’m just going through the motions a lot of the time. Sometimes I wonder if anyone really notices if I’m there or not. Work is okay, but at home, it’s just... quiet.
That sounds exhausting. When you notice that sense of going through the motions at home and wondering if anyone notices, what goes through your mind in those moments?

Um, I don’t know, maybe I just feel like it doesn’t matter much what I do. Sometimes I think, “Why bother saying anything?” It’s like, even if I try, it won’t really change anything. So I kind of just keep to myself.
When you have the thought “Why bother saying anything?” what feelings or physical sensations do you experience in your body?
I guess I just feel sort of heavy, like there’s this weight on my chest. Sometimes my stomach feels tight too. Mostly, I just feel kind of sad and tense. It’s hard to put it into words.
That sounds really tough. When you notice that heaviness and tightness, do you find yourself doing or avoiding anything differently than usual?

Yeah, I probably just get quieter. I might leave the room or keep busy with something else so I don’t have to talk much. I try not to make it obvious, but I don’t really want to be around people then. It just feels easier that way.
It makes sense you’d want to protect yourself by stepping back. What do you imagine might happen if you did share some of what you’re feeling with someone close to you?
I’m not sure. I guess I just worry they’d brush it off or not really understand. Maybe they’d even get annoyed or just change the subject. It’s hard to picture it going any differently.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

I don’t know, just… sometimes I worry I’ll mess something up if I’m not careful. I end up double-checking things a lot. It can get kind of stressful. I try not to think about it too much.

Honestly, I just try to stick to what I know. I go over everything again, maybe more than I need to. Sometimes I put things off for a bit if it feels too much. I don’t really talk to anyone about it.

I just think, like, “I’m not ready yet. What if I do it wrong?” I start imagining all the things that could go badly. It feels safer to wait, even though I know it doesn’t really fix anything.

I don’t know… I guess I worry it’ll all fall apart if I don’t have it figured out first. I feel like I’d make some big mistake and everyone would notice. It just seems like things could get out of hand really quickly. That makes me want to hold back even more.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been pretty hectic, actually. Work’s been piling up, and I’ve just been trying to keep my head down and get everything done. I haven’t really talked much with anyone outside of meetings. I guess I’m just tired.
When you say you’ve been tired, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind about how you’re handling everything?

Mostly I just keep thinking I have to push through it and not complain. Sometimes I worry that if I mess up or fall behind, people will see that I don’t really belong there. I guess I tell myself to just keep quiet and work harder. I don’t know, it’s like I’m always on edge.
That sounds really stressful. When you notice yourself feeling on edge, what do you usually do in response to those feelings?

I usually just try to keep busy so I don’t have to think about it too much. I stay late at work or bring stuff home instead of talking to people about how I’m feeling. Honestly, I just kind of keep to myself. It feels safer that way.
It sounds like staying busy helps you avoid those uncomfortable feelings. What do you think might happen if you allowed yourself to slow down or share how you’re feeling with someone?

I don’t know, I just worry that if I let my guard down or show I’m struggling, people might think less of me. Maybe they’d see me as weak or just not cut out for the job. Part of me feels like if I open up, it’ll just make things worse, like I’ll end up feeling even more alone. So I guess I’d rather just keep it to myself.
That fear of being seen as weak sounds really hard to carry. Can you remember a time when you did share something vulnerable with someone—how did that go?

Um, there was a time a while back, at my last job, where I tried to talk to a coworker about being overwhelmed. I thought maybe they’d understand, but afterward, things just felt awkward between us. It was like they didn’t know what to say, and we kind of stopped talking as much. That’s pretty much why I don’t really risk opening up anymore.
That experience sounds really disappointing and isolating. What do you think you might need from someone to feel more supported if you were to share how you’re feeling now?

Honestly, I think I just want someone to actually listen and not judge me or act weird about it. Just… to feel like it’s okay to not have it all together all the time. I don’t need advice or anything, just someone to get it. I guess I just want to feel like I’m not alone in this.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Oh, um, it’s been alright, I guess. Work’s been a bit busy, but nothing too out of the ordinary. I’ve just been trying to keep up with everything. Sorry, I don’t want to complain or anything!
It sounds like you’re managing a lot—what’s been going through your mind when things get busy at work?

Yeah, it can get kind of hectic. I mostly just try to make sure I’m doing everything people expect, you know? I really don’t want to let anyone down, so I just focus on getting things done as best as I can. Sometimes I worry about making mistakes, but I try not to show it.
What do you notice happens inside you—thoughts, feelings, or body sensations—when you worry about making mistakes?

Um, well, I guess I get a bit tense. My mind kind of races, like I start thinking, "What if I miss something important?" or "What if someone gets upset with me?" I feel this tightness in my chest, and sometimes my stomach feels off. I just try to push those feelings aside and keep working.
When you push those feelings aside and keep working, what impact does that have on how you feel or on your work?

Hmm, I guess it helps me get through things in the moment. But after a while, I end up feeling really tired and sort of drained. Sometimes I get frustrated with myself for not being able to handle it better. But I wouldn’t want anyone to notice I’m struggling, so I just keep going.
What do you imagine might happen if someone did notice you were struggling and you shared how you’re feeling?

Oh, um, I’m not really sure... I guess I worry they might think I’m not capable or that I can’t handle my job. I wouldn’t want to give anyone a reason to be disappointed in me. I’d feel pretty embarrassed, honestly. I just don’t want to cause any trouble or seem like a burden.
That sounds really tough to carry on your own. When those worries about being seen as incapable or a burden come up, what do you tell yourself about yourself or your situation?

Well, I just tell myself to keep it together and not make a fuss. I guess I think, “This is just how things are, so I have to deal with it.” Sometimes it feels like there’s not really any other option. I try to remind myself not to mess up or make things harder for anyone else.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Work’s been kind of stressful, especially with meetings. I’ve just been trying to keep my head down and get through things. Nothing major happened, really.

Honestly, I just get really nervous that I’m going to say the wrong thing or mess up somehow. Sometimes I worry that people are judging me, or that I’ll just look stupid if I speak up. I end up overthinking everything I might say. It’s a lot of pressure.

Most of the time, I just stay quiet and try not to draw attention to myself. I’ll avoid speaking unless I absolutely have to. I sort of just zone out and hope the meeting ends quickly. Afterwards, I beat myself up for not saying anything.

Honestly, when I’m in the moment, it feels pretty strong—maybe like 80 or 90 out of 100. It’s like this voice in my head that just takes over. After the meeting’s over, sometimes it fades a bit, but it’s still hard to shake off.

But, to be honest, I’ve also seen other people mess up and nobody seemed to care much—they just moved on. Sometimes when I have spoken up, nobody said anything negative, but I still feel like I did something wrong.

Mostly stuff about, like, money and work. It just feels like there’s always something else going wrong. I get this heavy feeling when I think about it. Sometimes I just want to ignore it all.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too out of the ordinary—I’ve just been keeping busy, you know? Work’s been a bit hectic, but that’s normal. I’m fine, really.
When you say work has been hectic, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind during those busy moments?

Um, I guess I mostly just worry about keeping up and not letting anyone down. I try to do whatever’s asked, even if it’s a lot, because I don’t want people thinking I can’t handle it. Honestly, sometimes I feel like I have to say yes to everything, just to make sure everyone’s happy with me. I don’t want to be seen as a problem.
That sounds like a lot of pressure. When you say you feel like you have to say yes to everything, what do you notice happens inside you—thoughts or feelings—when you consider saying no?

Hmm, well, honestly, when I even think about saying no, I get really anxious. It’s like, I start worrying that people will be upset with me or just… not want me around anymore. I end up feeling kind of panicky, like I’m about to mess everything up if I let someone down. It’s easier to just go along with what people want, even if I’m tired.
That sounds really tough to carry. When you feel that panic and worry about letting people down, what do you tell yourself about what it would mean if you did say no?

Um… I guess I kind of tell myself that people would think I’m too much trouble, or maybe that I’m not worth the effort. Like, if I say no, I worry they’ll just stop asking me or maybe just… stop including me. I know it probably sounds silly, but it feels really real in the moment. I just don’t want to be a burden to anyone.
That fear of being a burden sounds very real and painful. Can you recall a recent time when you said yes even though you wanted to say no—what were you feeling and thinking right before you agreed?

Yeah, actually, just a few days ago at work, someone asked me to cover their shift last minute. I was exhausted and really wanted to say I couldn’t, but my mind just started racing. I kept thinking, “If I say no, they’ll think I’m selfish or unreliable.” I felt really anxious and kind of desperate for them not to be disappointed in me, so I just said yes automatically.
That sounds like a really intense moment. After you said yes, how did you feel about your decision and the way you handled the situation?

Um, at first I felt a little relieved, just because I didn’t have to face anyone being upset with me. But then, pretty quickly, I started feeling kind of frustrated with myself for not speaking up. I was tired and overwhelmed, but I kept telling myself it was better than risking someone being annoyed or thinking less of me. I guess I just try to brush it off and move on.

ccd.relevant_history: I have often felt stuck in my life, like no matter how hard I try, I can't move forward. Past experiences of failure and lack of support have made me believe that change is impossible for me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to change, I will only end up feeling worse or getting hurt again. No one can really help me, so I have to just accept my situation. Trying to escape this feeling only leads to more disappointment.
ccd.coping_strategies: I avoid making big decisions or taking risks because I fear failure. Sometimes I procrastinate or distract myself with TV or social media to avoid facing how trapped I feel. I try to keep my feelings to myself rather than sharing with others.
ccd.situation: I am thinking about pursuing a new job opportunity that could improve my life, but I feel overwhelmed and unsure if I can handle the change.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I'm going to mess this up like I always do." "There's no way out of this mess." "If I fail, it will prove I’m stuck forever."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, hopelessness, and sadness.
ccd.behaviors: I put off applying for the job and avoid talking about it with friends or family. I spend a lot of time scrolling on my phone to distract myself from these worries.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Nothing much has changed. I’ve just been going through the motions, mostly. Some days just feel heavier than others, you know?
When you notice those heavier days, what kinds of thoughts or feelings tend to come up for you?

I just feel kind of weighed down, like there’s something holding me back. I start thinking that things aren’t really going to get better, no matter what I do. It’s hard to shake that feeling. Sometimes I wonder if it’s even worth trying to change things.
When you have those thoughts about things not getting better, what do you usually do next? How do you respond or cope in those moments?

I kind of just shut down, I guess. I’ll usually distract myself—like watching TV or scrolling on my phone—so I don’t have to think about it too much. I don’t really talk to anyone about it. Mostly, I just try to push those thoughts aside.
When you push those thoughts aside and distract yourself, how does that affect how you feel afterwards? Does it help, or do the feelings come back?

It helps for a little while, I guess—I don’t have to think about it in the moment. But eventually the feelings come back, sometimes even stronger. It’s like I’m just delaying things. I still end up feeling stuck.
What do you think might happen if you allowed yourself to sit with those feelings and thoughts instead of distracting yourself?

Honestly, I think it would just feel overwhelming. I worry that if I let myself really feel it, I’d get even more anxious or upset. I’m not sure I’d know what to do with all of it. It just seems easier to avoid it.
When you imagine feeling that overwhelming anxiety or upset, what do you think would be the most difficult part to handle?
Probably the fear that it’ll never go away. I worry that if I really let myself feel all of it, I’ll just get stuck in that place for good. It’s a scary thought. I guess I’m afraid of losing control.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like people don’t really want me around, especially in groups or when meeting new people. Growing up, I didn't get much attention or affection, which made me feel invisible and unwanted.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to connect with others, I’ll just be rejected or ignored because I’m not interesting or likable. It’s safer not to expect much from people since I’m probably going to be left out anyway.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to stay quiet and avoid joining conversations so I don’t risk being dismissed or hurt. Sometimes I keep my distance or leave social events early to protect myself from feeling unwanted.
ccd.situation: At a party where I don’t know many people, I notice others chatting and laughing, but I feel like I don’t belong and wonder if anyone wants me there.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one wants to talk to me. I’m just boring and don’t fit in here.”
ccd.emotions: Lonely, anxious, and rejected.
ccd.behaviors: I stick to the edges of the room, avoid approaching others, and sometimes leave the party early to avoid feeling worse.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Nothing special, just the usual stuff. I went to a friend’s get-together over the weekend, but I didn’t stay too long. I just kind of felt out of place.
When you noticed feeling out of place at the get-together, what thoughts were running through your mind in that moment?

I just kept thinking that no one really wanted me there. Like, everyone seemed to be having a good time with each other, but I wasn’t really part of it. I wondered if I looked awkward or if people were just being polite to me. It sort of made me want to leave early.
That sounds really tough to experience. When you had those thoughts about not fitting in or being awkward, how did those thoughts affect how you felt emotionally and physically?

Yeah, it’s not a great feeling. I felt kind of tense, like my chest was tight and my stomach was in knots. I got pretty anxious and just wanted to disappear, honestly. Emotionally I felt…kind of alone, even though I was around people.
It sounds like those thoughts really amplified your anxiety and sense of loneliness. Have there been other times recently when you’ve had similar thoughts or feelings in social situations?

Yeah, it happens a lot, honestly. Even at work, when people are chatting in groups, I usually hang back and don’t join in. I worry they’ll just see me as boring or not worth talking to. It’s like… I expect to feel left out most of the time.
When you notice yourself expecting to feel left out or thinking others might find you boring, what do you usually do next? How do you respond or cope in those moments?

Most of the time, I just stay quiet and keep to myself. I’ll avoid jumping into conversations because I don’t want to risk saying something dumb or feeling even more out of place. Sometimes I just find a reason to leave early or go somewhere else, so I don’t have to deal with it. It feels safer that way.

It makes sense that staying quiet feels safer when you’re worried about how others might judge you. Have you noticed any situations where you pushed yourself to join in despite those worries? If so, what happened?

Yeah, there have been a couple times where I tried to join a conversation, just to see what would happen. Honestly, sometimes people would respond, but it always felt kind of forced, like they were just being polite. Other times, I’d say something and the conversation would sort of move on without me. That just made me feel more awkward, so I usually don’t try anymore.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has a history of feeling disconnected from others and struggling with self-acceptance. They often reflect on past mistakes and perceive themselves as fundamentally flawed or bad. These feelings have persisted despite some external successes and positive feedback.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I am not morally upright, then I am unworthy of respect or love. Making mistakes means I am a bad person at my core. Others will judge me harshly if they see my true self.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid deep personal relationships and withdraws from situations where their values might be challenged. They also engage in self-criticism and try to overcompensate by being overly cautious or perfect in other areas of life.
ccd.situation: Facing a new opportunity for personal development that requires vulnerability and openness about their values and feelings.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "If I open up, people will see how immoral I really am." "I don’t deserve to grow or improve because I’m fundamentally bad." "They will reject me once they know the truth about me."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, and sadness dominate when considering personal growth or sharing personal experiences.
ccd.behaviors: The patient withdraws from potential growth activities, avoids discussing personal issues, and maintains emotional distance to protect themselves from perceived judgment.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too eventful. Just kept busy with work and things like that. Not much else to say, really.
When you say "just kept busy," how did that make you feel throughout the week?
I guess it helped distract me a bit, which was fine. But sometimes, when things slow down, I just start overthinking stuff. It gets a little heavy, I suppose. I try not to dwell on it too much.
When you notice yourself starting to overthink, what kinds of thoughts tend to come up for you?

Um...I don’t know, it’s hard to say. Sometimes I just remember things I wish I’d done differently. Other times I wonder if people see me the way I see myself. It’s not really stuff I like thinking about.
What feelings come up for you when you think about how others might see you compared to how you see yourself?
I mostly feel anxious, I guess. Maybe embarrassed, too. Sometimes I worry that if people really knew me, they wouldn’t like what they saw. It’s not a great feeling.
When you notice those anxious or embarrassed feelings, what do you usually do to cope with them?
I usually just keep to myself. I might avoid talking about certain things or just change the subject. Sometimes I make excuses to leave or not join in. It just feels safer that way.
What do you think might happen if you allowed yourself to share some of those thoughts or feelings with someone you trust?
I don’t know...I guess I worry they’d think less of me. Or maybe they’d see me differently, in a bad way. It’s hard to believe anyone would actually understand. I just don’t want to risk it.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated social rejection and criticism throughout their life, leading them to internalize a deep sense of unworthiness. They often feel they have done wrong by others, which reinforces their belief that they are immoral.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I am immoral, then people will think less of me and avoid me. I must hide my true self to avoid being judged or rejected. Making mistakes means I am a bad person.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid social gatherings and conversations to prevent potential judgment. They often apologize excessively and try to please others to mask their perceived flaws.
ccd.situation: Attending a small group meeting where the patient is expected to share their opinions and engage with others.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to say something stupid and everyone will see how bad I really am.” “They’ll think I’m a bad person if I don’t say the right thing.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, and sadness.
ccd.behaviors: The patient stays quiet during the meeting, avoids eye contact, and leaves early to escape the discomfort of feeling exposed.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it’s been alright, I guess. I’ve just been going to work and trying to keep busy. Nothing much happened, really.
When you say “trying to keep busy,” what kinds of things have you been doing to stay occupied?
Mostly just work stuff, and then when I get home I watch TV or clean up a bit. I try to keep my mind off things, I guess. If I have too much free time, I start overthinking.
What kinds of thoughts tend to come up when you find yourself overthinking?

I just start replaying things I said or did around people. Like, wondering if I came off the wrong way or if I upset someone without realizing. Sometimes I can’t shake the feeling that I did something wrong, even if it was a small thing. It just kind of sticks with me.
When you notice those thoughts about possibly upsetting someone, what feelings usually come up for you in those moments?
I feel pretty anxious, mostly. Sometimes I just feel… guilty, or kind of ashamed. Like I messed up and people probably noticed. It’s hard to stop thinking about it.
How do you usually respond when those anxious or guilty feelings come up—do you do or say anything to yourself or others?

Sometimes I’ll text the person later and apologize, even if I’m not sure I actually did something wrong. Or I just try to avoid talking to them for a while. I kind of replay what happened in my head, trying to figure out what I did wrong. Most of the time, I just try to lay low and not draw attention to myself.
When you apologize or avoid the person, what impact does that have on how you feel afterward?

If I apologize, I might feel a little relieved for a bit, but then I start worrying that I made it awkward or that they’ll think I’m weird. When I avoid people, it feels safer in the moment, but then I end up feeling alone. I guess either way, I still feel kind of bad. It’s like I can’t really win.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has a long-standing history of feeling inadequate and guilty, often blaming themselves for past financial mistakes. They grew up in an environment where money was tight and moral character was closely tied to financial success.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m struggling financially, it means I’m a bad person. People will think less of me if they find out I’m not managing my money well. I have to keep my financial troubles a secret to avoid judgment.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid discussing money issues with others and tries to hide financial problems. They may work excessively or avoid making financial decisions altogether to escape feelings of guilt and shame.
ccd.situation: Receiving a past-due notice for an unpaid bill and realizing they don’t have enough funds to cover their expenses this month.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m so irresponsible and immoral for letting this happen. I don’t deserve to have a stable life. Everyone will see me as a failure.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, and hopelessness dominate, making the patient feel small and unworthy.
ccd.behaviors: Withdrawal from friends and family, avoiding phone calls about finances, and procrastinating on addressing bills or seeking help.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Just busy with work and stuff. Nothing too exciting.
What kinds of thoughts have been going through your mind when you’re busy with work?
Um, I don’t know… mostly just trying to keep up and not mess things up. Sometimes I worry about forgetting something important. I guess I just feel kind of tense a lot of the time.
When you notice that tension or worry about forgetting something, what do you usually tell yourself in those moments?
I just think I need to do better, or that I should have planned things out more. Sometimes I get frustrated with myself. I try not to dwell on it, but it’s hard not to.
What do you notice happens to your mood or energy after you tell yourself you need to do better or get frustrated?
I usually feel kind of drained, honestly. It makes me want to just avoid things for a while. Sometimes I just get really quiet and keep to myself.
When you choose to keep to yourself and avoid things, what do you hope will happen or feel different?
I guess I hope the feeling will just go away if I don’t think about it. Maybe things will just sort themselves out on their own. It’s easier not to deal with it sometimes.
What do you think might happen if you did try to face those feelings or worries instead of avoiding them?
I don’t know. I guess I worry it’ll just get worse if I look at it too closely. Maybe I’ll realize I’m messing up even more than I thought. It just feels like a lot to deal with.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and rejected by family members, which made me believe that I wasn’t worthy of love or attention. Over time, this feeling has stuck with me, especially when I face challenges in forming close relationships. These early experiences have shaped how I see myself and others now.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t constantly try to please others, they will leave me or reject me. I have to put their needs before my own to avoid being alone. Relationships are fragile, and I must work hard to keep people around because I’m not naturally lovable.
ccd.coping_strategies: I often say yes to people even when I don’t want to, hoping they will like me more. I avoid sharing my true feelings or needs because I’m afraid it will push others away. Sometimes, I overwork myself trying to gain approval or avoid conflict.
ccd.situation: When a friend cancels plans last minute or doesn’t respond to my messages, I immediately feel like I’m being rejected or abandoned. This kind of daily disappointment reinforces my fear that I will always end up alone.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They don’t want to spend time with me because I’m not interesting or lovable.” “I’m destined to be alone.” “If I don’t keep trying so hard, I’ll lose everyone.”
ccd.emotions: I feel sadness, anxiety, and a deep sense of loneliness. At times, I also feel helpless and scared that things will never change.
ccd.behaviors: I try to call or text repeatedly to make sure people remember me. I avoid expressing my real feelings and instead focus on making others comfortable. Sometimes, I withdraw completely when I feel overwhelmed by rejection.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. I’ve just been keeping busy with work and stuff. Nothing too unusual, really. How are you?
Thanks for sharing—when you say you’ve been keeping busy with work, what kind of thoughts or feelings come up for you during your workday?

Oh, um, I guess I mostly just try to focus on what needs to get done. Sometimes I worry about making mistakes or disappointing people, but I try not to let it show. I just want everyone to feel like they can count on me. It’s probably nothing, though.
It sounds like you put a lot of pressure on yourself to be dependable—when you notice those worries about making mistakes or disappointing others, what do you tell yourself in those moments?

Hmm, I usually just tell myself I have to do better, or that I can’t mess up. I guess I worry people will be upset with me if I let them down. I try to remind myself that it’s not a big deal, but I still feel a bit anxious inside. I don’t really talk about it much, though.
That sounds really stressful. When you feel that anxiety and those thoughts telling you "you have to do better," how does your body feel or react?

Yeah, I guess it is kind of stressful. Sometimes my chest feels tight, and I notice my heart beating faster. I’ll get a little shaky or tense, especially if something goes wrong or someone seems upset. Mostly, I just try to push those feelings aside and keep going.
When you push those feelings aside and keep going, what usually happens to your anxiety or your thoughts afterward?

Usually, the anxiety sticks around for a while, even if I try to ignore it. I find myself replaying things in my head, wondering if I did something wrong or if someone’s upset with me. Sometimes it gets a bit exhausting, but I just try to move on and hope it passes. I don’t want to be a burden or make things awkward.
It sounds like those worries really linger for you. When you replay those moments in your head, are there any particular patterns or themes in what you tell yourself?

Um, I guess I usually think things like, “Why did I say that?” or “They probably think I’m annoying.” I worry I might have upset someone without realizing it. Sometimes I feel like maybe I’m just too much for people or not interesting enough. I know it sounds kind of silly, but I can’t help it.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and unsupported by my family, which made me believe that I couldn't rely on myself or others. These experiences left me feeling vulnerable and unsure about my ability to handle challenges independently. Over time, this led to a deep sense that I am always in need of help to get through difficult situations.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I believe that unless someone else is there to guide or support me, I won’t be able to succeed or manage my problems. I often think that asking for help is the only way to avoid failure, and that I’m too weak or incapable to handle things on my own. This makes me feel like I’m a burden to others, but also that I have no choice but to depend on them.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to seek constant reassurance from friends or family, frequently asking for advice or help even with small tasks. Sometimes, I avoid taking on responsibilities alone because I’m afraid I’ll mess up. At other times, I withdraw or feel stuck, waiting for someone else to take the lead or make decisions for me.
ccd.situation: I’m facing a new opportunity for personal growth, like starting a new job or taking on a big project, and I feel overwhelmed by the thought of doing it without a lot of support. I worry that I won’t be able to manage the pressures and that I’ll mess things up if I try to handle it all by myself.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “What if I can’t do this on my own? I’m probably going to fail if I don’t have someone there to help me every step of the way.” “I’m so needy—people are going to get tired of me asking for help all the time.” “I don’t have what it takes to succeed without someone else’s support.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious and overwhelmed, with a heavy sense of insecurity weighing me down. There’s also a deep sadness mixed with frustration, because I want to be more independent but feel trapped by my neediness. At times, I feel ashamed for relying so much on others.
ccd.behaviors: I reach out repeatedly to others for guidance and reassurance, sometimes even before I’ve tried to solve problems myself.
