I procrastinate or avoid taking initiative, waiting for someone else to step in. Occasionally, I withdraw or shut down emotionally when I feel my needs aren’t being met quickly enough.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been kind of up and down, I guess. Work has been extra hectic lately, and I’ve been feeling kind of on edge about some things that came up. I keep finding myself overthinking even small decisions, like what emails to send or how to phrase things, and it just gets really overwhelming. I don’t know, I just feel like everything is a bit much right now.
When you notice yourself overthinking those small decisions, what kinds of thoughts usually go through your mind?

Usually, it’s like this running commentary in my head, questioning if I’m making the right choice, or if I’m about to make a mistake that I won’t be able to fix. I’ll catch myself thinking things like, “What if I say the wrong thing and things spiral out of control?” or “Maybe I should ask someone else before I do this.” Sometimes I just get stuck, and I’ll stare at my screen for ages, worrying that whatever I do is going to turn out badly if I try to handle it alone.
When those thoughts come up, how do they affect how you feel physically or emotionally in that moment?

I get really tense, honestly—my shoulders get all tight and my stomach feels kind of knotted. Emotionally, it’s like this wave of anxiety just washes over me, and sometimes I feel embarrassed for not being able to handle things on my own. It’s almost like I can’t catch my breath; my mind just races and I start to feel pretty helpless. It makes me want to just hide or wish someone else could step in and make the call for me.
What do you usually do when you start to feel that tension and anxiety—do you have any go-to ways to cope or respond?

Yeah, I guess my first instinct is to reach out to someone—like, I’ll text a friend or a family member and ask what they think I should do, even if it’s something small. Sometimes I’ll just sit there waiting for a response before I move forward, which honestly makes me even more anxious if they take a while to reply. If I can’t get in touch with anyone, I tend to just freeze and put off the decision altogether. It’s like I’m stuck until someone reassures me or tells me what to do.
When you wait for others’ input or freeze in those moments, what do you notice happens to your anxiety or confidence over time?

Well, it’s weird, because in the short term I feel a bit of relief when someone finally responds and tells me what to do, but it doesn’t last very long. The next time something comes up, I just feel even less sure of myself, like I can’t trust my own judgment at all. If no one answers or if I have to wait a long time, the anxiety just kind of builds and I end up feeling even more helpless. I guess it’s like each time, my confidence gets chipped away a little more.
Have you noticed any situations where you felt more confident or able to make decisions without needing reassurance? What was different about those times?

Yeah, there have been a few times, but they feel pretty rare. Usually it's with really small things, like picking what to eat for lunch or choosing what movie to watch—stuff where the stakes feel super low and I know no one else will be affected if I mess up. In those moments, I guess I don’t worry so much about the outcome or about letting anyone down. But when it comes to bigger stuff, or anything related to work or other people, it feels way harder—I feel like I just freeze up again.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt like I didn’t measure up to my family’s expectations. Whenever I tried something new, I was met with criticism or indifference, which made me doubt my abilities and feel like I was always failing.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to improve or grow, I’m bound to mess up and be seen as useless. People won’t accept me unless I’m perfect, and if I show any weakness, I’ll be rejected or ignored.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid taking risks or trying new things because I’m afraid of failing. Sometimes, I push myself really hard to prove I’m not defective, but when I slip up, I get very down and withdraw.
ccd.situation: I was offered a chance to take on a new responsibility at work that could help me grow, but I hesitated to accept it because I worried I wouldn’t do well.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to mess this up and everyone will see I’m not good enough.” “If I fail, it’ll prove I’m defective.” “I’m better off not trying than embarrassing myself.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and fear of rejection wash over me. I feel hopeless and frustrated with myself.
ccd.behaviors: I declined the opportunity and spent the day avoiding thinking about work. Later, I isolated myself and didn’t share my feelings with anyone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been kind of up and down, I guess. Work’s been busy and something came up—a chance to take on a new task. I didn’t really feel up to it, so I let it pass. Other than that, nothing major.
What thoughts went through your mind when you decided not to take on that new task?

I just kept thinking I wouldn’t do a good job. Like, if I tried and messed it up, everyone would notice and judge me for it. I felt pretty sure it would end badly if I said yes. So I figured it was safer to just say no.
What feelings did you notice in your body or mind when you imagined others noticing and judging you?

Honestly, my chest got tight and my stomach felt tense. I could just picture everyone looking at me and thinking I’m not good enough. It made me feel nervous and… kind of embarrassed, I guess. I just wanted to hide.
When you think back to times you’ve taken on challenges before, can you recall any moments where things didn’t go perfectly, but you managed okay or even learned something?

Hmm… I’m trying to think. Most of what comes to mind is when things didn’t work out, and I just felt like I’d let people down. Maybe there were times I got through stuff, but honestly, I usually focus more on what went wrong than anything I might have done right. It’s hard to remember the good parts.
What do you think it would be like to notice both the things that went well and the things that didn’t, instead of just focusing on what went wrong?

I don’t really know—it feels strange to even try. I guess maybe I wouldn’t feel so weighed down or hopeless if I could see both sides. But it’s just easier for my mind to point out the mistakes. The idea of giving myself credit is kind of uncomfortable.
What might be a small, manageable way to practice noticing one thing you did well recently, even if it feels uncomfortable at first?

I could try to pick out one thing at the end of the day, even if it’s something small, like helping a coworker or finishing a task. Maybe I could write it down, so I don’t just brush past it. It’ll probably feel awkward, though. But I guess I could try, just to see how it goes.

ccd.relevant_history: I have always felt like things happen to me without my control, especially at work where I often get overwhelmed by deadlines and demands. In past jobs, I struggled with feeling powerless and doubted my ability to influence outcomes.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to stand up for myself, I might make things worse or get blamed. People in authority are usually too strong for me to challenge, so it’s safer to just accept what happens. Speaking up won’t change anything because I’m stuck in this situation.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid confrontation and keep my head down to avoid drawing attention. Sometimes I distract myself with social media or leave tasks unfinished because I feel defeated before I start.
ccd.situation: At work, my manager gave me extra tasks with tight deadlines, and I felt overwhelmed and unsure if I could manage it all.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “There’s nothing I can do about this.” “I’m always the one stuck with too much.” “I’m just a victim of circumstances here.”
ccd.emotions: Helplessness, frustration, anxiety.
ccd.behaviors: I procrastinate on the tasks, avoid asking for help, and isolate myself from colleagues to avoid showing I’m struggling.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Uh, it’s been kind of rough, honestly. Work’s been piling up and I just feel like I can’t keep up. I keep thinking maybe I should be able to handle it, but I’m just tired. I don’t know, maybe it’s just one of those weeks.
When you notice yourself thinking that you should be able to handle everything, what kinds of feelings or physical sensations come up for you?

I get this tight feeling in my chest, like I can’t breathe properly. Mostly I just feel anxious, kind of on edge all day. Sometimes I get headaches too. It’s like my body tenses up whenever I think about all the stuff I have to do.
When those anxious feelings and physical tension come up, what do you usually do in response?

Honestly, I usually just kind of freeze. I end up scrolling on my phone or staring at my computer without getting much done. Sometimes I just avoid talking to anyone at work. It feels easier to just zone out than try to figure out what to do first.
What thoughts go through your mind when you notice yourself zoning out or avoiding tasks like that?

I usually think, “What’s the point? I’m just going to fall behind anyway.” Sometimes it’s like, “Everyone else can handle this, why can’t I?” Or I’ll tell myself, “No matter what I do, it won’t make a difference.” It’s kind of this heavy feeling, like nothing I try will really help.
When you tell yourself those things, how does it affect your motivation or confidence to start or continue your work?

It just makes me want to give up before I even start. I feel like there’s no point in trying because I’ll probably mess up or get overwhelmed anyway. My confidence takes a big hit, so I just end up putting things off even more. It’s like I’m stuck in this loop.
Can you think of a recent time when you started a task despite feeling that way—what was different about that situation?

Yeah, actually, last week I had to finish a report, and I just forced myself to start it because it was really urgent. I still felt pretty anxious, but I guess the pressure pushed me to do it. I can’t say I felt confident, but I just kept telling myself I didn’t have a choice. It wasn’t easy, but I got through it somehow.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up in a family where conflicts were frequent and unresolved, I often felt powerless to change the situation or speak up for myself. Past experiences of being ignored or dismissed by family members have reinforced a sense that I can’t influence what happens around me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to assert myself, it won’t make any difference and might even make things worse. I have to accept things as they are because I don’t have the strength or ability to change them. Speaking up will only lead to rejection or further conflict.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to withdraw from family conversations and avoid expressing my feelings to prevent arguments. Sometimes I keep my opinions to myself, hoping that by staying quiet, I won’t cause more problems or feel vulnerable.
ccd.situation: During a recent family gathering, when a disagreement arose about holiday plans, I felt overwhelmed and stayed silent even though I had my own preferences. I felt like my voice didn’t matter and that I couldn’t influence the outcome.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one cares what I think.” “It’s pointless for me to say anything.” “If I speak up, I’ll just make things worse.” “I’m stuck and can’t change anything.”
ccd.emotions: I felt frustrated, sad, and powerless. There was also a sense of anxiety about causing more tension if I expressed myself.
ccd.behaviors: I withdrew from the conversation and avoided eye contact, keeping my opinions to myself. Later, I felt distant from my family and avoided future discussions to protect myself from feeling helpless again.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

I mean, it’s been whatever. Nothing really new—just the same stuff with my family, honestly. I don’t even know what you want me to say—things don’t really change for me. It’s kind of exhausting, to be honest.
That sounds tough, feeling stuck in the same patterns can be really draining—when you say it’s exhausting, what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you in those moments?

Honestly, it just feels pointless. Like, why even bother saying anything or trying to make things different? No one listens anyway, so what’s the use? I just end up feeling frustrated or like it’s all out of my hands.
It sounds like a sense of helplessness is coming up for you—can you tell me about a recent time when you felt this way with your family? What was happening then?

I mean, sure, whatever. We had this argument about holiday plans a few days ago. Everyone was talking over each other, and I just… sat there, didn’t say anything. What’s the point? They don’t care what I want anyway.
When you chose to stay quiet during that argument, what were you telling yourself about yourself or the situation in that moment?

I just kept thinking, “No one’s going to listen to me, so why bother?” And honestly, I felt like if I did speak up, I’d either get ignored or somehow make things worse. It always feels like I’m not really part of the conversation, just… there. So, yeah, I just told myself to keep quiet and get it over with.
That sounds really painful, feeling invisible in your own family conversations—how did you feel emotionally and physically when you stayed quiet during that argument?

I mean, I felt pretty crappy, if I’m being honest. It’s like this tight, heavy feeling in my chest—frustrated, sad, kind of anxious, all at once. I just wanted to get out of there or disappear altogether. Later, I felt kind of numb and just wanted to avoid everyone.
Thank you for sharing that—it sounds really intense. When you notice that tight, heavy feeling in your chest during those moments, what do you usually do next? How do you respond to those feelings?

I pretty much just shut down. I stop talking, avoid looking at anyone, and just hope it ends soon. Sometimes I’ll go to the bathroom or just leave the room for a bit. Honestly, I just want to escape the whole situation.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked by my family and friends, which made me believe that I wasn’t worthy of attention or love. Past relationships ended with partners saying I wasn’t attractive or interesting enough, reinforcing these feelings.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m not attractive, people won’t want to be close to me. I have to hide my true self to avoid rejection. It’s safer not to get too involved because I’ll just end up hurt or abandoned.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid social gatherings and keep conversations superficial to prevent others from noticing my flaws. Sometimes I make jokes or change the subject quickly when compliments or personal questions come up to deflect attention.
ccd.situation: I’m on a date, and my date pauses before responding to something I said, making me think they’re losing interest.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They’re probably thinking I’m boring or ugly. I bet they don’t want to see me again.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, sad, and rejected. There’s a knot in my stomach, and I’m worried I’m going to be alone.
ccd.behaviors: I withdraw from the conversation, become quiet, and avoid eye contact. I might cut the date short or make an excuse to leave early.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been… I guess fine, mostly normal, you know? Work’s been kind of busy, so that kept me distracted. Oh, I did try out this new coffee place, but honestly, I don’t really know if I liked it—the chairs were kind of uncomfortable, but I guess that’s not really important. Anyway, nothing too exciting happened.
When you think about the busy work and the coffee place, what thoughts or feelings come up for you in those moments?

Hmm… I guess at work I just try to keep my head down and not draw too much attention, so I don’t really think about much else when I’m there. At the coffee place, I was mostly wondering if people were looking at me or judging what I ordered—like, sometimes I feel like I stick out, or maybe that’s just in my head. Oh, and actually, the barista kind of hesitated before handing me my drink, and for a second I thought maybe there was something off about me. But, I mean, maybe they were just busy or something.
When you notice yourself worrying about what others might be thinking or feeling judged, how does your body feel or how do you usually react in those moments?

Um, well… I get kind of tense? Like my shoulders get tight, and sometimes my stomach feels weird, like there’s this sinking feeling. Usually, I just try to finish up whatever I’m doing fast and get out of there, or if I’m with someone, I’ll just nod along and not say much. Actually, sometimes I end up overthinking later—like replaying what happened in my head after I get home.
When you find yourself replaying those moments later, what kinds of thoughts or questions tend to come up for you during that overthinking?

Yeah, I guess I start wondering if I said something weird, or if I looked awkward. I’ll think, like, “Did I make a bad impression?” or “Maybe they thought I was annoying.” Sometimes I even remember little things, like how I laughed or what I was wearing, and I just feel embarrassed all over again. Oh, and then I sort of convince myself that they probably don’t want to see me again, even if there’s no real reason.
When you notice those thoughts about making a bad impression or feeling embarrassed, how strongly do you believe them on a scale from 0 to 100?

Hmm, I don’t know… I guess it depends, but in the moment, it feels really true, like maybe 80 or 90? Later on, if I try to be logical, it drops a bit, but it still kind of lingers, if that makes sense. Sometimes I wish I could just let things go, but my mind just keeps bringing them up. Oh, actually, it reminds me of this time in high school when I tripped walking up the stairs in front of everyone—honestly, I still think about that.
That sounds like a tough experience to carry with you. When you think about that high school moment, what feelings come up for you now?

Yeah, it’s weird how something like that just sticks, you know? I still feel embarrassed, even though it was so long ago—it’s like my face gets hot just remembering it. Sometimes I wonder if other people even remember, but for me, it’s hard to shake off. Actually, it makes me want to just blend in more so nothing like that happens again.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and unable to influence decisions in my family. Whenever conflicts arose, my opinions were dismissed, reinforcing a feeling that I had no control over what happened around me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to speak up, it won’t make a difference. No one listens to me, so it’s better to stay quiet. I don’t have the power to change my situation or make things better.
ccd.coping_strategies: I usually withdraw and keep to myself to avoid conflict. Sometimes I avoid family gatherings altogether because I feel overwhelmed and incapable of handling the interactions.
ccd.situation: During a recent family discussion about plans for the holidays, my suggestions were ignored, and decisions were made without my input.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They don’t care what I think.” “I’m useless in this family.” “There’s nothing I can do to change this.”
ccd.emotions: I feel sad, frustrated, and powerless. There’s a deep sense of loneliness and discouragement.
ccd.behaviors: I stayed silent during the conversation and avoided bringing up my ideas again. Later, I isolated myself and kept my distance from family members to protect myself from feeling hurt.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Uh... it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too special. Just the usual stuff.
When you say "the usual stuff," what kinds of things have you been doing or thinking about most days?
Mostly just work and keeping busy around the house. I’ve been spending time alone, reading or watching TV. I haven’t really done much else.
When you’re spending time alone like that, what kinds of thoughts usually come up for you?

I don’t know, not much really. Sometimes I just think about things that happened during the day. I guess sometimes I wonder if people notice me or care what I think. But I try not to focus on that too much.
What feelings come up for you when you wonder if people notice or care about what you think?
Um… I guess I feel kind of sad. Maybe a bit frustrated, too. It just makes me want to keep to myself. It’s easier that way.
When you choose to keep to yourself, what do you notice happens to those feelings of sadness and frustration?
They sort of fade into the background, I guess. I don’t think about them as much when I’m alone. But sometimes it still feels kind of heavy. It doesn’t really go away.
What do you think makes those feelings feel heavy, even when they’re more in the background?
I’m not sure. Maybe because it just keeps happening. Like, even if I try not to think about it, it’s still there underneath. It just feels like nothing really changes.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like I had to prove myself at work, especially after a few times when I struggled to meet deadlines. I tend to avoid asking for help because I’m afraid it will make me look weak or incapable.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t please everyone and do everything perfectly, people will think I’m useless. I must always be competent and in control, or else I’ll fail and be rejected.
ccd.coping_strategies: I often say yes to extra tasks even when I’m overwhelmed, trying to keep others happy. I avoid speaking up about difficulties and push myself to work harder, even if it means feeling exhausted or anxious.
ccd.situation: I’m given a new project at work with a tight deadline and high expectations from my boss.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to mess this up because I’m not good enough.” “If I don’t get this right, everyone will think I’m incompetent.” “I have to work extra hard to prove I’m capable.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, fear of failure, and a heavy sense of pressure. I also feel vulnerable and overwhelmed.
ccd.behaviors: I work long hours trying to get everything perfect, avoid asking for help, and try to keep everyone happy by agreeing to all requests, even when I’m exhausted.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been a little busy, but that’s pretty normal for me. I’m trying to keep up with everything, and I think I’m managing alright. I hope I’m not taking up too much of your time with all this.
It sounds like you’re juggling quite a bit—what’s been on your mind the most when you think about work or managing everything?

Yeah, I guess there’s always a lot going on. Lately, I’ve just been thinking about this new project my boss gave me. It’s got a tight deadline, and I really want to make sure I don’t let anyone down. I just want to do a good job, you know?
That sounds important to you—what kinds of thoughts come up for you when you think about meeting that deadline and doing a good job?

Um, well… I guess I just worry a bit about whether I can actually pull it off. Sometimes I catch myself thinking, what if I make a mistake or miss something? I don’t want people to think I can’t handle it. I usually just try to focus and work harder so I don’t mess up.
When you notice those worries about making a mistake or missing something, how do those thoughts affect how you feel or what you do next?

I guess I start to feel pretty anxious—my chest gets tight, and I get kind of restless. I usually end up staying late at work to double-check everything, even if I’m tired. I don’t really let myself slow down until I’m sure it’s perfect. I just want to make sure no one has any reason to doubt me.

It sounds like that anxiety really pushes you to keep going, even when you’re tired. What do you think would happen if you allowed yourself to slow down a bit, even if things weren’t absolutely perfect?

Oh, um, I’m not really sure. I mean, I guess I worry that if I slow down, I’ll miss something important or people might notice mistakes. That would feel really embarrassing for me. I just really don’t want anyone thinking I can’t handle things.
That fear of being seen as unable to handle things sounds really strong—how do you usually respond to those feelings of embarrassment or worry when they come up?

Honestly, I mostly just try to hide it. I put on a smile and act like everything’s fine, even if I’m nervous inside. I usually just keep working harder and don’t really let anyone see that I’m struggling. I don’t want to bother people with my problems.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

ccd.relevant_history: I've always felt like people don’t truly care about me, especially after my parents divorced when I was a kid. Over time, I started expecting that anyone close to me will eventually leave or stop loving me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I get close to someone, they will end up abandoning me. It’s safer not to let people in because then I won’t get hurt. People only stick around if I’m perfect or don’t show my flaws.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to keep people at a distance emotionally or avoid starting new relationships. When I feel someone pulling away, I either withdraw completely or try too hard to please them to avoid being left.
ccd.situation: I started dating someone new and after a couple of weeks, they didn’t text me back as quickly as usual. I immediately felt like they were losing interest and would leave me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They’re going to abandon me like everyone else.” “I’m not good enough for them to stay.” “If I don’t act perfect, they’ll leave.”
ccd.emotions: I felt anxious, sad, and scared. There was also a deep sense of loneliness and hopelessness about ever being loved.
ccd.behaviors: I stopped initiating contact, hoping they wouldn’t notice me slipping away. At the same time, I became overly eager to please when we did talk, trying to hold on to the relationship.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It's been alright, I guess. Nothing big happened, just work and stuff. I did feel kind of off a couple of days ago, but I’m not really sure why. Probably just tired.
When you say you felt "kind of off," can you describe what thoughts or feelings were going through your mind at that time?

I just felt sort of anxious, like something was wrong even if I couldn't put my finger on it. I kept checking my phone more than usual, waiting for a message from someone. It made me feel uneasy, like I was on edge the whole time.
What do you think might have made you feel like something was wrong, even if you weren’t sure what it was?

I guess... maybe because someone I’ve been talking to didn’t reply as fast as they usually do. It’s probably nothing, but I started worrying they were pulling away from me. I know it sounds kind of silly. But it just got stuck in my head.
What kinds of thoughts went through your mind when you started worrying that they might be pulling away?

I just kept thinking maybe they lost interest or got bored with me. Part of me felt like I must’ve done something wrong. I even wondered if I was annoying or if they just didn’t want to talk to me anymore. It made me feel kind of stupid for even caring this much.
When you notice those thoughts about being annoying or having done something wrong, how do they affect how you feel and what you do next?

Honestly, it makes me feel pretty down and nervous, like I’m bracing myself for bad news or something. I tend to back off and stop reaching out as much, even though I kind of want to. Sometimes I’ll try a little too hard to make sure I’m not bothering them, like being extra careful about what I say. It’s like I don't want to give them a reason to actually leave.
What do you think might happen if you were to reach out to them without trying so hard to avoid bothering them?

I worry they might get annoyed or feel like I’m too much, and then they’d just stop talking to me completely. It’s like I feel safer holding back, even if it doesn’t really make me feel any better. I guess part of me thinks I’d rather disappear first than have them reject me. It’s kind of exhausting.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has struggled with managing money for several years, often feeling overwhelmed by bills and unexpected expenses. Past experiences of financial instability have reinforced a sense of powerlessness. They have rarely sought help or advice, fearing judgment or failure.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I can’t control my finances perfectly, I’m a failure." "No one can help me; I have to handle this alone." "Money problems mean I’m not capable or strong enough."
ccd.coping_strategies: Avoiding looking at bank statements or bills to escape the stress. Withdrawing from conversations about finances or future planning. Occasionally spending impulsively to momentarily feel better, despite long-term worries.
ccd.situation: Facing a mounting pile of unpaid bills and an uncertain income next month, the patient feels trapped and unsure how to make ends meet.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m going to lose everything." "There’s no way out for me." "I’m just helpless when it comes to money."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, hopelessness, and frustration dominate, alongside feelings of shame and fear about the future.
ccd.behaviors: Procrastinating on addressing bills, avoiding phone calls from creditors, and isolating themselves from friends and family who might offer support.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Honestly, not great. I’d rather not get into all the details right now, but it just feels like one thing after another. I’m kinda tired of talking about it, to be honest. It’s not like anything’s going to change anyway.
I hear that it feels overwhelming and exhausting right now; when things pile up like this, what tends to run through your mind about how you’ll handle it?

I don’t know, I just... I mostly try not to think about it, actually. Whenever I do, it just freaks me out more. It feels like there's no way to actually fix anything, so what's the point? I just end up feeling stuck.
When you notice those thoughts that nothing can be fixed, what emotions come up for you in those moments?

I guess it’s a mix of things—mostly just anxiety, honestly. Sometimes I get really angry, like, why does it always end up like this? Then there’s this sort of... sinking feeling, like I’ve messed everything up. I’d rather not sit with those feelings for too long.
That sinking feeling sounds really tough; when you notice it coming on, what do you usually do to try to cope or manage those feelings?

I just try to distract myself, I guess. I’ll scroll on my phone or watch something, anything to not think about it. Sometimes I just ignore everything—like, I won’t even open my mail. It’s easier to pretend it’s not there than deal with it.
Distracting yourself makes sense when things feel overwhelming; have you noticed if avoiding those stressors tends to help you feel better long-term or if it sometimes leads to more stress later?

Honestly, it just makes things worse in the long run. Stuff piles up, and then I get even more stressed out when I finally have to face it. But at the moment, it feels like the only way to get any relief. I just… I don’t know how else I’m supposed to handle it.
It sounds like distraction offers short-term relief but adds pressure later; what do you think might happen if you tried facing one small thing at a time instead of avoiding everything?

I don’t know… I guess I feel like even doing one thing is just going to remind me how much else I’ve messed up. And what if I don’t do it right? Then I’ll just feel worse anyway. Honestly, it sounds kind of pointless.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has often felt overwhelmed by work demands and has struggled with a sense of control in previous jobs. They recall experiences where they were unable to influence outcomes despite their efforts, reinforcing a feeling of helplessness.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I can’t handle this, it means I’m weak." "No matter what I do, things won’t get better." "I have to keep pushing through alone or I’ll fail."
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid asking for help and works longer hours to try to manage tasks alone. They also ruminate about mistakes and worries, which leads to increased stress and burnout.
ccd.situation: Facing tight deadlines and high expectations from supervisors at work, the patient feels overwhelmed by the volume and complexity of tasks.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m going to mess this up." "There’s nothing I can do to fix this." "I’m not capable of handling this pressure."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, frustration, and a sense of hopelessness dominate the patient’s emotional experience during work pressure.
ccd.behaviors: The patient isolates themselves from colleagues, procrastinates on difficult tasks, and experiences difficulty concentrating, which further impacts performance.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been pretty intense—there’s a lot on my plate right now. I keep feeling like I’m behind, no matter how much I do. It’s just a bit exhausting.
When you notice that feeling of being behind at work, what kinds of thoughts usually run through your mind?

I usually just think, like, “I’m not going to catch up.” Or, “I’m probably screwing something up.” Sometimes I just feel like there’s nothing I can really do to make it better. It’s hard to shake off that kind of thinking once it starts.
When those thoughts come up, how do they affect the way you feel emotionally or physically?

I get anxious—like this tight feeling in my chest, and it’s hard to focus. I feel tense all over, and sometimes my stomach hurts. Emotionally, it’s mostly frustration and stress, but sometimes it just feels kind of hopeless. It’s like I’m stuck in it and can’t get out.
When you notice that tightness and those feelings of frustration or hopelessness, what do you usually do next?

I usually just keep working, hoping if I push through it’ll get better. Sometimes I end up just staring at my screen, not really doing anything. I don’t really talk to anyone about it. Mostly, I just try to handle it on my own.
What do you think happens to your work or your feelings when you push through versus when you find yourself just staring at the screen?

When I push through, sometimes I get things done, but it feels forced and I’m still stressed. If I’m just staring at the screen, I get even more behind and start feeling worse about myself. Either way, I don’t really feel any relief. It just keeps piling up, you know?
When you imagine a moment during your workday where you feel less stressed or more in control, what does that look like?

I guess it would be a time when I actually know what I’m doing and things are clear. Maybe if I finished something and didn’t immediately have a dozen more tasks waiting. It’d feel calm, like I could just breathe for a second. I don’t really remember the last time it felt like that, though.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and unable to influence what happened around me, which made me doubt my ability to handle challenges. Times when I tried to assert myself were met with criticism or dismissal, reinforcing a sense of weakness and vulnerability. These experiences have shaped how I see myself and my potential for personal growth.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to take control or make changes, I’ll probably fail or be judged harshly. It’s safer to avoid risks because I won’t be able to handle setbacks. People like me just don’t have the strength to improve or stand up for themselves.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to keep my thoughts and feelings to myself, avoiding confrontation or new challenges. I often procrastinate or give up quickly when things feel difficult. Sometimes I withdraw from social situations where I might feel exposed or vulnerable.
ccd.situation: I have the opportunity to take on a new project at work that could help me develop new skills and advance, but I feel unsure about whether I’m capable of handling it.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: What if I mess up and everyone sees I’m not good enough? I don’t have what it takes to succeed at this. It’s probably better if I don’t even try because I’ll just prove that I’m weak.
ccd.emotions: Anxiety and self-doubt take over, making me feel overwhelmed and discouraged. There’s also a deep sadness and frustration at feeling stuck and powerless.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid volunteering for the project or delay making any decisions related to it. I keep a low profile and limit my involvement to only what is absolutely necessary, missing chances for growth.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Work’s been busy, but nothing too out of the ordinary. I mostly just kept to myself and got through things. Not much else to say, really.
When you say you “kept to yourself,” what kinds of thoughts or feelings were coming up for you during those moments?

Um… I don’t know. I just felt like it was easier not to draw attention to myself. Sometimes I get kind of anxious if I think people are watching what I’m doing. I guess I just feel more comfortable staying in the background.
When you notice that feeling of anxiety about being watched, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind in those situations?

I usually start worrying that I’ll mess something up or say the wrong thing. I keep thinking people might be judging me. It just makes me want to disappear, honestly. I try not to let it show, but it gets pretty uncomfortable.
What do you imagine would happen if someone did notice a mistake or something you said that felt “wrong” to you?

I guess I worry they’d think less of me. Maybe they’d see me as not good enough, or just…weak. I don’t know, I just get this sense that it’d confirm whatever doubts they already have about me. It’s hard not to get stuck on that.
When you notice those doubts about how others might judge you, how do you usually respond to yourself in those moments?

Most of the time, I just keep quiet and try not to make it worse. I tell myself it’s better not to say anything than risk looking stupid. Sometimes I feel pretty frustrated with myself, but I just end up shutting down. I don’t really know what else to do.
Have there been times when you chose to speak up or take a risk despite these worries—what happened, and how did you feel afterward?

Not very often, to be honest. The few times I did, I felt really exposed, like everyone was waiting for me to slip up. Sometimes people didn’t react much, but I couldn’t stop replaying it in my head and thinking about what I could’ve done wrong. It usually just made me more nervous the next time.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced several past relationships where they felt powerless and unable to influence outcomes, leading to a growing sense of being out of control. They often recall moments where attempts to communicate were dismissed or ignored, reinforcing their helplessness.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I try to speak up, nothing will change." "I have to keep quiet or things will get worse." "No one will listen to me, so why bother?"
ccd.coping_strategies: They tend to avoid confrontation and withdraw emotionally to prevent potential conflict, often distracting themselves or changing the subject when conversations get too intense. Sometimes they use humor or tangential remarks to deflect attention from difficult topics.
ccd.situation: In a recent argument with their partner, the patient felt overwhelmed and unable to express their feelings clearly, instead veering off into unrelated topics to avoid the discomfort of the disagreement.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I'm not in control here." "This is going to blow up and I can't stop it." "Maybe if I say something else, it won't get worse."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, frustration, and a deep sense of helplessness.
ccd.behaviors: The patient changes the subject abruptly, avoids directly addressing the issue, and tends to shut down emotionally, leading to unresolved tension in the relationship.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work was kind of busy, and I had a lot on my plate, so I didn’t really get much downtime. Oh, and my cat knocked over a plant the other day—soil everywhere, took me forever to clean up. Anyway, it’s just been sort of the usual, nothing too exciting.
When you think about how busy work has been and the plant incident, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind?

Well, honestly, sometimes I just feel like things keep piling up, you know? Like, as soon as I finish one thing, something else happens—like the plant mess, or emails from work that never stop coming. I end up thinking, “Here we go again, can’t catch a break.” Oh, and actually, it reminds me of this one time in college when my roommate left the window open and all my papers got wet… man, that was a nightmare to sort out.
When you notice those thoughts like “Here we go again, can’t catch a break,” how do they affect how you feel in the moment?

Um, I guess I just feel sort of tense, like there’s this weight in my chest. Sometimes I get kind of frustrated or just tired—like, what’s the point of even trying to get ahead if something else is always going to go wrong? I usually just try to distract myself, maybe scroll through my phone or make a joke about it. Oh, speaking of jokes, did you ever hear the one about the cat who started gardening?
It sounds like those feelings of tension and frustration are pretty strong—when you distract yourself or make jokes, does that help you feel better, even if just for a little while?

Yeah, I mean, for a few minutes it kind of takes the edge off. Like, if I can laugh or just focus on something silly, it’s easier to ignore whatever’s bothering me. But then, once things quiet down, it all just comes back again, and I end up feeling even more tired. Oh, and sometimes I end up just zoning out and forgetting what I was doing in the first place—like yesterday, I tried to start dinner and ended up watching random cooking videos instead.
When you notice yourself zoning out or getting caught up in distractions like cooking videos, what do you think is happening for you in that moment?

Um, I guess it’s like I don’t really want to deal with whatever’s on my mind, so I just kind of check out. It’s weird, I’ll be halfway through a video and realize I haven’t even started what I was supposed to be doing. Sometimes I think, “Well, it’s not like it matters anyway if I don’t get everything right.” Oh, and speaking of cooking, there was this one time I tried to make lasagna and totally burned it—my smoke alarm is way too sensitive, by the way.

It sounds like there’s a part of you that wants to avoid those difficult feelings or thoughts; when you tell yourself “it’s not like it matters anyway,” what do you think that means about how you see the situation or yourself?

I don’t know, I guess… maybe I just feel like, no matter what I do, it’s not going to change much. Like, if I mess up or don’t follow through, it’s almost expected at this point, so why stress about it? It’s easier to just let things slide and not make a big deal. Oh, and actually, I remember in high school, I would always avoid group projects because I felt like my ideas would never get picked anyway.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored and dismissed by family and peers, which made me believe that I had little control over what happened to me. I was frequently told I was too sensitive or weak, reinforcing my sense of vulnerability and powerlessness in social settings.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I speak up or assert myself, people will reject or harm me. I must avoid conflict to keep myself safe because I am too weak to handle opposition. Being vulnerable means I am likely to be taken advantage of or hurt.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to keep my opinions to myself and withdraw from conversations to avoid confrontation. Sometimes I change the subject abruptly when discussions get too personal or challenging. I also try to please others to prevent negative reactions.
ccd.situation: During a group discussion at work, when my ideas were questioned, I felt overwhelmed and unsure if I should respond or stay quiet.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They think I’m stupid for saying that.” “If I defend myself, they’ll think I’m weak or annoying.” “I can’t handle being criticized; I’m just powerless here.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, and a deep sense of helplessness washed over me. I also felt vulnerable and fearful of being judged or rejected.
ccd.behaviors: I stayed silent and avoided eye contact, changing the topic when given the chance. I withdrew from the group afterward and avoided contributing in future meetings.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um… I guess it’s been okay, nothing major happened. Work was just the usual meetings and emails. Oh, actually, there was this one meeting where everyone was kind of talking over each other, and I just… well, I mostly listened. Oh, but my neighbor’s dog got loose again, which was kind of funny—he always seems to end up in my backyard for some reason!
When you noticed everyone talking over each other in the meeting, what were you thinking or feeling in that moment?

Oh, uh, I guess I felt kind of tense, like my shoulders got really tight. I started worrying that if I tried to say something, I’d just get interrupted or ignored, so I didn’t bother. It reminded me of family dinners when I was a kid—it was always so loud, and I could never really get a word in. Actually, speaking of family dinners, my mom used to make this weird casserole that nobody liked, but we all pretended we did… I don’t know why that popped into my head.
That sounds like a tough situation both at work and back then; when you think about those family dinners and trying to speak up, what feelings come up for you now?

Um… I guess there’s this kind of tightness in my chest, like I’m bracing for something. It’s weird, I still remember just wanting to disappear or make myself really small so nobody would notice me. I wanted to say something sometimes, but it felt pointless, like it wouldn’t matter. Oh, and I always ended up being the one who cleared the table, which is funny, because I still find myself doing that sort of thing at work—just, you know, trying to be useful in the background.

It sounds like those feelings of wanting to disappear and being overlooked are still quite present for you; when you notice yourself stepping back or taking on those ‘background’ tasks at work, what do you think that’s helping you avoid or protect yourself from?

Uh, I guess it just feels safer, honestly. Like, if I’m busy with the little things, maybe people won’t expect me to chime in or challenge me on stuff. I don’t have to risk saying something wrong or… you know, being seen in a way that makes me uncomfortable. I remember once in high school, I volunteered to help backstage for the school play instead of trying out for a part—kind of the same thing, I suppose.
That makes a lot of sense; when you think about moments when you did speak up or took a more visible role, even a little, what thoughts or feelings came up for you then?

Um, I usually just felt… really exposed, like everyone was suddenly watching me and waiting for me to mess up. My mind would go blank, or I’d start second-guessing everything I said—like, “Was that dumb? Did I sound weird?” It was almost like I could feel my face getting hot and I’d just want to shrink back again. Oh, actually, I remember in college I had to do a group presentation, and after I spoke, I was so sure everyone thought I was out of my depth—I couldn’t even focus on what anyone else was saying after that.
When you notice those feelings of exposure and self-doubt coming up, what do you usually tell yourself about what’s happening or about yourself in those moments?

Um… I guess I usually think something like, “I shouldn’t have said anything,” or, “Everyone probably thinks I’m clueless.” I start convincing myself I made a mistake just by speaking up at all. Sometimes, it’s like this voice in my head going, “See, you can’t handle this.” Oh, and then I end up replaying everything in my mind later, just kind of picking apart what I did wrong.

ccd.relevant_history: I've often felt like no matter what I do, things just spiral out of my hands. Growing up, I was told to stay quiet and not ask for help, so I learned to keep my struggles inside, which only made me feel more stuck and powerless over time.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I let go or try to change things, everything will fall apart because I have no control. Asking for help means I’m weak and will probably be let down or misunderstood. I have to manage on my own or else I’ll be overwhelmed and fail completely.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid making big decisions or trying new things because I’m afraid it will make things worse. Sometimes I procrastinate or distract myself with TV or social media to escape feeling overwhelmed. When stressed, I withdraw from friends and family because I don’t want to burden them or admit I’m struggling.
ccd.situation: I’m facing a big opportunity for personal growth, like taking on a new project or learning a new skill, but I feel stuck and unsure if I can handle it.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “What if I mess this up and everything goes out of control?” “I’m not capable of managing this, it’s too much for me.” “If I ask for help, people will see I’m a failure.” “I’m just not strong enough to handle this.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, helplessness, frustration, and a deep sense of being overwhelmed. Sometimes I also feel sadness and discouragement because I want to grow but don’t believe I can.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid starting or fully committing to the new challenge, procrastinate, and isolate myself. I might also overprepare on small details to feel a false sense of control but end up stuck and exhausted.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been… a bit rough, honestly. I mean, nothing really bad happened, but I just feel like there’s this constant pressure building up, you know? I keep thinking about this new project at work, and I keep putting off even starting it. I just kind of end up zoning out, scrolling through my phone or watching random shows instead.
When you notice yourself zoning out or scrolling instead of working on the project, what thoughts usually go through your mind?

It’s like this voice in my head just keeps saying, “You’re not ready for this,” or, “You’re going to mess it up if you even try.” Sometimes I worry that if I start, I’ll get in over my head and just make things worse for myself or my team. I keep thinking I should pull it together, but then I feel so overwhelmed that I just… don’t. And then I get frustrated with myself for not doing anything.
When you hear that voice telling you you’re not ready or that you might mess up, how does your body feel in those moments?

Uh, I guess I feel pretty tense—my chest gets tight, my shoulders kind of hunch up, and my stomach feels weird, almost like it’s twisting or sinking. Sometimes I get this restless feeling, like I have to move or do something, but I can’t figure out what, so I just end up fidgeting or sitting there. I notice my breathing gets shallow, too. It’s honestly exhausting, feeling like that over and over.
When your body feels that tense and restless, do you notice if your thoughts about the project or yourself change in any way?

Yeah, I think so. The more tense I feel, the more I start doubting myself—like, I go from worrying about the project to just thinking, “Maybe I’m just not cut out for this at all,” or, “I can’t handle things like other people can.” It almost feels like the project is this huge wall I can’t get over, no matter what I try. And then I start wanting to just forget about it entirely, even though that makes me feel guilty.
When you start thinking you’re not cut out for this or can’t handle things like others, what do you imagine would happen if you tried the project anyway, despite those fears?

I… honestly, I picture things going really badly. Like, I’d start working on it, and all the pieces would just fall apart—I’d make some dumb mistake or overlook something important, and then everyone would see I can’t handle it. I worry that people would be disappointed or frustrated with me, maybe even lose trust in me. And if that happened, I don’t really know how I’d bounce back from it—it just feels like it would confirm everything I’m afraid of about myself.
Have you had experiences in the past where you tried something challenging and it didn’t go as planned? If so, how did you respond, and what was the outcome?

Yeah, there have definitely been times like that. I remember in college, I tried to take on a group project that was a lot more work than I expected, and I ended up getting overwhelmed and missing some deadlines. I felt like I let everyone down, and I just kind of retreated—I stopped reaching out to people in the group and tried to handle it all on my own, but that only made things worse. After that, I promised myself I’d be more careful about what I took on, but I think ever since then I’ve been pretty hesitant to jump into anything that feels too big.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt neglected and like I wasn't important to my family. Financial struggles at home made me feel like I was a burden, and I learned to expect people to leave me when things got tough.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t have money or stability, people won’t want to be with me. I have to keep my guard up or I’ll be left alone. Being abandoned is just how things go for someone like me.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to avoid asking for help or sharing my worries because I’m scared it will push people away. Sometimes I spend impulsively to feel better temporarily, even though it makes things worse later.
ccd.situation: I’m worried about paying my bills this month and how my partner might react if I tell them I’m struggling financially.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They’re going to see I can’t handle my finances and leave me. I’m just not lovable if I can’t provide or be stable.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, fear of rejection, sadness, and a sense of loneliness.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid discussing money problems with my partner and sometimes hide bills or financial documents. I also withdraw emotionally, trying to protect myself from getting hurt.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it’s been alright, I guess. Work’s been kind of stressful, and I’ve been feeling a bit on edge. Nothing major happened, just… some stuff on my mind.
When you say “some stuff on your mind,” what kind of thoughts have been coming up for you during those stressful moments at work?

I don’t know, it’s just… I keep thinking about all the bills I have coming up. I get anxious that maybe I won’t be able to cover everything. Feels like if I mess up, things could fall apart fast. I try not to think about it too much, but it’s hard.
When you notice those anxious thoughts about the bills and things falling apart, how do you usually respond or cope with those feelings in the moment?

Honestly, I just try to push it down and not deal with it right then. Sometimes I’ll distract myself—scroll on my phone or do something else so I don’t have to think about it. I don’t really talk to anyone about it. I just feel like it’s better if I handle it on my own.
When you push those worries down or distract yourself, what happens to the feelings or thoughts afterward—do they tend to come back, change, or stay the same?

Yeah, they definitely come back. Sometimes even stronger, like late at night when I’m trying to sleep. It’s like I can’t escape them, no matter how hard I try. I end up feeling pretty exhausted from it all.
What do you usually tell yourself when those worries come back late at night and you’re struggling to fall asleep?

I tell myself I just need to figure things out on my own and that I shouldn’t bother anyone else with my problems. Sometimes I think, “If I was better at handling this, I wouldn’t be in this mess.” I also worry that if people knew how much I’m struggling, they’d just get fed up with me. It makes it really hard to relax or believe things will get better.
When you have those self-critical thoughts like “If I was better at handling this,” how do they affect your motivation or actions toward managing your situation?

Honestly, it just makes me feel stuck. I kind of shut down and avoid looking at my bills or trying to fix things, because I already feel like I’ve messed up. I get this urge to just hide from it all. Sometimes I don’t even want to talk to anyone—I just sort of go numb.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up in a family where mistakes were often met with harsh criticism, I learned early on that I wasn’t capable of handling things well. This has made me doubt my abilities in many areas of life, especially when dealing with family issues.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m not perfect, then I’m a failure. I must not mess things up or I’ll be seen as useless. Everyone expects me to manage family problems, but I always feel like I’m falling short.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid taking on responsibilities or speaking up during family conflicts because I’m afraid I’ll make things worse. Sometimes I withdraw or stay silent to avoid drawing attention to my perceived incompetence.
ccd.situation: During a recent family gathering, a disagreement arose about how to handle a financial issue, and I felt overwhelmed and unsure about what to say or do.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I have no idea what I’m doing.” “If I try to help, I’ll just mess everything up.” “They’ll think I’m completely useless.”
ccd.emotions: I felt anxious, frustrated, and deeply ashamed of myself.
ccd.behaviors: I stayed quiet and avoided contributing to the conversation, physically withdrawing from the group and feeling emotionally distant.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
I mean… it’s whatever. Same old stuff, honestly. Nothing really changed, just the usual family drama. Honestly, I’d rather not get into it.
I hear that the family drama feels tiring and maybe a bit overwhelming—what do you notice happening inside you when these situations come up?

I don’t know, I just get this knot in my stomach. It feels like... I just want to disappear or something. Trying to jump in or say anything just makes me more anxious, so I end up shutting down. It’s like, what’s even the point?

That sounds really tough, feeling stuck between wanting to speak up and then shutting down instead; when you notice that knot in your stomach, what thoughts usually run through your mind in those moments?

Honestly? Stuff like, “I have no clue what I’m doing,” or, “If I say something, I’ll just screw it up.” I just freeze because I know whatever I say will probably make things worse. Everyone seems to expect me to help, but I just feel useless. It’s exhausting.
It sounds like there's a lot of pressure and self-doubt in those moments; can you tell me about a time when you did speak up or try to help—what happened, and how did you feel afterward?

Yeah, there was this one time a while back. I tried to offer a suggestion when everyone was arguing, but they kind of brushed me off or acted like my idea was stupid. After that, I just felt embarrassed and even more convinced that I shouldn’t bother. It pretty much made me shut up for the rest of the night.

That sounds like a painful experience that reinforced your doubts; when you think back to that moment, is there any way you might see it differently now or consider what else might have been going on besides your idea being “stupid”?

I mean, maybe they were just stressed or not really listening to anyone at that point, but honestly, it still felt like they singled me out. I can’t help but think if my idea was actually any good, they wouldn’t have ignored it. I don’t know—maybe I’m just not good at this stuff. It’s hard to see it any other way.

It makes sense that feeling singled out would be painful and hard to shake; what do you think a friend might say to you if they were in your shoes and had those thoughts about their idea being no good?

I guess they’d probably tell me I’m being too hard on myself, or that just because one idea wasn’t taken seriously doesn’t mean I have nothing to offer. But honestly, it’s easy to say that to someone else. When it’s me, it just feels different, you know? I can’t just flip a switch and see it that way.

ccd.relevant_history: I've always felt like I don’t quite fit in with others, especially in social settings. Growing up, I was often teased about my looks, which made me believe there was something fundamentally wrong with me. These feelings have carried over into my adult relationships, making me hesitant to put myself out there.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m not attractive, people won’t want to get close to me or care about what I have to say. I have to hide parts of myself to avoid being rejected. Being liked or accepted depends mainly on how I look.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid social gatherings or stay quiet when I’m around others to keep from drawing attention to myself. Sometimes I distract myself by scrolling through social media, comparing myself to others instead of engaging. When I do interact, I might joke around or change the subject quickly to avoid deeper conversations.
ccd.situation: At a small party, I notice people laughing and talking easily, but I feel out of place and uncomfortable making eye contact or joining in. Someone compliments another guest’s outfit, and I immediately think about how I don’t look as good.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “Everyone is noticing how unattractive I am.” “If I try to talk, they’ll see I don’t belong.” “I’m not interesting enough to be part of this group.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, self-conscious, and sad. There’s also a sense of loneliness and frustration because I want to connect but feel blocked by my insecurities.
ccd.behaviors: I keep to myself in the corner, avoid starting conversations, and leave the party early. I might also check my phone repeatedly to escape the discomfort rather than engaging with others.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Hmm, it’s been… okay, I guess. Work’s been busy, nothing too out of the ordinary. I went to this small get-together over the weekend, but I didn’t stay very long. Oh, and my neighbor’s dog keeps barking at the weirdest times—it’s like, does he ever sleep?
When you think about staying at the get-together, what thoughts were going through your mind that made you decide to leave early?

Uh, well… I don’t know, I just felt kind of awkward, I guess. Like, everyone seemed to be having a good time and talking so easily with each other, and I just felt sort of… invisible? Or maybe too visible, I’m not sure. Actually, it reminded me of this time in high school when I tried joining a club, and I just ended up sitting in the back feeling really out of place—so I left early then too.
What feelings did you notice in your body when you felt invisible or out of place at the get-together and back in high school?

Um, I think my stomach felt kind of tight, and my hands got a little clammy. I kept fidgeting with my phone or my sleeves—just, like, looking for something to do with my hands, you know? There’s this weird heaviness in my chest, too, like it’s harder to breathe or something. Oh, and sometimes my leg starts bouncing without me even realizing it—my grandma used to joke that I had ants in my pants.
When those physical sensations show up, what do you usually tell yourself about what’s happening or what might happen next?

I guess I start worrying that people are noticing how nervous I am, or that I look weird just standing there. Sometimes I think, “They’re probably wondering why I even came.” It’s like, if I try to join in, I’ll just end up saying something awkward and make things worse. Actually, that happened once at a family dinner—someone asked me a question, and I froze up, then everyone just kind of moved on, which made me feel even more out of place.
How do those worries about being noticed or saying something awkward affect what you do in social situations?

Well, I usually just hang back and don’t really talk unless someone talks to me first. I’ll pretend to check my phone or look busy, just so I don’t have to make eye contact or start a conversation. Sometimes I even make up an excuse to leave early—like, say I have work to do or I’m not feeling well. Oh, and then later I end up replaying the whole thing in my head, which honestly just makes me feel worse.
When you replay those social situations in your mind later, what kinds of thoughts or conclusions do you often come to about yourself or the event?

Honestly, I just end up thinking I was awkward, or that people must’ve noticed how out of place I seemed. I start wondering if maybe I just don’t fit in anywhere, or if there’s something wrong with me. Sometimes I remember little things, like how I stood or what I was wearing, and convince myself that’s why people didn’t talk to me much. It’s kind of exhausting—like, I know I’m probably overthinking, but I can’t seem to help it.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and had to work hard to get any attention or support from my family. At work, I've struggled with feeling like I need to constantly prove my worth to be accepted or valued by my colleagues and supervisors.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t keep everyone happy, they will reject me or stop caring about me. I have to put others’ needs before my own to avoid being seen as a burden or needy. Asking for help means I’m weak and will let people down.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to agree with others even if I have different opinions, just to avoid conflict. I take on extra tasks to show I’m capable, even when I’m overwhelmed. I avoid expressing my own needs or frustrations to prevent disappointing anyone.
ccd.situation: At work, when my manager assigns me multiple urgent projects with tight deadlines, I feel overwhelmed but try not to show it. I worry that if I say no or ask for help, I’ll seem incapable and others will think less of me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I can’t say no; they’ll think I’m useless.” “If I don’t do this perfectly, they won’t like me.” “I’m such a burden if I ask for support.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, fear of rejection, sadness, and a feeling of being overwhelmed.
ccd.behaviors: I push myself to work longer hours and avoid asking for help. I suppress my stress and keep quiet about my needs. This often leads to burnout and feeling even more helpless.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um... it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been a bit busy, but nothing I can’t handle. Honestly, I feel bad even mentioning it—other people probably have it much harder. But thank you for asking, that’s really kind of you.
It sounds like you might be comparing your situation to others and feeling a bit guilty about your own experience—can you tell me more about what goes through your mind when you think that way?

Yeah, I guess I do that a lot. I just… I don’t want to make a fuss or seem like I’m complaining when, you know, everyone has their own stuff going on. Sometimes I think, “Who am I to say I’m struggling?” I feel like if I talk about it, I’m just making things harder for people.
When you have these thoughts about not wanting to make a fuss or feel like you're complaining, how does that affect what you do or how you feel afterward?

Um, I guess I just keep things to myself and try to handle everything on my own. I usually end up feeling kind of tense or worried, but I don’t want to bother anyone with it. I try to focus on being helpful instead. Sometimes I get really tired, but I tell myself it’s not a big deal.
It sounds like you’re carrying a lot on your own—when you notice feeling tense or worried but tell yourself it’s not a big deal, what impact does that have on your mood or energy levels later on?

Yeah, I guess after a while I start to feel kind of drained. Sometimes I get a bit sad, or just sort of… heavy, like everything’s piling up. But I don’t want anyone to worry about me, so I try to just push through it. I hope that’s okay—I mean, other people seem to manage, so I feel like I should too.
When you notice that heaviness and sadness building up, what do you usually do to try to cope or manage those feelings?

I usually just try to keep myself busy and focus on getting things done. Sometimes I’ll stay late at work or pick up extra tasks, so I don’t have time to dwell on how I’m feeling. I try not to think about it too much. I guess I feel like if I just keep going, it’ll pass and no one has to know I’m struggling.
When you push yourself to stay busy and avoid thinking about your feelings, how effective does that strategy feel in helping you feel better in the short term and the longer term?

In the short term, I think it helps a little—I feel productive, like I’m doing something right. But after a while, I just get really exhausted, and the stress kind of comes back even stronger. Sometimes it feels like I’m on a treadmill that never stops. I guess I’m not really sure how else to handle it, though.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like I don’t quite fit in at work and worry that people don’t really like me. Growing up, I was often told I wasn’t good-looking, which made me feel invisible and unworthy.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m not attractive, people won’t want to get close to me or value my opinions. I have to stay quiet and keep to myself to avoid being judged or rejected.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid social interactions at work and keep my thoughts to myself. I also try to work extra hard to prove my worth since I don’t feel like I can rely on being liked.
ccd.situation: When I’m under pressure at work and expected to contribute in meetings or social events, I feel like everyone is focused on how I look or what I say.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one wants to hear from me because I’m unattractive and boring.” “If I speak up, I’ll embarrass myself and confirm that I don’t belong here.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, lonely, and ashamed during these moments. There’s also a deep sadness that I’m not seen or valued for who I am.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet in meetings, avoid eye contact, and try to disappear into the background. Sometimes I isolate myself during breaks and avoid social gatherings.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been alright, I guess. Just work and the usual stuff. Nothing really special happened.
When you say "nothing really special," how does that make you feel about your days?
I don’t know... I guess it just feels kind of the same every day. I mostly just try to get through it. Sometimes I feel a bit tired, maybe.
What goes through your mind when you’re trying to get through the day and feeling tired?
Uh, I just keep my head down and focus on my tasks. I think about finishing things so I can go home. Sometimes I kind of hope nobody notices me too much. I don’t really like drawing attention.
When you hope not to draw attention, what thoughts or worries come up for you about being noticed?

It’s just... I get worried people might judge me or think something negative. Like, maybe they’ll think I don’t belong or that I’m weird. I don’t know, I just feel uncomfortable when the focus is on me. It’s easier if I just blend in.
Can you remember a recent situation at work where you felt this way—worried about being judged or feeling uncomfortable in the spotlight? What was happening then?

Yeah, there was a team meeting a couple of days ago. My manager asked for my opinion on something. I felt really nervous when everyone looked at me. I just kind of mumbled something and hoped they’d move on quickly.
What were some of the specific thoughts running through your mind when your manager asked for your opinion during that meeting?

Um... I was thinking, “Why are they asking me?” and “I probably sound stupid.” I also kept worrying that people would notice how nervous I was. Mostly, I just wanted it to be over as fast as possible.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and like my needs were a burden to my family. Whenever I asked for help, I was met with frustration or dismissal, which made me feel like I couldn’t rely on anyone.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I believe that if I ask for too much, people will get tired of me or leave. I’m convinced that needing others is a sign of weakness and that I have to manage on my own to avoid being a burden.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to hide my needs and avoid asking for help, even when I’m struggling. Sometimes I become overly clingy or demanding because I’m scared that if I don’t express my needs loudly enough, no one will notice.
ccd.situation: During a recent family gathering, I felt overwhelmed and needed support, but when I expressed this, I was told to stop being so needy and to handle things myself.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They think I’m being too much again; I’m just a burden to everyone.” “If I keep asking, they’re going to get tired of me and leave.”
ccd.emotions: I felt hurt, rejected, and really alone. There was also a strong sense of frustration and sadness that my feelings didn’t matter.
ccd.behaviors: I withdrew from the conversation and stopped sharing how I was feeling. Later, I avoided family events because I didn’t want to feel that way again.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

It’s been… I don’t know, kind of rough, honestly. I keep getting this feeling that no matter what I do or say, it just annoys people. I tried to talk to my family about something I needed help with, and it just blew up in my face. I guess I’m just tired of always feeling like this.
That sounds really tough. When you notice that feeling that you’re annoying people, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind in those moments?

I just think, “Here we go again, I’m being too much.” Or like… “Why can’t I just deal with things on my own like everyone else?” It almost feels pointless to even try asking for anything because I already know what they’re going to say. So, I end up wanting to just shut down or disappear.
It sounds like those thoughts make reaching out feel pretty discouraging. When you imagine shutting down or disappearing, what emotions do you notice coming up for you?

I feel… honestly, I’m just angry and hurt. It’s like, what’s the point of having people around if I can’t even rely on them? And then I just end up feeling really alone, like no one actually cares. Sometimes I even get mad at myself for wanting anything from them in the first place.
Feeling both angry and hurt makes a lot of sense given what you’ve described. When you feel that way, how do you usually respond or cope in the moment?

I just… I usually pull back. I stop talking, and I try to avoid everyone. Sometimes I’ll just leave early or find an excuse not to be there at all. It’s easier than having to deal with feeling rejected again.
Avoiding those situations seems like a way to protect yourself from that pain. When you pull back or leave early, how does that affect your connection with the people you care about afterward?

I mean, it just makes things worse, honestly. Afterward, I feel even more distant from them, and it gets harder to go back or reach out again. But at the same time, I just can’t stand the idea of putting myself out there and getting shot down. It’s like I’m stuck either way.
That sounds like a really difficult cycle to be caught in. When you think about the times you have reached out and had a better or different response than you expected, what was that like?

Honestly, I can’t really think of many times that happened. Maybe once or twice, someone actually listened or tried to help, but even then I felt awkward, like I was asking for too much. It’s hard for me to believe it’s possible for things to go differently. Most of the time, I just brace myself for disappointment.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt like no matter how hard I tried, I could never meet my family’s expectations. I was frequently criticized, which made me believe that there was something inherently wrong with me. Over time, these feelings solidified into a deep sense of being defective and unable to change.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m not perfect, then I am worthless and will never be good enough. Trying to improve is pointless because I’ll just fail again. People will reject me if they see the real me, so it’s safer to hide my flaws.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid taking on new challenges because I’m afraid of failing and proving my defects. I also withdraw from social situations where I might be judged, and sometimes I push people away before they have a chance to reject me.
ccd.situation: I recently tried to sign up for a leadership workshop at work, hoping to grow and improve my skills, but I stopped myself at the last minute, feeling overwhelmed and convinced I wasn’t capable.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to mess this up like I always do.” “Everyone will see how defective I am if I try and fail.” “There’s no point in even trying because I’ll just disappoint myself and others.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, ashamed, and hopeless. There’s also a deep sadness that I might never be able to change or be accepted for who I am.
ccd.behaviors: I cancel my registration for the workshop and avoid talking about my ambitions with colleagues. Instead, I keep to myself and stick with tasks I know I can do without much risk, limiting my personal growth.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um... my week’s been okay, I guess. Nothing major happened, just the usual stuff at work and home. I mean, I can’t say it was great, but I managed to get through it. Things feel a bit overwhelming sometimes, but I just try to keep my head down and not make waves.
When you say things feel overwhelming, can you tell me about a recent moment when you felt that way?

Yeah, um... I suppose the most recent thing was at work. There was this workshop coming up—something about leadership and skill building—and I thought about signing up. But when I actually went to do it, I just kind of froze and felt this wave of anxiety. I ended up not registering, even though I wanted to.
What were some of the thoughts going through your mind when you started to feel that wave of anxiety?

Honestly, a lot of things rushed through my head all at once. I kept thinking, “What if I mess up in front of everyone?” and “I’m probably not cut out for this.” I started doubting whether I’d be able to keep up or contribute anything useful. It just seemed easier to back out than risk embarrassing myself.
When you imagine yourself messing up or not keeping up, what do you think would happen afterward?

I guess I picture people looking at me differently, maybe thinking less of me or realizing I’m not as capable as they thought.
