I worry they’d talk about how I didn’t belong there, or maybe just quietly decide not to include me in things in the future. It’s almost like I’d be exposed somehow. That thought makes me feel really small, like I just want to disappear.
That sounds really hard to carry around. When you think about wanting to disappear in those moments, what do you usually do or feel physically?

Yeah, it is hard... When I get that feeling, it’s like my chest tightens up and I can’t really breathe properly. I start fidgeting or looking for an excuse to leave the room, or sometimes I just kind of go quiet and shut down. I get this urge to just hide somewhere or be invisible, so no one can see how uncomfortable I am. It’s exhausting, honestly.
Thank you for sharing that. When you notice those physical sensations and the urge to hide, have you found anything that helps you feel a little more grounded or calm in those moments?

Um, honestly, not really… Most of the time I just wait for the feeling to pass, or I distract myself by checking my phone or pretending to be busy. Sometimes I’ll duck into the bathroom for a few minutes just to get away. Nothing really makes it better—I just sort of ride it out and hope nobody notices how nervous I am.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like no matter how hard I try, people eventually leave me. Growing up, there were times when important people in my life disappeared without explanation, and that left me feeling deeply unworthy of lasting love or connection.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I get close to someone, they will leave me because I’m not truly lovable or worthy of care. It’s safer to expect abandonment than to hope for lasting relationships. Others will eventually reject me once they see who I really am.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to keep my distance from others emotionally or avoid getting too close, so I don’t have to face the pain of being left. Sometimes I push people away before they can leave me, just to protect myself. I also try to be overly accommodating, hoping to prevent others from abandoning me.
ccd.situation: I started a new relationship, and after a few weeks, my partner didn’t return my messages as quickly as before. I immediately felt like they were going to leave me and that I wasn’t good enough for them to stay.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: They’re probably losing interest because I’m not lovable. I’m going to be abandoned again, just like before. Maybe if I keep trying harder, they’ll stay, but deep down, I don’t think it will work.
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, sad, and desperate. There’s a heavy sense of loneliness and fear that I’ll be left alone again, which makes me feel hopeless and unworthy.
ccd.behaviors: I start texting my partner repeatedly, seeking reassurance and validation. I also withdraw emotionally at times, becoming distant or irritable to test if they really care or to protect myself from getting hurt.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Uh, well… I guess it’s been okay, mostly. Work’s been kind of busy, but manageable. I’ve been a little distracted, honestly, but I’m trying to just get through each day. Nothing major happened, I guess.
When you say you’ve been a little distracted, what kinds of thoughts or feelings tend to come up during those moments?

Um… yeah, so, it’s kind of hard to put into words. I think I just get caught up worrying about things, especially about my relationship. Like, if my partner doesn’t text me back quickly, my mind starts spiraling and I can’t really focus. It’s like…I just feel this knot in my stomach and everything else fades into the background.
That sounds really tough. When your mind starts to spiral like that, what kinds of thoughts usually go through your head about your partner or the situation?

Yeah, it’s… it’s not great. I start thinking maybe they’re upset with me, or that I did something wrong. Sometimes I convince myself they’re probably losing interest, or realizing I’m not worth their time. I know it might sound dramatic, but it just feels so real in the moment.
It makes sense that those thoughts feel very real and intense. How do these thoughts affect the way you behave or interact with your partner afterward?

Honestly, I end up texting them a lot—probably too much—just to feel some sort of reassurance. Sometimes I get kind of distant, though, like I don’t know if I should even bother. I might get a bit snappy, or I’ll just act like I don’t care, even though I do. It’s confusing, because part of me wants to pull them closer and another part wants to push them away before they can hurt me.
That push-pull dynamic sounds really challenging. When you notice yourself feeling torn between wanting closeness and pushing away, what do you usually tell yourself about those conflicting feelings?

Honestly, I usually just feel kind of stuck—like, I can’t win either way. I think, “Well, if I get too close, I’ll just end up getting hurt,” but at the same time, being alone feels awful. I tell myself it’s safer not to expect much, but I still can’t help wanting them to prove they’ll stay. It’s exhausting, trying to figure out what’s the right thing to do.
That sounds really exhausting indeed. When you tell yourself it’s safer not to expect much, how does that belief influence your mood or energy throughout the day?

It definitely drags me down, to be honest. I start feeling kind of hopeless, like, no matter what happens, it’ll end up hurting anyway. It makes me feel tired and heavy, like there’s not much point in trying sometimes. I go through my day, but there’s always this underlying worry or sadness just kind of lingering in the background.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has a history of feeling powerless in past relationships, often experiencing situations where their needs were overlooked or dismissed. They grew up in a family environment where expressing emotions felt risky, leading to a sense of being stuck and unable to influence outcomes.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "I can’t change how others treat me," and "If I try to assert myself, I’ll just make things worse." These beliefs reinforce the feeling of being trapped and prevent the patient from challenging difficult dynamics.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid confrontation and withdraws emotionally to protect themselves from further hurt, often staying silent or agreeing to things they don’t want just to keep the peace.
ccd.situation: In an argument with their partner, the patient feels overwhelmed and powerless when their concerns are dismissed, leaving them feeling isolated and stuck in the relationship.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "They don’t care about how I feel," "I’ll never be able to make things better," and "I’m just trapped in this situation with no way out."
ccd.emotions: Feelings of sadness, frustration, and helplessness dominate, accompanied by anxiety about the future of the relationship.
ccd.behaviors: The patient withdraws from the conversation, avoids discussing difficult topics, and may comply with the partner’s wishes to avoid further conflict, despite feeling unhappy.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work's been busy—my manager keeps piling on new tasks, which is stressful, but that’s nothing new. My partner and I had a disagreement the other day, but that kind of blew over pretty quickly… well, I mean, it didn’t really, but we just stopped talking about it. Oh, did I mention our neighbors got a new dog? It’s really cute, but it barks a lot at night—hard to sleep sometimes.
It sounds like there are a few different stressors on your mind—when you think about the disagreement with your partner that you stopped talking about, what thoughts go through your head?

Oh, um… I don’t know, I guess I just start thinking it’s probably not worth it to bring things up sometimes. Honestly, I worry that if I push too much, things will just get worse between us, so it feels safer to just let it go. It’s kind of like… well, sometimes I feel like my opinion doesn’t matter much anyway, so why cause more trouble? Sorry, I’m rambling—actually, did I tell you my friend’s moving to a different city soon? That’s been on my mind too.

It sounds like you might be feeling a bit unheard or uncertain about expressing your feelings—when you notice that sense of "my opinion doesn’t matter much," how does that affect what you do or say in your relationship?

Yeah, um… honestly, I just sort of shut down. I’ll nod along or agree, even if I don’t actually feel that way, because I don’t want to start anything. Sometimes I find myself just changing the subject or making a joke instead of saying what I really think. Actually, it’s kind of funny—my partner says I’m really easygoing, but I’m not sure that’s actually true.
That’s really insightful—when you choose to agree or change the subject instead of sharing what you really think, what emotions are you experiencing in those moments?

Uh, I guess… mostly I just feel kind of tense, maybe a little anxious, like my chest gets tight. Sometimes I feel sad afterward, like I missed a chance to be honest, but at the time it just feels easier to avoid conflict. There’s also this weird heaviness, like I’m stuck or something. Oh, and sometimes I just feel tired, like it takes too much energy to even try.
Those feelings sound really tough to carry—when you notice that tension or heaviness, what do you usually do next?

Honestly, I usually just keep to myself for a while—maybe scroll on my phone or go for a walk by myself. I’ll avoid talking about anything serious, just stick to small talk or not say much at all. Sometimes I’ll just go to bed early, even if I’m not really that tired. Oh, and I end up replaying stuff in my head a lot, wondering if I could’ve handled it differently.
When you find yourself replaying those moments in your head, what kinds of thoughts or questions tend to come up?

Um, I mostly think things like, “Why can’t I just say how I feel?” or “Maybe it’s my fault for bringing it up in the first place.” Sometimes I wonder if things will ever actually change, or if this is just how it’s always going to be. I’ll even catch myself thinking that maybe I’m just too much trouble, or that it’s easier for everyone if I just stay quiet. Sorry, I know I’m kind of jumping around—it’s just hard to keep it all straight sometimes.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced multiple job losses over the past few years and has struggled to keep up with bills, leading to a growing sense of financial instability. They have often felt unsupported by family and friends during these times, reinforcing feelings of isolation.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "I don’t have control over my financial situation." "No matter what I do, things just keep getting worse." "People like me never get a real break."
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid looking at bills or financial statements and withdraws from conversations about money. They may also distract themselves with TV or social media to escape the stress but rarely seek help or advice.
ccd.situation: Facing a mounting pile of unpaid bills and uncertainty about how to cover next month’s rent.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m trapped and there’s nothing I can do." "I’m just a victim of bad luck." "Why bother trying? It won’t change anything."
ccd.emotions: Helplessness, frustration, anxiety, and sadness.
ccd.behaviors: Withdrawing from friends and family, procrastinating on managing finances, and avoiding calls or letters from creditors.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Nothing really new. Just… same old stuff, you know. I’ve just been keeping to myself mostly.
When you say "same old stuff," what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you during those usual days?
I don’t know… it’s kind of hard to explain. I guess I just feel tired a lot, and like things are a bit much sometimes. I try not to think about it too much. Just… get through the day, mostly.
When you notice that feeling tired and overwhelmed, what do you usually tell yourself about those feelings or the situation?
Um… I don’t really say much to myself, I guess. Sometimes I just think, “Here we go again.” Or like, “What’s the point?” I usually just try to put it out of my mind.
What do you think happens when you try to put those thoughts and feelings out of your mind? How does that affect you?

I mean, I guess it helps for a little while. Like, I’ll turn on the TV or scroll on my phone to just forget about things. But then it all just comes back later anyway. Sometimes it feels even heavier after.
When those feelings come back heavier later, what do you notice about how you react or what you do then?
I usually just… avoid stuff more. Like, I won’t check my emails or open the mail. I kind of just want to shut everything out. I don’t really talk to anyone about it.
What do you imagine might happen if you did reach out to someone or faced those things instead of avoiding them?

Honestly, I don’t really think it would make much of a difference. People have their own problems, so I don’t want to bother them. And as for facing things… I’m not sure it would change anything. It just feels pointless sometimes.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up in a household where conflicts were frequent and unresolved, I often felt overlooked and unable to influence what was happening around me. This left me feeling small and without control in family situations.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to speak up or take charge, I’ll just make things worse or be ignored. It’s safer to stay quiet and not challenge others because I can’t really change anything anyway.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid family discussions or withdraw when tensions rise, hoping to keep the peace by staying out of the way. Sometimes I give in quickly to others’ demands to avoid conflict, even if it leaves me feeling frustrated or sad.
ccd.situation: During a family gathering, when disagreements start, I notice myself feeling tense and like I have no say in what’s happening or how decisions are made.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one cares what I think.” “I’m too weak to make a difference.” “If I say something, I’ll just mess things up.”
ccd.emotions: Feelings of sadness, helplessness, and anxiety arise, along with a deep sense of being overwhelmed and vulnerable.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet, withdraw from conversations, and avoid asserting myself, hoping to stay unnoticed and avoid further conflict.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been… alright, I guess. Nothing too major happened, but I did have a family dinner the other night. That was a bit stressful. I mostly just kept to myself.
What was going through your mind during the family dinner that felt stressful?

I just remember thinking I should probably stay quiet. There were a few moments when people started arguing, and I felt really tense. I kept hoping it would just end soon. Honestly, I didn’t feel like what I had to say would matter anyway.
When you noticed those moments of tension and your thought that what you had to say wouldn’t matter, how did that affect how you felt in your body?

I got pretty tense—I could feel my shoulders getting tight and my stomach felt kind of knotted up. My heart was beating faster too. I just wanted to shrink away or disappear, honestly. It’s like my whole body was on edge.
What did you do next when you noticed your body feeling so tense—did you try anything to help yourself feel more comfortable?

Mostly I just tried to stay quiet and not get noticed. Sometimes I’ll pretend to be busy with my phone or help out in the kitchen so I don’t have to join in. I kind of zone out a bit, just waiting for the whole thing to be over. I guess I just hope to get through it without anyone turning their attention to me.
When you use those strategies like zoning out or staying busy with your phone, how do you feel afterward—do those moments help you feel safer, or do they bring up other feelings?

It helps a bit in the moment, like I’m avoiding trouble. But after, I usually feel kind of sad and frustrated with myself. Sometimes I wish I could just speak up like everyone else does. It leaves me feeling a bit disconnected from my family.
What do you imagine might happen if you did try to speak up during those family dinners?

Honestly, I just picture it going badly. I worry people would ignore me or talk over me, or maybe I’d just make things more awkward. Part of me thinks I might say something wrong and make everyone upset. It feels safer to just keep quiet.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated instances of feeling powerless in social settings, such as being ignored or dismissed during conversations. These experiences have reinforced a belief that they cannot influence social outcomes, leading to a growing sense of helplessness over time.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "I can’t handle social situations," "If I try to speak up, I’ll just make things worse," and "Nobody listens to me anyway." These thoughts create barriers that prevent the patient from engaging confidently with others.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid social gatherings or stays quiet during interactions to minimize the risk of rejection or embarrassment. They may also withdraw emotionally, convincing themselves that staying detached protects them from feeling hurt.
ccd.situation: Attending a group meeting where the patient is expected to share their opinion but feels unsure and anxious about speaking up.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m going to say something stupid," "They won’t care what I have to say," and "I won’t be able to change anything, so why bother?"
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and frustration arise from anticipating rejection and feeling powerless in the social context.
ccd.behaviors: The patient remains silent during the meeting, avoids eye contact, and may leave early or avoid future meetings altogether.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Work was busy, and I had a couple of meetings. Nothing major happened. I’m just kind of tired, honestly.
When you say you feel tired, is it more physical tiredness, or do you notice any mental or emotional fatigue as well?
It’s more in my head, I think. Like, it’s not that I didn’t sleep enough, I just feel drained after being around people. Stuff like meetings leaves me kind of wiped out, even if I’m not doing much.
When you notice feeling drained after meetings or being around people, what kinds of thoughts usually go through your mind during or afterward?

I usually worry about what I said, or if I should have said something at all. Sometimes I just feel like no one really pays attention when I do talk, so I wonder what the point is. Afterward, I end up replaying the whole thing in my head, picking apart what happened. It's like I can't really relax even when it's over.
What feelings do you notice in your body or emotions when you’re replaying those moments and questioning yourself?

I get tense, mostly in my shoulders and jaw. Sometimes my chest feels tight, like I can’t quite take a deep breath. Emotionally, I just feel anxious and kind of sad. It’s frustrating because I wish I didn’t care so much.
When those anxious and sad feelings come up, what do you usually do to try to manage or cope with them?

I usually just try to distract myself. I’ll scroll on my phone or watch something mindless. Sometimes I just avoid talking to people for a while. It feels easier to pull back instead of dealing with it.
How do you feel afterward when you’ve distracted yourself or pulled back from talking to people—does it help reduce those feelings, or do they tend to come back?

It helps for a bit—like I don’t have to think about it for a while. But honestly, the feelings come back the next time I have to be around people. It’s like starting over every time. I guess I never really feel comfortable.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, you often felt overlooked or unsupported by your family, which made you feel like you had to rely on others to get through tough times. This has led to a pattern where you believe you can’t manage things on your own.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: You believe that if you don’t ask for help or make others aware of your needs, you won’t get the support you require. You also think that needing others is a sign that you can’t handle life independently.
ccd.coping_strategies: You tend to reach out frequently to family members or close friends for reassurance and assistance, sometimes even when you might be able to manage on your own. At times, you may avoid taking on new challenges alone because you feel overwhelmed.
ccd.situation: When a disagreement arises within your family, or you feel emotionally distant from them, you find yourself thinking you have no control or ability to improve the situation.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I can’t handle this by myself.” “If I don’t ask for help, I’ll be left struggling.” “People will think I’m weak if I admit I need support.”
ccd.emotions: You feel anxious, vulnerable, and sometimes ashamed about your needs. There is often a sense of sadness or loneliness tied to feeling helpless.
ccd.behaviors: You frequently check in with family members for guidance or comfort, sometimes excessively. You might withdraw from situations where you feel you won’t get support or hesitate to take initiative without someone else’s involvement.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Uh, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too out of the ordinary, just the usual stuff with my family. I had a disagreement with my sister, which kind of threw me off. I haven’t really been able to stop thinking about it.
What was going through your mind during and after the disagreement with your sister?

During it, I just kept thinking, “Here we go again, I can’t deal with this.” Afterward, I felt kind of stuck, like I didn’t know what to do to make things better. I kept replaying it in my head and wondering if I should reach out or just leave it alone. Mostly, I just felt really unsure and kind of helpless.
When you notice those thoughts like “Here we go again, I can’t deal with this,” how does that affect what you do or how you feel in the moment?

I guess it makes me feel kind of anxious and small, like I’m not up for handling what’s going on. Sometimes I just shut down and try to avoid talking more, or I end up texting someone else in my family to ask what I should do. It’s like I need someone to tell me how to fix things, because I don’t really trust myself to handle it alone. Honestly, it leaves me feeling kind of embarrassed about needing that help.
What do you think would happen if you tried to handle the situation with your sister on your own, without reaching out for advice?

I don’t know… I feel like I’d probably mess it up or make things worse between us. I worry she’d just get more annoyed with me, and then I’d really be on my own. It’s kind of scary to think about not having someone to back me up. I guess I just don’t feel confident that I could fix it by myself.
When you imagine the possibility of things going better than you expect, what might that look like?

I guess if things went better, maybe I’d actually say what I’m feeling to my sister, and she’d listen instead of just getting upset. Maybe we’d actually sort things out, and I’d feel like I did something right for once. It would be a relief not to have to run to someone else to help fix it. I think I’d feel a bit proud of myself, if that ever happened.
What small step could you take to express your feelings to your sister in a way that feels manageable for you?

Maybe I could just send her a text saying I don’t want us to be upset with each other. Nothing too deep, just let her know I care and want to talk when we’re both ready. That feels a bit less overwhelming than a big conversation. I think I could manage that.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has often felt excluded and criticized in social settings since childhood, leading to a persistent sense of not fitting in. Past experiences of rejection from peers and family have reinforced feelings of being flawed and unworthy of connection.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I try to engage, people will notice how flawed I am." "I have to be perfect to be accepted." "If I make a mistake, I will be rejected." These thoughts create a constant pressure to hide true feelings and avoid social risks.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid social gatherings and withdraws from conversations to prevent embarrassment. They also rehearse what to say in advance and rely on humor or self-deprecation to deflect attention from perceived flaws.
ccd.situation: At a small work gathering, the patient is asked to join a group conversation but feels uneasy and worries that others will judge them harshly.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "They think I’m awkward and weird." "I’m going to say something stupid." "I don’t belong here, and they’ll notice I’m not like them."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, and sadness arise as the patient feels exposed and vulnerable in the social setting.
ccd.behaviors: The patient stays quiet, avoids eye contact, and eventually leaves the conversation early to escape the discomfort and perceived judgment.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

It’s been okay, I guess. Work has been a bit stressful. I went to this small get-together with some people from the office, and I didn’t really feel comfortable. I just kind of kept to myself most of the time.
What thoughts were going through your mind while you were at the get-together that made you feel uncomfortable?

Um, I kept thinking that I didn’t really fit in with everyone else. I was worried they’d notice how awkward I felt. I kept second-guessing what I wanted to say, so I just stayed quiet instead. It was pretty hard to relax.
When you noticed those thoughts about not fitting in and feeling awkward, how did your body feel in that moment?

My chest felt tight, and my hands got kind of sweaty. I noticed my heart was beating faster than normal. I felt tense, like I just wanted to disappear or leave. It was hard to even make eye contact with anyone.
What did you do after noticing those physical sensations and feelings during the event?
I mostly stayed quiet and tried to look busy on my phone. After a while, I found an excuse to leave early. I just felt too uncomfortable to stay. I didn’t really talk to anyone before I left.
Looking back, what do you think you might have expected would happen if you had tried to join in a conversation or connect with someone there?

Honestly, I thought I’d say something stupid and people would judge me. I figured they’d notice how nervous I was and think I was weird. It felt safer to just keep to myself than risk making a fool of myself. I guess I just assumed they wouldn’t really want me there anyway.
When you catch yourself thinking that others would judge you or not want you there, do you find any evidence that supports or challenges those thoughts?

I mean, I don’t really have proof either way. No one actually said anything mean to me at the gathering. But at the same time, no one really went out of their way to talk to me either. It just sort of feels like I’m invisible, which makes me think I don’t belong.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overshadowed by my siblings who seemed to excel effortlessly, while I struggled to keep up. My parents frequently expressed disappointment in my academic and social achievements, which made me internalize the idea that I wasn’t capable of succeeding. These experiences have left me doubting my abilities and fearing failure in new challenges.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to take on new tasks, I’m bound to mess up because I’m just not capable like others. People will see me as inadequate if I reveal my true abilities. It’s safer not to push myself too hard because I’ll only confirm my incompetence and disappoint others again.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid situations where I might be evaluated or compared to others, such as speaking up in meetings or pursuing new projects. When I do try something new, I over-prepare to cover up my perceived weaknesses or procrastinate until the last minute to minimize the chance of failure. Sometimes, I distract myself with comfort activities to avoid feeling overwhelmed by self-doubt.
ccd.situation: I was offered a chance to lead a small project at work, which could help me develop new skills and gain recognition. However, the idea of managing responsibilities and being judged by my colleagues made me feel anxious and hesitant to accept the role.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “What if I’m not good enough to handle this? I’ll probably mess things up and everyone will realize I’m incompetent. Maybe I should just say no before I embarrass myself.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, overwhelmed, and ashamed. There’s also a deep sense of frustration mixed with sadness because I want to grow but feel stuck and powerless.
ccd.behaviors: I delay responding to the project offer, hoping the opportunity will pass without me having to face it. I avoid discussing the project with colleagues and keep my workload minimal to avoid drawing attention to my skills. This withdrawal reinforces my feelings of helplessness and keeps me from moving forward.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um… it’s been kind of a mixed bag, I guess. Work has been busy, and I’ve been feeling a bit on edge, but I suppose that’s not unusual for me. There’s something that happened that’s been on my mind a lot, but I’m not sure if it’s really worth talking about. I just… I don’t know, I’ve felt a bit out of sorts.
When you say you’ve felt a bit on edge and out of sorts, can you tell me more about what’s been going through your mind or what you’ve been feeling physically during those times?

Um, yeah… I guess it’s mostly this sort of tight, anxious feeling in my chest, like I’m bracing for something to go wrong. My thoughts have been kind of scattered, too—I keep replaying certain situations in my head and worrying about what could happen next. Sometimes I catch myself overthinking small things at work, like an email I sent or a meeting I was in, and I start wondering if I said the wrong thing. Physically, it’s mostly the tension and this restlessness, like I can’t quite relax.

It sounds like those worries and physical sensations come up a lot for you—when you notice yourself replaying those situations or thinking about what might go wrong, what do you usually tell yourself in those moments?

Honestly, I tend to be pretty hard on myself when that happens. I’ll start thinking things like, “Why did I say that?” or “I probably sounded clueless.” Sometimes I just tell myself that I should’ve known better or done better—like I’m always a step behind everyone else. I end up feeling almost embarrassed for even trying, and then I just want to shrink away or disappear a bit.
That sounds really tough, feeling like you’re being so critical of yourself. When those self-critical thoughts come up, how do they affect your mood or your behavior afterward?

Yeah, it definitely gets to me. After those thoughts hit, I usually feel pretty anxious and kind of down on myself—like there’s this heaviness that makes everything harder. Sometimes I’ll avoid talking to people or taking on new things because I just don’t want to risk messing up again. I end up procrastinating or just doing the bare minimum, which makes me feel even worse later on.

It sounds like those feelings and thoughts are creating a cycle that’s hard to break. When you notice yourself starting to avoid things or procrastinate, what do you usually do in those moments instead?

Um, well… I guess I mostly distract myself, honestly. I’ll scroll on my phone or watch YouTube, just to avoid thinking about whatever’s making me anxious. Sometimes I’ll tell myself I’ll get to it later, but then I just keep putting it off and feel more stressed as the deadline gets closer. It’s like I know I’m just delaying the inevitable, but it feels easier to escape for a bit than to face it head-on.

That makes a lot of sense—distraction can feel like a way to catch a break from the stress. When you do face those tasks or situations later on, what tends to happen? Are there any moments when you’ve noticed things going better than you expected?

Hmm… I guess sometimes, when I finally have no choice but to do the thing I’ve been avoiding, it doesn’t turn out as badly as I feared. Like, the world doesn’t end, and people don’t make a big deal out of it. But it still feels really uncomfortable leading up to it, and even if it goes okay, I end up thinking about all the little things I could’ve done better. It’s hard for me to actually feel good about it, even if I didn’t mess up in any obvious way.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like I don’t fit in with others and that I’m not good enough. Growing up, I was criticized a lot and told I wouldn’t amount to much, which made me believe I’m a failure.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m not perfect, people will think less of me. I must avoid making mistakes or showing weakness, or else I’ll be rejected. There’s no point trying because I’ll just mess things up anyway.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to keep to myself to avoid embarrassment and rejection. Sometimes I overprepare or avoid social situations entirely to prevent failure. Other times, I might put myself down before others do it first.
ccd.situation: I’m at a party where I don’t know many people, and I’m expected to join conversations and mingle.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to say something stupid and everyone will think I’m useless.” “Nobody wants to talk to me because I’m a loser.” “I always mess up in situations like this.”
ccd.emotions: Anxious, ashamed, and lonely. I feel a knot in my stomach and a sinking feeling like I don’t belong.
ccd.behaviors: I stay on the sidelines, avoid eye contact, and don’t join conversations. I might leave early or keep my responses very short when spoken to.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Work was kind of stressful, and I went to this party over the weekend. I didn’t really know many people there, so it was a bit awkward. I mostly just kept to myself.
When you were at the party and felt awkward, what kind of thoughts were going through your mind?

I just kept thinking I probably looked out of place. I was worried I’d say something dumb if I tried to talk to anyone. It felt like everyone else was more confident and had something interesting to say, and I just… didn’t. So I mostly hung back and hoped nobody noticed me.
What emotions did you notice in yourself while having those thoughts at the party?

I felt really anxious and embarrassed, honestly. My chest felt tight and I just wanted to hide. I guess I also felt kind of sad, like I didn’t belong there. It was pretty uncomfortable the whole time.
When you notice those feelings of anxiety and sadness in situations like that, what do you usually do next?

Usually, I just try to keep a low profile. I avoid starting conversations and stick to the edge of the room. Sometimes I think about leaving early so I don’t have to deal with it. I just don’t want to make a fool of myself.
Have there been times when you did try to start a conversation or engage more, and if so, what happened?

Yeah, I’ve tried a couple times. It usually feels forced, and I worry I’m coming across as awkward. Sometimes people are polite, but I always feel like they’re just being nice and don’t really want to talk to me. Afterward, I end up replaying everything I said and feeling embarrassed.
What do you think it would mean about you or your social skills if someone did respond politely but didn’t seem genuinely interested?

I guess it just makes me think I’m boring or not worth talking to. Like, if they’re only being polite, it must be because they don’t actually want me around. It kind of confirms what I already worry about—that I don’t really fit in. It’s hard not to take it personally.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like I don’t measure up to others, especially in social settings. Growing up, I was often criticized and told I wasn’t good enough, which made me avoid speaking up or expressing my true feelings.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to assert myself, people won’t like me or I’ll end up being rejected. It’s safer to just go along with what others want so I don’t cause trouble or get judged.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to agree with others even when I disagree, try to please everyone, and avoid conflict at all costs. Sometimes I stay quiet or withdraw because I worry I’ll say something wrong and embarrass myself.
ccd.situation: At a social gathering, someone asks for my opinion, and I feel pressure to respond but worry that what I say won’t be valued or might lead to rejection.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I say what I really think, they’ll see I’m a failure and won’t want to be around me.” “I’m just a loser who can’t do anything right.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, and sadness. I feel small, worthless, and scared of being judged or rejected.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet or give vague, agreeable answers. I smile and nod even when I disagree, trying to avoid drawing attention to myself or rocking the boat.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting, just the usual stuff. I’ve just been keeping busy with work and, you know, trying to keep up with everything. Thanks for asking!
When you say you’re trying to keep up with everything, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind during those times?

Oh, um, I don’t know… I just worry about not disappointing people, I guess. I don’t want to let anyone down, so I try to stay on top of things. Sometimes I think maybe I’m not doing enough, but I’m probably just overthinking it. It’s not a big deal, really.
When you notice yourself thinking you might not be doing enough, how does that affect the way you feel in the moment?

Hmm… I guess I start to feel kind of tense and anxious, like I should be doing more or trying harder. Sometimes I just feel a little down on myself, like I’m not measuring up. But I usually just push those feelings aside and try not to make a fuss. I don’t want to bother anyone with my issues.
What do you usually do when you push those feelings aside—do you find any particular ways to cope or distract yourself?

Um, I usually just keep myself busy, like focusing on work or chores so I don’t have to think about it too much. Sometimes I’ll just agree with whatever people want or need from me, so things stay calm. I try to avoid any situations that might get uncomfortable. Honestly, I just don’t want to mess things up or make anyone upset.
When you agree with others or avoid certain situations to keep things calm, what impact does that have on how you feel about yourself afterward?

Hmm… I guess, sometimes, I feel a bit relieved in the moment because there’s no conflict or awkwardness. But later, I kind of feel disappointed in myself for not speaking up or saying what I really think. It’s like, I wish I could just be more confident, but I’m scared I’ll mess things up if I do. So, I mostly just keep quiet.
Can you recall a recent time when you wanted to speak up or express yourself but chose to stay quiet instead? What was going through your mind at that moment?

Yeah, actually, just last weekend at a friend’s get-together, someone asked my opinion about something. I wanted to share what I really thought, but I just nodded and agreed with everyone else. I remember thinking, “If I say what I actually think, they’ll probably think I’m weird or just not as smart as them.” So I just stayed quiet and kind of smiled along.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt like I couldn’t meet my parents’ expectations, and they would criticize me harshly. In past relationships, I’ve repeatedly felt ignored or dismissed, which made me believe I’m not good enough to be truly loved or valued.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to connect with someone, I’ll just end up embarrassing myself or being rejected. People only stick around if you’re confident and successful, and since I’m not, they won’t want me. It’s safer to keep my distance than risk being hurt again.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid deep conversations or expressing my feelings because I get off track and start talking about unrelated things to distract from the discomfort. Sometimes I withdraw completely or joke around to mask my insecurity and keep others at arm’s length.
ccd.situation: I’m having a disagreement with my partner, and when they bring up something that bothers them, I quickly jump to unrelated topics instead of addressing the issue directly.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: They’re going to think I’m stupid or annoying if I try to explain myself. I always mess things up in conversations. Why bother, I’ll just make it worse anyway.
ccd.emotions: Anxious, ashamed, and defeated. There’s this heavy feeling of hopelessness and sadness that I’m not good enough to have a healthy relationship.
ccd.behaviors: I change the subject abruptly and avoid eye contact, which makes my partner frustrated and distant. I pull away emotionally and sometimes shut down, making it harder to resolve the conflict.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Uh, it’s been… okay, I guess? Work was kind of busy, so that kept me distracted. Oh, I did end up watching that documentary about whales I mentioned last time—did you know some of them can sleep with half their brain awake? Sorry, um, what was the question again?
That’s interesting about the whales! When you think about your busy work week, what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you during those busy moments?

Yeah, whales are wild, right? Um, during work, I guess I mostly just try to focus on getting things done—it’s easier if I keep busy, you know? Sometimes I get this feeling like, if I mess up or miss something, everyone’s going to notice and it’ll be a big deal. Actually, my coworker brought in cupcakes for her birthday the other day, and it reminded me of this time in middle school when I dropped a whole tray of snacks at a party and everyone laughed... Anyway, work is fine, just kind of stressful sometimes.
It sounds like there might be some worry about making mistakes and how others might react—how do you usually respond to those feelings when they come up at work?

Yeah, I guess I do get kind of nervous about that stuff. Usually, I just try to keep my head down and not say much, just focus on whatever task I’m supposed to be doing. Sometimes I’ll crack a joke or change the subject if people start talking about things I’m not comfortable with. Oh, and I like to go refill my coffee even if I don’t really need more, just to kind of escape for a minute.
When you use those strategies like keeping busy, joking, or taking a coffee break to manage your nervousness, how well do they help you feel calmer or more in control?

Uh, sometimes it helps a bit, I guess—it distracts me for a while. But honestly, I usually end up feeling kind of weird afterward, like I didn’t really deal with anything. And then I start overthinking what I said or did, and worry that I sounded awkward or made things worse. Oh, speaking of coffee, I tried this new hazelnut creamer—have you ever had that? It’s actually pretty good.
It sounds like those moments of distraction give you a break but then the worries come back—what do you notice happening in your thoughts or body when those worries return?

Yeah, the worries definitely come back. I start replaying conversations in my head, trying to remember exactly what I said or if I sounded dumb. My stomach gets kind of tight, and sometimes my chest feels heavy, like I can’t really relax. Oh, and I tend to bite my nails a lot, which is a habit I picked up as a kid—my mom used to get so annoyed about it, actually.
When you notice that tight stomach and heavy chest, along with replaying conversations, what do you usually tell yourself about those moments or about yourself?

I usually just think, “Ugh, why did I say that?” or, “I probably sounded so weird.” Sometimes I tell myself I should’ve just kept quiet or not said anything at all. It’s like, I never quite get it right, you know? And then I end up feeling like maybe people would just rather I not be around.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt isolated growing up and never really felt like I fit in with others. Past relationships have ended with me feeling rejected, which made me believe I’m someone people don’t want to be around.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I get close to people, they will eventually leave me because I’m not lovable. It’s safer to keep my distance than risk being hurt. No one truly cares about me the way I care about them.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid social gatherings and keep conversations brief to prevent people from getting too close. Sometimes I withdraw completely or distract myself with work to avoid feeling lonely.
ccd.situation: I’m at a party where I don’t know many people, and I notice others chatting and laughing together.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They’re all connected, but I’m on the outside. Nobody here wants to talk to me. I’m going to end up alone as always.”
ccd.emotions: Loneliness, sadness, and anxiety start to build up inside me. I feel a heavy sense of shame and hopelessness about my social life.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet in the corner, avoid eye contact, and eventually leave early to escape the uncomfortable feelings. I don’t try to reach out or engage with anyone.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

messages.content: How has your week been for you so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Work kept me busy, so that was a bit of a distraction. I went to this party over the weekend, but I didn’t stay long. I just didn’t really feel like I fit in there.
When you noticed that feeling of not fitting in at the party, what thoughts were going through your mind?

I was just kind of thinking that everyone else seemed comfortable, like they all knew each other and had something in common. I felt out of place. It was like I was just… there, but not really a part of things. I kept wondering if anyone even noticed I showed up.
What emotions did those thoughts about not fitting in bring up for you while you were at the party?

Mostly, I just felt really alone. There was this heaviness—sadness, I guess. I got pretty anxious too, like I didn’t know what to do with myself. I just felt out of place, and honestly, kind of ashamed.
When you felt that heaviness and shame, how did you respond or cope in that moment? Did you do or think anything to try to manage those feelings?

I kind of just kept to myself, standing off to the side. I didn’t try talking to anyone—I just waited for a bit and then left early. I kept thinking it was better to leave than to stick around feeling awkward. Honestly, I mostly just wanted to disappear at that point.
Looking back, is there any part of that situation where you noticed a different thought or feeling, even if just a small one, that didn’t fit with the idea that you should disappear?

I guess there was a moment when someone smiled at me and asked how I was doing. For a split second, I thought maybe I could try saying something and join in. But that feeling didn’t last long—I just felt too nervous to really go for it. Still, I did notice it, even if it faded pretty fast.
That moment sounds important. What do you think made it hard for that feeling of connection to last or grow stronger?

I think I just get stuck thinking people aren’t really interested in talking to me, even if they seem friendly. It’s like, I always expect it to go badly, so I pull back before I even give it a chance. I worry I’ll just end up feeling let down or embarrassed. It’s hard to believe anything different might happen.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced several episodes of financial instability in the past, including job loss and unexpected expenses. These events have reinforced a sense of vulnerability and lack of control over their financial situation.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t manage every detail perfectly, everything will fall apart. I can’t trust myself to handle money properly. Asking for help means I’m weak or failing.
ccd.coping_strategies: Avoiding looking at bills or bank statements, procrastinating on financial decisions, and withdrawing from discussions about money. Sometimes seeking reassurance from friends but feeling ashamed afterward.
ccd.situation: Facing upcoming bills and uncertain income, the patient worries about how they will cover expenses and keep up with financial responsibilities.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m completely out of control with my money." "I’m going to end up in debt again." "There’s no way I can fix this."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, helplessness, shame, and frustration.
ccd.behaviors: Avoiding checking financial accounts, withdrawing from conversations about money, and sometimes impulsively spending to escape the stress.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Work’s been a little stressful, and I’ve had some stuff on my mind. Just trying to get through the days, really.
Can you tell me more about what’s been on your mind and what specifically at work has felt stressful?

Yeah, I mean… mostly just money stuff, honestly. There's a bunch of bills coming up, and with work being kind of unpredictable, it’s hard not to think about it. I keep worrying I’ll mess something up. Work itself is just busy—lots of things piling up.
When you notice yourself worrying about the bills or messing up at work, what kinds of thoughts tend to come up for you in those moments?

I guess I just start thinking I’m not handling things right. Like, I should’ve planned better or something. Sometimes it feels like I’m slipping and there’s nothing I can do to stop it. It’s kind of overwhelming.
That sounds really tough. When you feel like you’re slipping or not handling things well, how do you usually respond or cope with those feelings?

Honestly, I try to just ignore it for a while. I’ll avoid looking at my accounts or opening bills because it just stresses me out more. Sometimes I end up distracting myself with other things, even if I know it’s not really helping. Other times, I just kind of shut down and don’t talk to anyone about it.
What do you notice happens afterward when you avoid those stressors or shut down—does it change how you feel or how the situation unfolds?

At first, I feel a bit of relief because I can pretend it’s not there for a while. But then the stress just comes back, usually worse, because I know I’m putting things off. I end up feeling even more anxious and kind of ashamed for not dealing with it. It’s like I’m just stuck in the same loop.
When you feel that cycle starting—the relief that turns into more anxiety and shame—what do you think might help break that loop or change how you respond?

I don’t really know. Sometimes I think maybe if I just forced myself to sit down and look at everything, it wouldn’t be so bad…but I always stop myself. Part of me feels like asking for help might make things easier, but I worry people will judge me or think I’m failing. It’s hard to believe there’s a way to really fix it.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated situations in past relationships where their needs were overlooked or dismissed, leading to feelings of powerlessness. They often felt unable to assert themselves or influence the outcome of conflicts, reinforcing a sense of helplessness.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I don’t keep others happy, they will leave me." "I have to put others’ needs before my own to avoid rejection." "Speaking up will only cause problems or make things worse."
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid conflict by agreeing with others even when they disagree, often suppressing their own feelings. They overextend themselves to please others, hoping to maintain harmony and prevent abandonment.
ccd.situation: In a recent argument with their partner, the patient felt ignored and unable to express their true feelings, fearing that pushing back would cause the partner to get upset or leave.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "If I say how I really feel, they’ll be angry with me." "I can’t change this situation; there’s nothing I can do." "I’m just helpless in making this relationship work."
ccd.emotions: Feelings of sadness, anxiety, and frustration. A sense of being overwhelmed and stuck.
ccd.behaviors: The patient withdraws emotionally and physically from the conversation, avoids expressing their opinions, and tries to smooth things over by agreeing or apologizing even when they feel wronged.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too out of the ordinary—just busy with work and stuff. I had a bit of a disagreement with my partner, but it’s probably not a big deal. I’ve just been trying to keep things calm at home.
When you think about that disagreement with your partner, what thoughts or feelings come up for you?

Oh, um, I don’t know... I mostly just feel kind of anxious, I guess. I worry that if I say too much, it might make things worse between us. I mostly just tried to apologize and move on, you know? I just don’t want to cause any more problems.
What do you notice happens inside you when you hold back your thoughts to avoid causing more problems?

Hmm... I guess I feel a bit tense, maybe kind of frustrated, but I try not to focus on it too much. I usually just tell myself it's better this way, so things don’t get out of hand. Sometimes I feel a little bit sad afterwards, but I don’t really know what else to do. I just want everyone to be okay.
When you feel that sadness after holding back, what do you imagine would happen if you shared some of your thoughts or feelings with your partner?

Honestly, I just worry that if I say how I really feel, they’ll get upset or maybe even leave. I know that probably sounds silly, but I can’t help thinking it. I just don’t want to risk it turning into an argument or making things worse. It feels safer to just keep it to myself.
What evidence do you have that sharing your true feelings would lead to your partner getting upset or leaving?

Well, I guess there have been times in the past—maybe not always with my current partner, but in other relationships—where things got worse when I spoke up. People have gotten angry or distant with me before. It just kind of sticks with me, I suppose. I’d rather avoid that happening again.
How might your current partner’s reactions be similar or different from those past experiences when you express your feelings?

Um, that’s a good question... I guess my partner doesn’t usually yell or anything like that. But sometimes they just sort of shut down or seem annoyed if I bring up something that’s bothering me. It’s not exactly the same as before, but I still get worried about it. I just hate the idea of making things uncomfortable between us.

core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt criticized and rejected by people I cared about, which made me believe I was inherently flawed and harmful to others. Over time, these experiences reinforced a deep sense that I am not just unwanted but actually dangerous to those around me. This background has shaped how I view myself and my relationships today.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I believe that if people get too close to me, they'll eventually see how bad I really am and get hurt or disappointed. I tell myself that showing my true self is risky because it will push others away or cause harm. I also think that I have to keep my distance or hide parts of myself to protect others and avoid being abandoned.
ccd.coping_strategies: To manage these feelings, I often avoid sharing my thoughts or feelings, keeping others at arm’s length to prevent rejection. I might withdraw or become overly compliant, hoping to minimize conflict and keep people from seeing my “dangerous” side. Sometimes, I blame myself harshly to preempt criticism from others.
ccd.situation: My partner asked me to talk about how I’m feeling after a disagreement, wanting to understand me better. I felt a surge of fear and hesitation, worried that opening up would reveal how toxic and bad I really am, and that they would want to leave me. The thought of being vulnerable triggered a flood of self-doubt.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I tell them the truth, they’ll see I’m really a bad person and want nothing to do with me.” “I’m dangerous to be around; I’ll only cause pain if I get close.” “It’s safer to stay quiet and keep my distance than risk being hurt or hurting them.” These thoughts felt overwhelming and convincing in the moment.
ccd.emotions: I felt intense anxiety, shame, and sadness all at once, along with a deep sense of loneliness. There was also a creeping fear of rejection and abandonment, like I was standing on the edge of losing the person I care about most. These emotions made me want to shut down and protect myself.
ccd.behaviors: Instead of opening up, I clammed up and avoided the conversation, giving short answers or changing the subject. I withdrew emotionally, putting up walls and distancing myself to keep my partner from seeing the “real” me. This led to feeling even more isolated and misunderstood.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been a bit up and down, honestly. I guess nothing too out of the ordinary on the surface, but I’ve felt kind of on edge, especially around my partner. There was this disagreement we had that’s still lingering in my head. I just keep replaying it, wondering if I messed things up.
When you think back to that disagreement, what specific thoughts or worries come up for you?

Well, I…I keep worrying that, by speaking up or even just having feelings about what happened, I’m going to somehow ruin things between us. Like, I get afraid that if I really say what I’m thinking or feeling, they’ll realize there’s something wrong with me. It’s almost like I’m convinced that being honest will only make them want to pull away. I don’t know—it’s hard to shake this feeling that maybe I shouldn’t have said anything at all.
That sounds really tough. When you notice these worries about being honest and the fear of pushing your partner away, how do you usually respond or behave in those moments?

Honestly, I just kind of freeze up. I start to give really short, vague answers, or I’ll change the topic so I don’t have to go into anything real. Sometimes I just pretend like I’m fine or joke around to deflect. Deep down, I’m just trying to keep the conversation as surface-level as possible so I don’t accidentally say something that’ll make things worse.

It sounds like avoiding deeper conversation feels like a way to protect yourself and the relationship. What do you think might happen if you allowed yourself to share more openly, even if it feels risky?

I don’t know, it’s hard to even imagine letting myself do that. I guess part of me is convinced that if I really opened up, my partner would see a side of me they don’t like—maybe even decide they don’t want to be with me anymore. It’s like I’m afraid something ugly or hurtful will come out if I let down my guard. I just keep thinking it’s safer for everyone if I keep certain things to myself.
That fear of being seen as “ugly” or “hurtful” feels really heavy—when you imagine your partner seeing that side of you, what do you think that says about how you see yourself?

I guess…I just have this feeling deep down that I’m sort of messed up or maybe even a bad person, even if I can’t always explain why. There’s this constant worry that there’s something fundamentally wrong with me that I can’t hide forever. So, I get scared that if someone gets too close, they’ll see it too and want to leave. It’s exhausting always having to keep that part of myself hidden.
Thank you for sharing that—it sounds like a heavy burden to carry. When you notice these thoughts about being “messed up” or “bad,” how do you usually talk to yourself in those moments?

I’m pretty hard on myself, honestly. It’s like, the moment I start thinking those things, I go into this spiral of self-blame and telling myself I should know better or that I always ruin things. Sometimes I just mentally beat myself up, almost like I’m trying to punish myself before anyone else can. Other times, I just feel numb and try to push everything down so I don’t have to deal with it.

ccd.relevant_history: I have always struggled with feeling like I don’t measure up morally, especially after some difficult decisions I made in the past that hurt people I care about. These experiences have left me with a deep sense of shame and a persistent worry that I am inherently a bad person.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I am immoral, then I must be unworthy of love or respect, and others will reject me if they truly knew who I am. I need to be perfect and avoid making mistakes to prove I’m not as bad as I feel inside.
ccd.coping_strategies: I often try to avoid situations where my values might be challenged or where I might have to take responsibility. Sometimes I overcompensate by being overly helpful or apologetic, hoping to make up for the sense of wrongness I carry inside.
ccd.situation: I am trying to take on a new challenge for personal growth, like starting a new hobby or course, but I find myself doubting whether I deserve to succeed or even try because of my past mistakes.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “What if I fail and prove once again that I am a bad person? I don’t deserve to improve or be happy. People will see that I’m immoral and reject me.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, ashamed, and hopeless, as if no matter what I do, I am stuck with this feeling of being fundamentally flawed and unworthy.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid pursuing new opportunities or give up quickly when things get tough. I might isolate myself from others or stay silent about my struggles to keep my flaws hidden.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um… I guess it’s been alright. Nothing major happened, I just kept myself busy with work and stuff. I tried to start reading a new book, but honestly, I didn’t get very far. I don’t know, I just get kind of stuck sometimes, like I’m not sure what the point is.
When you notice yourself feeling stuck or questioning the point of things, what kinds of thoughts usually go through your mind?

Well… I guess I start thinking, like, who am I kidding? What’s the point in trying something new when I’m just going to end up disappointing myself or someone else? Sometimes I wonder if I even deserve to have nice things or enjoy myself, especially when I think about mistakes I’ve made before. It just feels like maybe it’s better to not even try, you know?
That sounds really tough. When these thoughts about not deserving nice things or fearing disappointment come up, how do you usually respond or cope with those feelings in the moment?

Yeah, it’s hard to shake off, honestly. Most of the time, I just… avoid whatever I was trying to do, or I’ll distract myself with something mindless, like scrolling on my phone. Sometimes I’ll try to act like everything is fine around other people, but inside I feel kind of hollow or like I’m hiding something. And I guess I just sort of hope the feelings will pass on their own if I ignore them long enough.

It sounds like avoiding and distracting yourself helps in the short term but leaves those feelings unresolved. When you do try to act like everything is fine, what do you notice happening to your mood or energy afterward?

Yeah, it definitely feels like a temporary fix. After I pretend things are okay, I usually just feel even more drained, like I’m putting on a mask all day and then I’m just exhausted. Sometimes I get this heavy feeling, like guilt or shame, for not being honest or for not dealing with the real issue. It’s almost like I’m punishing myself, though I’m not sure why I keep doing it.
That sounds really exhausting. When you notice that heavy feeling of guilt or shame, what do you tell yourself about why you might be punishing yourself?

Um… I guess I just feel like I deserve it, in some way? Like, I messed up before—hurt people I cared about—so I shouldn’t just get to move on and feel normal or happy. Part of me thinks that if I keep reminding myself how badly I screwed up, maybe I’ll somehow make it right, or at least not forget how wrong I was. But honestly, it just makes me feel stuck, like I can’t ever really forgive myself.
That sounds really painful to carry. When you think about the possibility of forgiving yourself, what worries or doubts come up for you?

Honestly, it feels almost… wrong to even think about forgiving myself. Like, if I let myself off the hook, I’m just ignoring the hurt I caused, or pretending it didn’t matter. I worry that if I move on, it means I don’t care enough or that I’m just trying to escape responsibility. And sometimes I wonder if other people would judge me for even wanting to forgive myself.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and powerless in family decisions, which made me believe I had little control over my life. Conflicts at home were frequent, and I learned to keep my feelings to myself to avoid making things worse.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to speak up, nothing will change, and I’ll just make things harder for myself. It’s safer to stay quiet and accept my situation because fighting back feels useless. No one really listens to me or values my input.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to withdraw and avoid confronting family issues, hoping they’ll resolve on their own. Sometimes I distract myself with work or hobbies to escape feelings of frustration. I also rehearse what I want to say but usually decide not to share it.
ccd.situation: During a family dinner, a discussion arises about future plans, and my opinions are ignored or quickly dismissed by others.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “There’s no point in trying to speak up. I’m stuck and can’t change anything.” “They don’t care what I think.” “I’m trapped in this situation with no way out.”
ccd.emotions: Frustration, sadness, helplessness, and a sense of isolation.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet during the conversation, avoid eye contact, and mentally check out. Later, I might withdraw to my room or avoid family gatherings to prevent feeling this way again.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too special happened. Work kept me busy. Just the usual stuff at home.
When you say “the usual stuff” at home, what kinds of things did you notice yourself thinking or feeling during those moments?
Um… I don’t know, really. I just try to get through the day. Sometimes I feel a bit frustrated, but I don’t really show it. I mostly keep to myself when things get a bit tense.
What kinds of situations at home tend to feel tense or frustrating for you?

I guess it’s mostly when people are talking about plans or decisions, and I just kind of sit there. Sometimes everyone’s talking over each other, and I feel like what I have to say doesn’t really matter. It gets frustrating, but I usually just stay quiet. It’s easier that way.
When you notice that feeling like your thoughts don’t matter, what do you tell yourself about yourself or the situation in those moments?

Um…I usually just think that there’s no point in saying anything. Like, nobody’s really going to listen anyway. I sort of feel like I’m just stuck with how things are. It’s hard to imagine it being different.
How does thinking that there’s no point in speaking up affect how you feel or act afterward?
I end up feeling kind of down, I guess. I usually just go quiet and try to avoid everyone. Sometimes I just want to be alone for a while. It’s like, what’s the use in bothering?
When you choose to be alone after feeling down, how does that impact your mood or your thoughts about the situation later on?

It’s a bit of both. Sometimes being alone helps me calm down, but other times it just makes me feel more isolated. I end up thinking about what happened over and over. I guess I just get stuck in those thoughts for a while.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt criticized and blamed by my family, which made me believe I was a bad person. I rarely received praise or support, leading me to internalize feelings of guilt and shame.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I am immoral, then I must not be worthy of love or respect. People will reject me if they truly know who I am. I need to hide my flaws to avoid disappointment.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid sharing my true feelings with family to prevent conflict. Sometimes I over-apologize or try to please others excessively to make up for what I think are my moral failures.
ccd.situation: During a family gathering, I overheard relatives talking about a mistake I made at work, and I felt exposed and judged.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They think I’m a terrible person. I can’t believe I messed up again. I’m so selfish and careless.”
ccd.emotions: Shame, anxiety, sadness, and a deep sense of unworthiness.
ccd.behaviors: I withdrew from the conversation, avoided eye contact, and left the gathering early to escape feeling judged.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. I had a family thing over the weekend, so that brought up a lot. I’ve just been feeling a bit off since then. Not really sure how to put it.
When you say you’ve been feeling “off,” can you describe what that feels like for you in your body or mind?
Um… it’s kind of like this heaviness in my chest, and I keep replaying stuff in my head. My stomach’s been kind of tight too. I just feel tense… and sort of tired all the time.
What kinds of thoughts have you been replaying in your head during this time?

Mostly things I wish I hadn’t said, or stuff I did wrong. I keep thinking about how my family probably sees me. Sometimes it just feels like I can’t get it out of my head. It’s like I messed up and everyone knows it.
When you notice those thoughts about messing up and how your family sees you, what emotions come up for you in those moments?
I feel embarrassed. Sometimes I just feel really small, almost like I want to disappear. There’s this deep sadness, too. And honestly, a lot of shame.
How do you usually respond or cope when you start feeling that embarrassment, sadness, or shame?
I mostly just shut down. I try to keep quiet, or I leave the room if I can. Sometimes I avoid talking to people for a while after. I just don’t want to make things worse.
When you choose to shut down or withdraw, what do you notice happens to those feelings of sadness or shame afterward?

They don’t really go away. Sometimes it feels a bit better for a little while, but then I just end up feeling even more alone. The sadness kind of lingers, and the shame just sits there. It’s like I can’t shake it off.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated situations where they felt powerless in social settings, such as feeling ignored or dismissed by peers and authority figures. These experiences have reinforced a deep sense of being stuck and unable to change their circumstances.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I try to speak up, I will just make things worse." "No one will listen to me, so it’s safer to stay quiet." "I have no control over how others treat me."
ccd.coping_strategies: Avoiding social gatherings or withdrawing during conversations to prevent feeling overwhelmed. Using humor or changing the subject to divert attention away from themselves. Staying silent instead of expressing needs or opinions to avoid conflict or rejection.
ccd.situation: At a group meeting, the patient wants to share their ideas but feels overwhelmed and fears being dismissed or ignored by others.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "They don’t care what I have to say." "I’m just going to mess this up." "I’m stuck in this situation and there’s no way out."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, frustration, helplessness, and sadness.
ccd.behaviors: Remaining silent during the meeting, avoiding eye contact, and physically withdrawing by slouching or looking down. Later, the patient might ruminate on the interaction, feeling even more trapped.

messages.content: How has your week been for you so far?

Um, it’s been… okay, I guess. Work’s been kind of busy, so I’ve just been trying to keep up with everything. I actually saw this funny video about a cat getting startled by a cucumber—reminded me of my old cat, actually, she used to jump at everything. Anyway, yeah, just the usual stuff, nothing major.
When you think about the busy work you've been managing, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind?

It’s mostly just, like, trying to keep my head above water, you know? Sometimes I’ll be halfway through a task and suddenly forget what I was doing because someone interrupts me, and then I get flustered. Oh, speaking of which, I had this weird dream where I kept missing trains—like, every time I’d get to the station, the train would pull away. Not sure why that popped into my head, but I guess it kind of feels the same at work sometimes.
That sounds frustrating. When you notice yourself getting flustered after interruptions, what do you usually say to yourself in those moments?

Um, I guess I just tell myself to keep quiet and deal with it, not make a fuss. Sometimes I think, “Well, no one’s really paying attention anyway, so why bother?” Oh, actually, last week I tried to speak up about a scheduling thing, but I ended up just mumbling and letting someone else take over. I don’t know, it just feels safer not to push it.
What feelings come up for you when you decide to stay quiet instead of speaking up?

Mostly I just feel kind of…tense, maybe a bit anxious. Sometimes I get annoyed with myself for not saying something, but then I also feel sort of relieved that I didn’t risk making things awkward. Oh, and I guess there’s this heaviness after, like I’m carrying something around all day. It’s a bit like when you forget your umbrella and then it rains, and you’re just stuck getting wet—except it’s not about the weather.
That heaviness sounds tough to carry. When you think back to a time you did speak up, even if it was just a little, what was different about how you felt afterward?

Hmm…honestly, it doesn’t happen much, but I remember once in college I actually asked a question in class, and my heart was pounding. I felt kind of shaky after, but also weirdly proud, like I did something big. But then I spent the rest of the day replaying the moment, worrying if I sounded stupid. It’s kind of a mixed bag, I guess—some relief, but also a lot of overthinking.
When you find yourself replaying that moment and worrying about how you sounded, what do you notice about the impact that has on your mood or energy?

Yeah, it usually just drains me. I get really quiet and tired, and I just want to go home and be by myself. Oh, and sometimes I’ll distract myself by organizing stuff or scrolling on my phone for way too long. It’s like I’m trying to forget about it, but it keeps coming back anyway.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and not valued by my family, which made me believe that I wasn’t someone others wanted around. At work, I’ve noticed that I tend to keep to myself and avoid sharing my ideas, fearing I won’t be accepted.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I show too much of myself or speak up, people will reject me. It’s safer to stay quiet and not draw attention because I don’t deserve to be liked or included. Trying to connect with others only leads to disappointment.
ccd.coping_strategies: I usually avoid social events or casual conversations at work to keep from feeling exposed. When pressured, I try to work harder silently rather than ask for help or express my concerns, hoping that if I’m not noticed, I won’t be judged.
ccd.situation: At work, there’s increasing pressure to collaborate on a team project, and I’m expected to share my ideas in meetings. I feel the tension rising as deadlines approach and more people are paying attention to my contributions.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one really wants to hear what I have to say.” “If I speak up, they’ll think I’m annoying or useless.” “I’m just not the kind of person people like to be around.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, lonely, and discouraged. There’s a persistent ache of sadness and a quiet fear of being rejected or ignored.
ccd.behaviors: I stay silent during meetings, avoid eye contact, and keep to myself during breaks. I work extra hours alone to avoid depending on others or being noticed too much.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting. Work’s been a bit busy, but that’s pretty normal. I haven’t really done much outside of that.
When you say work has been busy, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind during those busy times?

I just try to keep my head down and get things done. Sometimes I wonder if I’m keeping up with everyone else. I guess there’s this nagging feeling like… maybe I’m not doing enough. But I just try to focus on the tasks.
That nagging feeling of not doing enough—what does it make you think about yourself or your abilities?

I don’t know, I guess it makes me question if I even belong there sometimes. Like, maybe I’m not as good as the others. I get worried that people might notice I’m struggling. It’s hard to shake that feeling.
When those thoughts about not belonging or struggling come up, how do you usually respond or cope with them in the moment?

Usually, I just try to stay quiet and not draw any attention. I’ll keep working on my own, maybe put in some extra hours if I need to. I don’t really talk to anyone about it. It feels easier to just handle it myself.
What do you think might happen if you were to share these feelings with a coworker or someone you trust?

I’m not really sure. I guess I worry they might not understand, or maybe they’d just think I’m being dramatic. Maybe they’d get annoyed or see me differently. It just feels risky to put that out there.
That worry about how others might react—have there been times when you shared something and the response was different from what you expected?

Um... I can’t really think of a time, to be honest. I don’t usually open up much, so I don’t have a lot of experience with that. I kinda just keep things to myself most of the time. It feels safer that way.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced several past relationships where they felt unable to influence outcomes or assert their needs, leading to feelings of powerlessness. This pattern has been reinforced by repeated experiences of feeling dismissed or ignored by partners, contributing to a deep-seated sense of helplessness.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "I can’t make a difference in how others treat me," "If I try to change things, it won’t work," and "It’s safer to just go along with whatever happens." These beliefs create a mindset where the patient expects failure or rejection if they attempt to take control.
