ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid confrontation or difficult conversations, often withdrawing or staying silent to prevent conflict. They may also distract themselves with hobbies or work to escape feelings of frustration or sadness related to relationship issues.
ccd.situation: During a recent disagreement with their partner, the patient felt overwhelmed when their partner dismissed their concerns about feeling unappreciated. The patient found themselves unable to express their feelings fully and feared that speaking up would only make things worse.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "They don’t care about how I feel," "There’s nothing I can do to change this," and "If I say something, it will just make things worse." These thoughts reinforce the sense of helplessness and discourage taking action.
ccd.emotions: The patient feels sadness, frustration, and anxiety, mixed with a sense of resignation. There is also an underlying feeling of loneliness that comes from feeling unheard and powerless in the relationship.
ccd.behaviors: The patient withdraws emotionally and physically, avoiding further discussion or confrontation. They might also ruminate quietly, suppressing their feelings rather than addressing the issues directly, which perpetuates the cycle of helplessness.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been kind of busy, so I’ve been trying to stay on top of everything there. Actually, I tried a new recipe last night—have you ever made homemade pizza? Mine turned out kind of weird, but it was fun to try.
What went through your mind when you noticed the pizza didn’t turn out as you expected?

Oh, well, I just thought, “Of course, I messed it up.” I mean, nothing ever turns out the way I hope, honestly. But it wasn’t a big deal—I just kind of laughed it off and ended up cleaning the kitchen for a while. My partner didn’t say much about it; they just scrolled on their phone.
When you think about your partner’s reaction, how did that make you feel about the pizza and about yourself?

I don’t know... I guess it made me feel a bit stupid for even bothering with the pizza in the first place. Like, why did I put in the effort if no one really cares? Honestly, it sort of reminded me of other times when I try to do something nice or different and it just... doesn’t really matter. Anyway, I guess it’s not that important—there are worse things, right?
What do you notice happening inside you physically or emotionally when you think about those times you’ve tried and felt like it didn’t matter?

Um, I guess I just feel kind of heavy, like there’s this tightness in my chest. Sometimes I get this sinking feeling in my stomach too. It’s sort of like I want to just disappear, or maybe distract myself with something else. Oh, that reminds me—did you ever try those mystery documentaries on Netflix? They’re actually pretty interesting.
It sounds like those feelings of heaviness and wanting to disappear show up pretty strongly; when you notice those sensations, what thoughts usually come to your mind?

Yeah, I guess when that happens, I just sort of think, “What’s the point?” Or maybe, “No one really notices anyway.” Sometimes I wonder if I’m just making things harder for myself by caring so much. But then I just end up scrolling on my phone or tidying up the house—oh, I picked up this new organizing trick for closets, actually, and it’s oddly satisfying.
When you catch yourself thinking, “What’s the point?” or “No one really notices,” what do you imagine might happen if you didn’t care as much?

Hmm, I’m not really sure… I guess if I didn’t care, maybe things would be less disappointing, you know? Like, maybe I wouldn’t feel so let down when stuff doesn’t work out or people don’t react the way I hope. But then again, if I stopped caring, maybe that would just make me invisible. Oh, and speaking of invisible, did you ever see that old movie where the guy turns invisible? It’s a bit cheesy but kind of fun.

ccd.relevant_history: I have often felt powerless in my past relationships, especially when things didn’t go my way. Growing up, I was frequently told that my feelings didn’t matter and that I had to just accept what others decided. This has left me feeling like I have no real control over what happens around me or how people treat me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to take charge, I’ll only end up making things worse or being rejected. It’s safer to just go along with what others want because I can’t change the outcome anyway. Trying to assert myself feels like fighting against a losing battle where I’m destined to fail.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid confrontation and keep my opinions to myself, hoping that if I stay quiet, things won’t get worse. Sometimes I withdraw emotionally or physically from situations where I feel overwhelmed, convincing myself that it’s better not to rock the boat. At times, I might also second-guess myself constantly, trying to predict and prevent conflict.
ccd.situation: When my partner makes decisions without consulting me, I feel like I have no say in what happens between us. It feels as though my needs and feelings are invisible, and I’m just along for the ride, powerless to influence the relationship in any meaningful way.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I have no control over this.” “Nothing I do will change how they act.” “If I speak up, I’ll just make things worse or push them away.” “I’m always stuck in this helpless position.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious and frustrated, but mostly a deep sadness and hopelessness that things will never improve. There’s also a sense of fear—fear of being abandoned or ignored if I assert myself.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet and don’t express my true feelings or needs, even when it bothers me. I might withdraw or avoid spending time with my partner to protect myself from feeling hurt. Sometimes I just go along with whatever they decide, numbing myself to the reality that I’m not being heard.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um… it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too out of the ordinary happened, but—I don’t know—I’ve just been feeling kind of stuck. There have been a few moments with my partner where I wanted to say something, but I ended up just keeping quiet instead. It’s hard to explain, but sometimes it just feels easier not to say anything at all.
When you think about those moments with your partner, what kinds of thoughts or worries come up that make it feel easier to stay quiet?

Well, honestly, I just get this sort of heavy feeling, like… if I speak up, it’s not really going to help anything. I worry that maybe they’ll get annoyed, or think I’m being difficult, or even just… ignore what I’m saying anyway. Sometimes I picture it in my head, like I’m making a big deal over nothing, and then I end up feeling kind of bad or embarrassed for even wanting to say something. So I just sort of tell myself it’s better to let it go.
That sounds really challenging. When you imagine your partner getting annoyed or ignoring you, what do you notice happening inside you—like in your body or emotions?

Yeah, it’s not a great feeling. I notice my chest gets kind of tight, and I start to feel anxious—almost like this wave of dread. Sometimes I even feel a bit sick to my stomach, and there’s this urge to just disappear or leave the room. Emotionally, I guess there’s this mix of anxiety and… I don’t know, sadness? Maybe even a little bit of shame, like I shouldn’t even need to bring it up in the first place.
That’s a lot to carry in those moments. When you feel that mix of anxiety, sadness, and shame, what do you typically do to try to manage or cope with those feelings?

Usually, I just try to push the feelings down and distract myself with something else—like scrolling on my phone or finding something to do around the house. If it gets really overwhelming, I’ll sometimes go for a walk or just leave the room for a bit so I don’t have to deal with anyone. I kind of go into “autopilot” and just avoid talking about whatever’s bothering me. It’s like I convince myself that if I ignore it, maybe it’ll just go away on its own.
When you notice yourself going into “autopilot” and avoiding the issue, what do you think happens to those feelings or thoughts afterward—do they change or stay the same?

Honestly, they don’t really go away. Maybe for a little while I can distract myself, but the feelings just come back later—sometimes even stronger. It kind of builds up, like this heaviness that doesn’t really leave. And then I start thinking about everything I didn’t say, and it just makes me feel even more stuck.
That sounds really tough. When you think about the things you didn’t say, what kinds of thoughts or beliefs come up about yourself or your relationship?

I guess I start to wonder if my feelings really matter at all, or if I’m just supposed to kind of go along with things no matter what. I sometimes end up thinking that I’m not really important in the relationship, or that maybe I’m just too much or too sensitive. There’s also this thought that, no matter what I do, it won’t really make a difference anyway. It makes me feel small, like I don’t really have a place to stand up for myself.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced several financial setbacks over the past year, including job loss and mounting debt, which have significantly impacted their self-esteem and sense of security. They often recall past failures and feel they have let themselves and their family down.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I can’t manage my money well, I’m a failure." "People will think less of me if I’m struggling financially." "I don’t deserve to be successful or happy because I’m useless."
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid looking at bills or financial statements, withdraws from social situations to hide their struggles, and sometimes distracts themselves with excessive screen time or overeating to escape feelings of worthlessness.
ccd.situation: Facing overdue bills and mounting debt, the patient worries about how they will manage their finances and fears judgment from others.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m such a waste, I can’t do anything right." "I’m worthless because I can’t keep things together." "No one will respect me if they find out how bad I am with money."
ccd.emotions: Overwhelmed, ashamed, anxious, and hopeless, feeling trapped in a cycle of failure and self-criticism.
ccd.behaviors: Avoids opening mail, procrastinates on addressing financial issues, isolates themselves from friends and family, and experiences difficulty sleeping due to stress.

messages.content: How has your week been for you so far?
Honestly, it’s been pretty rough. I don’t really want to get into it, but things just feel like they’re piling up. I’m tired of pretending things are okay. I guess that’s all I want to say right now.
It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy load right now—when you say things are piling up, could you tell me more about what kinds of things are feeling overwhelming?

I don’t know, it’s just… everything. Bills, stuff at home, not having work. Feels like every time I try to deal with one thing, something else just drops on top of me. I don’t even want to look at my mail anymore.
That sounds really exhausting. When you notice those overwhelming feelings, what kinds of thoughts tend to go through your mind?

Honestly? Mostly just that I’m screwing everything up. Like, why can’t I just handle this like a normal person? I keep thinking I’m just messing up my life and my family’s too. It’s hard not to feel like a total failure.
That sounds really painful to carry. When you have those thoughts about messing up or being a failure, how do they affect what you do or how you feel physically?

I just shut down, I guess. I don’t want to talk to anyone, and I end up just sitting around doing nothing—or I’ll eat junk or scroll on my phone for hours to distract myself. My chest gets tight and I can’t sleep most nights. It just feels like I’m stuck.

Thank you for sharing that—it helps me understand what you’re going through. When you notice that tightness in your chest and difficulty sleeping, is there anything you try to do to help yourself feel a little better in those moments?

Honestly, I don’t really do anything that helps. I just kind of wait for it to pass or distract myself. Nothing actually makes it better, it just keeps coming back. Sometimes I wonder if it’ll ever get easier.

It makes sense that distraction feels like the only option when things are so intense. Have there been any times, even small ones, when something you did or thought helped ease those feelings, even just a little?

I don’t know. Maybe if I can forget about everything for a bit by watching TV or playing a game, it helps for like, a few minutes. But as soon as I stop, everything just comes rushing back. So… I guess nothing really works for long. Most of the time, I just end up feeling worse for wasting more time.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always struggled with feeling like I’m not a good person, especially when I make mistakes or hurt others, even if it was unintentional. This has made me doubt myself a lot and avoid taking on new challenges because I fear being judged as bad or immoral.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m immoral, people will reject me or think less of me. I have to hide my true self to avoid being seen as a bad person. Making a mistake means I’m a failure and can’t be trusted.
ccd.coping_strategies: I often try to keep my feelings and thoughts to myself to avoid conflict or criticism. Sometimes I push myself to work harder to prove I’m not immoral, but other times I just avoid situations where I might be judged.
ccd.situation: I was asked to lead a project at work, but I felt overwhelmed and worried I would mess things up or let others down.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to fail and everyone will see that I’m really a bad person.” “If I make a mistake, it proves I’m immoral.” “They’ll think I’m not fit for this role.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, and sadness.
ccd.behaviors: I procrastinated on starting the project and avoided asking for help because I didn’t want to admit I was struggling. I also withdrew from my coworkers and kept my distance.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been kind of stressful though. I’ve just been trying to keep up and not mess anything up. Nothing too exciting, really.
What kinds of thoughts go through your mind when you’re feeling stressed at work?

Mostly I just worry that I’m going to make a mistake and screw things up. Sometimes I catch myself thinking, “What if everyone realizes I don’t know what I’m doing?” It makes me second-guess myself a lot. I end up overthinking even the small decisions.
When you notice yourself thinking, “What if everyone realizes I don’t know what I’m doing?” how does that affect how you feel and what you do next?

I usually feel kind of nervous, and I start to doubt myself even more. Sometimes I freeze up and have trouble making decisions. I tend to just keep quiet and not ask for help, even if I need it. I don’t really want people to see that I’m struggling.
Can you remember a recent situation at work when you felt this way—nervous, doubting yourself, and hesitant to ask for help? What was happening then?

Yeah, actually, last week my manager asked me to lead this new project. I felt really overwhelmed because it seemed like a big responsibility. I kept thinking I’d mess something up and everyone would notice. So instead of reaching out for advice or support, I just kind of put things off and tried to handle it all on my own.
What do you imagine might happen if you did reach out for advice or support when you feel overwhelmed like that?

Honestly, I worry people would think I’m not capable or that I shouldn’t have been chosen to lead in the first place. I’m afraid they’d see me as weak or not good enough. Maybe they’d even talk about me behind my back. It just feels safer to keep my struggles to myself.

Honestly, nothing great. I keep thinking about how I mess up even the small stuff—at work, with friends, whatever. It’s like, no matter what I do, it never feels like enough. I just can’t shake this feeling that I’m always disappointing someone.

I usually just shut down. I’ll stop talking or make up some excuse to leave, like I did at that dinner. Most of the time, I just go home and distract myself with TV or something—anything to not think about it. I don’t really know what else to do.

Honestly, I just end up feeling more frustrated, like I’m failing at even that. Sometimes I get angry and just give up trying. So I usually go back to distracting myself or just avoiding everyone. Nothing really changes, I guess.

It sounds like you’re quite concerned about how others might perceive your work; how do those worries about making mistakes affect how you feel in the moment?

Yeah, I guess I do get pretty anxious—I feel it in my stomach, like a tightness. I just want people to be happy with what I do, so I get nervous that I’ll let them down. Sometimes I feel a bit…invisible, or like I’m just not measuring up. But I try to push those feelings aside and keep going.

Um, I usually just try to work harder, double-check everything, and make sure I don’t miss anything important. I’ll stay late if I have to, just to fix little things that might bother someone else. If I’m really nervous, I might keep quiet and avoid saying much in meetings. I don’t want to make things awkward or draw attention to myself.

I guess it helps a little in the moment because I feel like I’m doing everything I can to keep people happy. But sometimes, afterwards, I still worry I didn’t do enough or that people just see me as boring or not interesting. It’s kind of exhausting, honestly. But I feel like I have to do it, just in case.

Oh, I’m not sure… I worry people might notice if I say something wrong or miss something important. I guess I feel like they’d be disappointed or maybe even judge me. I just… I don’t want to stand out for the wrong reasons. It feels safer to just keep my head down, even if it’s tiring.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been kind of busy, so I’ve just been trying to keep up. Oh—and I tried to make plans with this friend, but it didn’t really work out, so I just ended up staying home. Anyway, not much else—just the usual stuff, nothing exciting.
When you think about the plans with your friend not working out, what thoughts or feelings come up for you?

I mean, I try not to let it bother me, but I did feel a little disappointed, I guess. Sometimes I start thinking, like, maybe they just had something else going on, but then it’s hard not to wonder if I did something wrong. It’s probably silly to overthink it—maybe I’m just being sensitive. Honestly, it’s not even the first time something like this has happened.
When those thoughts about possibly doing something wrong come up, how do they affect what you do or how you feel afterward?

I guess I start feeling kind of anxious, like I’m replaying the last few times we talked and second-guessing everything I said. It makes me want to just pull back and not reach out again, just to avoid feeling awkward or rejected. Instead, I end up distracting myself with work or Netflix or something. Oh, also, last night I reorganized my entire bookshelf just to keep busy—so that took a while.
What do you notice happens to your feelings of connection or closeness with your friend when you pull back and avoid reaching out?

Well, honestly, I start to feel pretty distant, not just from them but from people in general. It’s strange—like, the more I avoid reaching out, the lonelier I feel, even though I’m doing it to protect myself. Then I just end up thinking, “What’s the point?” Sometimes I wonder if I’m always going to be like this, just kind of stuck on the outside.
That sounds really tough. When you catch yourself thinking, “What’s the point?” what do you imagine might happen if you did reach out to your friend despite feeling anxious?

Um, I don’t know… Part of me thinks maybe it would be okay, like maybe they’d be happy to hear from me. But I also get this sinking feeling that they’d just ignore me, or I’d come across as needy or desperate. I guess I worry I’d just end up feeling worse if they don’t respond. Oh, and then I start thinking about all the times people have pulled away before—it’s like a whole spiral.
When you notice that sinking feeling and those worries, what do you tell yourself about your worth or about what you deserve in friendships?

Honestly, I usually just start thinking maybe I’m not the kind of person people want to be close to. Like, maybe there’s something about me that just doesn’t fit, or I’m too much somehow. I know it sounds dramatic, but sometimes it feels like I’m just not meant to have those kinds of close friendships other people seem to have. It’s weird—sometimes I even convince myself I’m better off alone, but it gets pretty lonely.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um… it’s been kind of rough, honestly. I mean, work was stressful, but that’s not really new. I guess what’s been bothering me more is stuff at home. My partner’s just been acting… different, and I can’t really shake the feeling that something’s off.
When you notice your partner acting differently, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind about what might be happening?

Well, I guess… my mind sort of jumps to the worst-case scenarios. Like, I start thinking maybe I did something wrong or maybe they’re just tired of me. It’s hard not to wonder if I’m somehow causing the distance, you know? I keep replaying things in my head, trying to figure out what I did to make them pull away.
When you replay those moments in your head, how does that affect the way you feel and how you behave around your partner?

Honestly, it just makes me feel really anxious and on edge. Sometimes I get super quiet around them because I don’t want to say the wrong thing, or I end up over-apologizing for stuff that doesn’t even matter. Other times I just sort of… pull back, try not to bother them, even though I really want to talk. It feels like I’m constantly second-guessing myself and what I should do.
What do you notice happens in your relationship when you become quiet or pull back like that?

Usually it just feels like there’s even more distance between us, which makes me feel worse. I can tell they notice that I’m acting differently too, but I don’t really know how to fix it. Sometimes they’ll ask if I’m okay, but I just say I’m fine because I don’t want to be a burden. Then I end up feeling even more alone, like I’m making things awkward for both of us.
What do you think might happen if you shared with your partner how you’re really feeling instead of saying you’re fine?

I don’t know… Part of me thinks maybe it would help, but I also get scared that I’ll come off as too needy or emotional. I worry they’ll get frustrated or think I’m just being difficult, and then they’ll want even more space from me. It’s like I want to be honest, but I’m afraid it’ll push them away even more. Sometimes I just feel safer keeping things to myself, even though it’s really lonely.
When you have thoughts about being “too needy” or “being difficult,” what evidence do you have that supports or challenges those beliefs?

Well, I guess the main thing that makes me think it’s true is stuff that’s happened before—not just with my partner, but in past relationships and even with family. Like, I remember times when I tried to talk about how I was feeling, and people would either get annoyed or kind of shut down, so I ended up just feeling embarrassed or like I made things worse. But… I don’t know, sometimes my partner does say they care and that I can talk to them. It’s just hard to believe that’s really true, or that it won’t somehow backfire if I open up too much.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and dismissed by family and friends, which made me believe I wasn’t worth their attention or affection. I learned to stay quiet and agreeable to avoid conflict and rejection.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t please others, they won’t like me or want to be around me. I have to put other people’s needs before my own to be accepted. Being myself might push people away because I’m not lovable as I am.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to say yes to everything and avoid expressing my true feelings to keep others happy. I often apologize even when it’s not necessary and suppress my own needs to avoid causing any discomfort.
ccd.situation: I’m at a small gathering where people are chatting and forming new connections. I feel nervous about joining the conversation and worry about being left out or ignored.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They probably don’t want me here.” “If I say something wrong, they’ll think I’m annoying.” “I have to be nice and agree with everyone so they don’t reject me.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and a deep sense of loneliness. I feel vulnerable and worried that I won’t be accepted or liked.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet and try to smile a lot, nodding along without sharing much about myself. I avoid drawing attention to myself and work hard to agree with others to keep the mood positive.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting happened. I’ve just been keeping busy with work and stuff. Sorry, I don’t really have much to share.
That’s alright—sometimes just sharing what’s on your mind can be helpful. What kinds of thoughts have been going through your head during your work or daily activities lately?

Oh, um… I guess I’ve been overthinking a lot, like worrying if I’m doing enough or if people at work notice me. Sometimes I wonder if I’m annoying people without realizing it. I keep second-guessing what I say, even about small things. Sorry, I know that probably sounds silly.
It sounds like those thoughts are making you feel uncertain about yourself—how do those worries usually affect how you feel in the moment?

Yeah, I guess I just get really anxious, like my stomach gets tight and I start to feel kind of down. I try not to let it show, though. I just smile and go along with whatever’s happening. I don’t want to bother anyone or make things awkward.
When you notice your stomach tightening and feeling down, what do you usually do next—do you try to distract yourself, or do you stay with those feelings for a while?

Um, I usually just try to push those feelings away and focus on what other people need or want. I kind of stay in the background and just listen. Sometimes I’ll just agree with whatever people are saying so things stay easy. I figure it’s better not to make a fuss or draw attention to myself.
It sounds like you often put others’ needs before your own to keep things smooth—how do you feel about yourself afterward, when you’ve been in that kind of background role?

Honestly, I feel kind of... empty, I guess? Like I was there, but not really part of things. I sometimes wonder if people even notice me at all. But I tell myself it’s better that way, so no one gets upset with me.
That feeling of emptiness sounds really tough—what do you think might happen if you expressed your own thoughts or needs more openly in those moments?

I don’t know… I worry people might not like what I have to say or they might think I’m difficult. I guess I’m afraid they’ll pull away or just stop including me. I feel like I’m too much sometimes, so it’s safer to just stay quiet. Sorry, I hope that makes sense.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like I don’t measure up to others, especially when it comes to how I look and what I have. Growing up, I didn’t get much positive attention or support, which made me doubt my worth.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m not attractive, then people won’t like me or want to be around me. If I can’t handle my money well, it proves I’m not good enough or responsible.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid looking at my bank statements or talking about money because it makes me feel anxious and ashamed. Sometimes I distract myself with shopping or eating to feel better for a little while.
ccd.situation: I just checked my bank account and realized I don’t have enough money to cover all my bills this month.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m such a failure. If I were better, I wouldn’t be in this mess. No one would want to be with someone who can’t even manage their own finances.”
ccd.emotions: I feel ashamed, anxious, and hopeless.
ccd.behaviors: I shut down and avoid dealing with my finances, skip calls from creditors, and isolate myself from friends and family.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been kind of rough, honestly. I had a lot on my mind, especially about money. I checked my account and it wasn’t good. I’ve been feeling pretty stressed out about it.
When you noticed your account balance, what specific thoughts went through your mind?
Mostly just... “I messed up again.” I kept thinking, “Why can’t I get it together?” It’s embarrassing. I felt like I just wanted to avoid the whole thing.
What feelings did those thoughts bring up for you in that moment?
I felt really ashamed and kind of hopeless. My chest got tight, and I just wanted to hide. It’s like this wave of anxiety hits me and I don’t know what to do with it.
When you notice that wave of anxiety, what do you usually do to cope or respond?

I usually just try to distract myself. Sometimes I’ll scroll through my phone or eat something, even if I’m not hungry. I don’t really want to talk to anyone, so I end up staying by myself. It feels easier to just not deal with it for a while.
What do you think happens to those feelings of anxiety and shame after you distract yourself or avoid the situation?

They don’t really go away. Maybe for a little bit, I can forget about them, but then they come back even stronger. It’s like I’m just putting it off, and then I feel even worse later. Sometimes I feel guilty for avoiding everything too.
When you notice those feelings coming back stronger, what do you wish you could do differently in that moment?

I wish I could just face it instead of running away. I want to be able to look at my situation without freaking out. I’d like to feel more in control, I guess. Mostly, I just wish I didn’t feel so bad about myself all the time.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt rejected by people I care about, especially in romantic relationships. Growing up, I remember being told I wasn’t pretty enough or interesting enough to be liked, which made me doubt my worth.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I want others to like me, I have to be agreeable and avoid causing any problems. If I’m not constantly pleasing others, they will notice my flaws and stop caring about me.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to put others’ needs before my own and agree with everything they say, even when I don’t want to. I avoid expressing my true feelings because I’m afraid it will push people away.
ccd.situation: I’m on a date and the other person seems distracted, not making much eye contact or engaging deeply in conversation.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They must think I’m boring or unattractive. I’m probably messing this up because I’m not interesting enough.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, sad, and ashamed, worried that I’m not good enough to be loved or wanted.
ccd.behaviors: I try harder to be charming and agreeable, laughing at jokes I don’t find funny and steering the conversation to topics I think they’ll like, even if I don’t care about them.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting, just work and the usual stuff. I did go out on a date, which was… fine, I think. How are you?
Thanks for sharing that. What went through your mind before and during the date?

I was honestly pretty nervous beforehand, just hoping I wouldn’t say anything weird or mess things up. During the date, I kept wondering if I was coming across okay, or if they were maybe bored. I tried to just focus on them and ask questions, so I wouldn’t seem self-centered or anything. I don’t know, I just really wanted it to go well.
It sounds like you were quite focused on how you were being perceived. What do you think would have happened if you had said something you thought was weird or made a mistake?

Oh, um...I guess I worry they’d notice right away and probably lose interest. I feel like if I say the wrong thing, they’ll just think I’m awkward or boring. Maybe they'd just want to leave or not talk to me again. I really don’t want to give them any reason to not like me.
That sounds like a lot of pressure to put on yourself. When you notice those thoughts about being awkward or boring, how do you usually respond to them or what do you do next?

I just try even harder to seem interesting or agreeable, I guess. Like, I’ll laugh at their jokes or agree with whatever they say, even if I don’t really think that way. Sometimes I end up talking less about myself, so I don’t risk saying something wrong. I just want them to like me, so I kind of…hide what I’m really thinking.
It sounds like you’re trying to protect yourself by not showing your true thoughts. How do you feel afterward, when you’ve held back like that?

Um…honestly, I usually feel kind of drained. And a little sad, I guess, because I’m not really sure if they liked me or just the version of me I showed them. Sometimes I regret not being more myself. But I also feel like if I was, they might not have liked me at all.
That must be tough to feel torn between wanting to be liked and wanting to be yourself. Can you tell me about a time when you felt comfortable being yourself with someone—what was different then?

Hmm…that’s a good question. I think with my best friend from college, I could just relax and say whatever was on my mind. She never made me feel like I had to impress her, so I didn’t worry so much about saying the wrong thing. But that doesn’t really happen often—I usually just get this anxious feeling that I have to watch what I say.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced ongoing financial instability since losing their job six months ago. They have struggled to find new employment and have had to rely on savings, which are quickly depleting. Past experiences of financial difficulty have reinforced feelings of vulnerability and lack of control.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I don't have money, I can't manage my life." "No matter what I do, things will never get better." "Asking for help is a a sign of weakness." These beliefs make the patient feel stuck and doubtful about improving their situation.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to withdraw from social interactions and avoid discussing their financial problems. They sometimes distract themselves with TV or internet browsing but feel guilty afterward. They rarely seek advice or support due to fear of judgment.
ccd.situation: The patient receives a reminder about an upcoming bill they can’t afford to pay. They feel overwhelmed thinking about how to cover basic expenses and worry about the consequences of falling behind.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I'm completely powerless to fix this." "I’ll never be able to get out of this situation." "I’m a failure and can’t do anything right."
ccd.emotions: The patient feels anxious, hopeless, and ashamed. There is a persistent sense of fear about the future and a heavy weight of despair.
ccd.behaviors: They avoid opening bills and delay making financial decisions. They isolate themselves and limit communication with friends or family about their struggles. This passivity further entrenches their sense of helplessness.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It's been okay, I guess. Just kind of the same as usual. Nothing much has changed.
When you say "the same as usual," what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you during these usual days?
Um, I don’t know… I guess I just feel kind of tired most of the time. I worry about stuff, like bills and things I need to do. Mostly I just try not to think about it too much.
When you notice those worries about bills and tasks, what do you usually tell yourself or think in response?

I usually just think there’s not much I can do about it right now. Sometimes I try to distract myself or hope it’ll work itself out somehow. It’s hard to picture things getting better. I don’t really talk to anyone about it.
What do you notice happens to your mood or energy when you try to distract yourself or hope things improve on their own?

I guess I feel kind of guilty afterward, like I should be doing more. It doesn’t really make me feel better, just sort of numb. My energy stays pretty low most days. Sometimes I just end up feeling worse.
When you say you feel guilty about not doing more, what do you think would happen if you did try to take small steps toward those worries instead of distracting yourself?

I’m not sure. I worry I’d probably just mess things up or it wouldn’t make any difference anyway. It feels kind of pointless a lot of the time. I usually just feel overwhelmed when I think about trying.
What’s one small, manageable thing you might feel okay trying, even just to test whether it could help a little?
Maybe I could try looking at one bill instead of avoiding all of them. I’m not sure if it would help much, but I guess I could try. It just feels pretty hard to start.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has faced ongoing challenges at work and in personal relationships, often feeling overwhelmed and unsupported. Past experiences of failure and criticism have reinforced a sense of being stuck and unable to change their circumstances.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to improve things, I will only end up feeling worse. No matter what I do, I won’t be able to escape this situation. Asking for help won’t make a difference because people don’t really care.
ccd.coping_strategies: Avoiding difficult conversations and decisions, withdrawing from social interactions, and distracting themselves with passive activities like watching TV to escape feelings of frustration and helplessness.
ccd.situation: Feeling stressed after a long day when work deadlines pile up and personal problems seem too much to handle all at once.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I can’t do anything about this.” “I’m stuck and there’s no way out.” “If I try harder, it won’t change anything.”
ccd.emotions: Frustration, sadness, hopelessness, and anxiety.
ccd.behaviors: Withdrawing from others, procrastinating on tasks, and avoiding addressing problems directly.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been kind of rough, to be honest. Work has been extra stressful, and it just feels like things keep piling up. I’ve been pretty tired and just trying to get through each day.
When you say work has been extra stressful, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind in those moments?

I just keep thinking I’ll never catch up, no matter how hard I try. Sometimes it feels like I’m just spinning my wheels and not really getting anywhere. I guess I start to wonder what the point is of pushing myself if nothing’s going to change. It’s just… a lot.
That sounds really challenging. When you have those thoughts about not catching up and questioning the point, how do you typically respond or cope in the moment?

Honestly, I just sort of shut down. I might scroll on my phone, or put on the TV—anything to not think about it. I avoid talking to people because I don’t really want to explain how I’m feeling. Usually I just wait for it to pass.
When you choose to distract yourself or avoid talking about it, what happens to your feelings or stress afterward?

It doesn’t really go away, if I’m being honest. I guess I feel a little better for a while, but then everything just comes back once I stop distracting myself. Sometimes I end up feeling even worse, like I wasted time and things are still just as bad. It’s kind of a cycle.
That cycle sounds exhausting. Can you tell me about a time recently when you felt a bit more in control or less overwhelmed, even if just for a moment?

Hmm… I’m not sure. Maybe when I finished a small task at work, like replying to some emails I’d been avoiding—it gave me a tiny sense of relief. But honestly, those moments don’t last very long before everything feels heavy again. Most of the time, it’s hard to imagine things getting any better.
What do you notice about your thoughts or feelings right before and after completing one of those small tasks?

Before I do it, I usually feel anxious and kind of dread starting. While I’m actually doing the task, I’m just focused on getting it over with. After it’s done, there’s a bit of relief, but it fades fast. Pretty soon, I’m back to stressing about everything else I haven’t handled yet.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt powerless in my relationships, especially when I try to express my needs but end up feeling ignored or dismissed. Growing up, I learned that keeping the peace meant not rocking the boat, which left me feeling stuck and unheard.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I stand up for myself, others will reject or abandon me. It’s safer to keep others happy than to risk conflict. I don’t have control over how people treat me, so I have to adapt to their expectations.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to agree with others even when I disagree inside, avoid bringing up my feelings, and go out of my way to please people to avoid conflict or rejection.
ccd.situation: I’m in a conversation with my partner where I want to share my feelings, but I worry that if I do, they might get upset or leave me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I say what I really feel, they’ll be angry or disappointed.” “I’m stuck—I can’t change how this will go.” “I have to keep them happy to keep the peace.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and a deep sense of helplessness.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet about my feelings, agree with my partner even if I disagree, and try to do things to please them instead of expressing what I need.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess! Nothing too out of the ordinary. Just keeping busy and trying to make sure everyone around me is doing alright. I hope that’s what you mean?
It sounds like you’ve been focused on others a lot—how has that been for you emotionally?

Oh, um, I don’t know... I guess I haven’t really thought about it much? I just want to make sure everyone’s happy, you know. Sometimes it feels a little tiring, but it’s not a big deal. I’m used to it, I think.
When you notice that feeling of tiredness, what thoughts usually go through your mind about yourself or the situation?

Hmm... I guess I just think I should be able to handle it, and I don’t want to let anyone down. I don’t really like the idea of making things harder for other people by bringing up how I feel. I sort of tell myself it’ll all work out if I just keep going.
That sounds like a lot of pressure to carry—what do you think might happen if you were to share how you’re feeling with someone close to you?

Oh, um... I’m not really sure. I guess I worry they might get upset with me, or maybe think I’m being difficult. I just don’t want to cause any trouble or make things awkward. It feels safer to just keep it to myself, honestly.
When those worries about others getting upset come up, how do you usually respond to those thoughts? Do you find yourself challenging them or accepting them as true?

I usually just sort of accept them, I guess. I don’t really question it—if I start to think someone might be upset, I just try to avoid saying anything that could make it worse. I’d rather just go along with things than risk someone being mad at me. It feels easier that way.
It sounds like avoiding conflict feels safer, but I’m curious—what do you notice happens to your feelings or energy when you keep going along without expressing your own needs?

Yeah, um… I guess after a while, I start to feel kind of drained, and maybe a little down. Sometimes I feel invisible, like what I want doesn’t really matter. But I don’t want to seem selfish. So, I just try to push those feelings away and focus on making things okay for everyone else.

ccd.relevant_history: The client has struggled with money management since early adulthood, often feeling overwhelmed by bills and expenses. They have experienced multiple setbacks, including job losses and debt, which have reinforced feelings of inadequacy and failure.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I can’t handle my finances, it means I’m not good enough. Asking for help will only show others how broken I am. I have to manage everything on my own or I’ll be a burden.
ccd.coping_strategies: The client tends to avoid opening bills or checking their bank account to escape feelings of fear and shame. They also isolate themselves to hide financial struggles and sometimes impulsively spend to momentarily relieve stress.
ccd.situation: The client received a notice about overdue bills and is worried about making ends meet this month.
ccd.automatic_thoughts:
“I’m completely hopeless with money.”  
“I’m going to mess everything up again.”  
“No one can trust me to handle this.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, hopelessness, and self-doubt.
ccd.behaviors: Avoiding phone calls from creditors, procrastinating on budgeting or financial planning, withdrawing from conversations about money, and sometimes impulsive spending to cope with distress.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

It’s been kind of stressful, honestly. I got another letter about my bills, and I’m worried I won’t be able to pay everything this month. I keep trying not to think about it, but it’s hard. I just feel stuck.
When you think about those bills and feeling stuck, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind?

I just keep thinking I’m never going to get it right. Like, no matter what I do, I always end up messing things up. Sometimes I wonder if I’ll ever be able to handle this stuff. It makes me feel pretty bad about myself.
What do you notice happens to your mood or energy when you have those thoughts about not getting it right?

I feel really drained, like I just want to crawl into bed and not deal with anything. My anxiety gets worse, and I start to feel hopeless. It’s hard to find motivation to do much of anything when I’m like that. I mostly just want to avoid it all.
When you begin to avoid dealing with your bills, what do you notice happens next?

At first, it feels like a bit of relief to not look at them. But then the anxiety just builds up, and I get more worried the longer I avoid it. Sometimes I end up spending money on something I don’t really need just to distract myself. After that, I feel even worse.
What do you think might happen if you tried to face one small part of your bills instead of avoiding them?

Honestly, I get pretty nervous just thinking about it. I worry I’ll see something that makes me feel even more overwhelmed. But maybe if I just looked at one bill, it wouldn’t be as bad as I imagine. I’m not sure—I’ve never really tried it that way.
What would be a manageable first step you could take to look at one bill without becoming too overwhelmed?

Maybe I could pick just the smallest bill and open that one first. I guess I could set a timer so I don’t spend too long stressing over it. I could even do it somewhere I feel comfortable, like at my kitchen table. That might make it a little bit easier to try.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like I don’t quite fit in with others, especially at work where people seem to connect easily. Past experiences of rejection and feeling ignored have made me expect that I’ll be left out or alone.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t say much or keep my feelings to myself, people won’t reject me outright. Trying to open up is risky because I’m convinced it will just confirm that no one really likes me. I have to manage my emotions quietly to avoid being a bother.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid sharing personal thoughts or feelings at work, staying quiet and keeping to myself. I focus on my tasks to stay under the radar and minimize chances of conflict or rejection. Sometimes, I withdraw further when I feel overwhelmed.
ccd.situation: At work, I’m under pressure to collaborate on a team project, but I feel anxious about speaking up because I worry others will judge or exclude me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I say something wrong, everyone will think I don’t belong.” “No one really wants me here or wants to work with me.” “I’m going to be left out again, just like always.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, lonely, and hopeless. There’s a heavy weight of sadness mixed with fear of being rejected or ignored.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet in meetings and avoid contributing unless directly asked. I keep my distance from colleagues and sometimes leave work early to avoid social interactions.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been kind of busy. Not much else to say, really.
When you say work has been busy, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind about that?
Uh, I don’t know… I just try to keep my head down and get things done. Sometimes I worry a bit about messing up, but I try not to think about it too much. Mostly, I just focus on finishing my tasks.
When you notice the worry about messing up, what feelings or physical sensations do you experience in those moments?
I guess I feel kind of tense, like my chest gets tight. Sometimes my stomach feels off too. It’s hard to relax when that happens. I just try to get through it.
What do you usually do when you notice that tension and discomfort—are there certain actions or thoughts that help, or maybe make it feel worse?

I usually just get quieter and try to focus on what I’m doing. I don’t really talk to anyone—I just keep working. If I think too much about it, it kind of makes things worse, so I just try to ignore it. Sometimes I just want to leave early if it gets really bad.
When you think about leaving early or withdrawing, what do you imagine might happen if you stayed and faced the situation instead?

Um… I guess I worry that I’ll say or do something stupid and people will think less of me. Maybe they’ll notice I’m nervous or awkward. I just feel like I’d stand out in a bad way if I stayed. It’s easier to just slip out.
That sounds really uncomfortable. When you imagine people thinking less of you, what evidence do you have that supports or challenges that belief?

I don’t know… I mean, I’ve had times before where people just sort of ignored me or didn’t really include me. Sometimes in meetings, nobody really reacts when I talk, so I guess that feels like proof. But I’m not sure, maybe I’m just not very noticeable. I don’t really have any examples where I felt like people definitely wanted me there.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated failures and criticism in personal and work settings, leading to a growing sense of inadequacy. They often felt unsupported during childhood and developed the belief that they are not good enough or capable. These experiences have reinforced their view of themselves as flawed.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try something new, I will mess it up and be judged. I need to avoid making mistakes or others will see how defective I really am. It’s safer to keep my distance and not take risks because I’m not capable of handling challenges.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid difficult tasks or social situations to prevent failure. They withdraw emotionally and physically when feeling overwhelmed, sometimes procrastinating or giving up early. They may also seek reassurance repeatedly but feel it doesn’t last.
ccd.situation: Facing a new project at work with unclear instructions and tight deadlines, the patient feels overwhelmed and unsure if they can meet expectations.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to screw this up like I always do.” “If I mess up, everyone will see how useless I am.” “I can’t handle this; I’m just not good enough.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, and hopelessness dominate the patient’s emotional state. They feel tense, worried, and deeply discouraged about their ability to succeed.
ccd.behaviors: The patient delays starting the project and avoids asking for help, hoping the problem will resolve itself. They isolate themselves from coworkers and may engage in negative self-talk, further reducing their motivation and productivity.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Uh, it’s been alright, I guess. Work’s been kind of stressful—there’s this new project I’m supposed to start, but I haven’t really made much progress yet. I’m just having trouble getting into it. Mostly just trying to get through the days.
When you think about the new project, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind?

I just keep thinking I’m probably going to screw it up somehow. It’s got a lot of parts I don’t really understand yet, and I feel like I’m already behind. I guess I just don’t feel very confident about it.
What do you notice happens to your mood or energy when you have those thoughts about messing up or being behind?

I get really anxious and kind of tense, like a knot in my stomach. It just makes me want to avoid the whole thing. I end up putting it off and then I feel even worse about myself. I guess I just start feeling pretty hopeless about it all.
When you catch yourself wanting to avoid the project, what do you tell yourself about what might happen if you try to work on it anyway?

I usually think that if I try, I’ll just mess something up or not do it right. Then people will notice and probably judge me for it. I worry everyone will see that I don’t really know what I’m doing. It feels safer to just not draw attention to myself.
Can you think of a time in the past when you tried something new or challenging and it didn’t turn out perfectly, but you still managed to get through it or learn from it?

Um… I guess there was this one time last year when I had to give a presentation at work. I was really nervous and stumbled over some parts, but I did finish it. People didn’t seem as critical as I expected—they even said it was helpful. But I remember just focusing on what I messed up, not what went okay.
What might it feel like to remind yourself of that presentation experience when you start to worry about the new project?

Honestly, it’s hard to believe it would make a difference. I mean, I know I got through that presentation, but my mind still goes straight to what could go wrong now. Maybe if I really tried to remember that things didn’t completely fall apart last time, it might help a little. It just feels like I’m always about to mess up anyway.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like no matter what I do, things just spiral out of control, especially when unexpected problems come up. Growing up, I was told to just accept things as they are, which made me feel powerless to change my circumstances.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I can’t control every detail, then I’m failing and things will fall apart. It’s safer to avoid taking risks because I might lose control even more. Others expect me to manage everything perfectly, or they’ll think less of me.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to avoid situations where I might feel overwhelmed, like turning down new responsibilities or withdrawing from conversations about problems. Sometimes I distract myself with TV or scrolling on my phone to avoid thinking about what’s stressing me.
ccd.situation: When my boss suddenly changes a project deadline and gives me little time to adjust, I feel completely thrown off and unsure of what to do next.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “This is a disaster, I’m totally out of control again.” “I’m going to mess everything up and everyone will be disappointed.” “I can’t handle this; there’s no way to fix it.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, frustration, and a deep sense of helplessness wash over me. I feel tense and overwhelmed, like I want to shut down or escape.
ccd.behaviors: I procrastinate on starting the project because I feel paralyzed, and I avoid asking for help because I’m afraid it will show I’m incapable. Sometimes I snap at people or withdraw from team discussions to keep others at a distance.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Uh, it’s been… I guess kind of hectic. My boss changed a deadline last minute, which threw me off a bit. Oh, and my neighbor’s dog keeps barking at weird hours—makes it hard to focus, you know? Anyway, I tried to just get through my usual stuff, but honestly, I can’t even remember what I ate for dinner last night.
That sounds frustrating—what were you thinking or telling yourself when your boss changed the deadline and the dog was barking?

Honestly, I just thought, “Here we go again, everything’s going sideways.” It’s like, why can’t things just be simple for once? I mean, my mom used to say, “Life isn’t fair,” but that never made it feel any better. Actually, it kind of reminds me of when I was a kid and my brother would suddenly mess up my Lego buildings—out of nowhere everything just falls apart.
When those thoughts like “Here we go again, everything’s going sideways” come up, how do they affect the way you feel or what you do next?

Well, I start feeling really tense, like my chest tightens up and I just want to hide. Sometimes I’ll just sit there staring at my screen, not able to do anything—almost like I’m frozen. I know I should probably talk to someone or ask for help, but I usually end up just scrolling through my phone instead. Actually, I watched this whole series on Netflix last weekend instead of working on anything important.
When you notice yourself feeling that tension and wanting to hide or distract yourself, what do you think would happen if you tried doing something different in that moment?

Hmm… I don’t know, I guess I just feel like if I tried to do something different, like actually tackling the work or talking to my boss, I’d probably just mess it up or say something dumb. Or maybe I’d just get more stressed out because I’d realize how much I have to do. Sometimes I think people are watching to see if I’ll drop the ball, you know? Oh, and last time I tried to ask for help on a group project in school, the group kind of ignored my questions, so I just stopped asking after that.
It sounds like you worry about being judged or making mistakes when you reach out—how do those fears influence what you end up doing or avoiding?

Yeah, I guess I just tend to keep things to myself instead of asking for help, even if I’m drowning. Sometimes I’ll avoid group chats or meetings, or just nod along so no one notices I’m lost. It’s kind of like when you’re at a party and you don’t know anyone, so you hang out at the snack table pretending to be busy. I know it probably makes things harder, but it feels safer.
That makes a lot of sense—what might feel different or safer for you if you tried sharing a little bit, even just with one person you trust?

I don’t know… Maybe if I picked the right person, it wouldn’t feel so risky. Sometimes I think about texting my friend Jess when things get rough—she’s usually pretty understanding—but then I worry I’ll just sound like I’m complaining, or she’ll get tired of hearing it. I guess I haven’t really tried, so I can’t say for sure how it would go. Actually, last time I sent her a meme about being overwhelmed, she just sent back a laughing emoji, which didn’t really help but didn’t make things worse either.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored or dismissed by important people in my life when I tried to express myself. I learned early on that keeping others happy was the safest way to avoid conflict or rejection. This has made me doubt my abilities and feel powerless in relationships.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t please others, they won’t like me or will leave me. I have to be perfect or useful to be accepted. If I show my true feelings or opinions, I might be judged or rejected because I’m not good enough.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to agree with others even when I disagree, avoid sharing my own needs, and put others’ desires before my own to keep the peace. I might also over-apologize or downplay my mistakes to avoid criticism.
ccd.situation: When my partner asks for help with a task, I worry that if I don’t do it well, they’ll be disappointed in me or think I’m useless.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I can’t do this right; I’m just not capable.” “If I mess up, they’ll think I’m a failure.” “I have to get this perfect or they won’t want me around.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, and sadness arise because I feel like I’m not good enough and fear losing connection.
ccd.behaviors: I overcommit to help even when I’m overwhelmed, double-check my work obsessively, and avoid expressing when I’m struggling or need support.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Oh, um, it’s been pretty normal, I guess. Nothing really special happened. I just tried to keep up with work and help out at home. I hope that’s okay—sorry, I’m not sure what you want to hear.
It’s absolutely okay; thank you for sharing that. When you think about a typical day this week, what’s one moment that stood out to you, either positively or negatively?

Hmm, let me think… Well, my partner asked me to help organize some paperwork for them. I guess I got kind of nervous about it because I didn’t want to mess anything up. I probably spent a little too long double-checking everything, but I just really wanted it to be right for them. Sorry, I hope that’s the kind of thing you meant.
That’s a helpful example, thank you. When you noticed yourself feeling nervous about organizing the paperwork, what were some of the thoughts going through your mind at that moment?

Um, I guess I just kept thinking that I really didn’t want to make any mistakes. I worried they might be disappointed if I missed something or did it wrong. I kind of felt like I had to get it perfect, or they’d think less of me. Sorry, I know that probably sounds silly.
It doesn’t sound silly at all; those thoughts seem important to you. How did feeling that way affect what you did while organizing the paperwork?

Oh, um, I probably went over it way more times than I needed to. I kept checking and re-checking to make sure everything was exactly how they wanted. I didn’t say anything, but I felt pretty tense the whole time. I just didn’t want to risk them thinking I couldn’t handle it.
That sounds really stressful. When you imagine how your partner might respond if a small mistake happened, what comes up for you?

Honestly, I just feel worried that they’d be let down or maybe even annoyed with me. I guess I worry they’d see me as kind of…not very helpful, or like I’m not good enough. I know it’s probably not a huge deal, but it feels really important to me to not get things wrong. I just really don’t want them to be upset with me.

It sounds like maintaining their approval feels really important to you. When you notice those worries about being not good enough or disappointing your partner, how do you usually cope with or respond to those feelings?

Um, I guess I just try even harder to make sure I don’t mess up. I’ll go out of my way to fix things or take on more, even if I’m tired. Sometimes I keep my feelings to myself because I don’t want to bother anyone. I just want to make sure everyone’s happy with me, I guess.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt criticized and misunderstood by my family. I was rarely praised, and whenever I made mistakes, they were met with disappointment or anger. This made me believe that I wasn’t good enough and that I was somehow a bad person.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I am not perfect, then I am a bad and immoral person. Others will reject me if they see the real me. I must hide my flaws to avoid being judged or abandoned.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to keep my feelings to myself and avoid sharing my true thoughts or mistakes. I try to control situations to prevent conflict and sometimes withdraw from family interactions to protect myself from criticism.
ccd.situation: During a family gathering, my relatives were discussing moral values and responsibilities, and I felt singled out when someone implied that certain behaviors were wrong.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They must think I’m a bad person. I’m immoral and don’t belong here. They’re judging me and will reject me if they knew the truth.”
ccd.emotions: I felt ashamed, anxious, and deeply sad. There was also a sense of loneliness and fear of being excluded.
ccd.behaviors: I stayed silent and avoided eye contact, withdrawing from the conversation. Later, I excused myself early from the gathering to avoid further judgment.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um... it’s been alright, I guess. Nothing too special happened. Just the usual, work and family stuff.
When you say “the usual,” is there a particular moment or situation recently that stood out to you, either positively or negatively?
Uh, well… there was a family thing over the weekend. Just a get-together. It wasn’t a big deal or anything, but I didn’t really enjoy myself. I left a bit early.
What thoughts were going through your mind during that family get-together that made you want to leave early?

I don’t know… I just felt uncomfortable, I guess. People were talking about things that made me feel out of place. I started thinking maybe I don’t really fit in with them. It just felt easier to go home.
When you noticed those feelings of discomfort and not fitting in, what did you tell yourself about yourself or the situation?
I’m not sure. I guess I just thought maybe they were judging me. Like I was doing something wrong by being there. I felt like maybe I didn’t belong.
That sounds really tough to feel judged and out of place. In those moments, how did your body feel or react to those thoughts?
Um… I felt kind of tense. My chest got tight, and I wanted to disappear. I just tried to keep quiet and not draw attention. It was hard to relax.
When you notice that tightness in your chest and the urge to disappear, what do you usually do next?
I usually just try to keep to myself. I stop talking and avoid looking at people. Sometimes I find a reason to leave early. It just feels safer that way.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has faced repeated criticism and neglect during childhood, leading to deep feelings of inadequacy. They often felt overlooked both at home and school, which has shaped their negative view of themselves over time.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m not good enough, people will reject me. Trying hard is pointless because I’ll just fail anyway. I must avoid showing my weaknesses to keep others from thinking less of me.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to withdraw from social situations and avoids taking on new challenges to prevent potential failure. They also engage in self-criticism silently to motivate themselves, though this often backfires and increases their distress.
ccd.situation: At work, the patient was asked to lead a project meeting but felt overwhelmed and worried that they would mess up in front of colleagues.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to screw this up and everyone will see what a loser I am.” “I don’t know what I’m doing; I’m just wasting everyone’s time.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, and sadness dominate the patient’s experience during and after the meeting.
ccd.behaviors: The patient avoids speaking up in meetings afterward and misses deadlines due to procrastination, reinforcing their belief that they are worthless.

messages.content: How has your week been for you so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Work felt pretty stressful. There was a meeting I had to run, and I just kept worrying I’d mess it up. I haven’t really wanted to talk to anyone about it.
When you think about the meeting, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind?

Mostly I just keep replaying everything I said, wondering if I sounded stupid. I felt like people were probably thinking, “Why is he even running this?” I can’t shake the feeling that I looked completely out of my depth. It’s hard not to dwell on it.
What do you notice happens to your feelings or body when those thoughts come up?

I get really tense, like my shoulders and neck just tighten up. My stomach feels sick too, kind of heavy. It’s like this wave of anxiety hits me all over again. Sometimes I just want to disappear so I don’t have to feel it.
When you have those feelings and want to disappear, what do you usually do next?

Most of the time, I just keep quiet and try not to draw attention to myself. I usually avoid talking in meetings after something like that. Sometimes I make excuses to leave early or just zone out. I guess I try to hide so people won’t notice me.
What do you think might happen if you did speak up or participate more in meetings despite feeling that way?

Honestly, I worry I’d say something wrong or embarrassing. People might judge me or think I’m not good enough for the job. I just imagine making a mistake and everyone noticing. That thought makes me freeze up even more.
Have there been times when you spoke up in meetings or at work and things went better than you expected? What was that like?

Yeah, there have been a couple of times when I managed to say something and it was actually fine. People just listened and moved on, or sometimes even agreed with my point. It felt kind of surprising, like maybe I didn’t completely mess up after all. But it’s hard to hold onto that feeling for long—my mind just goes back to worrying about the next time.

ccd.relevant_history: I've always struggled with feeling like I’m not good enough, especially at work where I worry a lot about making mistakes. Growing up, I often felt overlooked and told I wasn’t important, which stuck with me. These feelings get worse when I’m under pressure or when others expect a lot from me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don't keep everyone happy, they’ll reject me or think less of me. I have to work extra hard to prove I’m not a burden. If I fail or mess up, it means I’m worthless and should just give up.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to agree with whatever others want, even if it stresses me out, to avoid conflict or disappointment. Sometimes I push myself to the point of exhaustion to feel like I’m worth something. Other times, I withdraw or avoid speaking up because I feel I don’t deserve attention.
ccd.situation: At work, my manager gives me a tight deadline for a big project, and I feel overwhelmed by the pressure to deliver perfect results.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to mess this up and everyone will see how useless I am.” “I don’t deserve to be here or have this job.” “If I say no or ask for help, they’ll think I’m weak.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, sadness, and a deep sense of hopelessness.
ccd.behaviors: I stay silent when I’m overwhelmed, avoid asking for help, and try to work longer hours to compensate. Sometimes I freeze and procrastinate because I feel so hopeless about succeeding.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been pretty busy, but I’m managing. I don’t want to complain—there are people with much bigger problems. Sorry, I’m probably making it sound worse than it is!
It sounds like you’re trying to keep things in perspective, but I’m curious—what kinds of thoughts go through your mind when you say you’re “managing” work?

Oh, well… I guess I just try to focus on getting things done and not let anyone down. Sometimes I get worried that I’m missing something or not doing enough, but I tell myself I just need to push through. I don’t want to be a bother or make mistakes, you know? I just hope people are happy with what I do.
When you notice those worries about missing something or not doing enough, how do you usually respond emotionally in that moment?

Um, I guess I get a bit anxious, honestly. I start feeling tense, like my chest is tight, and I worry about what people might think of me. Sometimes I just try to push those feelings aside and keep working. Sorry, I know it's not a big deal—I probably just overthink things.
It sounds like those anxious feelings really affect you physically and mentally; when you push those feelings aside to keep working, what happens to the anxiety afterward?

Yeah, I mean, sometimes the anxiety just sits there in the background, kind of buzzing, but I try not to let it show. If I keep busy enough, I can mostly ignore it for a while. But other times, it just builds up, and I end up feeling really exhausted or even a bit sad later. I’m sorry, I probably just need to toughen up.
It seems like keeping busy helps you cope, but the anxiety and sadness still come up later—what do you notice happening in your thoughts or behavior when those feelings build up?

Well, I guess I get kind of quiet and just keep to myself more. I start worrying that I’m letting people down or not doing things right, so I double-check my work a lot. Sometimes I’ll stay late just to make sure everything’s perfect, even if I’m really tired. Honestly, I just don’t want anyone to think I’m not trying hard enough.
When you find yourself staying late and double-checking your work, what do you tell yourself about what might happen if you didn’t do those things?

Um, I guess I worry people will notice if I don’t go the extra mile. I feel like if I mess up or miss something, everyone will realize I’m not really good enough to be there. I just don’t want to disappoint anyone or make them think less of me. Sorry, I know that probably sounds silly.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has faced ongoing financial struggles, including job instability and mounting debt, which have reinforced feelings of inadequacy and failure over time. These experiences have contributed to a deep sense of hopelessness and self-criticism.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I can’t manage my money, I’m a failure." "People will reject me if they see how badly I’m doing." "I have no value if I can’t take care of myself."
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid looking at bills or financial statements, withdraws socially to hide their struggles, and sometimes distracts themselves with excessive TV or social media to escape negative feelings.
ccd.situation: Facing overdue bills and uncertainty about how to cover rent this month, feeling overwhelmed by the financial pressure.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m completely useless." "I’m a burden to everyone around me." "There’s no point in trying anymore."
ccd.emotions: Hopelessness, shame, sadness, anxiety.
ccd.behaviors: Avoiding calls from creditors, isolating from friends and family, procrastinating on budgeting or seeking help.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been kind of rough, honestly. I’ve just been stressed and tired. I guess I’m having trouble keeping up with everything. It feels like stuff keeps piling up.
Can you tell me more about what kinds of things have been piling up for you?
Mostly money stuff, I guess. Bills I haven’t paid yet, some overdue notices showing up. I keep trying to ignore them, but it just makes me feel worse. I can’t seem to get a handle on any of it.
When you notice those bills or overdue notices, what thoughts usually come to mind?
Honestly, I just think, “I can’t do anything right.” Sometimes I tell myself I’m a screw-up. It feels like I’m just letting everyone down. It’s hard not to think that I’m messing up my whole life.
That sounds really painful to feel that way about yourself; what emotions do you notice when you have those thoughts?
Mostly I just feel hopeless, like there’s no way out. I get this heavy feeling in my chest, kind of like shame too. Sometimes I just want to hide from everyone. It gets overwhelming pretty fast.
When you feel that heavy, overwhelming emotion, what do you usually do next?

I usually just sit by myself and try to distract myself, maybe watch TV or scroll through my phone. I don’t really talk to anyone. Sometimes I just avoid looking at the bills altogether. I know it’s not helping, but I don’t know what else to do.
It sounds like avoiding the bills gives some temporary relief; what do you think might happen if you tried looking at them little by little?

I don’t know... I feel like I’d just freak out or get even more stressed. It’s like I’m bracing myself for bad news every time. Part of me thinks I’ll just see how badly I’m failing. I’m not sure I can handle that right now.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

ccd.relevant_history: I grew up feeling like I had to earn love by being perfect and never causing trouble. My parents were emotionally distant, and I often felt ignored or rejected when I expressed my needs. Past relationships have ended with partners leaving me, reinforcing my fear of abandonment.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t keep everyone happy, they will leave me. People only stay close if I don’t rock the boat or show my true feelings. I have to be careful not to upset others because that means I’m unworthy of love.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to please others by agreeing with everything they say and avoiding conflict. I often put others’ needs before my own to avoid rejection. Sometimes I stay silent even when I’m upset, hoping that by being “nice,” people will stay around.
ccd.situation: My partner didn’t respond to my message for a few hours after we had a disagreement.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: They must be mad at me and probably don’t want to be with me anymore. I’m probably going to be left alone again because I’m not lovable. Maybe I said something wrong and now they’re going to abandon me.
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and fear of being alone. I feel vulnerable and rejected, like I’m not good enough to be loved.
ccd.behaviors: I send multiple messages apologizing and trying to fix things quickly. I avoid bringing up my own feelings to keep the peace. I check my phone obsessively, hoping for reassurance and connection.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting, just the usual stuff. I’m trying to keep busy and make sure things go smoothly at work and at home. Sorry, I don’t really know what to say—I guess I’m doing alright.
It sounds like you’re focusing on managing your responsibilities—what kinds of thoughts go through your mind when you think about keeping things running smoothly?

Oh, um... I guess I just worry that if I mess something up, people will get upset with me. I really don’t want to cause any problems or disappoint anyone. Sometimes I catch myself double-checking things just to make sure everything’s okay. I just feel better when everyone’s happy with me, I suppose.
That sounds like a lot of pressure to avoid upsetting others—what do you notice happens to your feelings or body when you worry about disappointing people?

Yeah, I guess it does feel like a lot sometimes. I start to get kind of tense, and my stomach feels tight. I get a bit restless, like I can’t relax until I know everything’s okay with everyone. I just really hate that anxious feeling, and I try to make it go away by fixing things quickly.
When you notice that tension and restlessness, what do you usually tell yourself about what might happen if things aren’t perfect or if someone isn’t happy?
