Um, I guess I start thinking that maybe they’ll be mad at me, or that I did something wrong. I worry a lot that they’ll pull away or just not want to be around me anymore. It’s kind of like, if I’m not careful, I could mess everything up and then I’ll be left on my own. I know that probably sounds a little dramatic, sorry.
That fear of being left alone sounds really intense—how do you usually respond or cope when those thoughts about being abandoned or rejected come up?

Oh, um, I usually try to make it right as quickly as I can. Sometimes I apologize a lot, even if I’m not sure I did anything wrong. I might check in with the person more than I should, just to see if we’re okay. I don’t really like talking about what I’m feeling, so I just kind of push it down and focus on keeping things calm.
It sounds like you put a lot of effort into fixing things and avoiding conflict—how do you feel inside when you push down your own feelings to keep the peace?

Honestly, it feels kind of heavy, like I’m holding my breath or something. Sometimes I get sad or even a bit lonely, but I just don’t want to risk making things worse by bringing it up. I’m worried that if I talk about what’s bothering me, people might get upset and leave. I guess I just hope things will blow over if I stay quiet.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt criticized and overlooked by my family, which made me believe I wasn’t good enough. I’ve carried a sense of being fundamentally flawed and afraid to disappoint others. These early experiences shaped how I view myself and interact with people now.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t please others, they won’t like me or will reject me. I have to put others’ needs before my own to be accepted. Showing my true feelings might lead to conflict or abandonment.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to avoid upsetting anyone by agreeing with them even when I disagree. I often suppress my own needs and feelings to keep the peace. I over-apologize and take on extra responsibilities to feel valuable.
ccd.situation: My coworker asked me to help finish a project on a tight deadline, and I’m already overwhelmed with my own tasks.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: If I say no, they’ll think I’m lazy or don’t care, and I’ll disappoint them. I’m not capable of handling this, and I’ll just mess things up. I have to do it, even if it stresses me out.
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, fear of rejection, feeling overwhelmed, and a low sense of self-worth.
ccd.behaviors: I agree to help even though I’m stressed, stay late to finish the work, avoid expressing how overwhelmed I feel, and neglect my own needs.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Oh, um... it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too out of the ordinary. Work has been kind of busy, but I’m managing. How are you?
I’m glad to hear you’re managing despite the busy work. When you say work has been busy, what thoughts or feelings come up for you during those times?

Uh, well, I just try to get everything done and not let anyone down. Sometimes I worry that I’m not doing enough or that I’ll make a mistake. I guess I feel a bit anxious about it, but I try not to let it show. I just want things to go smoothly for everyone, you know?
It sounds like you have a lot of pressure on yourself to meet expectations and avoid mistakes. When those worries about not doing enough come up, what do you usually tell yourself in those moments?

Honestly, I usually just tell myself to work harder and not complain. I think, “Everyone else seems to handle things, so I should be able to too.” I worry that if I say I’m struggling, people will think I’m not capable. So I try to just push through it and keep everyone happy.
That sounds really challenging, feeling like you have to push through and keep everything together. How do you feel physically and emotionally when you’re pushing yourself like that?

I guess I feel pretty tired a lot of the time, and sometimes my chest feels tight or I get headaches. Emotionally, I just feel kind of on edge—like I’m waiting for someone to notice I’m not doing enough. But I try not to make a big deal out of it. Other people have it worse, so I shouldn’t complain.

It sounds like you’re carrying a lot on your shoulders and trying to minimize your own experience. When you think about the idea that others have it worse, what impact does that belief have on how you treat yourself?

Um, I guess it makes me feel like I don’t really deserve to take a break or ask for help. I end up just telling myself to suck it up and keep going. I feel kind of guilty if I focus on myself too much. I don’t want to seem selfish or like I can’t handle things.
That sounds like a heavy burden to carry, feeling guilty for prioritizing your own needs. What do you imagine might happen if you allowed yourself to take a break or ask for support?

Honestly, I worry people would think less of me or get frustrated. I’m afraid they might see me as unreliable or weak. I just really don’t want to let anyone down. I’d rather just handle things quietly, even if it’s hard.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and criticized by my family, which made me believe that I wasn’t truly accepted or loved. Over time, I started expecting rejection in my social life, always bracing myself for others to push me away. These early experiences have left me feeling isolated and hesitant to open up to people.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I let people get close, they will eventually reject me because I’m not good enough. It’s safer to keep my distance or change the subject quickly to avoid uncomfortable feelings or confrontation. People don’t really want to spend time with me, so I have to protect myself by not showing my true thoughts.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid deep conversations and quickly shift topics when things get personal. Sometimes, I laugh off serious comments or distract others with unrelated stories to keep the mood light and avoid revealing my vulnerabilities. At times, I withdraw from social events if I feel like I might be judged or rejected.
ccd.situation: At a small gathering with friends, someone asked me about my recent struggles, and I suddenly felt overwhelmed. Instead of answering honestly, I started talking about a random news story to steer the conversation away from myself.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They’re going to think I’m weak or annoying if I share this.” “I’m already boring them; they’ll want to leave soon.” “I’m just going to mess this up and be rejected again.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, sadness, and a sense of loneliness.
ccd.behaviors: Changing the subject abruptly, avoiding eye contact, laughing nervously, and physically withdrawing from the group.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been kind of busy, but nothing too out of the ordinary. Actually, did you see the weather yesterday? It was so strange—sunny one minute, pouring the next. It reminded me of this time back in high school when we all got stuck in a sudden rainstorm at a football game and everyone was scrambling for cover, it was actually kind of funny.
That sounds like quite a vivid memory—when you think back to that moment at the football game, what feelings or thoughts come up for you now?

Hmm, I guess I mostly remember everyone laughing and running around, trying not to get soaked. It was sort of chaotic but also kind of fun in a weird way. But honestly, I also felt a little left out because my friends ran off together and I wasn’t sure if I should follow them or just stay put. Oh, and speaking of football games, do you remember that play a few years ago when the quarterback just dropped the ball out of nowhere? That was wild.

It sounds like that moment brought up some mixed feelings for you—both fun and a bit of uncertainty about where you fit in; when you felt left out back then, what did you tell yourself about the situation or about yourself?

Um, I guess I just thought maybe I wasn’t really part of the group, you know? Like, maybe they wouldn’t notice if I wasn’t there or something. I ended up just pretending I was looking for my jacket so I didn’t have to awkwardly stand around. Oh, and I remember the concession stand ran out of popcorn that night, which was so annoying—everyone was kind of annoyed but also making jokes about it.

That sounds like a tough feeling to carry—wondering if you mattered to the group; when you think about those thoughts that you might not be noticed or included, how do they affect how you act or feel in situations like that now?

Yeah, I guess it just makes me really hesitant to put myself out there, or like, speak up. I usually end up just kind of hanging back and waiting for someone to notice me, but that doesn’t really happen. Sometimes I just start talking about random stuff so it doesn’t get uncomfortable—like, did you know it’s actually illegal to own just one guinea pig in Switzerland? I read that the other day and thought it was so weird!

It sounds like holding back has become a way to protect yourself from feeling left out; when you find yourself staying quiet or talking about random things to avoid discomfort, what do you hope will happen in those moments?

I guess I just hope people won’t see how awkward I feel or notice if I’m not really joining in. Maybe if I keep things light or funny, they won’t ask me anything personal or realize I don’t know what to say. Sometimes I’m just hoping the attention shifts to someone else. Oh, and sometimes it actually works—like, people laugh or move on, and then I can just relax a little.
It makes sense that you’d want to avoid feeling exposed by keeping things light; when that strategy works and people laugh or shift focus, how do you feel about yourself in those moments?

Um, I guess there’s some relief, like I dodged a bullet or something. But also, sometimes I feel kind of empty after, or like I missed out on actually connecting with people. I don’t know, it’s a weird mix—part of me is glad, and part of me feels sort of invisible. Oh, speaking of connecting, have you ever tried those team-building games at work where everyone has to share something personal? Those always make me super nervous.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt like no matter how hard I tried, I couldn’t meet my parents’ expectations, which made me believe I was just not capable. This belief stuck with me into adulthood, making it hard to trust my abilities in everyday tasks.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try something and don’t get it right immediately, it means I’m just not good enough. People will see me as a failure if I mess up, so it’s safer not to put myself out there too much.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid taking on new challenges or responsibilities because I’m worried I’ll fail. Sometimes I distract myself with TV or social media to avoid thinking about my mistakes or what I should be doing.
ccd.situation: After receiving some constructive feedback at work about a project I handled, I started doubting my skills and felt overwhelmed by the thought of trying again.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I can’t do anything right. I’m just not cut out for this job.” “Everyone must think I’m useless now.” “Why even bother trying if I’m going to mess up again?”
ccd.emotions: I felt anxious, ashamed, and really discouraged, like I wanted to just hide and avoid everyone.
ccd.behaviors: I procrastinated on fixing the issues in the project and avoided talking to my boss about it. I also withdrew from team meetings and stopped volunteering for new tasks.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Uh, my week? It’s been… I guess kind of all over the place. Work has been busy, but then again, I always feel like I’m behind, you know? Oh, actually, did you see that news story about the guy who found a cat in his car engine? That reminded me of when my neighbor’s cat disappeared for three days and everyone thought she ran away, but turns out she was just hiding in the shed behind some paint cans.
It sounds like feeling behind at work is on your mind—what kinds of thoughts go through your head when you feel that way?

Yeah, I mean, when I’m at my desk and I see all these tasks piling up, I just start thinking, “Great, here we go again, I’m already messing this up.” It’s almost like this running commentary in the back of my mind, second-guessing everything I do. But then sometimes I just start thinking about random stuff, like if I left the oven on or whether I should get a haircut—I guess it’s easier than thinking about work sometimes. Actually, speaking of haircuts, I tried cutting my own hair once during lockdown and it went so badly, I had to wear a hat for a week!
When those self-critical thoughts come up, how do you usually respond to them or what do you do next?

Um, I guess I kind of freeze up. Or I end up scrolling on my phone for way too long instead of getting anything done, which just makes me feel worse later. Sometimes I try to tell myself I’ll feel better if I just take a break, but then the break turns into like, two hours of watching cooking videos—even though I never actually try the recipes. Oh, and the other day I started reorganizing my bookshelf instead of replying to an email I was worried about, which is weird because I don’t even read half those books.
It sounds like you’re noticing a pattern of avoidance when things feel overwhelming—what do you think makes it hard to start or keep going with your work tasks?

Yeah, I guess… I just get really anxious that I’ll mess things up, so it feels safer to put it off. And then the longer I wait, the bigger the task feels in my head, so it’s like this loop I can’t get out of. Sometimes I think maybe other people just don’t get so worried about messing up, or they know something I don’t. Oh, that reminds me—my friend once told me she never checks her work twice and just “wings it,” which I can’t even imagine doing!
That sounds really tough, feeling stuck in that loop. When you imagine “winging it” like your friend does, what feelings or thoughts come up for you?

Honestly, it sounds terrifying. I feel like if I did that, everything would fall apart and everyone would notice how clueless I really am. My mind just goes to all the mistakes I’d probably make and how people might talk about it behind my back. Actually, this one time in school I tried to wing a presentation and completely blanked out—I still get embarrassed thinking about it, even though it was years ago.

That experience sounds really painful and still feels very present for you. When those worries about making mistakes and being judged come up now, how do you usually cope or try to manage those feelings?

I mostly just try to avoid situations where I might mess up, honestly. Like, I’ll skip meetings or not volunteer for things if I think there’s any chance I could make a mistake in front of people. Sometimes I just keep really quiet and hope no one notices me—kind of like flying under the radar. Oh, and I’ll overthink emails for ages, rewriting them ten times before sending, which is probably why my replies are always late.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like I have to rely on others to get through tough situations, especially at work. Growing up, I didn’t get much encouragement to handle things on my own, so I often felt like I was a burden when asking for help.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t ask for help, I won’t manage, and people will think I’m weak or useless. I have to depend on others because I’m not capable of handling things by myself.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to stay quiet during meetings and avoid volunteering for new tasks because I’m afraid I’ll mess up without support. Sometimes I seek reassurance from colleagues repeatedly to feel more secure.
ccd.situation: At work, when my manager assigns a challenging project with tight deadlines, I feel overwhelmed and unsure if I can handle it alone.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I can’t do this without someone stepping in to help me.” “If I don’t ask for help, I’ll fail and disappoint everyone.”
ccd.emotions: Anxious, insecure, and a bit helpless.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid taking initiative and often delay starting tasks until I get explicit support. I also check in excessively with coworkers to confirm I’m on the right track.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too out of the ordinary. Work’s been kind of busy, but that’s normal. I don’t know, nothing much else to say, really.
When you say work has been busy, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind during those busy moments?

I just, um… I guess I start to worry if I’m doing things right. Sometimes I catch myself thinking I should probably double-check things with someone. I don’t really feel confident just going ahead on my own. It sort of makes me second-guess myself a lot.
What feelings come up for you when you start to doubt yourself or feel unsure about your decisions at work?

Mostly I just feel… nervous, I guess. And maybe a little bit frustrated with myself. Sometimes I just wish I could feel more sure about things. It can get kind of overwhelming if I think about it too much.
When you notice that nervousness and frustration building, what do you usually do in response?

Um, I usually just keep quiet and try not to draw attention. Sometimes I’ll send a quick message to a coworker to check if I’m doing things right. I wait for their reply before I move forward. I guess I tend to put things off until I feel more certain.
How do you feel while you’re waiting for that response from your coworker?

Honestly, I feel pretty anxious. I keep checking to see if they’ve replied yet. I worry that maybe I’m bothering them or asking something obvious. It’s hard to focus on anything else until I hear back.
When you notice yourself worrying about bothering them or asking obvious questions, what thoughts come to mind about yourself or the situation?

Um, I sometimes think I should be able to figure things out by myself. It makes me wonder if I’m just not good enough at my job. I worry they might think I’m a bit of a hassle. I try to brush it off, but those thoughts kind of stick with me.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like no matter what I do, I can’t change the way things are in my relationships. Growing up, I watched my parents argue but never saw anyone really take control or make things better, so I guess I learned that I’m just stuck with whatever happens.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to speak up or make a change, it won’t matter because I’m powerless to influence others. People won’t listen to me or take me seriously, so it’s safer to just accept things as they are rather than risk making things worse.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid bringing up problems or sharing how I feel because I’m afraid it won’t help and might just create more tension. Sometimes I withdraw or stay quiet to keep the peace, even when it makes me feel lonely or frustrated inside.
ccd.situation: When my partner and I have disagreements, I feel overwhelmed and like there’s nothing I can do to improve the situation or get my feelings across effectively.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: There’s no point in trying to talk about this; they won’t understand or change. I’m just going to end up feeling worse or ignored, so it’s better to stay quiet.
ccd.emotions: I feel helpless, anxious, and sad. Sometimes there’s also a sense of frustration and loneliness because I want things to be different but don’t see a way forward.
ccd.behaviors: I shut down during arguments, avoid expressing my thoughts or feelings, and sometimes withdraw emotionally or physically from my partner to cope with the discomfort.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um... it’s been kind of up and down, I guess. Work was pretty stressful, but that’s not really new. Things at home have just felt a bit tense lately, and I’m not totally sure why. Sorry, I know that’s kind of vague.
Thank you for sharing that. When you notice the tension at home, what kinds of thoughts or worries tend to come up for you?

Well, I guess I mostly just start wondering if it’s something I’ve done or if I should be doing something to fix it, but I honestly don’t know what that would even look like. Sometimes I just think, like, “Here we go again, nothing’s really going to change.” It’s kind of a feeling of, I don’t know, being stuck or like I can’t really do anything to make things better. And then I end up just keeping quiet because I don’t want to make things worse.
That sounds really frustrating. When you have those thoughts about being stuck or not knowing what to do, how does that affect how you feel emotionally in the moment?

Yeah, it is pretty frustrating. I usually end up feeling this mix of anxiety and sadness, and sometimes I just get this heavy, kind of hopeless feeling, like no matter what I do, it won’t make much difference. There’s also this sense of being alone with it, even though my partner’s right there. I wish I could just say what I’m feeling, but it always seems so much easier to just keep it to myself.
It sounds really tough to carry those feelings alone. When you imagine sharing what you’re feeling with your partner, what thoughts or fears come up for you about how they might respond?

I guess what comes up first is this worry that they’ll just brush it off or not really understand what I’m trying to say. I worry I’ll just end up feeling more alone, or that they’ll get defensive and we’ll end up arguing instead of actually talking. Sometimes I think maybe they’ll see me as too sensitive or like I’m just making a big deal out of nothing. Honestly, it just seems safer to stay quiet to avoid all that.
Thank you for being so open about that. When you notice those worries about your partner’s reactions, do you find any moments or experiences where things have gone differently than you expected?

Um, I have to think about that... I guess there have been a few times when I did try to say something, and it didn’t blow up—it wasn’t some huge disaster or anything. But I still kind of walked away feeling like I hadn’t really gotten through, or maybe like I didn’t explain myself well enough. Sometimes they’ll listen, but I just end up second-guessing myself after, wondering if it made any real difference. So even when it’s not bad, I’m left questioning if it was worth bringing up.
It sounds like those moments are mixed—somewhat positive but also leaving you unsure. What do you think might help you feel more confident or clear when you do decide to share what’s on your mind?

That’s a good question. Honestly, I’m not really sure—I guess if I felt like what I was saying was actually being heard and taken seriously, that would help. Sometimes I just get so caught up overthinking how I’m coming across or worrying about their reaction that I kind of lose track of what I wanted to say in the first place. Maybe if I had a better idea of how to get my thoughts out without it turning into a big confrontation, I’d feel more comfortable.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and like I had to rely on others to get by. My family rarely encouraged independence, and I was frequently told I couldn’t manage things on my own.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t ask for help, I’ll be completely lost. People won’t want to support me if I don’t show I need them. I have to be careful not to seem too demanding or I’ll push people away.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to keep my feelings to myself and avoid sharing too much, hoping others will notice when I need support. Sometimes I wait too long to ask for help, or I over-apologize when I do, to avoid seeming like a burden.
ccd.situation: I’m trying to take on a new project at work that requires me to step outside my comfort zone and rely less on colleagues. I’m feeling unsure about whether I can handle it on my own.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “What if I mess up and no one is there to fix it? I’m probably going to need help and bother everyone. Maybe I’m just not capable enough to do this.”
ccd.emotions: Anxious, uncertain, and a bit ashamed of needing support. I also feel vulnerable and worried about being seen as weak.
ccd.behaviors: I hesitate to start the project independently and delay asking for assistance until I’m overwhelmed. I avoid sharing my concerns openly and try to manage on my own for as long as possible.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been alright, I guess. Just work and stuff, nothing too exciting. I’ve been a little busy but nothing out of the ordinary. Not much else to say, really.
When you say "just work and stuff," what kind of thoughts come up for you during your workdays?
Uh, I don’t know. Sometimes I get a bit nervous about making mistakes or not doing things right. I try not to think too much about it, though. I just focus on getting through the day.
What do you notice happening inside you—the thoughts or feelings—when you start to feel nervous about making mistakes?

I guess I start to worry that I’ll mess something up and people will notice. There’s this sort of pressure, like I have to get it right or else. It makes me feel kind of tense. Sometimes I just hope no one asks me too many questions.
When those worries about messing up and being noticed come up, how do you usually respond or cope in the moment?

Mostly, I just keep quiet and try to deal with it on my own. I don’t really talk about it with anyone. I figure it’s better if I handle it myself, even if it’s hard. Sometimes I wait to see if things get better before saying anything.
How does handling those worries on your own affect how you feel throughout the day or afterward?

It can feel pretty exhausting, to be honest. I just end up more stressed by the end of the day. I sometimes wonder if I’m making things harder for myself, but I don’t really know what else to do. It’s just kind of what I’m used to.
What do you think might happen if you tried sharing some of these worries with someone at work or a friend?

I don’t know… I guess I worry they might think I’m being a bit much or bothering them. Or maybe they’d just see me as someone who can’t handle things. It feels risky, so I usually just keep it to myself. I don’t want to make anyone uncomfortable.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced ongoing criticism and rejection from family members, leading to feelings of inadequacy and self-doubt. Past attempts to seek approval have often been met with disappointment, reinforcing negative self-perceptions.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I don’t follow strict moral rules, I am a bad person." "Others will reject me if they see my flaws." "I must hide my mistakes to avoid being judged."
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to withdraw from family interactions to avoid confrontation and criticism. They also engage in self-criticism and ruminate over perceived moral failings, which increases feelings of shame and isolation.
ccd.situation: During a family gathering, the patient accidentally forgets to mention an important event, causing relatives to express subtle disappointment. They perceive this as proof they are failing morally and personally.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I messed up again. I’m such a terrible person." "They must think I’m dishonest or bad." "I don’t deserve to be around them."
ccd.emotions: The patient feels deep shame, sadness, and anxiety. There is also a strong sense of guilt and fear of further rejection or disappointment.
ccd.behaviors: The patient withdraws from conversations, avoids eye contact, and leaves the gathering early. They isolate themselves afterward to cope with feelings of worthlessness and immorality.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been…whatever, I guess. Just usual stuff with my family. I don’t know, it’s fine. Can we talk about something else?
Of course. When you say it’s “fine,” what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you in those usual family moments?
I don’t know, I just… I always feel like I’m doing something wrong around them. Like they’re waiting for me to mess up. It gets old, honestly. I try not to think about it, but it’s hard.
That sounds really tough. When you notice those thoughts about messing up, what do you usually do next—how do you respond to those feelings or thoughts?
Honestly, I just shut down. I stop talking and try to get away as soon as I can. It’s not like anything I say is going to help anyway. I’d rather just avoid it than make things worse.
It makes sense you’d want to protect yourself. What do you notice happens afterward when you avoid or withdraw—does it affect how you feel or how things go with your family later?

Yeah, I guess I just end up feeling worse. I get stuck thinking about everything I did wrong, and then I just keep replaying it in my head. It’s like I can’t let it go. And my family just acts like nothing happened, which makes me feel even more alone.
That sounds really painful to be caught in that cycle. When you find yourself replaying those moments, what kinds of thoughts or beliefs come up about yourself or the situation?

I don’t know… I just feel like I must be the problem. Like, if I can’t even do something simple right, what does that say about me? I start thinking I’m just… not a good person. And then I wonder if they all see it too.
Those thoughts sound very harsh and heavy to carry. When you think, “I’m not a good person,” what evidence do you notice that supports or challenges that belief?

Honestly, it always feels like there’s evidence against me. Like, if I forget something or mess up once, that’s all anyone remembers. I can’t really think of much to challenge it—I mean, it’s not like anyone in my family ever says otherwise. It just feels true.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated setbacks in school and work, leading to feelings of inadequacy. They often compare themselves unfavorably to peers and have a history of critical familial feedback that reinforced a sense of failure.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: “If I can’t do things perfectly, I’m worthless.” “People will think less of me if I make mistakes.” “I’m bound to mess up no matter what I try.”
ccd.coping_strategies: Avoiding challenging tasks to prevent failure, withdrawing from social situations to hide perceived inadequacies, and overworking in an attempt to prove worth but often feeling exhausted and discouraged.
ccd.situation: The patient received feedback from their supervisor that their recent project wasn’t up to standard, which triggered feelings of disappointment and self-doubt.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m such a loser, I can’t do anything right.” “I’ll never be good enough for this job.” “They must think I’m useless.”
ccd.emotions: Sadness, shame, anxiety, and a sense of hopelessness.
ccd.behaviors: Avoiding future projects, procrastinating, isolating themselves from coworkers, and ruminating over mistakes late into the night.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Work’s been a bit rough. My supervisor said some stuff about my project not being up to par. Honestly, I just feel kind of tired.
When your supervisor said that about your project, what thoughts went through your mind?
I just felt like… here we go again, I messed up. I thought maybe I’m just not good enough for this job. It’s like no matter what I do, it’s never enough.
That sounds really tough. When you notice those thoughts about not being good enough, how do you usually respond or what do you do next?

I usually just sort of shut down. I try to keep my head down at work and avoid taking on anything new, just in case I mess up again. Sometimes I just want to disappear into the background, you know? It’s easier than risking more criticism.
It makes sense you’d want to protect yourself. What feelings come up for you when you think about disappearing into the background or avoiding new tasks?
I mostly just feel… sad, I guess. And kind of embarrassed. Like, I should be able to handle things, but instead I’m hiding. There’s a lot of shame around it.
Thank you for sharing that. When you notice those feelings of sadness and shame, what do you tell yourself about what those feelings mean?

I usually just think it’s proof I’m not cut out for this, or that there’s something wrong with me. It feels like everyone else can handle things, but I just can’t. I end up telling myself I should just try harder, but it never feels like enough.
It sounds like those thoughts are really harsh and exhausting. Have there been times when you’ve handled a difficult situation at work successfully, even if it didn’t feel perfect?

I mean, I guess there have been times where something went okay, or I got through a tough day. But I don’t really feel proud about it, it just feels like I barely scraped by. Most of the time I just focus on the parts I messed up. It’s hard to remember the good stuff when the mistakes feel so big.

ccd.relevant_history: I've struggled with money for years, never feeling like I can get ahead no matter how hard I try. Growing up, I often heard that I wasn’t good enough to succeed, which sticks with me even now.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t have financial success, it means I’m worthless and will never amount to anything. People will see me as a loser if I can’t manage my money well.
ccd.coping_strategies: I avoid looking at bills or checking my bank account because it just makes me feel worse. Sometimes I distract myself with TV or games to forget about my money problems.
ccd.situation: I just found out I don’t have enough money to cover my rent this month, and I’m feeling overwhelmed.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: I’m such a failure. I can’t do anything right. I’m going to mess everything up again.
ccd.emotions: Anxious, hopeless, ashamed.
ccd.behaviors: I shut down and avoid making any calls or seeking help. I isolate myself and stop trying to figure out solutions.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been rough, honestly. Got some bad news about my bank account and now I’m not sure how I’m gonna pay my rent. I just feel pretty stressed out.
When you think about the rent and your bank account situation, what specific thoughts go through your mind?

I just keep thinking that I messed up again. Like, I should’ve planned better or seen this coming. It feels like I keep making the same mistakes over and over. It’s hard not to feel like I’m just screwing everything up.
What do you notice happens to your feelings and actions when you have those thoughts about messing up?

I just feel kind of frozen, to be honest. Like, the more I think about it, the more hopeless I get. I end up not doing anything, just sitting there. Sometimes I just try to distract myself so I don't have to deal with it.
When you say you feel frozen and hopeless, what do you imagine would happen if you took a small step toward addressing the situation instead of distracting yourself?

Honestly, I get this tight feeling in my chest just thinking about it. I imagine I'd mess it up or make things worse somehow. It feels safer to just avoid it. I guess I worry I'll end up feeling even more ashamed if I try and fail.
That’s a really understandable worry. Can you think of a time in the past when you tried something challenging and, even if it was hard, you managed to handle it or learn something from it?

Um… I guess there was a time last year when I was behind on a bill, and I actually called the company to ask for help. It was super uncomfortable, but they gave me a payment plan. I remember being really nervous, but it did work out better than I expected. Still, I kind of forget about those times when things get bad again.
What do you think might help you remember or remind yourself of that experience when you’re feeling stuck or overwhelmed now?
I’m not really sure. Maybe writing it down somewhere I can see it, I guess? Or just trying to remind myself that I got through it before. It’s hard to believe it in the moment, though.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt unsupported when dealing with money issues, and whenever I asked for help, I was told to manage on my own. This has made me feel like I can’t rely on others and that I’m always struggling alone.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I admit I need help, people will see me as a burden and reject me. I won’t be able to handle financial problems on my own, so I’m stuck in this helpless situation. Asking for help means I’m weak and needy.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to avoid discussing my financial problems with friends or family because I’m afraid of being judged. Sometimes I distract myself with small purchases or entertainment to feel better temporarily, even though it makes things worse later.
ccd.situation: I just received my monthly bills and realized I don’t have enough money to cover everything. I’m worried about how I’m going to make ends meet this month.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m so needy and useless; I can’t even take care of my basic needs.” “If I ask for help, people will think less of me.” “I’m stuck, and nothing will ever get better.”
ccd.emotions: I feel overwhelmed, ashamed, and anxious. There’s also a deep sadness that comes from feeling trapped and helpless.
ccd.behaviors: I withdraw from friends and family, avoiding conversations about money. I also procrastinate on paying bills because it feels too stressful to face the problem head-on.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Honestly, it’s been pretty rough. I got my bills and realized I don’t have enough to pay them all. I don’t know, I just feel kind of stuck right now. Can we talk about something else?
It sounds like that’s been really overwhelming for you. When you say you feel stuck, what thoughts tend to come up for you in those moments?

I just keep thinking, “How did I end up here again?” It’s like no matter what I do, I mess things up. I just feel…kind of useless, I guess. I don’t really want to talk to anyone about it because what’s the point?
That sounds really painful to feel that way about yourself. When you notice those thoughts like “I mess things up” or “I’m useless,” how do they affect what you do next?

I don’t know, I just shut down. I start avoiding everything—calls, emails, even opening my bank app. It’s easier to just ignore it all than face it. And honestly, sometimes I just end up wasting time online or buying stupid little things I don’t even need.
Avoiding those things sounds like a way to protect yourself from feeling overwhelmed. What do you notice happens afterward, when you’ve avoided or distracted yourself like that?

I mean, it helps for like five minutes, but then I just feel worse. The bills are still there, and now I’ve probably made things harder for myself. I just end up feeling guilty and even more stressed. It’s like this stupid cycle I can’t get out of.
That cycle sounds really tough to be stuck in. When you think about breaking that cycle, what’s one small step you might feel able to try, even if it feels challenging?
I don’t know. It all just seems impossible. I guess maybe I could try opening one bill, but honestly, I don’t see how that changes anything. It just feels like more proof that I can’t handle things.
Opening one bill sounds like a small but meaningful step. When you imagine opening it, what’s the worst thing you fear might happen, and what might be a more balanced way to view that situation?

The worst is just seeing the number and feeling instantly crushed—like, “Great, here’s more proof I suck at this.” I don’t know how to see it differently. I can’t just trick myself into believing I’m not screwing up. It just feels like no matter what, I’m always falling behind.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced several situations where they felt overwhelmed and unable to influence outcomes, such as difficulties at work and strained relationships. These experiences have reinforced a sense of powerlessness and loss of control over daily events.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t have control, things will fall apart. I can’t trust myself to handle problems. Asking for help means I’m weak or failing.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid making decisions or taking action, hoping problems will resolve on their own. They may also procrastinate or withdraw from social interactions to minimize stress.
ccd.situation: A typical day when unexpected changes at work require quick decisions, leaving the patient feeling uncertain and pressured.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I can’t handle this right now.” “Everything is slipping away from me.” “I’m going to mess this up and everyone will be disappointed.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, frustration, helplessness, and sadness.
ccd.behaviors: The patient delays responding to work demands, avoids asking for support, and isolates themselves afterward to cope with the stress.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

It’s been kind of rough, to be honest. Work’s been all over the place and I just feel like I’m always trying to catch up. Some days I just want to stay in bed and not deal with any of it. I guess I’m just tired.
When you notice that feeling of wanting to stay in bed and avoid things, what thoughts usually go through your mind?

Usually I think something like, “What’s the point? I’m just going to mess things up anyway.” Sometimes it feels like no matter what I do, it won’t really make a difference. I just… get stuck there for a while, I guess.
What do you notice happens to your mood or energy after having those thoughts?

I feel pretty drained, honestly. It’s like all my motivation just disappears, and I get really anxious or kind of numb. I end up sitting there doing nothing, even though I know I have stuff to do. It just feels heavy.
When you think about the tasks you have to do, what goes through your mind about how you might handle them or what might happen?

Mostly, I worry I’ll screw something up or miss something important. I keep thinking I’m not prepared or I’ll make the wrong call. Sometimes I just hope the problem will go away if I ignore it for a bit. But it usually just makes everything worse.
Can you describe a recent situation where you felt this way and how you responded to those worries?

Yeah, there was this project at work last week that suddenly changed direction. My boss wanted answers fast, and I just froze. I kept telling myself I’d figure it out later, but I ended up avoiding emails and messages for most of the day. Eventually, I just apologized and said I needed more time, but I felt pretty awful about it.
What do you think was going through your mind right before you started avoiding the emails and messages that day?

I remember thinking, “I have no idea what I’m doing. If I try to respond now, I’ll just mess it up.” I felt like everyone was counting on me and I’d let them down. It was easier to just not deal with it for a while, even though I knew that wouldn’t help.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated feelings of rejection in close relationships throughout their life, leading to a persistent sense of isolation. They often recall moments where they felt ignored or dismissed by family and peers, which reinforced their belief that they are not worthy of love.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I show my true self, people will reject me." "I have to keep my feelings to myself or others will think less of me." "I am not the kind of person others want to be around."
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to withdraw from social situations and avoids opening up to others, preferring to stay quiet rather than risk rejection. They also engage in self-critical thoughts to justify their distance, believing it protects them from further hurt.

ccd.automatic_thoughts: "No one will want to talk to me." "If I say something, they’ll think I’m boring or annoying." "I’m just not the kind of person people care about."

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting. Just the usual stuff. Not much to say, really.
When you say "nothing too exciting," what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you in those moments?
I don’t know, I guess it just feels kind of… plain. Sometimes I feel a bit left out, like I’m not really part of things. It’s hard to explain. I just try not to think about it too much.
What goes through your mind when you start feeling left out or not part of things?
I usually just think that maybe people don’t really notice me. Or like, they probably don’t care if I’m around or not. I sort of feel invisible, I guess. It makes me want to keep to myself even more.
When you notice these thoughts about feeling invisible, how does your body usually respond or feel in those moments?
Um, I get kind of tense, I think. My chest feels tight and my stomach feels weird. I just want to leave or hide somewhere. It’s uncomfortable.
What do you usually do when you start feeling that tightness and the urge to hide?

I usually just leave if I can, or try to find a quiet spot away from everyone. Sometimes I’ll just stay quiet and hope no one talks to me. I don’t really want to draw attention to myself. It feels easier that way.
Have you noticed any times when you’ve felt included or connected with others, even if briefly? What was different about those moments?

Um, maybe a couple times, I guess, like when someone actually asks how I’m doing or seems interested. It doesn’t happen a lot, but… yeah, I notice it. I still feel kind of unsure, though, like maybe they’re just being polite. It’s hard to really believe it’s genuine.

core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like I don’t belong and that people avoid me because there’s something wrong with me. Growing up, I was often criticized and told I caused problems, which made me believe I’m a bad person deep down.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If people get close, they’ll see how dangerous and toxic I really am. I have to keep my distance or hide parts of myself to avoid hurting others or being rejected. It’s safer to expect the worst about myself.
ccd.coping_strategies: I often withdraw from social situations or stay quiet to avoid causing trouble. Sometimes I push people away before they can leave me. I also try to overwork myself to prove I’m not worthless.
ccd.situation: I’m trying to open up about my feelings during a personal growth workshop, but I worry that sharing will reveal how bad and harmful I really am.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: If I say how I really feel, they’ll think I’m a burden or dangerous. They’ll want to leave or cut me off if they know the truth about me.
ccd.emotions: Anxious, ashamed, lonely, and scared of being rejected or abandoned.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet and avoid sharing my thoughts even when prompted. I might leave the workshop early or avoid further sessions to protect myself from judgment.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Work kept me busy, so I didn’t really have much time to think. There were a couple of moments where I felt a bit off, but I just pushed through. Nothing major happened.
When you say you felt "a bit off," can you describe what was going through your mind or how you were feeling in those moments?

I just felt kind of uneasy, like I was on edge. I guess I was worried about saying or doing something wrong around other people. It’s hard to explain—mostly I just wanted to keep to myself and not draw attention. I felt tense, like I needed to be careful.
What kinds of thoughts were running through your head when you felt that tension and the need to be careful around others?

I kept thinking that if I said something the wrong way, people might not like it or might get upset with me. Sometimes I worry that I might say something that makes things worse, even if I don’t mean to. It feels safer to just stay quiet. I guess I’m afraid of messing up or causing problems.
When you imagine saying something that might upset others or cause problems, what do you think would happen next?

I think they’d probably just get annoyed or maybe even angry with me. Or they’d start avoiding me after that. I worry they’d see me differently, like I’m someone they shouldn’t be around. I don’t know—I just feel like it’d confirm what they already think, that there’s something wrong with me.
How do you feel about yourself when you notice these worries about how others might judge or react to you?

Honestly, I feel kind of ashamed. Like, why am I always the one who’s worried about messing things up? Sometimes I feel like there must be something seriously wrong with me if I have to try this hard just to seem normal. It makes me feel really alone.
When you notice those feelings of shame and loneliness, what do you usually do to cope with or manage them?

Most of the time, I just keep to myself and stay quiet. If I can, I distract myself with work or something else that keeps me busy. Sometimes I just leave situations early so I don’t have to deal with it. I try to avoid opening up because it feels safer that way.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

ccd.relevant_history: I've always felt like I have to earn people’s approval to be accepted, especially at work where I worry I’m not good enough. Past experiences of being overlooked or criticized have made me believe that if I’m not perfect, I’ll be rejected.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t please others and avoid conflict, I can prevent being rejected. Showing my true feelings or standing up for myself will only push people away. I must always be agreeable to maintain relationships and avoid loneliness.
ccd.coping_strategies: I often agree with others even when I disagree, avoid speaking up in meetings, and go out of my way to help colleagues to feel accepted. Sometimes I stay late or take on extra work just to prove my worth and avoid rejection.
ccd.situation: At work, I’m given a new project with tight deadlines and high expectations from my manager, making me feel intense pressure to perform perfectly and not disappoint anyone.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I don’t get this project right, they’ll think I’m useless and reject me.” “I have to say yes to everything or they’ll stop liking me.” “If I mess up, I’ll be alone and ignored.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, fear of rejection, sadness, and a feeling of being overwhelmed.
ccd.behaviors: I overwork myself trying to meet everyone’s expectations, avoid saying no even when I’m overwhelmed, and hesitate to share my honest concerns or ask for help.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess! Work’s been a little busier than usual, but I’m managing. My manager gave me a new project, so I’m just trying to make sure I do everything right. I hope I don’t let anyone down.
What thoughts go through your mind when you think about this new project and the possibility of letting someone down?

Well, I guess I just really want to make sure I don’t mess up. I keep thinking, “What if I don’t do it perfectly?” or “What if my manager isn’t happy with it?” I know it probably sounds silly, but I worry they might think I’m not good enough if I don’t get everything right. I just really want them to be pleased with my work.
When you have those worries about not doing the project perfectly, how do those thoughts affect how you feel and what you do?

Um, I guess I feel kind of anxious, like my chest gets tight and I get really tense. I start double-checking everything, sometimes even triple-checking, just to be sure I’m not making any mistakes. I also tend to stay late at work just to make sure nothing goes wrong. I guess I just want to be sure I’m not disappointing anyone.
What do you notice happens when you spend extra time double- or triple-checking your work—does it help reduce your anxiety, or does it make you feel more stressed?

At first, it helps a little—I feel calmer if I check things over and know I didn’t miss anything obvious. But after a while, I start to feel even more stressed, because I keep finding tiny things to fix or worry about. It’s like I can’t relax, even after I finish. I just keep wondering if I did enough or if someone’s going to notice something I missed.

Have there been times when you completed a project and your manager or others gave you positive feedback, even if you didn’t check everything multiple times? How did that affect your thoughts or feelings?

Oh, um, yes, that has happened before. Sometimes my manager says I did a good job, even when I felt like I didn’t check every single detail perfectly. It’s nice to hear, but I still feel a bit uneasy, like maybe I just got lucky that time. I guess I worry that next time they might notice something I missed.
What do you think it would mean for you if your manager noticed a small mistake—how might that affect your view of yourself and your work?

Hmm, I guess I’d feel pretty terrible, honestly. I’d worry that they’d see me as careless or not good enough. I know mistakes happen, but I can’t help but feel like it would confirm that I’m not measuring up. I’d probably feel embarrassed and anxious about what they think of me.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated situations where they felt powerless and unable to influence outcomes, leading to a persistent sense of being overwhelmed. Past experiences of criticism when asserting themselves have reinforced their belief that they must please others to avoid conflict or rejection.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t keep others happy, they will be upset with me or leave me. I have to stay in control by managing how others see me. Feeling out of control means I’m weak or failing, so I must avoid situations that challenge me.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient often agrees with others even when they disagree, avoids expressing their true feelings, and takes on extra responsibilities to maintain peace. They might also withdraw quietly when feeling overwhelmed rather than asking for help.

ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I say no, they’ll think I’m useless and I’ll lose their support. I can’t handle saying no right now—I’m already behind.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, fear of rejection, feeling overwhelmed, and a sense of helplessness.
ccd.behaviors: The patient agrees to help immediately, suppressing their own needs and pushing themselves further into stress, which leads to exhaustion and resentment later.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um, it’s been alright, I guess. Work’s been pretty busy, but I’m managing. I ended up helping out a few colleagues with some stuff, so that took up a bit more time than I expected. But nothing too major, really.
When you think about the extra time you spent helping your colleagues, what thoughts or feelings come up for you?

I suppose I feel a bit tired thinking about it, but I don’t want to let anyone down. I worry that if I don’t help, people might get annoyed with me or think I’m not doing enough. I just want things to go smoothly at work, you know? I guess sometimes I wonder if I’m actually doing okay.
What do you notice happening inside when you start to wonder if you’re actually doing okay at work?

Um, I guess I get kind of anxious. My chest feels tight, and I start running through everything I did, trying to make sure I didn’t mess anything up. I worry a lot about whether people are happy with me or if I said the wrong thing. It’s hard to relax—I keep thinking I should be doing more.
When those anxious feelings and worries about doing more come up, how do you usually respond or cope in the moment?

Usually, I just push myself to keep going. I try to do whatever people ask, even if I’m tired or busy. I just think it’s easier to say yes and get it done, rather than risk anyone being upset. Sometimes I end up skipping breaks or staying late, but I don’t really mention it to anyone.
What do you think happens to your energy or mood after pushing yourself like that for a while?

Honestly, I get pretty drained. It’s like I’m running on empty, but I don’t want anyone to notice. Sometimes I feel frustrated with myself, or a bit resentful, but I usually just try to brush it off. I don’t want to make a fuss or seem like I can’t handle things.
When you notice that frustration or resentment building up, what do you tell yourself about those feelings or about asking for support?

Um, I usually just tell myself to let it go—it’s not a big deal compared to what others are dealing with. I feel like I should just handle it on my own and not bother anyone. Sometimes I worry people would think I’m being difficult if I spoke up. So I just keep it to myself and try to move on.

ccd.relevant_history: Throughout my life, I have struggled with feeling like I can’t succeed no matter how hard I try. I often compare myself to others and come up short, which has made me believe that I’m just destined to fail. These experiences have built up a deep sense of helplessness about my ability to grow or improve.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m not perfect or successful right away, it means I’m worthless and will never be good enough. Trying new things is risky because it just proves I’ll mess up again. People will judge me harshly if I show any weakness or make mistakes.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid challenges or new opportunities because I’m afraid of failing and confirming that I’m a failure. Sometimes I procrastinate or give up quickly to protect myself from disappointment. I might also criticize myself harshly to stay “realistic” and prepare for negative outcomes.
ccd.situation: I was offered a chance to take on a new project at work that could help me develop skills and advance my career, but I immediately felt overwhelmed and doubted my ability to succeed. The thought of possibly failing made me want to decline the opportunity.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to mess this up like I always do.” “I’m just not smart or capable enough to handle this.” “If I fail, everyone will see that I’m a loser.” “There’s no point in even trying because I’ll just disappoint myself again.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious and nervous about the possibility of failing. There’s a heavy sadness and disappointment in myself before I even start. Sometimes I feel ashamed and worthless, like I don’t deserve success or respect.
ccd.behaviors: I find myself hesitating to say yes or even thinking about declining the project to avoid the pressure. I might distract myself with other tasks or procrastinate instead of preparing. If I do try, I might not put in my full effort because I already expect to fail.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um... it’s been okay, I guess. Work’s been kind of stressful, actually. Something came up that I’m supposed to consider taking on, but I haven’t really made a decision yet. Honestly, I’ve just been feeling sort of tense about it all week.
What thoughts have been going through your mind when you think about this decision you need to make at work?

Well, I keep thinking that I’m probably not the right person for it, like I’ll just end up making a mess of things. It’s hard not to worry that I’ll let everyone down if I say yes and then can’t do it well. Sometimes I just replay all the times I’ve tried something before and it didn’t go right, and that kind of makes me freeze up. I don’t know... it just feels really overwhelming.
When you notice those thoughts about not being the right person or worrying about letting others down, how do those thoughts affect what you feel in your body or your emotions?

I definitely get this tight feeling in my chest, like it’s harder to breathe. My stomach twists up and I feel kind of jittery, almost like I want to escape. Emotionally, I just get really anxious and, I guess, sort of sad—like I’m already disappointed in myself even though nothing’s happened yet. Sometimes I feel a heavy kind of shame, like I’m just inherently not good enough.
That sounds really tough to carry around. When those feelings of anxiety, sadness, and shame come up, what do you usually do next? How do you tend to respond or cope in those moments?

Usually, I try to distract myself—like I’ll check my phone, scroll through social media, or suddenly remember some other “urgent” task I should do instead. If I’m being honest, I’ll sometimes just put off making any decision at all, hoping it’ll go away or someone else will handle it. There are times where I just criticize myself in my head, telling myself I need to get it together, but that doesn’t really help. Most of the time, I guess I just avoid dealing with it until I have no choice.
When you avoid making the decision or distract yourself, how does that impact your anxiety or feelings about the situation later on?

At first, it sort of helps—like the anxiety backs off for a little while if I just push it out of my mind. But honestly, the longer I avoid it, the worse it feels when I think about it again. It’s like this pressure keeps building up, and then I start feeling guilty on top of everything else because I know I’m not really dealing with it. By the time I have to face it, I feel even more stressed and convinced that I can’t handle it.

It sounds like avoidance gives you temporary relief but then makes things harder later. Can you think of a time when you faced a difficult task or decision and it didn’t turn out as badly as you expected? What was going through your mind then?

Hmm... I guess there was this time a few months ago when my manager asked me to give a short presentation at a team meeting. I was really anxious leading up to it and kept thinking I’d mess up or forget what to say. But afterward, people said I did a good job and no one seemed to notice the parts where I stumbled. Even then, though, I remember mostly feeling relief that it was over, rather than actually proud of myself.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced several situations where they felt powerless, such as being overlooked at work and having conflicts in personal relationships. These repeated experiences have reinforced a deep sense of victimhood and helplessness over time.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "I can't change what happens to me," "Other people have control over my life," and "Trying to stand up for myself will only make things worse."
ccd.coping_strategies: Avoiding confrontation, withdrawing from social situations, and ruminating on negative experiences instead of seeking support or solutions.
ccd.situation: Feeling overwhelmed by a heavy workload and criticism from a supervisor, the patient feels stuck and unable to improve their situation.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "There's nothing I can do to fix this," "I'm always the one who gets blamed," and "No one cares about how I feel."
ccd.emotions: Sadness, frustration, and helplessness.
ccd.behaviors: Withdrawing from colleagues, procrastinating on tasks, and avoiding discussing problems with the supervisor.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

It’s been kind of tough, honestly. Work feels like a lot right now, and I keep feeling like I’m getting singled out for mistakes. I guess I’ve just been trying to get through it. Nothing really good stands out.
When you notice yourself feeling singled out for mistakes at work, what kinds of thoughts usually go through your mind?

I just keep thinking, “Why is it always me?” Like, no matter how hard I try, I end up getting blamed. Sometimes I wonder if there’s even a point in speaking up, because it never seems to change anything. It just feels hopeless a lot of the time.
What emotions do you notice when you have those thoughts about being singled out and feeling hopeless?
Mostly I just feel really sad and frustrated. Sometimes I get this heavy feeling, like I want to just give up. I also feel angry, but I don’t show it. It’s like I’m stuck and nothing I do matters.
When you feel that heaviness and want to give up, what do you usually do next—do you take any actions or try to cope in certain ways?

Usually, I just keep to myself. I stop talking to people at work and try to avoid everyone. Sometimes I just sit at my desk, staring at my screen, not really getting anything done. I know I should probably do something, but I just end up shutting down.
What do you think might happen if you tried to speak up or connect with someone at work instead of shutting down?

Honestly, I think it would just make things worse. I imagine people wouldn't really listen or they'd just blame me more. Maybe they'd think I'm making excuses. Part of me feels like it's safer to just stay quiet.
Have there been times in the past when speaking up or sharing how you feel at work led to a different outcome than you expected?

Not really, at least not that I can remember. Most of the times I’ve tried to bring something up, it kind of just backfired or got brushed off. It makes me feel like I’m not being heard. After that, I just stopped bothering.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt criticized and never quite good enough in social settings. I learned early on that making others happy was the only way to avoid conflict or rejection, which made me doubt my own worth and morality.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t please others, they will think I am a bad person. Being moral means always putting others’ needs before my own. If I am not perfect in social situations, I am fundamentally flawed.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to agree with everyone and avoid expressing my own opinions. I often apologize even when I haven’t done anything wrong. I avoid confrontation at all costs, even if it means sacrificing my own comfort.
ccd.situation: At a group dinner, I find myself constantly agreeing with others and suppressing my true feelings because I’m afraid they will think poorly of me if I disagree or say something wrong.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They must think I’m a terrible person if I don’t go along with this.” “I’m selfish if I speak up for myself.” “If I make a mistake, that proves I’m immoral.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, guilt, shame, and a deep sense of sadness.
ccd.behaviors: Nodding along silently, avoiding eye contact, agreeing quickly, and staying quiet even when I want to say something. Later, I might ruminate on what I should have said and feel worthless.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been pretty normal, I guess. Nothing too exciting happened. I tried to keep busy with work and seeing some friends. Sorry, I don’t really have much to complain about—it’s all fine!

It sounds like things feel pretty steady right now. When you say “it’s all fine,” what does that feel like for you inside—are there any particular thoughts or feelings that come up when you reflect on your day-to-day?

Uh, I guess I just, um… I don’t know, I try not to overthink things. Most days, I just hope I don’t mess anything up or say the wrong thing. Sorry, I know that probably sounds weird—really, it's okay. I just prefer it when things are calm, I guess.
It makes sense that you want to keep things calm. When you notice the hope of not messing up or saying the wrong thing, what kinds of situations or moments tend to bring those worries up the most?

Oh, um, probably when I’m around a group of people, like at dinner or something. I get kind of nervous about saying something stupid or, I don’t know, upsetting someone by accident. I don’t want people to think badly of me, so I usually just go along with whatever everyone else says. It’s not a big deal or anything—I mean, I’m just more comfortable that way.

It sounds like being in group settings can feel a bit tense because of worries about how others might judge you. What do you notice happening inside you—thoughts, feelings, or physical sensations—when you decide to go along with what others say instead of sharing your own thoughts?

Um, I guess I feel kind of, I don’t know, anxious? My chest gets tight and I start thinking, like, “I hope they don’t think I’m weird,” or “What if I say something wrong?” I also feel a bit guilty, honestly, like I should say what I really think, but then I worry I’m being selfish. I just… I’d rather avoid any problems, even if it means staying quiet.

That sounds like a tough balance between wanting to be yourself and wanting to avoid conflict. When you notice those thoughts like “I hope they don’t think I’m weird” or “What if I say something wrong,” how much do you think those thoughts influence your decision to stay quiet?

Oh, um, a lot, I think. It’s almost like I can’t help it—I just kind of freeze up and feel like it’s safer not to say anything. I don’t want to risk people thinking I’m a bad person or, uh, not liking me. It probably sounds silly, but I get stuck in my head about it.

It makes sense that those thoughts feel very powerful in the moment. When you get stuck in your head like that, have you ever noticed any times when you were able to speak up or express yourself despite those worries? What was different about those moments?

Hmm… I guess there have been a few times, like with close friends or family, where I could say what I really thought. Usually it’s when I’m pretty sure they won’t get mad at me or think badly of me. I feel more relaxed around them, but I still worry a bit. It’s just easier when I know they’ll understand or won’t judge me harshly.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and rejected by my family and peers, which made me believe that I was not worthy of love or attention. These experiences have left me feeling isolated and hesitant to open up to others, fearing further rejection. Over time, these feelings have solidified into a deep belief that I am fundamentally unlovable.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I think that if I don’t try too hard or if I keep my distance, I won’t get hurt as much. I also believe that people will eventually see the real me as undesirable and leave, so it’s safer not to get close. This makes me feel like I have to prove myself or hide parts of who I am to be accepted.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to withdraw from social situations or avoid expressing my true feelings because I’m afraid of being rejected or judged. Sometimes I try to please others excessively, hoping that if I’m useful or agreeable, they will like me. When things don’t go well, I often blame myself and retreat further into isolation.
ccd.situation: I recently started a new hobby group where I hoped to make friends, but when I didn’t receive much attention or invitations to hang out, I felt invisible and unwanted. This made me question whether anyone there actually likes me or wants me around.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one really wants me here.” “I must be boring or annoying if they don’t invite me.” “I’m just not someone people choose to be with.” “If I show who I really am, they’ll reject me.”
ccd.emotions: I feel sad, lonely, and anxious. There’s also a strong sense of shame and hopelessness about my ability to connect with others. At times, I feel a dull ache of emptiness and fear that I will always be alone.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid reaching out or trying to make plans with people from the group. I keep my feelings to myself and don’t share much about who I am. This leads me to spend more time alone, which only reinforces my belief that I am unlovable.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, I guess it’s been okay... Nothing particularly special happened. I went to that new hobby group thing again, but honestly, I just sort of sat on the sidelines. It was alright, I guess, just felt a bit out of place.
What was going through your mind when you were sitting on the sidelines during the group?

Mostly, I was just kind of wondering if anyone even noticed I was there. I kept thinking, like, maybe they just didn’t care or maybe I wasn’t interesting enough for them to talk to. There were a few times I thought about joining someone’s conversation, but I worried I’d just come off as awkward or interrupt. So I just... stayed quiet and tried not to seem too out of place.
What do you think would happen if you did join a conversation, even if it felt a bit awkward at first?

Um, I don’t know, honestly. Part of me thinks maybe it would go okay, like maybe someone would respond and it wouldn’t be a big deal. But there’s this other part that just keeps thinking I’d probably say something weird or boring, and they’d just sort of politely look away or not really include me after that. I guess I’m sort of scared I’d just confirm that I don’t really fit in, you know?
When you notice that part of you doubting yourself and expecting rejection, what do you say to yourself in those moments?

Honestly, I usually just kind of shut down. I tell myself things like, “Don’t bother, you’ll just embarrass yourself,” or “They’re not interested, so why try?” Sometimes I even think I must just be annoying for wanting to join in. It’s hard to convince myself there’s any point, so I just stay quiet.
How do you feel physically and emotionally when those thoughts like “Don’t bother” or “You’ll just embarrass yourself” come up?

I get this heavy feeling in my chest, kind of like a weight pressing down. My stomach feels tight too, almost like I’m bracing for something bad. Emotionally, it’s mostly this mix of sadness and a sort of sinking disappointment in myself, like I’m letting myself down again. I just feel really small and kind of invisible, if that makes sense.
That makes a lot of sense. When you notice that heaviness and those feelings of sadness and invisibility, what do you usually do next?

Usually, I just withdraw even more. I don’t really try to talk to anyone—I just sort of fade into the background and hope no one notices how uncomfortable I feel. Sometimes I’ll find an excuse to leave early, or I’ll pretend to be busy with something on my phone. I just want to get away from the situation as quickly as possible.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always struggled with feeling like I don’t measure up, especially when trying to improve myself or take on new challenges. Past experiences where I felt overlooked or criticized have made me doubt my value.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to grow or change, I’m just setting myself up to fail and prove that I’m worthless. People don’t really want to see me succeed or change because I’m just a waste.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid new opportunities or give up quickly because I don’t believe I’ll do well. I keep my thoughts to myself and don’t reach out for support, staying reserved to avoid drawing attention to my perceived flaws.
ccd.situation: I’m thinking about starting a new hobby or course to improve myself, but I hesitate because I feel like it won’t matter or I’ll just mess it up.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “What’s the point? I’m just going to fail and be a disappointment again.” “I don’t deserve to get better or be proud of myself.”
ccd.emotions: I feel discouraged, anxious, and a bit hopeless. There’s also a deep sadness underneath it all.
ccd.behaviors: I procrastinate or avoid signing up for the course altogether. When I do try, I’m very self-critical and withdraw from others to hide my struggles.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting. I mostly just kept to myself. Didn’t really do much out of the ordinary.
When you say you mostly kept to yourself, what kinds of thoughts or feelings were coming up for you during that time?

I don’t know, I just… felt kind of tired. I guess I was thinking about some stuff, like whether I’m doing enough or if I should be trying harder. Mostly I just felt sort of… blah. Not sure how to explain it.
What do you notice happening inside you when you have those thoughts about not doing enough or needing to try harder?
Um… I guess I just feel really heavy. Like, kind of anxious, and it makes me want to just… stop trying. It’s hard to see the point sometimes. I end up just sort of shutting down.
When you notice that feeling of heaviness and the urge to shut down, what do you usually do next?
I mostly just kind of… distract myself. Like, I’ll watch something or scroll on my phone. Sometimes I just don’t do anything and sit there, honestly. I try not to think about it too much.
How do you feel after you’ve spent some time distracting yourself or sitting quietly like that?

I don’t really feel better, if I’m honest. It’s more like the heaviness is still there, just pushed aside for a while. Sometimes I feel frustrated with myself for not doing more. Other times I just feel numb.
When you feel that frustration or numbness afterward, what do you tell yourself about those feelings or about yourself?

I don’t really say much to myself, I guess… just kind of think, “This is just how I am.” Sometimes I feel like maybe I’m just not meant to get past this stuff. Like, maybe I just don’t have it in me. I try not to dwell on it, but it sticks around.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve struggled with money management since I was a teenager, often feeling guilty for spending on myself or asking for help. Growing up, I was told that needing money meant I was irresponsible and bad. These messages made me believe I’m a bad person whenever financial problems come up.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t have enough money, it means I’m a failure and a bad person. People will judge me or think less of me if they find out I’m struggling financially. I have to hide my money problems to avoid being seen as immoral or worthless.
ccd.coping_strategies: I avoid talking about money or asking for help because I’m ashamed. Sometimes I try to distract myself by buying small things to feel better temporarily. Other times, I isolate myself to avoid judgment or confrontation about my financial situation.
ccd.situation: I recently realized I’m behind on some bills and don’t have enough money to cover everything. I’m worried about how this will affect me and what others will think if they find out.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m a terrible person for letting this happen.” “If people knew, they’d think I’m dishonest or irresponsible.” “I don’t deserve to have financial stability or respect.”
ccd.emotions: Ashamed, anxious, hopeless, lonely.
ccd.behaviors: Withdrawing from friends and family, avoiding checking bills or bank statements, and not asking for help even when I’m overwhelmed.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been a bit stressful, I guess. I’ve just been trying to keep up with things, but it feels like a lot lately. I haven’t really talked to anyone about it. I’ve mostly kept to myself.
When you say it feels like a lot, what kinds of thoughts or worries go through your mind during those times?

I just keep thinking about all the things I have to take care of, like bills and stuff I’ve put off. I worry that I’m messing things up or not handling things the way I should. Sometimes I just feel really anxious about it, like I’m letting myself and others down. It makes me want to avoid even thinking about it.
What do you notice happening in your body or your mood when you start to think about those bills and responsibilities?

I get this tight feeling in my chest and my stomach feels kind of heavy. My mind races and it’s hard to focus on anything else. I just feel anxious and sort of ashamed. Sometimes I just want to hide or distract myself so I don’t have to deal with it.
When you feel that urge to hide or distract yourself, what do you usually do, and how does it affect your anxiety afterward?
