Sometimes I just avoid looking at my bank account or ignore the bills for a while. Other times, I’ll scroll on my phone or maybe buy something small just to feel a bit better, even if it doesn’t last. The distraction works for a little bit, but then I feel worse when I remember what I’ve been avoiding. The anxiety usually comes back even stronger.
What do you think might happen if you tried facing one small task related to your bills or responsibilities instead of avoiding it?

Honestly, I get really nervous just thinking about it. I feel like if I look at the bills, I’ll just confirm that I’ve screwed up. Part of me worries that it’ll make me feel even more ashamed. But I also wonder if maybe it could help a little, I don’t know.
That nervousness and worry sound really strong—when you imagine looking at a bill and feeling you’ve “screwed up,” what specific thoughts come up about yourself or the situation?

I just think, “I’m so careless, how did I let it get to this point?” Or, “Other people wouldn’t mess this up like I have.” It feels like proof that I’m not responsible or that something is wrong with me. I get scared that if anyone else found out, they’d think badly of me too.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve always felt like no matter what I do, things just spiral out of control. Growing up, I didn’t have much say in family decisions, and that made me feel powerless and unsure of myself.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I can’t control everything, something bad will happen. I have to keep things in order or else I’ll be overwhelmed and fail. Asking for help means I’m weak and can’t handle life on my own.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to plan everything down to the last detail and avoid situations where I might feel uncertain. Sometimes I shut down or avoid making decisions when things feel too chaotic. I also avoid asking for support because I don’t want to seem helpless.
ccd.situation: Trying to take on new challenges to grow personally but feeling like things are slipping out of my hands and I’m not managing well.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m losing control." "If I can’t manage this perfectly, I’ll mess everything up." "I’m just not capable of handling this."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, frustration, and sadness mixed with a sense of helplessness.
ccd.behaviors: I procrastinate or avoid tasks that feel overwhelming, become tense and irritable, and sometimes withdraw from others to hide my struggles.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

It’s been kind of rough, honestly. I’ve just been feeling behind on a lot of things, and it’s like I can’t quite catch up. I keep thinking if I just try harder, maybe I’ll manage. But it’s exhausting.
What kinds of things are you feeling behind on, and what goes through your mind when you notice that?

Mostly work stuff, but also some things at home—like chores piling up or messages I haven’t replied to. When I notice it, I get this sort of tight feeling in my chest, and my mind just starts racing. I keep thinking, “I should be able to handle this.” It feels like everything is building up too fast for me to keep up.
When you notice that tight feeling and racing thoughts, what do you usually do next?

Sometimes I just freeze up and can’t decide what to tackle first, so I end up scrolling on my phone or just kind of zoning out. Other times I try to make these super detailed lists, but even that feels overwhelming. I might snap at someone if they interrupt me. Honestly, I just want to hide until it all goes away.
What do you think happens when you hide or zone out—does it help the feelings or make things feel different afterward?

It helps for a little bit, I guess. I get some relief when I can just check out for a while. But once I come back to things, it all just feels even heavier, like I’m even further behind than before. Then I feel pretty frustrated with myself for not handling it better.
When you feel frustrated with yourself after that, what kinds of thoughts or beliefs come up about yourself or your abilities?

I end up thinking I just can’t handle things like other people can. I tell myself I should be able to manage, and the fact that I keep slipping up means something’s wrong with me. I also worry that if I keep messing up, everything will just fall apart. It’s like I’m always on the edge of losing control.
That sounds really overwhelming. When you imagine things falling apart or losing control, what do you picture happening?

I picture missing deadlines at work or letting people down because I forgot something important. I see my apartment getting messy, bills piling up, and people noticing that I can’t keep it together. It’s like everything would just collapse at once, and I wouldn’t know where to start fixing it. I worry people would look at me differently or think less of me.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and dismissed by my family, especially when I expressed my needs or feelings. These experiences made me believe that people don’t really want to be close to me, and I’ve carried that feeling into my adult relationships.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I show who I really am or get too close to someone, they will eventually reject me. It’s safer to expect disappointment than to hope for acceptance. I have to be cautious and not let myself get vulnerable because that’s when I get hurt.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to keep my distance emotionally, avoiding deep conversations or sharing too much about myself. Sometimes I try to please others excessively to avoid conflict or rejection, but if that doesn’t work, I withdraw completely to protect myself.
ccd.situation: I recently started dating someone new, and after a few dates, I noticed they didn’t respond to my messages as quickly as I hoped. I found myself worrying that they were losing interest and that I would be rejected again.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They probably don’t like me as much as I thought.” “I must have said something wrong or been too much for them.” “They’re going to leave me just like everyone else does.”
ccd.emotions: I felt anxious and sad, like a heavy weight in my chest. There was also a familiar sense of loneliness and hopelessness creeping in, making me want to pull away before I got hurt.
ccd.behaviors: I stopped reaching out as often and avoided initiating conversations. I also kept my feelings to myself instead of sharing my concerns, which made it harder to build trust and closeness with this person.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been kind of up and down, I guess. Work was busy, and I’ve just been trying to keep myself distracted, you know? I went out a couple times, but I guess I haven’t really been feeling all that great. I don’t know, I just get this sort of heavy feeling lately.
When you notice that heavy feeling, what kinds of thoughts tend to go through your mind?

Hmm... honestly, I start replaying a lot of little things in my head, like conversations or texts, just over and over. I catch myself wondering if I messed something up or maybe said something weird, or if people notice how awkward I can be. Sometimes I feel like I’m just kind of…outside of things, like people wouldn’t really miss me if I wasn’t around. It’s exhausting, trying to figure out what I did wrong all the time.
That sounds really tough. When you have these thoughts about messing up or feeling awkward, how does that affect the way you act or what you do afterward?

Yeah, it definitely affects me. I usually just shut down a bit—I stop reaching out or making plans, even if I actually want to see people. Sometimes I’ll overthink what to say or just not say anything at all, just to avoid making things worse. It’s like I sort of pull back into myself until it feels a bit safer again.
When you pull back and avoid reaching out, what do you notice happens to those feelings of being left out or not being missed?

Um, honestly, it’s kind of a double-edged sword. On one hand, pulling back feels safer because at least I can’t embarrass myself or get rejected if I don’t put myself out there. But then, after a while, the loneliness kind of creeps in harder, and I start to feel even more invisible. It just turns into this cycle where I want connection but I’m too afraid to risk it.
That cycle sounds really painful. Can you think of a recent time when you wanted to reach out or connect but felt held back by those fears? What was going through your mind in that moment?

Yeah, actually, just a few days ago I was thinking about texting someone I’ve been seeing lately—we’d had a couple of nice dates. I kept staring at my phone, trying to come up with something to say, but these worries just kept popping up like, "What if they’re already losing interest?" or "Maybe I’m coming on too strong." I told myself it was better to just wait, even though I really wanted to talk to them. I ended up not sending anything and felt pretty down about it all night.
That hesitation sounds really frustrating. When those worries like "What if they’re already losing interest?" come up, what evidence do you notice that supports or challenges those thoughts?

Um… I mean, I guess the main thing that made me worry was that they didn’t reply to my last message as quickly as before. In my head that just turned into, “They must not care as much as I thought.” But if I’m being honest, nothing really bad actually happened—like, they still responded later and were friendly. Even so, it’s hard for me to shake that first panicky feeling, so all I can focus on is the idea that I said something wrong or pushed them away somehow.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up in a household where decisions were mostly made by others, I often felt powerless and overlooked. This left me feeling like I had little control over my own life. Family conflicts have reinforced this feeling, making me believe I can't change my circumstances.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to assert myself, it won’t make a difference because I’m stuck in this situation. Speaking up will just cause more problems or rejection. I have to accept things as they are because I don’t have the strength to change them.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to keep my feelings to myself and avoid confrontation to prevent conflict. Sometimes I withdraw from family gatherings or conversations to protect myself. I also try to distract myself with work or hobbies to escape these feelings.
ccd.situation: During a family discussion about plans and responsibilities, I feel like my opinions don’t matter and that I have no say in what happens.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one cares what I think.” “I’m stuck and there’s nothing I can do.” “If I speak up, they’ll just ignore me or get mad.”
ccd.emotions: Hopeless, frustrated, lonely, and anxious.
ccd.behaviors: I stay silent during conversations, avoid participating in decisions, and sometimes leave the room to get away from the tension. I also avoid family events when I feel overwhelmed.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Nothing special. Just kept myself busy with work mostly. Family stuff was a bit tense, but I tried not to get too involved.
What was going through your mind when the family situation felt tense?
I mostly just kept thinking that it’s better if I stay quiet. I didn’t really feel like my opinion would matter anyway. Honestly, I just wanted the whole thing to be over as quickly as possible.
When you thought your opinion wouldn’t matter, how did that affect how you felt in that moment?

I felt pretty small, I guess. Kind of invisible, like I was just there but not really part of things. It made me feel alone, even though I was surrounded by people. I also felt this heaviness in my chest, like I wanted to leave the room.
That sounds really tough. When you noticed that heaviness in your chest and feeling alone, what did you do next?

I mostly just sat there quietly and waited for it to be over. Sometimes I’d pull out my phone or look busy, just to avoid talking. If it got too much, I’d find an excuse to leave the room for a while. I try to keep my head down so no one looks at me or asks me anything.
When you used your phone or left the room, how did that help or affect how you were feeling?

It helps a little, I guess—it’s like a distraction, so I don’t have to think about how out of place I feel. But honestly, it doesn’t really fix anything. The heaviness is still there when I come back. It’s just a short break from feeling stuck.
What do you think might happen if you shared your thoughts or feelings with someone in your family instead of staying quiet?

I worry they’d just brush me off or not really listen. Sometimes I think it might even make things worse, like maybe someone would get annoyed or start an argument. I guess I just expect it not to go anywhere. It feels safer to keep things to myself.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient often feels overwhelmed by everyday responsibilities and has a history of relying heavily on others for support. They recall past experiences where asking for help was met with frustration or rejection, reinforcing a sense of being a burden.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t get help, I won’t be able to manage on my own. People will think less of me if I ask for too much. I have to keep struggling because no one else will step in.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to withdraw when feeling overwhelmed, avoiding asking for help to prevent rejection. They may also over-apologize or try to do everything themselves, which leads to exhaustion.
ccd.situation: Feeling stressed by multiple deadlines at work and household chores piling up, the patient notices they are struggling to keep up and unsure how to manage everything.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I can’t do this alone. I’m such a burden to everyone around me.” “If I ask for help, they’ll get annoyed or think I’m weak.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, frustration, sadness, and a sense of helplessness.
ccd.behaviors: Avoids reaching out for support, isolates themselves, and pushes through tasks despite feeling overwhelmed, which often leads to burnout.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It's been kind of tough, honestly. Work has been piling up, and I just feel like there aren't enough hours in the day. I’m trying to keep up, but I guess I’m struggling a bit.
When you notice the work piling up and feeling like there aren’t enough hours, what kinds of thoughts go through your mind?

I usually just think, “How am I supposed to get all this done?” Sometimes it’s like, “I’m falling behind and everyone else seems to manage fine.” It makes me worry that I’m not good enough at handling things.
When you have thoughts like “I’m falling behind” or “I’m not good enough,” how do those thoughts affect how you feel in the moment?

I feel pretty anxious, honestly. Sometimes it’s like this heavy pressure in my chest, and I just want to shut everything out for a bit. I get frustrated with myself, too. It’s hard not to feel kind of helpless.
What do you usually do when you start to feel that heavy pressure and frustration—how do you respond or cope in those moments?

I usually just keep my head down and try to push through on my own. Sometimes I’ll ignore messages or avoid talking to people because I don’t want to deal with questions. I might go for a walk or just sit by myself for a while. I guess I don’t really ask anyone for help.
When you choose to push through alone and avoid reaching out, what impact does that have on your feelings or your ability to manage the work?

Honestly, it just makes me feel even more tired and stressed. The work doesn’t really get easier—it just feels heavier the longer I do it by myself. Sometimes I end up making small mistakes because I’m so worn out. I guess I just feel more alone, too.
Can you recall a time when you did reach out for support or took a different approach—how did that affect the situation or how you felt?

Yeah, there have been a couple of times I tried asking for help. It didn’t always go well—sometimes people seemed annoyed, or like I was bothering them. That kind of stuck with me, made me feel embarrassed for even asking. I guess that’s part of why I don’t do it much now.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has struggled with financial instability for several years, feeling unable to improve their situation despite efforts to budget and seek additional work. Past experiences of debt and unexpected expenses have reinforced a sense of being stuck and powerless.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I try to change things, I’ll just make it worse or fail again." "No matter what I do, I won’t be able to get out of this." "I don’t have control over my financial future."
ccd.coping_strategies: Avoiding looking at bills or bank statements, procrastinating on budgeting or financial planning, withdrawing from discussions about money, and sometimes spending impulsively to distract from stress.
ccd.situation: Facing mounting bills and uncertainty about paying rent next month, the patient feels overwhelmed by their financial situation and worries about what will happen if they can’t make ends meet.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m trapped and there’s no way out." "I’m going to lose everything." "I’m just not good with money."
ccd.emotions: Helplessness, anxiety, hopelessness, and sadness.
ccd.behaviors: Withdrawing from friends and family, avoiding financial tasks, procrastinating on seeking help or advice, and feeling immobilized when trying to make decisions about money.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been kind of rough, to be honest. I’ve just felt stressed out most days. Things with money have been especially tough this week. I’m having trouble sleeping because I keep thinking about it.
When you notice yourself thinking about money and feeling stressed, what are some of the specific thoughts that come up for you?

I keep thinking that I’m never going to catch up. Sometimes it feels like no matter what I do, it’s just not enough. I worry that something bad is going to happen if I can’t pay for everything. I also get this feeling like I’m just not good with money, so what’s the point in even trying?
That sounds really challenging. When you have those thoughts about not being good with money or things not being enough, how do you usually respond or cope in those moments?

Usually, I try to push it out of my mind. Sometimes I just avoid looking at my bills or bank account, because it’s too overwhelming. Other times, I end up distracting myself—like watching TV or scrolling on my phone—just to not think about it. Honestly, I don’t really do anything helpful, I just kind of ignore it and hope it’ll go away for a bit.

Avoiding those worries makes a lot of sense when it feels overwhelming. What do you notice happens afterward, both with your feelings and the situation itself, when you avoid dealing with the money worries?

At first, I feel a bit of relief because I’m not thinking about it. But then it comes back even stronger, and I end up feeling more anxious than before. The bills pile up or I get reminders, so the stress just gets worse. I start feeling kind of ashamed and even more stuck.
It sounds like the relief is temporary but the stress builds up again over time. When the anxiety and shame come up, what do you tell yourself about those feelings or about your situation?

I usually just think that I’ve messed things up again, or that I should’ve done something differently. Sometimes I tell myself that I’m never going to figure this out. It feels like I’m always a step behind, no matter what I do. I just end up feeling really hopeless about it.

That sense of being stuck and hopeless must be really hard to carry. Can you remember a time when you felt more in control or hopeful about managing your money or a similarly stressful situation? What was different then?

I’m not sure. Maybe there were a few times when I got a new job or picked up extra hours and felt like things might get better. But it never really lasted—I’d get an unexpected bill or something would go wrong. Even then, it felt like I was just barely keeping my head above water. I don’t really remember feeling fully in control, just maybe less panicked for a little while.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored and criticized by my family, which made me believe I wasn’t good enough. I rarely received positive attention, and any mistakes I made were met with harsh judgment. This left me feeling invisible and unworthy.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m not perfect or useful to others, I’m a failure and don’t deserve kindness. People will only accept me if I constantly prove my worth. I must hide my true feelings to avoid being rejected or abandoned.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to keep my head down and avoid sharing my feelings to prevent conflict. Sometimes I push myself to do things perfectly to earn approval. Other times, I withdraw completely because I feel like no one cares anyway.
ccd.situation: During a family gathering, I noticed no one asked how I was doing or seemed interested in me. It felt like I was invisible again, just like when I was younger.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one cares about me. I’m a burden to my family. I don’t deserve to be here or to be happy.”
ccd.emotions: I felt deeply sad, lonely, and ashamed. There was also a heavy sense of hopelessness and emptiness.
ccd.behaviors: I stayed quiet and avoided talking to anyone, withdrawing into myself. Later, I isolated myself in my room and avoided family interactions altogether.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Nothing special happened. I went to a family thing, but I mostly just kept to myself. I haven’t really felt like talking to anyone much.
What was going through your mind when you decided to keep to yourself at the family event?

I just felt like no one really cared if I was there or not. Whenever I tried to join in, I felt kind of awkward, like I didn’t belong. I guess I figured it was easier to stay quiet and out of the way.
When you notice that feeling of not belonging or awkwardness, what kinds of thoughts or images come up for you?

It’s like… I picture myself just fading into the background, barely even there. Sometimes I think, “Why am I even here?” I tell myself people probably wouldn’t notice if I left. It just feels pointless, honestly.
That sounds really tough. When you think “Why am I even here?” or that people wouldn’t notice if you left, how does your body feel in those moments?

I feel heavy, like there’s this weight pressing down on my chest. My stomach gets tight and I just want to disappear. Sometimes my throat feels tight too, like it’s hard to speak even if I wanted to. Mostly, I just feel tired and empty.
When you notice those physical sensations and feelings of wanting to disappear, what do you usually do next?
I usually just go quiet and keep to myself. If I can, I’ll find a place to be alone, like my room. I try not to draw any attention. It’s easier to just stay out of the way until things are over.
What do you think might happen if you tried reaching out or sharing how you’re feeling with someone at those times?

Honestly, I don’t think it would make much difference. I’m worried people would just brush it off or not really get it. I guess I’m afraid they’d think I’m just being dramatic or looking for attention. It feels safer just to keep it to myself.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient grew up in a family where emotional expression was limited and affection was often withheld. Experiences of inconsistent caregiving and perceived rejection have contributed to feelings of insecurity and fear of abandonment.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "I have to keep my feelings to myself to avoid being rejected." "If I get too close, people will leave me." These beliefs reinforce a guarded approach to relationships, emphasizing self-protection over connection.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to withdraw emotionally and minimize sharing personal thoughts to avoid potential hurt. They may also avoid initiating closeness or confrontation, preferring silence over conflict to keep the peace.
ccd.situation: During a family gathering, the patient notices relatives engaging warmly with each other but feels distant and excluded. They perceive subtle cues as signs that they are not truly accepted or wanted.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "They don’t really care about me." "I’m going to be left out or forgotten like always." "If I try to join in, I’ll just be rejected."
ccd.emotions: Sadness, loneliness, and anxiety arise, accompanied by a deep sense of worthlessness and fear of abandonment.
ccd.behaviors: The patient stays quiet, avoids eye contact, and physically distances themselves from others. They may leave early or disengage mentally to protect against anticipated rejection.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be abandoned.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Just the usual stuff. Nothing too exciting, really. I’ve mostly kept to myself.
When you say you’ve mostly kept to yourself, what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you in those moments?
Um… I don’t know, I guess I just feel more comfortable that way. It can get a bit lonely sometimes, but it’s easier than dealing with…awkwardness. I just try not to think about it too much.
What kind of awkwardness do you find yourself trying to avoid, and what do you imagine might happen in those situations?

I guess I worry about saying the wrong thing, or that people might not really want me around. Sometimes I feel like I don’t fit in, so I’d rather just stay quiet. If I put myself out there, I’m scared it’ll just feel worse. Like I’ll end up being ignored or… not wanted.
When you notice those worries about not fitting in or being ignored, what goes through your mind about yourself in those moments?

Um… I guess I start thinking maybe there’s something wrong with me. Like, maybe I’m just not interesting or enough for people to really care. It makes me want to just disappear a bit, honestly. I kind of expect people to forget about me anyway.
That sounds really tough. When you have those thoughts about not being enough or wanting to disappear, how do you usually respond or cope with those feelings?

I usually just… go quiet, or kind of zone out. Sometimes I’ll leave early, or just keep to myself so nobody notices anything. I don’t really talk about it with anyone. I just try to act like everything’s fine.
What do you think might happen if you were to share some of these feelings with someone you trust?
I’m not sure. I guess I worry they wouldn’t really get it or they’d think I’m being dramatic. Maybe they’d just brush it off. It just feels safer to keep it to myself.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt powerless in my family and learned that standing up for myself only led to conflict or rejection. This has made me expect that I won’t be able to change difficult situations in my relationships.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to assert myself, others will get upset or leave me. It’s safer to keep others happy, even if it means ignoring my own needs. I am not strong enough to handle conflict or stand my ground.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to agree with others quickly, avoid expressing my true feelings, and go out of my way to please people to prevent arguments or rejection. Sometimes, I withdraw emotionally when I feel overwhelmed.
ccd.situation: My partner disagrees with a plan I suggested, and I immediately worry that I have upset them or that they might leave me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’ve messed things up again.” “If I don’t give in, they’ll be angry or leave.” “I’m just a victim in this and can’t change anything.”
ccd.emotions: Anxious, helpless, sad, and afraid of being abandoned.
ccd.behaviors: I quickly apologize and back down, trying to fix things by agreeing even if I don’t want to. I avoid bringing up my real opinions and try to keep the peace at all costs.

messages.content: How has your week been so far? Are there any moments or experiences that stood out to you?

Um, my week’s been okay, I think. Nothing major happened, really. I mean, there was a little disagreement with my partner about what to do this weekend, but it was fine—I probably made it bigger in my head than it was. Other than that, I’ve just been trying to keep up with things.
When you noticed yourself making the disagreement bigger in your head, what kinds of thoughts were going through your mind at that moment?

Oh, um, I guess I just started worrying that I’d messed things up somehow. I kept thinking they’d be annoyed with me, or maybe even regret being with me. I know it sounds silly, but I just really don’t want to upset anyone, especially people I care about. I sort of felt like it was my fault for suggesting something they didn’t want to do.
That sounds really tough—feeling responsible for how others might feel. When you had those worries about upsetting your partner, how did your body feel or how did you act in response?

Yeah, it is a bit uncomfortable. I remember feeling really tense, like my stomach was in knots and my chest felt tight. I apologized right away and told my partner it was totally fine to do whatever they wanted instead. Honestly, I just wanted the whole thing to blow over as soon as possible.
It makes sense that you wanted to smooth things over quickly. After you apologized and tried to move past it, did your thoughts or feelings about the situation change at all?

Um, not really, to be honest. I mean, my partner seemed fine after, but I still kept replaying it in my head, worrying that I was too pushy or something. I felt kind of silly for making a big deal out of it in my mind, but I couldn’t help it. I just hoped I hadn’t annoyed them too much.
When you found yourself replaying the situation and worrying afterward, what did you notice about how those thoughts affected your mood or energy?

I guess I felt kind of drained and down, like I just wanted to hide for a bit. It made me anxious, and I started doubting myself a lot—even about other little things. I was quieter for the rest of the day and tried not to bring up my own ideas much after that. I know it’s probably not that big a deal, but it sticks with me.

It sounds like those thoughts really weighed on you and influenced how you felt and acted. When you think about doubting yourself and holding back your ideas, what do you imagine might happen if you shared your thoughts more openly?

Hmm, I guess I just worry that if I say what I really think, it’ll cause more arguments or make people upset with me. I always sort of imagine the worst, like they’ll get frustrated or just decide it’s not worth it to be around me. I know that’s probably not always true, but it’s hard not to think that way. I don’t want to risk pushing anyone away.

ccd.relevant_history: I've faced several job losses in the past few years, which has made it really hard to feel stable financially. Every time I try to get ahead, something unexpected happens, and I end up stuck or falling behind again.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t have complete control over my money, I’ll never be safe. Asking for help feels like admitting I’ve failed and that I’m weak. No matter what I do, I can’t change my situation or make things better.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid looking at bills or bank statements because it stresses me out. Sometimes I distract myself with TV or social media to escape worrying. Other times, I get angry and lash out at those around me when I feel overwhelmed.
ccd.situation: I just found out my rent is going up next month, and I’m not sure how I’m going to afford it with my current income.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: I’m trapped and there’s no way out. I’ll never be able to pay this increase. This is just going to get worse, and I’m stuck in this mess forever.
ccd.emotions: I feel overwhelmed, scared, and helpless. It’s really upsetting to think about how little control I have over my finances.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid calling my landlord or looking for other housing options. I shut down and sometimes snap at friends or family when they ask how I’m doing. I also procrastinate on budgeting or planning because it feels hopeless.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Honestly, it's been pretty rough. I got a notice that my rent is going up, and I just… I don’t even want to deal with it. I don’t feel like talking about it, honestly. Every time I think about my finances, it just makes me angry.
That sounds really frustrating—when you notice that anger coming up about your finances, what thoughts tend to run through your mind?

I mean, what’s the point in thinking about it? It’s not like getting mad or worrying is going to fix anything. I just feel like, no matter what I do, nothing changes—it always gets worse somehow. It makes me want to just ignore the whole thing.
It sounds like you feel pretty stuck and maybe hopeless about the situation; when you imagine facing your finances instead of avoiding them, what feelings or fears come up for you?

I just feel panicked, honestly. Like, if I actually look at my bank account or my bills, I’ll realize I’m totally screwed and there’s no way out of it. It’s like this heavy weight in my chest, and I just want to run away from it. Facing it makes me feel stupid, like I should’ve been able to handle things better.
That panic and self-judgment sound really intense—when you notice that inner voice saying you should’ve handled things better, how do you usually respond to or cope with those thoughts?

Usually, I just shut down. I’ll zone out on my phone for hours or put on a show just to stop thinking. Sometimes I get really irritable and snap at people if they ask how I’m doing. I don’t really know how else to deal with it, honestly.

It makes sense that you’d want to escape those overwhelming feelings; when you’re zoning out or snapping at others, do you notice any shifts in how you feel afterward, or does the discomfort tend to stay the same?

Honestly, it doesn’t really help for long. Maybe I feel numb for a bit, but the stress is still there when I stop distracting myself. And if I’ve snapped at someone, I usually just feel guilty on top of everything else. So it just sort of builds up, I guess.
That cycle sounds really exhausting—when you think about times you’ve faced a stressful situation and managed it differently, what’s one small thing you did that felt at all helpful or manageable?

I don’t know… maybe once or twice I actually forced myself to look at my bills, even though I hated it. I guess it felt a little better, at least for a minute, just knowing what I was dealing with. But even then, it’s like, what’s the point if nothing really changes? It always feels like I’m just waiting for the next disaster.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

ccd.relevant_history: I've often felt like no matter what I do, people eventually drift away from me. Growing up, I noticed that showing my true feelings sometimes pushed others away, so I learned to keep people happy instead of being myself.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t keep everyone pleased, they’ll leave me. Being liked means sacrificing my own needs and opinions. I have to avoid conflict at all costs or I’ll end up alone.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to say yes to every request and avoid sharing anything that might upset others. I often suppress my own feelings and opinions to make sure I’m not causing any trouble. Sometimes I overwork myself to prove I’m valuable.
ccd.situation: At work, there’s a lot of pressure to meet deadlines, and my boss has been giving direct feedback. I worry that if I don’t respond perfectly and keep everyone happy, I’ll be isolated or disliked by my team.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "If I mess up or speak up, they’ll think I’m difficult and won’t want to work with me." "I’m probably annoying them without realizing it." "No one really cares about me here."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and loneliness. I feel tense and helpless, worried that I’m already being judged and rejected.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid expressing my true opinions in meetings and agree with others even when I disagree. I take on extra tasks to stay busy and keep people’s approval, often feeling drained and overwhelmed afterward.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, uh, it’s been okay, I think. Work’s been a little busy, but nothing I can’t handle. I guess I’m just trying to keep everything running smoothly. Sorry, I don’t want to complain—I know other people have it tougher.
It sounds like you’re juggling a lot right now; what kinds of thoughts go through your mind when things get busy at work?

Um… I guess I mostly just worry about making sure I don’t let anyone down. I try to double-check everything and make sure people are happy with what I’m doing. Sometimes I catch myself thinking I might be missing something or that maybe I annoyed someone without realizing it. But, I mean, it’s probably just me overthinking.
When you notice those worries about missing something or annoying someone, how do you usually respond to those thoughts or feelings?

I usually just try to work harder or be extra careful, you know? I’ll go over my emails or messages a bunch of times to make sure I didn’t say anything wrong. And if I think someone might be upset, I’ll try to check in with them, or just agree with whatever they need. I guess I just want to make sure everything’s okay between us.
That sounds like a lot of effort to keep things smooth—how do you feel emotionally after you’ve double-checked or checked in with someone?

Honestly, I usually feel kind of relieved for a little bit, like maybe I did the right thing. But then, sometimes I just get tired, or a bit on edge, like I have to keep being careful. I guess I worry that even small things could mess things up with people. Sorry, I probably sound silly.
It makes sense that you’d feel relieved and then tense again; when you notice yourself feeling tired or on edge, what do you tell yourself about those feelings?

Um, I usually just tell myself to keep it together and not make a big deal out of it. I feel like if I show I’m stressed or tired, it might bother others. So I just try to push it down and act normal. I don’t want anyone to think I’m being difficult or needy.
That sounds like a lot to carry on your own; what do you think might happen if you allowed yourself to express some of that stress or tiredness to someone you trust?

Oh, um, I don’t know… I worry they might think I’m complaining or being too much. I guess I’m afraid it would annoy them, or maybe they’d just want to avoid me. I’d rather just handle it myself than risk making things awkward. I don’t want anyone to feel burdened by me.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored and unimportant within my family. My achievements were rarely acknowledged, and mistakes were harshly criticized, which made me feel like I didn’t matter. This pattern has stayed with me into adulthood, especially during family interactions.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I speak up, I’ll be dismissed or rejected. I’m a burden to those around me. Nobody truly values me or wants me around. It’s safer to keep my feelings to myself and avoid drawing attention.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to withdraw during family gatherings and avoid sharing my thoughts or feelings. I try to stay quiet and blend into the background to avoid conflict or criticism. Sometimes, I distract myself with work or solitary activities to escape the discomfort.
ccd.situation: During a recent family dinner, when a disagreement arose, I stayed silent even though I had something to say because I felt like my opinion didn’t matter and that I would only make things worse.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one cares what I think.” “I’m just a nuisance.” “I don’t deserve to be here or to take up space.” “If I say something, they’ll push me away.”
ccd.emotions: I felt sad, hopeless, and deeply lonely. There was also a strong sense of shame and anxiety about being judged or rejected.
ccd.behaviors: I withdrew physically and emotionally, avoided eye contact, and kept my thoughts to myself. I left the room early to be alone and avoided future family events for a while.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Just the usual stuff. Nothing much to mention, really.
When you say "the usual stuff," can you tell me about a specific moment or event that stood out to you recently, even if it felt small?
I went to dinner with my family a few days ago. There was a bit of an argument about something, but I just kept quiet. Honestly, nothing else comes to mind.
What were you thinking or feeling during that argument when you chose to stay quiet?
I don’t know... I guess I just felt like it wasn’t worth saying anything. It’s easier to just let them talk. I didn’t really feel comfortable joining in.
What do you imagine might happen if you did share your thoughts during those moments?
I’m not sure. Maybe they wouldn’t care or they’d just ignore what I said. Sometimes I worry they might just get annoyed with me. It just seems safer not to say much.
When you notice those worries about being ignored or annoying others, what do you tell yourself about your own needs or feelings?
I don’t really think about my own needs much. I just try to keep things smooth and not cause trouble. Most of the time, I feel like what I want doesn’t really matter anyway.
How does it feel inside you when you put your own needs aside to keep things smooth?
It just feels kind of empty, I guess. Sometimes a bit sad, but I try not to dwell on it. Mostly I just feel tired.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has a long history of feeling undervalued and criticized at work, often believing they are not good enough despite positive feedback. They have struggled with low self-esteem since adolescence and have experienced several periods of intense stress related to job performance.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t work perfectly, I’m a failure. People will reject me if I show any weakness. I must always prove my worth through success, or I am nothing.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tries to avoid making mistakes by working excessively and double-checking every task, often isolating themselves to focus. They may push through exhaustion and avoid asking for help, fearing judgment or rejection.
ccd.situation: The patient is facing a tight deadline at work with high expectations from their manager, causing intense pressure to perform flawlessly.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m going to mess this up and everyone will see I’m useless." "I don’t deserve to be here or to keep my job." "If I fail, it means I’m worthless."
ccd.emotions: Overwhelming anxiety, deep sadness, shame, and feelings of hopelessness.
ccd.behaviors: Working late into the night, withdrawing from colleagues, avoiding breaks, and ruminating on perceived mistakes. They may also neglect self-care and avoid talking about their struggles.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

It’s been kind of tough, to be honest. There’s a lot going on at work right now, and I feel like I'm barely keeping up. I haven’t really slept well this week either. I guess I just feel pretty worn out.
When you notice yourself feeling worn out and struggling to keep up at work, what kinds of thoughts typically go through your mind?

Mostly I just keep thinking that I'm not doing enough, or that I’m probably messing something up. I worry that people are going to notice if I slip up, and then they’ll think I can’t handle things. Sometimes I just feel like I don’t belong there at all.
When you have those thoughts about not doing enough or not belonging, how does that affect the way you behave or cope during the workday?

I end up working longer hours than everyone else, just to make sure everything is perfect. I hardly take breaks, and I pretty much keep to myself so no one can see if I’m struggling. I double-check everything over and over. I guess I just try to stay invisible and avoid making any mistakes.
What do you notice happens to your feelings or energy levels after working those long hours and trying to stay invisible?

I usually feel completely drained, like there’s nothing left in me. Sometimes I get really down, and it’s hard to shake off that heaviness. I also start to feel kind of hopeless, like no matter how much I do, it’s never enough. It just gets harder to find any motivation.
When you start feeling that heaviness and hopelessness, what do you typically do next—do you keep pushing through, take a break, or something else?

Most of the time I just keep pushing through, even if I’m exhausted. Taking a break feels almost impossible, like I don’t deserve it unless everything’s done perfectly. I usually just keep working or go home and lay in bed, but I can’t really relax. It’s like I’m stuck in this cycle and don’t know how to stop.
What would it feel like or mean to you if you allowed yourself to take a break before everything was perfect?

Honestly, it makes me feel really anxious just thinking about it. I’d probably worry that people would see me as lazy or not committed. Part of me believes I’d be letting everyone down. It just feels safer to keep going, even when I’m exhausted.

ccd.relevant_history: The client has a history of struggling with self-esteem and has often felt judged by family members for past mistakes. Financial difficulties have increased recently, leading to heightened stress and feelings of inadequacy.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I can't manage my money well, it means I'm a bad person." "People will think less of me because I’m irresponsible." "I have to hide my financial problems to avoid being rejected."
ccd.coping_strategies: The client tends to avoid discussing financial issues with others and may procrastinate on addressing bills or budgeting. They sometimes distract themselves with binge-watching TV or overeating to escape uncomfortable feelings.
ccd.situation: The client received a past due notice on a credit card bill and is worried about how this will affect their reputation and relationships.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I'm such a failure for letting this happen." "I’m immoral because I can’t take care of my responsibilities." "Everyone will think I’m a terrible person."
ccd.emotions: Upset, anxious, ashamed, hopeless.
ccd.behaviors: Avoiding calls from creditors, isolating from friends and family, neglecting to open or sort through mail related to finances.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Honestly, not great. I’ve just been kind of overwhelmed with stuff, and it feels like things keep piling up. I don’t really want to get into it right now. It just sucks, that’s all.
I’m sorry you’re feeling that way. When you say it sucks, what kind of thoughts or feelings come up for you in those moments?

I don’t know, it’s just this constant stress, like I can’t get anything right. I keep messing things up and it’s exhausting. Sometimes I wonder if there’s even a point in trying to fix things anymore. It’s just... a lot.
That sounds really tough. When you notice those thoughts about not being able to get things right or questioning if it’s worth trying, what do you usually do next?

I usually just shut down, honestly. I’ll put on some show and try not to think about it. Sometimes I just ignore stuff I should be dealing with, like bills or messages. It’s easier to pretend it’s not there for a while.
Avoiding those tasks might give some relief temporarily—how do you feel afterward, once the distraction or avoidance has passed?
Honestly, worse. It’s like this wave of guilt hits me and I just feel even more useless than before. Then the anxiety comes back, even stronger. I start thinking I’m never going to get it together.
When that wave of guilt and anxiety hits, what do you say to yourself in those moments?

I usually just beat myself up, like, “How could you be so stupid?” or “Why can’t you just handle this like everyone else?” It’s always something along those lines. I just feel like I’m failing at being a decent person. It gets pretty harsh in my head.
Those are really harsh messages to give yourself. Have you noticed any times when you’ve been able to say something kinder or more understanding to yourself instead?

I don’t know. It’s hard to even think like that, honestly. Most of the time I just can’t shake the feeling that I don’t deserve any kindness, especially from myself. I guess sometimes I’ll try to tell myself everyone messes up, but it never really sticks.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated challenges at work, often feeling overwhelmed by expectations and perceiving themselves as unable to meet standards. Past experiences of criticism from supervisors have reinforced feelings of inadequacy and failure.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "I must not make mistakes or I’ll prove I’m worthless." "If I don’t succeed immediately, I’m a complete failure." "Others are always judging me negatively."
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to withdraw and avoid taking on new tasks to minimize risk of failure. They also ruminate silently about perceived shortcomings and avoid discussing struggles with colleagues or supervisors.
ccd.situation: Facing a tight deadline at work with multiple competing demands and pressure from management to deliver flawless results.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I'm going to mess this up like I always do." "Everyone will think I’m useless." "I can’t handle this; I’m just not good enough."
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, and a deep sense of hopelessness.
ccd.behaviors: The patient becomes quiet and reserved, avoids asking for help, procrastinates on key tasks, and isolates themselves from coworkers.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Work’s been kind of busy. Nothing too exciting. I just tried to get through it.
When you say you just tried to get through it, what kind of thoughts were going through your mind during those busy moments?
Um... I don’t know, really. I just kept thinking I needed to finish everything and not mess up. I guess I was kind of worried about not doing things right. It just felt like a lot sometimes.
What usually goes through your mind when you start feeling worried about not doing things right?

I just start thinking that I’m probably going to screw something up. Or like, people will notice I can’t handle it. Sometimes I worry they’ll think I don’t belong there. It sort of makes me want to just keep my head down and not draw attention.
When you imagine others noticing or thinking you don’t belong, how does that affect what you do or how you feel in those moments?
It makes me feel pretty anxious. I usually just get quieter and try not to say much. I don’t really ask for help, even if I’m struggling. I just hope I can get through it without anyone noticing.
What do you think might happen if you did ask for help when you’re struggling?

I don’t know… I guess I worry they’ll think I should know what I’m doing already. Or maybe they’ll get annoyed, or see me as a burden. I just don’t want to look incompetent. So, I kind of just keep things to myself.
When you picture someone getting annoyed or seeing you as a burden, what evidence do you have that this would actually happen?

I’m not sure. I mean, there have been a few times in the past where my boss sounded frustrated if I asked too many questions. And once someone sort of sighed when I needed help. I just remember those things. I guess it sticks with me.

ccd.relevant_history: I often feel like no matter what I do, I can’t influence how others treat me or how situations unfold in my relationships. Growing up, I was frequently told to keep the peace and not cause trouble, which made me feel powerless and dependent on others’ approval.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t keep others happy, they will reject me. I can’t change what’s happening, so I have to just go along with whatever others want. Expressing my true feelings will only make things worse or push people away.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to say yes to everything and avoid conflict, even when it makes me uncomfortable. I over-apologize and suppress my own needs to avoid upsetting others. I often second-guess myself and hesitate to speak up.
ccd.situation: My partner didn’t ask for my opinion about weekend plans again, and I felt like my thoughts didn’t matter in the relationship.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I have no say in this. They don’t care what I want.” “If I speak up, I’ll just cause problems.” “I’m powerless to change anything.”
ccd.emotions: Helplessness, sadness, anxiety, and frustration.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet and agree with the plans even though I’m upset. I avoid bringing up my feelings and try to keep the peace, even if it means feeling invisible.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Oh, um… it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too unusual. I’ve just been trying to keep up with everything at work and at home. Thanks for asking.
When you think about managing everything at work and home, what thoughts come to mind?

I just, um… I guess I keep thinking I need to make sure everyone is happy with me. It’s important to me that things go smoothly, so I try not to cause any issues. Sometimes I worry that if I mess up or say the wrong thing, people might get upset. I try not to overthink it, though.
What do you notice in your body or emotions when those worries about messing up or upsetting others come up?

Oh, well… I guess I just feel a little tense, maybe kind of nervous? My shoulders get tight, and sometimes my stomach feels off. I try to brush it off and just act normal, but inside I feel… I don’t know, a bit anxious, I guess. Sorry, I know that sounds silly.
It doesn’t sound silly at all; feeling anxious in those moments makes a lot of sense. When you notice that tension and anxiety, how do you usually respond or cope with those feelings?

Thank you, that’s kind of you to say. Um, mostly I just try to go along with whatever’s happening and not make a big deal out of things. I’ll usually just smile and agree, even if I’m a bit uncomfortable. I guess I just don’t want to cause any trouble.
When you choose to smile and agree despite feeling uncomfortable, what impact does that have on how you feel afterward?

Honestly, sometimes I feel sort of invisible afterward, like my opinion doesn’t really matter. But I tell myself it’s better than making things awkward or tense. I do feel a bit sad sometimes, but at least no one’s upset with me. I guess I just try to move on and not think about it too much.
That sounds like a tough balance to maintain. When you notice those feelings of sadness or invisibility, what do you wish you could say or do in those moments instead?

I don’t know…I sometimes wish I could just say how I’m really feeling, or at least ask for what I want. But I worry it’ll just make things worse or make people annoyed with me. Part of me wants to be more honest, but it’s hard. I’m not really sure how to start.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt neglected and criticized by my family, which made me believe that I wasn’t good enough to be loved. Experiences of rejection during family gatherings reinforced this feeling that I don’t belong.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I show my true self, people will push me away. I have to keep my distance to avoid being hurt. Trying to connect with family only leads to disappointment.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid family events or keep conversations superficial to protect myself. Sometimes, I withdraw emotionally and avoid expressing my feelings to prevent potential rejection.
ccd.situation: During a recent family dinner, I noticed my uncle didn’t acknowledge my achievement, and my relatives seemed distant, which made me feel isolated.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They don’t care about me,” “I’m going to be rejected again,” “I’m not worth their attention.”
ccd.emotions: I felt sad, lonely, and anxious throughout the evening.
ccd.behaviors: I stayed quiet, avoided eye contact, and left early to avoid further discomfort.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Nothing too exciting happened. I went to a family dinner a couple of days ago, but I didn’t stay long. I just felt a bit out of place.
When you noticed feeling out of place at the family dinner, what kinds of thoughts were going through your mind?
I kept thinking people didn’t really want me there. It felt like I was just… in the way. Honestly, I wondered if anyone would have even noticed if I hadn't shown up.
What feelings did those thoughts bring up for you in that moment?
Mostly I just felt a mix of sadness and anxiety. I kind of felt really small, like I wanted to disappear. There was this heavy feeling in my chest the whole time.
When you felt that heaviness in your chest and the urge to disappear, how did you respond or what did you do next?

I just got really quiet and tried not to draw attention to myself. I avoided looking at people and kept my answers short if anyone talked to me. Eventually, I found an excuse to leave early. I just couldn’t handle being there much longer.
Looking back on that evening, is there anything you might notice about how your thoughts, feelings, and actions connected or influenced each other?

Yeah, now that I think about it, I guess the more I thought people didn’t care, the worse I felt. That made me pull back and sort of shut down. And then, because I was so quiet, no one really talked to me, which made me feel even more out of place. It’s like a loop I can’t get out of.
That sounds like a challenging cycle. Have you noticed this kind of pattern in other situations as well?

Yeah, it happens a lot, especially with my family. Even in other groups, like at work or with friends, I sometimes catch myself holding back or not saying much. I just assume people aren’t that interested in having me around. It’s hard to break out of it.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and like my needs were a burden to others. I learned early on that asking for help was seen as weakness, so I usually kept things to myself, which left me feeling isolated and unsupported.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I ask for help, people will think I’m weak or annoying. I have to manage on my own because no one will really be there for me. Being needy means I’m a burden, so it’s safer to hide my struggles.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid reaching out to others even when I’m overwhelmed. Sometimes I distract myself with work or TV to ignore how alone I feel. Other times, I push myself to do everything myself, even when it’s exhausting.
ccd.situation: I had a stressful day at work and felt overwhelmed with tasks piling up. When I thought about asking my partner for help, my mind wandered to all the times they seemed irritated when I needed support.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I ask for help, they’ll think I’m weak or annoying. I shouldn’t bother them with my problems. I’m just needy and can’t handle things on my own.”
ccd.emotions: Frustration, loneliness, anxiety, and a deep sense of sadness about being a burden.
ccd.behaviors: I shut down and avoid talking about my struggles, trying to handle everything alone. I may withdraw from my partner and isolate myself, making it harder to get the support I need.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been kind of a blur, honestly. Work’s been hectic, and I’ve just been trying to keep up, you know? Oh, and my cat knocked over my coffee yesterday morning—I swear, she’s on a mission to ruin all my clean shirts. Sorry, that’s not really what you asked.
It sounds like things have felt a bit overwhelming and maybe a little chaotic. What was going through your mind when your cat knocked over your coffee?

Oh, I mean, at first I was just annoyed—it was early, I was already running late, and then there’s coffee everywhere. I just thought, “Great, one more thing to deal with.” But, uh, I guess it also made me remember last week when my partner got kind of irritated that I snapped at the cat. Then I started worrying I was making too big a deal out of nothing, and maybe I just need to keep it together better.
When you noticed yourself worrying about how you reacted, what feelings or thoughts came up for you about yourself in that moment?

I guess I just started feeling kind of…embarrassed? Like, “Why can’t I just handle these little things without freaking out?” And then I started thinking, maybe I’m just too much sometimes, or maybe I shouldn’t even say anything when I’m stressed. Sorry—uh, now I’m thinking about how my partner always seems so calm, and I wonder if I’m just not as good at dealing with stuff.
That comparison to your partner sounds pretty tough. When you notice those thoughts about not being as good at handling stress, what do you usually do next?

Honestly, I usually just get quiet. I’ll sort of disappear into my phone or just start cleaning up or something, so I don’t have to talk about it. Sometimes I’ll put on a show and zone out for a while, just to push it all away. Oh, and then I end up forgetting what I even needed to get done in the first place.
It sounds like zoning out helps you avoid those difficult feelings for a bit. When you come back to the tasks or feelings you were trying to push away, what usually happens?

Usually, everything just feels even heavier, honestly. Like, I come back to my to-do list and it’s even longer or I feel even more behind. Sometimes I think about reaching out to my partner, but then I remember times when it felt like I was bothering them. Actually, last month there was this one night where I tried to talk about how tired I was, and—I’m rambling, sorry.
It sounds like reaching out feels risky because of how it’s been received before. What was going through your mind during that night when you tried to share how tired you felt?

Yeah, I just remember feeling…hesitant, I guess, like I was walking on eggshells. I kept thinking, “What if they get annoyed again? What if I’m just making things harder for them?” So I tried to keep it light, but inside I felt so drained and just wanted someone to say it was okay to feel that way. And the weird thing is, even after that, I felt kind of silly for even bringing it up at all.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt like I’m not good enough in my relationships, and this feeling has been with me since I was a kid. Whenever someone gets upset with me, I immediately think it’s because I’ve messed up or don’t matter. I tend to avoid conflict and try really hard to make others happy, even if it means ignoring my own needs.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t keep everyone happy, they’ll leave me or think less of me. I have to put others first because my feelings aren’t important. Showing my true self might make people reject me, so it’s safer to just please them.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to agree with others no matter what, even if I don’t feel the same way. I avoid speaking up or expressing disagreement because I’m scared it will cause problems. Sometimes I push my own feelings aside just to keep the peace.
ccd.situation: My partner seemed upset after I didn’t respond quickly to their message, and I immediately worried that they were mad at me and might want to leave.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I must have done something wrong. They probably think I’m useless. I don’t deserve their love or attention.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and shame wash over me, making me feel small and unworthy.
ccd.behaviors: I apologize repeatedly, try to fix the situation by being extra nice, and avoid bringing up my own feelings or concerns so I don’t upset them further.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too out of the ordinary. I’ve just been trying to keep up with everything at work and make sure I’m not letting anyone down. Sorry, I know that sounds a bit vague—I guess nothing major happened.
It sounds like you’ve been feeling some pressure to meet expectations at work; what thoughts go through your mind when you worry about letting people down?

Yeah, I guess I do worry about that a lot. I just feel like if I mess up or don’t do things perfectly, people might get frustrated with me or think I’m not good enough. Sometimes I find myself going over little things I said or did, just to make sure I didn’t upset anyone. I know it’s probably silly, but I can’t help it.
That sounds really stressful to carry around. When you replay those moments in your mind, what feelings tend to come up for you?

Um, I guess I mostly just feel anxious, like there’s this knot in my stomach that won’t go away. Sometimes I feel kind of sad, too, like I’ve let people down even if I didn’t mean to. I get a bit embarrassed about it, honestly. Sorry, I hope that makes sense.
That makes a lot of sense, and it’s okay to feel that way. When you notice that knot of anxiety or the sadness, what do you usually do to cope or respond in those moments?

Oh, um, I usually just try to fix things right away—like I’ll apologize a lot or try to make up for it somehow, even if it wasn’t a big deal. I also tend to keep my feelings to myself so I don’t make things awkward for anyone else. I just really want everyone to be okay with me. Sorry, I know I probably worry too much.
It sounds like you put a lot of effort into smoothing things over for others. How do you feel after you apologize or try to fix the situation—does it usually help ease your anxiety or sadness?

Honestly, sometimes it helps a bit for a while, like I can breathe easier if they seem okay with me again. But then I start worrying if I was annoying for apologizing too much, or if they still might be upset and just not saying it. So, I guess it’s like a temporary relief, but the worry doesn’t really go away for long. I usually just try to push those feelings aside and move on.
It sounds like the relief you get is short-lived, and the worry keeps coming back. When you try to push those feelings aside, what do you notice happens to your anxiety or mood afterward?

Um, I guess the anxiety kind of lingers in the background, even if I try not to think about it. Sometimes it just makes me feel tired or a bit low, like there’s always something I need to fix or make better. I try not to show it, though—I don’t want to bother anyone with my feelings. Sorry, I know that probably sounds a bit much.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored by my family and was rarely praised or comforted, which made me feel like I wasn’t worth loving. Past relationships ended with me feeling rejected, reinforcing the idea that I am unwanted.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to get close to people, I will just end up being rejected or abandoned. I have to hide my true self to avoid being disliked or left alone.
ccd.coping_strategies: I keep my distance from others and avoid opening up because I’m afraid of being hurt. Sometimes I overcompensate by trying too hard to please people, hoping they will accept me.
