ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one will want me there. I’ll just be ignored or left out like always. I’m not interesting or worth their time.”
ccd.emotions: I feel anxious, lonely, and sad. There’s also a quiet sense of hopelessness and shame.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid signing up or going to the group. If I do go, I stay quiet and keep to myself, making sure not to draw attention.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting happened. I mostly just went to work and stayed in. I thought about maybe trying out this art class, but I haven’t actually signed up yet.
What thoughts have been coming up for you when you think about signing up for that art class?

I keep wondering if I’d even fit in there, or if people would just ignore me. It’s like, what if I show up and nobody really wants me around? I just picture myself sitting alone while everyone else talks to each other. That kind of makes me not want to bother.
When those thoughts about not fitting in or being ignored come up, how do they make you feel in your body?
I feel tense—like my shoulders get all tight and my chest feels heavy. Sometimes my stomach kind of knots up. It’s almost like I want to shrink away or disappear. It’s pretty uncomfortable, honestly.
What do you usually do or avoid doing when you notice those uncomfortable feelings and thoughts?

Most of the time, I just end up not going at all. I tell myself it’s fine and I probably wouldn’t have enjoyed it anyway. If I do force myself to go, I stay quiet and keep to the side. I try not to draw attention, just in case.
How does avoiding or staying quiet in those situations affect how you feel afterward?

Afterwards, I usually feel kind of disappointed in myself. There’s this mix of relief that I didn’t embarrass myself, but also regret for not giving it a real chance. Sometimes I feel more alone than before. It’s like I missed out, but I also don’t know how to do it differently.
When you think about giving it a real chance, what would you consider a small step that feels manageable for you?

I guess maybe just signing up for the class, without putting pressure on myself to talk a lot or make friends right away, might be doable. Or maybe I could show up and just say hi to one person, even if it’s brief. That sounds scary, but not impossible. I think starting small might help me not feel so overwhelmed.

ccd.relevant_history: I've always felt like I don't quite fit in, especially when it comes to relationships or making friends. Growing up, I was often teased about my looks, and those experiences stuck with me, making me doubt my worth.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I’m not attractive, then people won’t want to be close to me. I have to hide my true self to avoid rejection. It’s safer not to try too hard because I’m bound to be overlooked or ignored.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid social gatherings or situations where I might be judged. Sometimes I distract myself with work or binge-watch shows to escape these feelings. Other times, I try to overcompensate by being overly nice or funny, hoping others will like me despite how I feel inside.
ccd.situation: I was at a small party where I didn’t know many people, and I noticed others seemed to be pairing off or chatting easily. I felt invisible and like everyone was looking past me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “Everyone’s probably thinking I’m ugly or boring. Why would anyone want to talk to me? I don’t belong here.”
ccd.emotions: Lonely, anxious, ashamed, and sad.
ccd.behaviors: I stayed quiet in a corner, avoided eye contact, and eventually left the party early to avoid feeling worse.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Uh, it’s been alright, I guess. Work’s been kind of busy, so I’ve just been trying to keep up with everything. Oh, actually, I saw this squirrel outside my window yesterday—it was carrying this giant piece of bread, like way too big for it. Made me laugh for a second, which was nice.
That sounds like a small but positive moment—what was going through your mind when you saw the squirrel carrying that big piece of bread?

Yeah, it was kind of cute, honestly. I just thought it was funny how determined the squirrel was, like, it didn’t care that the bread was way too big. For a second I sort of envied that—just, you know, going after something even if it seems ridiculous. Then I got distracted remembering I had a ton of emails to reply to, so I didn’t really think much more about it.
When you thought about the squirrel’s determination and then shifted to your emails, what feelings or thoughts came up for you about your own tasks?

Hmm, I guess I just felt kind of… tired? Like, sometimes I wish I could be that carefree about things instead of always worrying if I’m doing stuff right. Speaking of emails, I actually got one from my old high school the other day about a reunion—totally random. Made me think about people I haven’t talked to in years and whether I’d even want to go.
What thoughts or worries come up for you when you consider the idea of going to that reunion or reconnecting with those people?

Honestly, it’s kind of stressful to even imagine going. I just picture myself standing there not knowing what to say or feeling awkward—like I’d be the odd one out or something. It reminds me of how it felt back in school, you know, like everyone else just sort of fit together and I was on the outside. Oh, and I remembered that one time in gym class where I tripped during dodgeball and everyone laughed… not my favorite memory.
That sounds like a really tough memory to carry—how do you usually cope with those feelings of awkwardness or being on the outside when they come up?

Um, I guess I try to just keep myself busy most of the time, like with work or little things around the house. Sometimes I’ll just put on a show or a movie I’ve seen before, so I don’t have to think too much. Or I’ll just scroll through my phone until I get sleepy. Oh, and I volunteer to help with stuff at work a lot, probably more than I should, just to fill up my schedule.
It sounds like staying busy helps you manage those uncomfortable feelings—what happens inside for you when you slow down or have a quiet moment to yourself?

Oh, I don’t really like it when things get quiet, honestly. That’s usually when my mind starts going over all the weird or embarrassing stuff I’ve done, or I start worrying that people don’t really like me. It’s like, I can’t really turn it off. Actually, sometimes I’ll start cleaning even if it doesn’t need it, just so I have something to do.

ccd.relevant_history: I’ve often felt overlooked or dismissed in past relationships, which has made me believe that I can’t stand up for myself or make a difference. Growing up, I was taught to keep peace by avoiding conflict, so I learned to put others' needs before my own to feel safe.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I speak up or assert myself, people will reject or abandon me. It’s safer to keep my feelings hidden and try to please others so I don’t cause problems. I have to be accommodating to avoid being alone or unloved.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to agree with others even when I don’t want to, hoping they’ll like me more. I avoid expressing my true feelings and needs to prevent conflict. Sometimes I over-apologize or take blame to keep the peace.
ccd.situation: My partner disagrees with me about how to spend the weekend, and I feel unsure about sharing my own preferences because I don’t want to upset them or cause an argument.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I say what I really want, they’ll get mad and leave me.” “I’m not strong enough to handle disagreement.” “It’s better if I just go along with whatever they want.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, fear of rejection, sadness, feeling small and insignificant.
ccd.behaviors: I stay silent or give in quickly, putting their wishes before mine. I avoid bringing up my feelings and try to keep the mood light to prevent tension.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting happened. I tried to keep busy and just go with the flow, you know? I hope that’s alright.
It sounds like you were trying to manage things by staying busy—what kinds of thoughts were going through your mind as you went about your week?

Well, I guess I mostly just wanted to make sure everyone around me was okay. I kept thinking, like, if I do what people expect, things will stay calm. Honestly, I didn’t really focus on what I wanted much. I’m not sure it’s that important anyway.
When you think about putting others’ needs first and not focusing on what you want, how does that make you feel inside?

Oh, um… I’m not really sure. I mean, I guess it’s just normal for me. Sometimes I feel a little tired or maybe kind of invisible, but it’s not a big deal. I just don’t want to make things difficult for anyone.
That sounds like a lot to carry quietly—what do you notice happens inside you when you feel tired or invisible in those moments?

Yeah, I suppose it does feel a bit heavy sometimes. When I feel like that, I usually just try to brush it off and not think about it too much. Honestly, I start worrying that if I say anything, people might get annoyed or pull away, so I just keep things to myself. I guess I just hope it’ll pass if I don’t make a fuss.
It sounds like you’re concerned about how others might react if you share your feelings—what do you imagine might happen if you did express how you’re feeling to someone you trust?

Um… I think I worry they might not understand, or maybe they’d think I’m being too sensitive. Sometimes I’m afraid they’d get upset with me, or just kind of back away. I really don’t want to be a burden or cause any trouble. So, I usually just smile and act like everything’s fine.
When you imagine those reactions from others, what thoughts go through your mind about yourself in those moments?

Well, I guess I just think maybe I’m making too big a deal out of things. Like, maybe I should just be stronger or not let things get to me. Sometimes I feel like I’m just… not as important as other people. I know that sounds silly, sorry.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

ccd.relevant_history: I often felt ignored or dismissed by people I cared about when I was younger, which made me believe that I’m not worth their time. Over the years, these experiences have built up a sense that I’m likely to be rejected no matter what I do.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I let people get close, they’ll eventually leave or reject me. It’s safer to expect rejection so I’m not caught off guard. Trying too hard to be liked just sets me up for disappointment.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to keep my distance from others or avoid opening up because it feels like the safest way to protect myself from getting hurt. Sometimes I try to please people excessively to avoid being rejected, but it often feels exhausting and doesn’t work.
ccd.situation: I invited a coworker to grab lunch, but they said they were busy and didn’t suggest another time. I started thinking about what that might mean about how they see me.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They probably don’t want to spend time with me because I’m not interesting or likable. I’m just going to be rejected again.”
ccd.emotions: I feel sad, lonely, and anxious, like I’m not good enough to be accepted or cared for.
ccd.behaviors: I avoid reaching out to others after that and keep to myself during breaks, convincing myself it’s safer not to get close.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be rejected.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been okay, I guess. Work has been a little stressful, and I’ve just been keeping to myself mostly. Nothing major happened, just the usual stuff.
When you say work has been stressful, can you tell me more about what specific situations or thoughts come up for you during the workday?

Well, there was this one thing—I asked a coworker if they wanted to have lunch, and they just said they were busy. After that, I couldn’t stop wondering if maybe they just didn’t want to hang out with me. I kept thinking about it all day. It kind of made me feel like I shouldn’t bother reaching out to people.
What do you think that response from your coworker might mean about you or your relationship with them?

I guess my first thought is that they probably didn’t want to spend time with me. Maybe I’m not interesting or just not someone people want to get to know. It makes me feel like I’m always going to be on the outside. I try to brush it off, but it sticks with me longer than I want it to.
When you notice those thoughts about not being interesting or being on the outside, how do they affect what you do or how you interact with others afterward?

Honestly, I just kind of shut down. I stop trying to talk to people and keep to myself more. It feels easier to just avoid reaching out so I don’t have to deal with feeling unwanted. I know it probably makes things worse, but it’s hard to do anything else.
What do you imagine might happen if you did reach out to someone despite feeling that way—how do you think they would respond?

I always think they’ll just turn me down or won’t seem interested. Or maybe they’ll say yes out of pity, which honestly feels almost as bad. I guess I just assume it won’t go well. It’s hard to picture someone actually wanting to spend time with me.
Have you ever had an experience where reaching out led to a positive or neutral outcome, even if it was small or brief?

Yeah, I guess there have been a few times where someone said yes and it was fine, nothing bad happened. But even then, I still worry that maybe they were just being polite or didn’t really mean it. It’s hard for me to believe that anyone would genuinely want to spend time with me. Those doubts just kind of stick around, even after things go okay.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated setbacks in personal and professional development, often feeling overlooked and unsupported. Past attempts at growth were met with external obstacles and internal doubts, reinforcing a sense of powerlessness. Family dynamics emphasized compliance and minimized personal ambitions, contributing to feelings of being trapped.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I try to change, I will just get hurt or fail again. There’s no point in fighting against things I can’t control. People are more likely to take advantage of me if I show my true needs. Staying quiet keeps me safe from disappointment.
ccd.coping_strategies: Withdrawing from new opportunities to avoid potential failure, staying silent in group settings, and relying on routine to maintain a sense of control. The patient tends to avoid asserting themselves or seeking help, preferring to minimize visibility to reduce risk.
ccd.situation: Facing a chance to take on a leadership role at work that could lead to growth but also carries risk and increased visibility among colleagues.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “If I try to lead, I’ll just mess up and everyone will see how incapable I am.” “I’m not strong enough to handle the pressure.” “Others will blame me if things go wrong.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and a deep sense of defeat. The patient feels overwhelmed and vulnerable, with a creeping sense of hopelessness about their ability to succeed.
ccd.behaviors: Declining the leadership opportunity, avoiding conversations about career advancement, and withdrawing from social interactions at work to avoid scrutiny. The patient may also ruminate on past failures, reinforcing their sense of helplessness.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It's been okay, I guess. Nothing really out of the ordinary happened. Work was the usual. I mostly just kept to myself.
When you say you kept to yourself, what thoughts or feelings were going through your mind in those moments?

I guess I just didn’t feel like talking much. Sometimes it’s easier to just focus on what I have to do and not get involved. I don’t really know what to say most of the time anyway. I just feel kind of... tired, I guess.
When you notice that tired feeling, what do you think it means or tells you about yourself?

I don’t know, maybe it just means I’m not cut out for all the stuff other people seem to handle. Like, maybe I should just stick to what I know and not try too hard. Trying just feels exhausting sometimes. It doesn’t really seem worth it most days.
What do you think might happen if you did try a little more, even when it feels exhausting?
Honestly, I worry that I’d just end up messing things up. People would probably notice and think less of me. I don't like that idea. It feels safer to just do what I usually do.
When you imagine others thinking less of you, what kind of thoughts or images come to mind?
I picture people looking disappointed, or maybe even annoyed with me. Like, shaking their heads or whispering. I just feel small, like I don’t really belong there. It’s not a good feeling.
Have you noticed any times when you’ve tried something new or different and the outcome was better than you expected?

I can’t really think of any, to be honest. Usually when I try something new, it just feels uncomfortable and I end up wishing I hadn’t. I guess I remember small things going okay, but nothing major stands out. Most of the time I just end up second-guessing myself.

core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated rejection and criticism in past relationships, leading to a deep sense of being unlovable and inherently bad. They often felt blamed for conflicts and learned to avoid expressing true feelings to keep others from leaving.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I must always be agreeable and put others first to avoid being rejected or abandoned. If I show my real self, people will see how dangerous and toxic I am and push me away. It’s safer to hide my true feelings and needs.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tries to please others constantly by agreeing with them and suppressing their own opinions or desires. They avoid confrontation and may apologize excessively to keep the peace, even when hurt or upset.
ccd.situation: In a recent interaction with a close friend, the patient noticed the friend seemed distant and less responsive, making the patient worry that they had done something wrong or were being rejected.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I must have upset them because I’m bad.” “They probably think I’m toxic and want to stop talking to me.” “If I don’t say what they want to hear, they’ll leave me.”

ccd.behaviors: The patient withdraws emotionally, tries harder to please the friend by agreeing with everything, and avoids sharing their true feelings to prevent conflict or rejection.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting—just the usual stuff. I’ve been keeping busy with work and trying to catch up with friends a bit. Sorry, I don’t want to complain or anything!
It sounds like you’ve been managing a lot—what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you during your typical day?

Oh, I don’t know… I guess I just try to keep things going, you know? Sometimes I worry a bit about saying the wrong thing or annoying people, but it’s probably just me overthinking. Most of the time I just try to stay positive and not bother anyone. Sorry, is that what you meant?
That makes sense—when you notice yourself worrying about saying the wrong thing, what usually goes through your mind in those moments?

Um… I guess I start to feel like maybe I’ve messed something up or that people won’t want to be around me. I try to figure out what I might have said or done wrong, even if it’s something small. I just don’t want anyone to get upset with me. I hope that doesn’t sound silly.
That sounds really tough to carry around—how do these thoughts and feelings affect what you do next in those situations?

I usually just try to make sure I’m being extra nice or agreeable, so things don’t get worse. If I think I upset someone, I’ll apologize a lot, even if I’m not sure what I did. Sometimes I just stay quiet or avoid sharing how I really feel. I just really don’t want anyone to get mad at me or leave.
It sounds like you put a lot of effort into keeping the peace—when you hold back your true feelings or stay quiet, what impact does that have on you personally?

Um… I guess it can feel a little lonely sometimes. Like, I’m not really being myself, but I don’t want to make things difficult for anyone. Sometimes I feel a bit sad or left out, but I just try to ignore it. I’d rather keep things calm than risk upsetting someone.
That sounds really challenging to balance—when you notice those feelings of loneliness or sadness, what do you tell yourself about having those emotions?

Honestly, I usually just tell myself that I shouldn’t make a big deal out of it. Other people have bigger problems, so I feel like I should just handle it on my own. I worry that if I show I’m upset, people will think I’m being difficult or dramatic. So I just try to push those feelings down and focus on making things better for everyone else.

ccd.relevant_history: I've always felt like I was a burden to my family and friends, even when they try to show me they care. Growing up, I didn’t get much encouragement, and I often felt invisible or like I was failing everyone around me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t meet everyone’s expectations perfectly, it means I’m a failure. People only stick around if you’re useful or successful. If I’m not worth anything, then I have no right to be happy or even exist.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to isolate myself and avoid asking for help because I feel like I’ll just be rejected or make things worse. Sometimes I distract myself with TV or scrolling on my phone to avoid thinking about how bad I feel.
ccd.situation: I missed a deadline at work because I was feeling overwhelmed, and my boss pointed it out in front of the team. I felt like I completely let everyone down.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I’m such a failure. I don’t deserve to be here or to keep my job. Everyone must think I’m useless."
ccd.emotions: I felt deeply ashamed, hopeless, and anxious. There was also a heavy sadness that made me want to disappear.
ccd.behaviors: I avoided talking to my boss and coworkers afterward, didn’t respond to messages, and considered calling in sick the next day to avoid facing anyone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been kind of rough, honestly. Work has been stressful, and I messed something up. I just keep thinking about it. I guess I’m having a hard time letting it go.
What kinds of thoughts go through your mind when you think about that mistake you made at work?
I just keep thinking that I screwed everything up. Like, everyone probably thinks I’m incompetent now. I feel like I let people down, and maybe I don’t even belong there. It’s a pretty awful feeling.
When you notice these thoughts about being incompetent or not belonging, how do they affect your mood or the way you feel physically?
I feel really anxious, like there’s this tight feeling in my chest. Sometimes I get shaky or kind of nauseous. Mostly, I just feel really heavy and sad. It makes me want to just hide from everyone.
When you feel that urge to hide or withdraw, what do you usually do, and how does that affect your feelings afterward?

I usually just avoid people. I’ll ignore messages or keep to myself, even if someone tries to talk to me. Sometimes I just distract myself with my phone or TV. It helps for a bit, but then I feel even more alone.
What do you think might happen if you allowed yourself to reach out to someone instead of avoiding them when you’re feeling this way?

Honestly, I just worry that I’d say the wrong thing or bother them. I feel like people would just get annoyed or think less of me. Part of me knows maybe that’s not true, but I can’t shake the feeling. It’s easier to just keep quiet.
When you notice that part of you questioning those worries, what evidence comes to mind that supports or challenges the idea that people would get annoyed or think less of you?

I guess sometimes people have been patient with me or tried to check in, so that kind of makes me question my worries a bit. But I also remember times when I felt ignored or like I was an afterthought, so that sticks with me more. Even if someone says they care, I just have a hard time believing it. It’s like I’m always waiting for the other shoe to drop.

ccd.relevant_history: Throughout my life, I’ve often felt overlooked and rejected, especially in social and romantic situations. Growing up, I rarely received positive affirmations about my appearance or worth, which made me internalize the idea that I am not attractive or lovable. These experiences have deeply shaped how I view myself and interact with others.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: I tend to believe that if I’m not physically appealing, I won’t be accepted or valued by others. I also think that in order to be liked or loved, I have to meet certain standards of attractiveness, which I never seem to reach. This makes me feel like I’m always falling short and that people won’t invest in me emotionally.
ccd.coping_strategies: I often avoid social gatherings or dating because I’m afraid of being rejected or judged for how I look. Sometimes I try to hide or downplay myself, hoping not to draw attention to what I see as my flaws. Other times, I might excessively seek reassurance about my appearance, but it rarely feels lasting or genuine.
ccd.situation: I was invited to a close friend’s party where many people I don’t know well would be attending, including some potential romantic interests. I felt anxious and unsure about going because I worried about how I would be perceived and whether anyone would find me attractive or worth talking to.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one will want to talk to me because I’m not good-looking.” “If I don’t look perfect, I’ll just be ignored or dismissed.” “I’m going to embarrass myself because I’m not attractive enough.”
ccd.emotions: I felt anxious, sad, and ashamed. There was a deep sense of loneliness and hopelessness, as if I was doomed to be invisible and unloved. I also experienced frustration with myself for feeling this way, which made me want to withdraw even more.
ccd.behaviors: I ended up staying at home and avoiding the party altogether. When I do go out, I tend to keep to the sidelines, not initiating conversations or showing much of myself. Sometimes I obsessively check my appearance in mirrors or my phone, trying to fix perceived flaws.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um… it’s been pretty average, I guess. Nothing major happened, but I did get invited to a party by one of my friends, which threw me off a bit. I’ve mostly just been keeping to myself and trying to get through the days. I don’t know, I just feel kind of… uneasy, I suppose.
When you think about the invitation to the party, what kind of thoughts go through your mind?

Well… I guess the first thing I think is that I probably won’t really fit in, or that no one will really want to talk to me if I go. I worry a lot about how I’ll come across, especially around people I don’t know well. It’s like, I imagine myself just standing awkwardly in a corner or getting ignored. Honestly, sometimes I just feel like I might end up regretting even showing up.
When you imagine yourself standing awkwardly or being ignored, how does that make you feel in your body?

Um… it makes me feel really tense, I guess. My stomach sort of knots up, and I get this heavy feeling in my chest, like it’s harder to breathe. Sometimes my face gets hot, too, like I’m embarrassed just thinking about it. It’s honestly exhausting, even before anything actually happens.
What do you usually do when you start to feel this tension and heaviness—do you find yourself avoiding certain situations or doing something to cope?

Yeah, I guess I do tend to avoid things a lot. Like with the party, I ended up just not going at all because it felt easier than putting myself through all that anxiety. Even when I do force myself to go out, I usually stick to the edges of the room and try not to draw attention. Sometimes I just find an excuse to leave early, or I’ll spend ages in the bathroom fixing my hair or checking my phone.
When you notice yourself avoiding or sticking to the edges at social events, what do you tell yourself about what might happen if you tried to engage more?

I guess I tell myself that if I tried to talk to people or actually put myself out there, I’d just end up embarrassing myself. Like, maybe I’d say something awkward, or they’d look at me and think I’m not worth their time. Part of me just assumes they wouldn’t be interested or would find me off-putting somehow. It’s hard not to picture people just brushing me off or wishing I’d go away.
Have there been times in the past when you surprised yourself by how people actually responded when you did try to engage, even if you were worried they might react negatively?

I mean… sometimes, I guess there have been moments where things didn’t go as badly as I expected. Like, once in a while someone will talk to me and it’s not horrible, or they seem genuinely friendly. But even when that happens, a part of me wonders if they’re just being polite or if they secretly think I’m weird. I can’t really relax or believe it’s real, so I end up overanalyzing everything afterward.

core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt ignored and rejected by my family, which made me believe I wasn’t worthy of love or attention. Past experiences of being excluded at family gatherings reinforced my sense of isolation.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I get close to others, I will end up hurt or abandoned. People will leave me because I am not lovable. It’s safer to keep my distance than risk being rejected again.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to avoid family events or withdraw emotionally to protect myself from feeling hurt. Sometimes I act distant or shut down conversations to prevent others from getting too close.
ccd.situation: During a family dinner, everyone was chatting and laughing, but I felt disconnected and left out, as if no one really wanted me there or cared about what I had to say.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one here wants me around. I’m always going to be alone because I’m not good enough for them.”
ccd.emotions: I felt sad, lonely, and rejected. There was also a sense of frustration and helplessness.
ccd.behaviors: I stayed quiet, avoided eye contact, and eventually left the room to be alone. I didn’t try to join the conversation or express how I was feeling.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bound to be alone.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

I don’t know, it’s just been... whatever. Nothing really changes. Family stuff is the same as always—everyone just does their thing and I sort of float around. Honestly, I don’t even know why I bother showing up sometimes.
When you think about showing up and feeling like you’re just floating around, what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you in those moments?

I mean, what do you expect? Obviously I feel like I don’t belong there. It’s like, no matter what I do, nobody really cares if I’m around or not. Honestly, sometimes I just want to disappear so I don’t have to deal with it.

That sounds really painful to feel like you don’t belong and that others don’t notice you; when you have those thoughts about wanting to disappear, what do you notice happening inside you emotionally or physically?

I don’t know, I just get this heavy feeling in my chest—like something is pressing down on me. Sometimes I feel like I want to cry, but then I just get angry instead. And then I sort of shut down, you know? I just stop talking and hope people leave me alone.
When you notice that heavy feeling in your chest and the shift from wanting to cry to feeling angry, what do you usually do next, and how does that affect your interactions with others?

Honestly, I just pull back even more. If someone tries to talk to me, I’ll either snap at them or give one-word answers until they stop asking. It’s just easier that way, so I don’t have to pretend everything’s fine. But then they act surprised I’m in a bad mood, like they have no idea why.

It sounds like pulling back feels like a way to protect yourself, but it also seems to create some distance with others; what do you think you need from the people around you when you’re feeling this way?

I don’t even know what I need, honestly. Part of me wants someone to actually notice and care, but at the same time, I don’t really trust that they would. I just get tired of feeling invisible or like I’m just in the way. So I end up just keeping to myself because it feels safer, even if it sucks.

That sounds really exhausting, feeling stuck between wanting connection and not trusting it will happen; when you think about reaching out or sharing more with someone, what’s the part of you that holds you back from doing so?

I guess I just expect it to go badly. Like, if I say something about how I’m really feeling, it’ll either get brushed off or make things even more awkward. I’ve tried before, and it just felt pointless. So now I just keep it to myself, even though it’s lonely.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced multiple financial setbacks in recent years, including job instability and unexpected expenses. These events have reinforced a sense of unpredictability and lack of control over their financial situation.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I cannot manage my finances perfectly, then I am failing. I have to keep a tight grip on my money or everything will fall apart. Asking for help means I am weak and incapable.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to avoid looking at bills or bank statements to reduce anxiety but then feels overwhelmed when issues pile up. They may also withdraw from discussing financial matters with others and try to control spending rigidly without flexibility.
ccd.situation: Facing bills and mounting expenses, the patient feels overwhelmed by the uncertainty of their financial future and worries about making ends meet.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I have no control over this situation.” “I’m going to lose everything.” “There’s nothing I can do to fix this.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, hopelessness, frustration, and a low sense of self-worth.
ccd.behaviors: Avoiding checking finances, isolating from others to hide struggles, and either overspending impulsively or becoming overly restrictive in budgeting.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um... it’s been okay, I guess. Just the usual stuff, nothing special. I’ve been busy with work and things at home. Nothing much to report, really.
When you say "the usual stuff," are there any specific moments or situations recently that stood out to you, even if they felt small?
Well… I got some bills in the mail, and I kind of just left them on the table. I know I should open them, but I just didn’t feel up to it. Other than that, not much else happened.
What thoughts or feelings come up for you when you think about opening those bills?
I just feel kind of tense, I guess. Like, I don’t really want to know what’s in there. It’s… overwhelming. I’d rather just not think about it.
When you notice that feeling of tension and wanting to avoid the bills, what do you tell yourself about the situation or about yourself?

I don’t know... I try to tell myself it’s not a big deal, but it still feels heavy. Sometimes I just think, “I can’t deal with this right now.” Or, like, “What’s the point? It’s always something.” I don’t really like thinking about it too much.
When you think “I can’t deal with this right now” or “It’s always something,” how does that affect what you do next?

I usually just walk away or distract myself with something else. Sometimes I’ll scroll on my phone or just leave the bills where they are. I guess I kind of hope they’ll just go away if I ignore them. It’s not helping, but it feels easier at the time.
What do you notice happens afterward, either in your feelings or your situation, when you choose to distract yourself instead of dealing with the bills?

After a while, I just start feeling more stressed. The bills are still there, and I know I haven’t dealt with them. Sometimes it gets harder to relax, even when I’m not thinking about them directly. It’s like there’s this pressure in the back of my mind.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and blamed in my family, which made me believe I wasn’t good enough. In past relationships, I have been afraid to express my true feelings, fearing rejection or conflict.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I speak up or assert myself, people will get upset or leave me. I have to put others’ needs before my own to keep them happy. If I’m not perfect or accommodating, I am unlovable.
ccd.coping_strategies: I try to please others by agreeing with them and avoiding disagreements, even when I feel hurt. I avoid bringing up my own needs or concerns to prevent conflict. I often apologize excessively to smooth things over.
ccd.situation: My partner seemed distant last night and didn’t respond much when I tried to talk about our plans for the weekend.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I must have done something wrong again.” “If I push too hard, they’ll get angry and leave me.” “I’m just not good enough for them.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, sadness, and fear of abandonment.
ccd.behaviors: I withdrew and stopped trying to communicate, hoping to avoid making things worse. I kept myself busy with chores to distract from my feelings and didn’t share what was bothering me.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

Um, it’s been okay, I guess. Work was a little busy, but nothing I couldn’t handle. I’ve just been trying to keep up with things and not let anyone down. Sorry, I don’t really have much to complain about.
It sounds like you’re managing a lot right now—when you think about keeping up and not letting anyone down, what kind of thoughts go through your mind?

Oh, um, I guess I just feel like I need to make sure everyone’s happy with me, you know? I worry that if I mess up or disappoint someone, it’ll be a big deal. Maybe I think about whether I said or did the right thing. I just really don’t want to be a burden.
That sounds like a lot of pressure to carry. When you notice those worries about disappointing others, how does your body feel or how do you usually react in those moments?

Yeah, sometimes I feel kind of tense, like my shoulders get all tight. My stomach gets a bit knotty, too. Usually I just try to stay quiet and not make things worse. I guess I focus on what I can do to fix things or distract myself.
Noticing that tension and tightness sounds important—when you try to fix things or distract yourself, what usually happens to your feelings or the situation afterward?

Um, honestly, I guess the feelings don’t really go away. I might feel a bit better for a while if I keep busy, but the worry kind of sticks around underneath. It’s like, I’m just waiting to see if things get worse. I try not to show it though.
It sounds like the worry stays with you even when you’re busy. When you find yourself waiting for things to get worse, what kind of thoughts or predictions come up about what might happen?

Yeah, sometimes I just keep thinking, like, maybe I’ve messed up without realizing it. I worry that someone will get upset with me, or maybe want to distance themselves. I guess I start to imagine people pulling away or being disappointed. I try to tell myself it’s probably nothing, but it’s hard to believe that sometimes.
Those thoughts about people pulling away sound really tough. When you try to tell yourself it’s probably nothing, what helps you believe that, or what makes it hard to believe?

Um, sometimes if the other person acts normal later, I can kind of relax and think maybe it was just in my head. But if they still seem distant or quiet, it’s a lot harder—I keep wondering what I did wrong. I guess I just always think it’s my fault, even if there’s no real proof. It’s hard not to jump to that conclusion.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has faced ongoing financial difficulties, including job instability and mounting debt, which have made them feel powerless in managing their situation. Past experiences of relying on others for financial help have reinforced a sense of vulnerability and lack of control.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: "If I try to take control, it won’t make a difference." "Other people are in charge of my financial fate." "I can’t change what’s happening to me."
ccd.coping_strategies: Avoiding budgeting or financial planning, procrastinating on addressing bills, and withdrawing from discussions about money to escape feelings of stress and helplessness.
ccd.situation: The patient is worried about paying upcoming bills and fears losing their home due to financial strain.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: "I'm never going to get out of this mess." "No matter what I do, I’m stuck." "I’m just a victim of bad luck."
ccd.emotions: Hopelessness, anxiety, sadness, and frustration.
ccd.behaviors: Withdrawing from friends and family, avoiding looking at bank statements, and not seeking help or advice about financial options.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?

It's been a rough week, to be honest. I just keep thinking about all the bills piling up and I can't seem to focus on anything else. I don't really want to talk to anyone about it. It all just feels pretty overwhelming.
When you notice those thoughts about the bills piling up, what typically goes through your mind in that moment?

I usually just think, "Here we go again." Like, no matter what I do, it’s just going to get worse. I feel like I’ll never get out from under it. It makes me want to just ignore everything for a bit, even though I know that doesn’t help.
That sounds really tough. When you think, “Here we go again,” and feel like things will only get worse, how does that affect what you do next?

Honestly, I just want to shut down. I’ll avoid checking my bank account or opening any new bills. Sometimes I just lie on the couch and try to distract myself with TV. I know I should probably do something about it, but I just can’t bring myself to.
It makes sense you want to shut down when it feels overwhelming. What do you notice happens to your feelings or stress levels after you spend time distracting yourself this way?

I guess it helps for a little while, like I can forget about everything for a bit. But then as soon as I stop, all the worry just comes rushing back, sometimes even worse. I feel kind of guilty too, like I’m just making things harder for myself. It’s like I’m stuck in a loop I can’t get out of.
That cycle sounds really exhausting. When you notice that guilt creeping in, what do you tell yourself about your ability to handle the situation?

Honestly, I just start thinking that I’m not strong enough to deal with any of it. I tell myself I should be able to handle it, but I just can’t. It feels like no matter what I try, it won’t actually help. I end up feeling pretty useless.
It sounds like you’re being really hard on yourself. When you think you’re not strong enough, what would you say to a close friend who was feeling the same way?

I’d probably tell them they’re not alone and that it’s not all their fault. I’d try to remind them that even small steps can make a difference. I’d definitely be more understanding with them than I am with myself. I guess it’s easier to be kind to someone else.

ccd.relevant_history: The patient has experienced repeated rejection and criticism in past relationships, leading to a deep sense of not being good enough. They often felt judged for their values and actions, which reinforced a negative view of themselves.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t always make others happy, they will leave me or think badly of me. Being a good person means never upsetting anyone. If I’m immoral, people will reject me.
ccd.coping_strategies: The patient tends to agree quickly with others, avoids expressing their own needs, and apologizes excessively to prevent conflict. They suppress their own feelings to maintain harmony, even at personal cost.
ccd.situation: During a disagreement with their partner about spending time apart, the patient worries they have done something wrong and fears their partner will think less of them.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m a bad person for wanting time alone; this means I’m immoral and unlovable.” “If I upset them, they might leave me.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, and sadness dominate, accompanied by a feeling of being unworthy of love and acceptance.
ccd.behaviors: The patient immediately tries to soothe their partner, apologizing repeatedly and suppressing their own needs to avoid conflict and rejection.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Oh, um... it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too exciting, really. I’ve just tried to keep busy and not cause any problems for anyone. Sorry, I’m not sure if that’s what you meant.
It sounds like you’ve been focusing on staying busy and avoiding conflict—what kinds of thoughts go through your mind when you’re trying to keep things calm?

Um, well, I guess I just worry a lot about saying the wrong thing or making someone upset. I keep thinking, “What if I mess up or disappoint them?” Honestly, I find myself kind of replaying conversations in my head, hoping I didn’t offend anyone. I just want people to be happy with me, you know?
That sounds really challenging. When you notice yourself replaying those conversations, what feelings tend to come up for you in the moment?

Yeah, it can be pretty exhausting sometimes. Usually, I just feel kind of anxious, like my chest gets tight. I also end up feeling guilty, even if I’m not sure I did anything wrong. Sometimes I just wish I could stop thinking about it, but it’s hard.
It sounds like those anxious and guilty feelings are quite intense. What do you usually do when you notice these thoughts and feelings starting to take over?

Um, I guess I just try to fix things right away, like apologizing a lot or checking if the other person is okay with me. Sometimes I’ll just agree with whatever they want, even if I’m not totally comfortable. I really don’t want to make things worse, so I kind of just go along with it. I know it’s probably not the best, but it feels safer that way.
It makes sense that you want to keep things peaceful. When you go along with others even if you feel uncomfortable, how do you feel about yourself afterward?

I mean, I feel relieved at first, like maybe I avoided a problem. But after a while, I just feel kind of empty or frustrated with myself. Sometimes I wonder why I can’t just say what I really want. Honestly, I end up feeling pretty small—like my own needs just aren’t that important.
That sounds really tough, feeling like your needs get lost. Can you remember a recent time when you did express what you really wanted—what happened, and how did that feel?

Um… honestly, it doesn’t happen very often. But a few days ago, I told my partner I needed some time alone to just recharge. Right after I said it, I felt super nervous—they seemed kind of upset, or at least not happy about it. I immediately regretted bringing it up and felt really bad, like maybe I’d done something wrong.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt overlooked and unimportant in my family. When I tried to express myself, my opinions were dismissed or ignored, which made me feel like I didn’t matter. These experiences have left me believing that I’m not valuable to those around me.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I show my true self or speak up, others will reject or ignore me. I must stay quiet and keep my feelings to myself to avoid being seen as a burden or failure. It’s safer not to expect much from others because I don’t deserve their attention or care.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to hold back during family conversations and avoid sharing my thoughts or feelings. I keep my distance emotionally to protect myself from potential rejection. Sometimes, I withdraw or stay silent, hoping to avoid conflict or disappointment.
ccd.situation: During a family gathering, when everyone is sharing updates and plans, I stay quiet and listen without contributing. I feel like my input isn’t wanted or valued, so I hold back from speaking up.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “No one cares what I have to say.” “I’m just wasting everyone’s time.” “If I say something, they’ll think I’m stupid or annoying.”
ccd.emotions: I feel sad, lonely, and invisible. There’s also a quiet sense of shame and anxiety about being judged or dismissed.
ccd.behaviors: I stay silent and withdrawn, avoiding eye contact and participation. I might leave the room early or find a way to disengage from the family interaction.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Um… it was okay, I guess. Nothing special really happened. Just the usual stuff at work and at home.
When you say “nothing special,” what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you during those usual moments?

I don’t know… I guess I just kind of go through the motions. Sometimes I feel like I’m not really part of things, even when I’m around people. It’s hard to explain. I just try not to think about it too much.
That sounds difficult—when you notice feeling like you’re not really part of things, what do you tell yourself about that experience?

Um, I usually just think it’s better if I don’t say much. I just tell myself it doesn’t really matter what I say anyway. People probably don’t notice if I’m quiet. It just feels easier to keep to myself.
When you tell yourself it doesn’t matter what you say and that it’s easier to keep to yourself, how does that affect what you do next or how you feel?

I end up just staying quiet and not joining in. Sometimes I feel kind of left out or alone, but I’m used to it. I don’t really expect people to want to hear from me. It makes things feel a bit heavy, I guess.
When you feel left out or alone and that heaviness, what do you usually do to try to cope with those feelings?

I usually just keep to myself more, maybe go to my room or find something to do alone. Sometimes I distract myself with my phone or music. I try not to make it obvious that I’m upset. I just wait for the feeling to go away.
When you distract yourself or wait for the feeling to pass, do you notice if those feelings change at all, or do they tend to stay the same?

Most of the time, the feelings just stay there in the background. They don’t really go away, even if I try to ignore them. Sometimes they get a bit less intense, but they always kind of linger. It’s like they’re just waiting for me.

core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

ccd.relevant_history: Growing up, I often felt blamed for problems in the family and was told I caused pain to others. I learned to keep my distance because I believed showing my true self would only make things worse. These experiences made me feel deeply flawed and unlovable.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If people get to know the real me, they will see how dangerous and toxic I am and will leave or reject me. I have to protect others from me by hiding my feelings and not getting too close. Being bad means I don’t deserve kindness or connection.
ccd.coping_strategies: I avoid opening up or sharing my feelings with family members and friends. I try to control situations to prevent conflict or harm. Sometimes I withdraw completely to keep others safe from me, even if it makes me feel lonely.
ccd.situation: During a family gathering, someone brings up a past argument, and I feel everyone is silently blaming me for how things went wrong.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “They think I’m the problem. I’m poisonous and will hurt them if I say anything. I don’t belong here.”
ccd.emotions: I feel ashamed, scared, and isolated. There’s a heavy weight of guilt and sadness that makes me want to disappear.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet and avoid eye contact, physically removing myself from the group. I might leave early or avoid future gatherings to keep from causing trouble.
ccd.core_belief_fine: I am bad - dangerous, toxic, evil.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
It’s been alright, I guess. Nothing major happened. I just kept to myself mostly. Family stuff was kind of tense this weekend, but I tried to stay out of it.
When you say the family stuff was tense, what kinds of thoughts or feelings were coming up for you in those moments?

I felt pretty anxious, honestly. It’s like, when things get tense, I automatically worry that I did something wrong or that people are upset because of me. I tried to just stay quiet and not draw attention. I mostly felt nervous and kind of… out of place.
When you notice yourself feeling nervous and out of place, what do you usually tell yourself about the situation or about yourself?

I guess I start thinking that maybe everyone would be better off if I just kept my mouth shut. Sometimes I tell myself it’s safer if I don’t get involved. I worry that if I say the wrong thing, I’ll just make everything worse. So I just try to disappear, pretty much.
What do you think might happen if you tried speaking up or sharing your thoughts during those tense moments, rather than staying quiet?
Honestly, I’m scared it would just blow up. Like, people would get upset or blame me for making things worse. I worry they’d see me as the problem. I don’t really trust that it would go well.
Have you had any past experiences where speaking up led to those kinds of negative reactions, or is this more of a worry based on what might happen?

Yeah, it’s happened before. Sometimes when I tried to explain myself or just say how I felt, things ended up getting more heated. People would say I was being difficult or that I was just making things about me. After a while, I just stopped trying because it never seemed to help.
When those situations happened, was there ever a time when expressing yourself led to a more positive or understanding response, even if it was rare?

I’m trying to think… Maybe once or twice, someone seemed to listen, but it didn’t really last. Most of the time, things would just go back to the old patterns. It’s hard for me to believe that speaking up will really change anything. I usually just end up feeling worse about myself after.

ccd.relevant_history: I often felt overlooked and unsupported growing up, especially when I struggled with tasks or social situations. My family expected me to manage on my own, which made me doubt my abilities and feel like I couldn’t rely on myself or others.
ccd.intermediate_beliefs: If I don’t do everything perfectly, people will be disappointed or upset with me. I have to keep others happy to avoid rejection because I’m not capable on my own.
ccd.coping_strategies: I tend to agree with others quickly and say yes to requests, even when I’m uncomfortable, to avoid conflict or criticism. I often over-apologize and downplay my own needs to make sure I’m accepted.
ccd.situation: In a group meeting at work, I’m asked to share my ideas, but I feel unsure and worry that what I say won’t be good enough.
ccd.automatic_thoughts: “I’m going to mess this up.” “They probably think I’m useless.” “If I say something wrong, they will judge me.”
ccd.emotions: Anxiety, shame, and a strong fear of being rejected or seen as incompetent.
ccd.behaviors: I stay quiet or give very brief responses, avoid eye contact, and try to make myself small so I don’t draw attention. I might also agree with others’ opinions even if I don’t truly believe them.

messages.content: How has your week been so far?
Oh, um, it’s been okay, I guess. Nothing too out of the ordinary. Work’s been a little busy, but I’m managing. I hope that’s alright—sorry, I know that’s not very specific.
It’s completely okay to start there. When you say work has been busy, what kinds of thoughts or feelings come up for you during those busy moments?

Um, I guess I just worry about keeping up and not making mistakes. I find myself double-checking things a lot because I don’t want to let anyone down. Sometimes I get a bit anxious that I’ll say or do the wrong thing. Sorry, I know I shouldn’t overthink it so much.
That sounds really challenging. When you notice yourself double-checking or feeling anxious about making mistakes, what do you usually tell yourself in those moments?

Oh, um, I usually just try to tell myself to not mess things up. I worry people are watching and judging what I’m doing, so I keep thinking, “Don’t say anything stupid.” Sometimes I even think, “They probably think I’m not really good at this.” I know it sounds silly—I probably shouldn’t let it get to me so much.
It makes sense that those thoughts would feel uncomfortable. How do these thoughts about being judged or not being good enough affect the way you behave or feel during work?

Um, I guess I get quieter, like I don’t want to draw attention to myself. Sometimes I just agree with what others say so I don’t risk sounding wrong. I’ll try to keep my head down and just do what’s asked of me. Honestly, I feel kind of tense and worried most of the time, but I’m not sure what else to do.

It sounds like staying quiet helps you feel a bit safer, but it also keeps you feeling tense. When you go along with others’ ideas even if you’re unsure, what do you notice happens afterward—how do you feel about yourself or the situation?

Um, well, I feel relieved at first because I didn’t make waves or upset anyone. But later I usually start second-guessing myself, like maybe I should’ve spoken up or maybe people notice I’m just agreeing. Sometimes I feel kind of disappointed in myself, honestly. I’m sorry, I probably just make things more complicated than they need to be.
It sounds like there’s a mix of relief and self-doubt after those moments. When you think about speaking up, what’s the most difficult part or the thing that worries you the most?

I guess I’m really scared I’ll say something wrong and everyone will think less of me. I worry they’ll see that I don’t really know what I’m talking about. I don’t want people to be disappointed or annoyed with me. I just—um, I really don’t want to look foolish in front of them.
